## Abstract

This critical literature review accounts for an academic discussion that has been grouped under the labels of Marine Social Sciences, Maritime Sociology, Sociology of the Oceans, and Social (or Human) Oceanography. It also considers writings that, without suggesting a "new discipline" in their titles, emphasize the expected contributions of social sciences to fisheries, coastal management, and, more broadly, the ecosystem approach. This review aims to summarize these proposals, suggesting ways to group them according to their interdisciplinary ambition, reasoning styles, or comprehensive view of the issue.

Critical Review of Literature on the Interaction of Social Sciences in Contemporary Marine Studies

Armando Alonso Pérez Pérez

Introduction

The convergence of social sciences with other branches of human knowledge is a significant aspect

of the history of academic disciplines. Rather than arbitrary intersections between two distinct and

independent paths, we observe intertwined developments that connect the context of society and

the individual with both technological materiality and the most elusive conceptual abstractions. For

example, investigations into cellular development during a particular era provide metaphors for

observations on social rise or decline (Esposito, 2014); conversely, the identification of numerical

regularities in population records, for instance, laid the groundwork for the probabilistic

understanding of natural phenomena (Hacking, 1990).

The field of ocean sciences, or oceanography, has amassed a vast body of knowledge since its

foundation in the late 19th century. A snapshot of the accumulated knowledge about the sea in

1942 is encapsulated in the book The Oceans. Their Physics, Chemistry and General Biology

(Sverdrup et al.), which contained 1,087 pages and seven attached maps. Today, the Encyclopedia

of Ocean Sciencescomprises six volumes with a total of 4,361 pages (Steele et al., 2001). This

increase reflects a theoretical body encapsulated in an encyclopedic format, adding to the current

volume of oceanographic data: the equivalent of a digital duplicate of the ocean itself

1

(Lehman,

2016).

Expanding the scope to account for the relationships between techno-scientific knowledge and the

sociopolitical life that sustains it(particularly in relation to the United Nations' Sustainable

Development Goals), we find a starting point in the mid-20th century. During this period, questions

emerged such as: Is it possible to reorient the development of science and technology, which seems

to be autonomous? Does control over nature also imply control over human beings? How does the

scientific worldview impact traditional institutional frameworks? In essence, under what conditions

will humans (divided in so many ways) be able to capable not only of avoiding destruction but of

living together relatively well on the planet? (Habermas, 1968; Hoffman, 1966).

1

Only the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) of the USA has a catalog with 91,948

data sets (https://data.noaa.gov/dataset/)

The incorporation of these questions into decision-making models aimed at linking knowledge with

action to achieve interconnected sociopolitical goals, along with the pursuit of increasingly

consensual knowledge, marks the origin of the relationship between science and technology for

environmental management (Haas et al., 1977). This expansion and interconnection of sociopolitical

objectives, crystallized in planning, have called upon the social sciences from the beginning

2

,

perhaps as disciplines that closely relate to symbolically constructed domains. Today, this call comes

from contemporary marine studies.

While concerns about aquatic resources and spaces are not new to humanity, the extent, intensity,

and diversity of these concerns today are unprecedented: food, hydrocarbons, minerals,

desalinated water, ornaments, genetic resources, scientific information, navigation, cablingand

pipelines, tourism and recreation, marine energies, geoengineering, waste management,

conservation, territorial limits, and military activities (Jouffray et al., 2020; Kolodziej, 2020). Equally

diverse are the perspectives from which social sciences are asked to intervene in this array of

interests, from their strongholds in sociology, political science, law, psychology, anthropology,

ethnography, history, and geography.

This critical literature review accounts for an academic discussion that has been grouped under the

labels of Marine Social Sciences, Maritime Sociology, Sociology of the Oceans, and Social (or Human)

Oceanography. It also considers writings that, without suggesting a "new discipline" in their titles,

emphasize the expected contributions of social sciences to fisheries, coastal management, and,

more broadly, the ecosystem approach. This review aims to summarize these proposals, suggesting

ways to group them according to their interdisciplinary ambition, reasoning styles, or

comprehensive view of the issue.

2

Furthermore, the institutional incorporation of Social Sciences into the international order can be observed

in the following sequence of departments within the United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural

Organization (UNESCO): Social Sciences and Philosophy/Social Sciences Section 1946-1948; Department of

Social Sciences, 1948-1965; Department of Social Sciences within the Social Sciences, Humanities and Culture

Sector (SHC), 1965-1974; Sector for Social Sciences and their Applications, 1976-1984; and, Social and Human

Sciences Sector (SHS), 1984-present day. (https://atom.archives.unesco.org/unesco-social-and-human-

science-sector-and-its-predecessors).

Method

This compilation was conducted through a search in the databases of Science Direct, Sage Journals,

Springer Link, Jstor, Taylor & Francis Group, and on the platforms of Academia and Research Gate.

A total of 34 academic articles, 5 book chapters, and complementary documents

3

related to the

theme of social sciences and marine studies were reviewed. The article that prompted this critical

review was “Marine Social Sciences: Looking Towards a Sustainable Future”(2020) by Emma

McKingley, Tim Acott, and Katherine L. Yates. From this, its bibliography was examined, and those

works specifically mentioning a link between social sciences and marine studies were selected. For

this citation tree exercise, the software Citation Tree (https://www.citationtree.org/), which draws

on data from Crossref and Semantic Scholar, was used.

To organize the selected material, the articles were thematically categorized according to their focus

and scope: the conceptualization issues of marine social sciences, their most representative

historical episodes, the type of theoretical framework they fit into, and the experience of fisheries.

An Ocean of Perspectives

In 1908, the inaugural issue of the Internationale Revue der gesamten Hydrobiologie und

Hydrographie noted that the spheres of biological and geological-hydrographic knowledge of waters

were inseparable because “...whether in the form of rivers, lakes, or oceans, they are never a factor

that shapes the earth without simultaneously being a medium for life, and conversely, they are

never a medium for life without exerting a significant influence in shaping the earth’s surface”

(Sverdrup et al., 1942, p. 1). In a similar vein, 113 years later, the United Nations’ program “Decade

of Ocean Science for Sustainable Development 2021-2030”asserts that “The term ‘ocean science’

includes disciplines from both natural and social sciences, encompassing interdisciplinary topics; the

technology and infrastructure that support marine sciences; the application of this science for

societal benefit, including knowledge transfer and applications in regions lacking scientific capacity;

and the interfaces of science with public policy and innovation \[…\]. Ocean science recognizes,

respects, and embraces local and indigenous knowledge” (Implementation Plan Summary, 2021, p.

11).

3

Among them: 1 book project, 1 column, 4 conference programs, 2 conference calls for papers, 4 editorials,

1 manifesto, 1 memorandum, 2 book reviews, and 1 summary.

While this marks an institutional milestone in recognition, the concept has been addressed in

various academic circles for over two decades, whether with an emphasis on defining a maritime-

social space (Steinberg, 1999; Gordinier, 2015; Crockford, 2020; Lambach, 2022), from the

perspective of sociology (Poole, 1981; Kolodziej et al., 2010; Cocco, 2013; Longo et al., 2016;

Hannigan, 2017; Picken, n.d.), through the creation of research communities (MARE Conference;

McKinley et al., 2022; Narchi et al., 2019), or through an interest in fisheries(Symes et al., 2010;

Jentoft et al., 2014; Delgado et al., 2022). This notable intellectual productivity aligns with the role,

qualities, and expectations placed on what we now call the global ocean: engine of development,

origin and condition of life, thermal regulator, resource source, and final frontier. If each of these

labels reflects the interests of our societies (culture, social institutions, and individuals)

4

, it is not

surprising that each dimension of knowledge has something to say about the sea.

Within this ocean of viewpoints lies an underlying orientation: the ambition to frame the

relationship between society and nature differently (Longo, 2016). Although almost the entire vision

is approached from the perspective of sustainability (i.e., it includes considerations of economic

growth, well-being, and ecosystems), the nuances lie in whether the authors are focused on a)

proposing a new discipline or sub-discipline, or b) reviewing experiences of the interaction between

social sciences and marine studies.

Marine Social Sciences: Discipline or Sub-discipline?

It is useful to have a notion of the social sciences as understood by the academic circle cited here.

In the most general sense, Flyvbjerg (cited in Bavinck et al., 2020) describes them as practical

wisdom for addressing and acting on social problems within a given context. Regarding their areas,

Smith (2002) classifies them into two groups: the first includes psychology, anthropology, sociology,

political science, and law, collectively encompassing humanity, the body, society, and decision-

making. The second group includes economics, geography, history, and archaeology, where there is

an emphasis on the material relationships of humanity in space and time. In any case, they are

4

This, according to the understanding of modern societies outlined by Habermas in his Theory of

Communicative Action. Culture is articulated into validity aspects around issues oftruth (science and

technology), justice (law and morality), and taste (art). The basic institutions of society (family, church, legal

order, etc.) form functional systems (economy, state administration). Finally, the individual undergoes

processes of socialization that endow them with personality structures and serve for autonomous orientation

in their adult life.

granted privileged access to understanding the complex interplay between social dynamics and

environmental contexts human behavior for specific purposes —in this case, to analyze and change

the relationship with the marine environment.

The diagnosis is that this relationship has become problematic not only due toundesirable

consequences such as pollution and conflicts of interest but also from the very conceptualization.

Proponents of creating a new discipline or deriving a sub-discipline (primarily from sociology or

ethnography) criticize the land/sea dichotomy

5

because the complexity of the "global ocean" has

emergent processes that transcend a sharp differentiation: Shouldthe paradigm of "terrestrial

sociology" be extended to the marine realm? Should such phenomena be explained from a

terrestrial perspective? Is a uniquely marine theory possible? (Cocco, 2013; Kolodziej, 2020 & 2022).

Additionally, maritime topics have historically been dominated by the natural sciences, leaving

human dimensions relegated (McKinley et al., 2020; Van Putten et al., 2021).

Historical perspectives provide insights into the attempts to propose a new discipline or derive a

sub-discipline. The earliest effort comes from William R. Rosengren, who, in a 1973 editorial titled

"Sociological Approach to Maritime Studies: A Statement and Example," largely outlined the general

agenda as understood today. He recognized a plurality of interests attempting to establish

definitions of the marine environment and its specific uses. He also emphasized the importance of

analyzing non-economic rationalities related to the sea, such as symbolic values and aesthetic

implications at local levels.

It wasn't until 1981 that Michael Poole, in "Maritime Sociology: Towards a Delimitation of Themes

and Analytical Frameworks," revisited the definition and delimitation ofmaritime sociology. He

shared with Rosengren a sociological theoretical framework: "thesocial and cultural forces that

determine definitions of the marine environment, which in turn shape its use and exploitation" (p.

2). Poole provided a list of topics for analyzing this environment: the concept of the social system,

community and association, social action and culture, differentiation and stratification, processes,

change and technology, and conflict and deviance. Thus, in the internal historiography of attempts

to establish a new discipline or sub-discipline, Rosengren and Poole laid foundational work that

contemporary scholars continue to build upon. In the historiography of maritime social sciences,

5

Picken points out that "Underwater realities mobilize the binaries and dualisms that make sense in social

space, highlighting how these are historically situated on land" (2014, p. 12).

Poole and Rosengren emerge as precursors of the discussion (Cocco, 2013 & 2015; Kolodziej et al.,

2015 & 2022; Hannigan, 2017).

However, there is a parallel history in Poland. There, interest in maritime sociology has been present

since the institutionalization of sociology after World War II, with Ludwik Janiszewski as its main

proponent. From a theoretical standpoint, he introduced the concept of "marinization" in 1971,

describing the process through which human activity relates to the sea (socio-culturally), influences

social reality, and fosters the emergence of marine elements inboth material and non-material

aspects (Kolodziej, 2020). Although his approach emphasizes the impact of general economic

activity, it aims to name an ancient social process (predating industrialization and urbanization) that

permeates humanity. Among the approaches to the subject, this one shares more characteristics

with a general theory of society rather than an analysis of social subsystems related to marine

activities.

John Hannigan (2017) acknowledges that while maritime sociology has addressed social realities

surrounding the ocean –such as maritime professions, fishing and seafaring families, port cities, and

naval culture –since at least the mid-20th century, new contexts –ecology, piracy, immigration,

economy, and management –warrant formulating a critical political economy to address the current

state of capitalism and the ocean crisis along with its accompanying geopolitics. In the realm of

human-nature relations, he considers that post-humanist currents aptly critique the economic

order. Nonetheless, Hannigan clarifies that his proposal (Sociology of the Oceans) serves more as a

thematic glue than a conceptual or theoretical body. In other words, it would be a sub-discipline of

environmental sociology that, nonetheless, would address the qualitatively different properties of

the ocean as a space being constructed and contested.

The perspectivethat the analysis of capitalism deserves central attention is shared by several

authors (Clark, 2008; Longo, 2016; Bavinck, 2018; Lambach, 2022). In this vein, and with respect to

Latin America, the works of Gustavo Moura from Brazil and Nemer Narchi from Mexico propose a

human or social oceanography. Both, from the perspectives of human geography, ethnography, and

anthropology, emphasize the colonial and Eurocentric legacy of oceanography, the technical-

instrumental outcome of its inquiries, and how these, in service of industrialization, have caused

environmental degradation and a history of dispossession of local coastal communities. To counter

this, it is a priority to enhance marine databases with local knowledge; this, in turn, supports ethno-

sciences which have epistemological (structuring the production of knowledge), philosophical (how

reality is perceived), and cognitive (discovering universal patterns of ordering reality) value (Narchi,

2010). Moura (2020), from the perspective of socio-environmentalism, critiques the reductionist

nature of modern rationality and advocates for an "indigenous peoples' political praxis" that, in its

local knowledge, inherently includes a historical protection of biodiversity.

The Ocean as a Social Space

A common theme in the reviewed literature is the conceptualization around "the construction of a

social space." From our own perspective, this can be explained by various factors: the influence of

the linguistic turn in philosophy since the early 20th century (Habermas, 2002), the particular success

of Peter L. Berger and Thomas Luckmann's book “The Social Construction of Reality” (1966), and a

specific context: "\[social construction\] is one of the many ideas being fiercely contested inthe

American culture wars" (Hacking, 1999, p. 11).

The concept of social space "makes reference to the sensually perceptible, imaginations, and

memories as a product of society producing its own specific space. Löw develops an understanding

of space as relational arrangements of social goods and living beings at places" (Kolodziej, 2022, p.

17). Based on this framework, we find books such as “Framing the Ocean, 1700 to the Present:

Envisaging the Sea as Social Space” (Gordinier, 2015) and “The Social Construction of the Ocean”

(Steinberg, 2001). Emilio Cocco argues that "in a truly maritime sociological approach, the sea is not

only a medium but a social space, which would not be merely ‘used by society’ but rather represent

‘a space of society’" (2013, p. 5). Crockford, highlighting what kind of ocean has been emphasized

in its construction, underscores an extractivist root: the foundation of the "Blue Economy" lies in

the techno-science of deep ocean engineering, and the social license to operate, developed through

corporate social responsibility, is merely an extension of terrestrial mining practices (2020).

This perspective sharpens the critique of conceptions of the global ocean that serve

industrialization, military uses, or spatial appropriation. Lambach (2022) helps us distinguish

between two functions of technology in the construction of oceanic space: a causal function that

produces new spaces for specific uses, and an interpretive function that imbues these spaces with

social meanings and values or facilitator, and another as a medium. In the former, technology

creates potentials for action by modifying a scale (e.g., improved bathymetry enables the

identification of extractive resources). In the latter, the various modes of ocean representation act

as a bridge between interested parties and the environment: coastal mapping can have different

strategic uses depending on whether the interested party is an NGO or an oil company.

When specifying the tools used in theorizing the social construction of oceanic space, we find

borrowings from systems theory, economic geography studies on the spatiality of capitalism,

political geography on territoriality, and poststructuralist concepts on social conflict in the

production of space. All of these aim to renew a critique of territorial economic policy (Steinberg,

2001). Regarding the social sciences, Steinberg notes, "Within the social sciences more generally,

the bulk of research has focused on one or another use of the marine environment, but not on the

ocean as an integrated space that is a product of –as well as a resource for –a variety of human

uses" (p. 10).

From the perspective of its proponents, viewing the ocean as asocial space aids the normative

frameworks of ecosystem conservation in analyzing conflicting actors and interests toreach

consensually approved decisions. Thus, "New marine spatial planning (MSP) policies seek to

categorize and represent ocean spaces and activities in an effort to provide a solution to long-

standing controversies stemming from individual sector-based management" (Boucquey et al.,

2016). This approach aims to identify where biases favoringone appropriation over another exist

within the conceptualization and practices of maritime management. According to Boucquey, the

same tool can serve both to reinforce an exploitative attitude toward the environment

(neoliberalization of nature) and to expand the capacities of a community and other environmental

actors.

Past Experiences: Fisheries and Social Sciences

If there is any area within the maritime universe where the interaction with social sciences can be

documented, it is in fisheries. Since the 1970s, the research agenda in this field has expanded due

to formulations on the management of common resources (Elinor Ostrom) and the co-management

theory (public administration and fisheries) of Jentoft (Symes, 2010). One of the landmark works of

that era is James M. Acheson's called “Anthropology of Fishing” (1981). For some, it is clear that

decades of experienceinthe social sciences should suffice to address the challenges posed by

current and future marine industrialization: "... marine social science must enhance understanding

of the dynamics of industrial development in ocean and coastal spaces, including their governance

contexts" (Knol-Kauffman et al., 2020).

Another guiding element within this ocean of perspectives is the recognition of two main trends:

conservation and industrialization. Arbo et al. (2018) outline that interests around the sea are

concentrated on "Blue Growth" and environmental concerns. In this arena of contention, natural

scientists, often with substantial help from social scientists, tend to interpret the ocean as a sick

entity and a valuable natural and cultural heritage that must be protected and restored. Conversely,

industrialists, particularly engineers, emphasize the sea as an economic frontier with much

untapped resource potential. It is important to remember that these typologies serve to simplify

the discussion, as in reality, there is a blend of perspectives.

Amidst these conflicting interests, social sciences are projected as a pillar in management based on

scientific recommendations. One interpretation is that social sciences are essential for

understanding and intervening in modernization practices and limiting the privatization of

resources. Consequently, they are seen as having the potential to formulate governance

alternatives, anticipate future trends, envision desirable futures, and facilitate just social processes

(Arbo et al., 2018). This role can be categorized as strengthening capacity-building in marine and

coastal management. Smith (2002) comments, "Perhaps the best —and most problematical—

example is fisheries management, where large-scale biological research programs input into

decision-making which also involves technical gear components, the economics and management

of the industry itself on both catching and processing and marketing sides, and political and legal

inputs as well."

But what has become of the social science programs actually implemented in fisheries management

and orientation? As noted in footnote 2 of this article, the presence of social science departments

within the UN structure throughout history might surprise those who perceive social sciences as

being sidelined in marine matters. Significant examples include the establishment of the European

Social Science Fisheries Network in 1996, aimed at socially orienting fisheries research (community-

based solutions,local co-management, and rights-based approaches, territorial usage rights,

community quotas, etc.) (Symes et al., 2010). This effort was significant, leading to the

establishment of the MARE (Maritime Research) Center and symposium in 2000, which brings

together social scientists, organizes courses and international conferences, and publishes a journal

with a broad focus on social sciences, not just fisheries, called “Maritime Studies”.

Arbo (2018) even asserts that these decades of interaction between social sciences and fisheries

have developed a critical niche for understanding transformationsin the ocean and coastal

economies, housing, cultures, and social practices, although he notes this is within the context of

Northern Europe. This review is supported by other evaluations of the subject. For instance, the

article "A Decade Incorporating Social Sciences in the Integrated Marine Biosphere Research Project

(IMBeR): Much Done, Much to Do?" (Van Putten et al., 2021) highlights ten years during which social

scientists have been involved in regionally based science programs

6

. Within this conglomerate, there

was a section called the Human Dimensions Working Group, whose task was to understand the

interactions between human and oceanic systems, recognizing that humans not only influence the

marine system but also depend on it for goods and services.

The perceptions among participants in these programs suggest that for greater connection between

scientists of different disciplines, there needs to be established research priorities, access to

funding, interdisciplinary communication, and a general awakening of interest. In this regard,

several authors have focused on generating an agenda of research topics and priorities (McKinley

et al., 2020 & 2022; Bennett, 2019; Fish, 2011; Smith, 2002; Bavinck, 2018; Silver, 2015). Another

revealing perception, particularly regarding real interactionbetween natural scientists, engineers,

and social scientists, is that the former, while recognizing the value of sociology, anthropology,

history, and other disciplines, think that these may not be relevant to all the objectives and activities

of regional programs (Van Putten et al., 2021, p. 9). They perhaps see more clearly the relevance of

social knowledge in legal matters, public policy, and science communication.

According to another comprehensive survey (1200 participants) aimed at defining important issues

in maritime matters (Lacroix et al., 2016), nine main challenges were identified (p. 6):

1) Understanding the evolution of marine ecosystems

2) Ensuring the supply of food and therapeutic products from the sea

3) Improving ocean colonization

4) Developing technologies for the exploitation of marine resources

6

The historical sequence of the Integrated Marine Biosphere Research Project is as follows: in 2002, the

International Geosphere-Biosphere Programme (IGBP) and the Scientific Committee on Oceanic Research

(SCOR) created the Integrated Marine Biogeochemistry and Ecosystem Research. In 2016, the name was

changed to Integrated Marine Biosphere Research, reflecting a focus on the human dimensions of global

change and ocean sustainability. It continues to be funded by SCOR and is part of the Future Earth Global

Research Projects.

5) Risk prevention

6) Developing biotechnologies for industrial and health uses

7) Developing education, sustainable tourism, and responsible social practices

8) Establishing international maritime law adapted to current circumstances

9) Systematizing and globalizing the monitoring and control of marine activities

The conclusion is clear: the maritime powers —referring to the United States, the European Union,

and China in this study—show a clear interest in securing their national resources rather than

promoting international cooperation for safe trade and sustainable exploitation.

Reflection from the fisheries sector reappears in this context. Even within governance frameworks

that include social sciences, there are indications that social aspects and the dimension of

knowledge are inadequately theorized and represented (Jentoft et al., 2014). Improvements in

governance schemes include contributions regarding the conceptualization of "community," as

there are often problems of exclusion due to generalization in fisheries. Communities and their

relationships are highly diverse, so a typology encompassing modes of life, livelihood, or work could

better inform environmental policies

7

(Delgado et al., 2022). This concern, with an emphasis on

communities, is present in social impact analyses (due to undesirable dependency on "green

infrastructures") (Arkema et al., 2017), their integration into a "trans-global oceanic world"

(Wozniak, 2013), and attention to "missing layers" of analysis.

Marine Social Sciences: A Panoramic Reflection

The current enthusiasm for reflecting on social sciences and maritime studies appears to stem from

various sources, as observed in this review: from the proposals for a new discipline or sub-discipline,

the influence of social construction theory of spaces, criticism of capitalism, and fisheries-focused

approaches. Undoubtedly, all these efforts aim to foster a change in human relationships with the

7

In fact, this kind of criticism was the basis for the 2002 reform of the political-legal framework of the

European Union known as the Common Fisheries Programme (CFP), as it brought about problems such as a

highly centralized decision-making system applied extensively to highly diverse European fishing areas, both

politically and ecologically. The reform introduced an ecosystem approach with emphasis on resource

conservation and the development of long-term management plans, as well as the establishment of networks

of Regional Advisory Councils that brought together key local stakeholders in specific areas to provide practical

advice on fisheries public policies (Symes et al., 2010).

marine environment, whether through conceptualization or the implementation of governance

tools. Viewed from a systemic perspective, the increasing complexity due to the multitude of

competing interests and the vast empirical and physical knowledge of the ocean makes

sustainability goals a Herculean task. Social sciences are called upon primarily for their capacity to

decipher issues related to behavior, institutional development, and impact assessments (Symes et

al., 2010).

Given the highly diversified nature of this field, the very plurality of perspectives in social sciences

is a controversial element in itself. Advocates for creating a new discipline or sub-discipline are not

only interested in defending the specificity and relevance of social analysis regarding oceanic basin

issues but also see the opportunity to renew social sciences through the metaphors and qualities

evoked by the ever-moving water: fluidity, plasticity, resistance to control, etc. (Cocco, 2013;

SSaSea, n.d.; Picken, n.d.). Other perspectives, even within the governance framework and its

practical demands, express dissatisfaction with reducing social sciences to mere development

indicators or impact assessment tools (Moura, 2020; Arbo et al., 2018).

In the questfor a foundation of social knowledge, there are proposals that attempt to include

philosophical perspectives on the issue. See, for example, the text "Five Questions to Understand

Epistemology and Its Influence on Integrative Marine Research" by Katie Moon and others (2021),

or the inclusion of the term "phronetic dimension" (referring to Aristotle's philosophy) in Jentoft's

(2014) reflection on knowledge in fisheries governance in the European Union. There seems to be a

struggle and difficulty in proposing new approaches that simultaneously contain higher-order

organizing elements. This challenge is not only theoretical but also evident in the names given to

various attempts to create communities of social researchers interested in the sea.

John Hannigan (2017) has noted that several otherproposals have failed to take off,and his

rebranding of Maritime Sociology as the Sociology of the Oceans seeks to avoid the same fate.

Nonetheless, groups have emerged and disappeared without significant impact. Even those with

institutional support and specific funding —such as the European Social Science Fisheries Network,

previously mentioned— have faced endings that leave questions. Despite three years of European

Commission funding, which allowed it to hold several workshops on socially-oriented policy, gather

a core group of 30 researchers, and develop an extensive publication program, it failed to provide a

lasting institutional framework (Symes et al., 2010, p. 270).

Some of the currently active academic communities focused on the interaction between social

sciences and maritime studies include the Marine Social Science Network, the CoLaboratory for

Social Oceanography, the Maritime Sociology group at the Institute of Sociology at the University of

Szczecin (Poland), and the Centre for Maritime Research (MARE). These groups maintain their

identity as active networks with mailing lists, regular communications

8

, conference calls,

publications, and a more or less defined focus.

Regarding the breadth of this bibliographic review, even having considered academic groups from

Latin America and Poland (omitted in McKinley's survey in the article "Development and Expansion

in the Marine Social Sciences: Insights from the Global Community" \[2022\]),a more in-depth

accounting of interest groups in the Asia-Pacific region is still needed. We know that in China, since

2001, the Institute of Marine Culture and Society was founded (Kolodziej, 2022), and the Chinese

Sociological Association has already established professional committees in maritime sociology

(Hannigan, 2017). The same diagnosis seems to hold despite the "cultural distance" from the West:

"a sociological perspective is needed to inform management, with social scientists being required

to balance the over-confident other scientists in other fields because the importance of knowledge

and the role of 'experts' is deeply social" (Ning’er cited in Hannigan, 2017).

Paradoxically, social sciences seem to be experiencing a new boom due to the demands arising from

oceanic issues, but simultaneously, they face a demand for accreditation: their value, utility, and

practical application. This is part of theirdisciplinary history. Categories and proposalsfor new

approaches continue to proliferate, whether from radical critiques of the system or frameworks

adapted to capacity-building in specific governance tasks. In any case, the struggle to consolidate

the ocean as a socio-environmental object is underway, presenting an opportunity to test

interdisciplinarity within the grand context of human history with the sea.

8

The journals that accept perspectives on social sciences and marine topics are numerous. Some examples

include Maritime Policy & Management, MaritimeStudies, Transnav: International Journal on Maritime

Navigation & Safety of Sea Transportation, Constanta Maritime University Annals, Pomorstvo/Scientific

Journal of Maritime Research, Scientific Journals of the Maritime University of Szczecin, Journal ofCoastal

Research, Asia-Pacific Journal of Marine Science & Education, and International Maritime Health.

## Abstract

The sea is certainly not one of the favourite research topics of sociology and despite a recent and new popularity among historians and geographers, the sea remains generally a stranger to contemporary sociologists. Conversely, I think that to frame and shed light on the wider theoretical implications of maritime sociology would be beneficial to all possible approaches within sociological research. In this article I intend to give my contribution to the fulfillment of the expectation of above by discussing the ways sociology can understand the sea as a truly social space, beyond any abstract dichotomies separating “grounded” societies and offshore voids. Also, I try to suggest some concrete moves to relocate maritime sociology within the wider sociological debate and to develop the heuristic potential of the sea.

## Key takeaways  ![sparkles](https://a.academia-assets.com/images/icons/sparkle.svg)  AI

1. Maritime sociology needs integration into broader sociological debates to enhance theoretical understanding.
2. The sea serves as a significant social space, challenging terrestrial biases in sociology.
3. Historical contexts highlight the sea's role in shaping modern society and identity formation.
4. Current maritime sociology lacks a unified theoretical framework despite extensive empirical research.
5. Sociologists should redefine sea-related professional categories and explore evolving social dynamics among maritime communities.

[Oceans, Space and Society: Towards a Blue Sociology (with Palgrave MacMillan)](https://www.academia.edu/9098567/Oceans_Space_and_Society_Towards_a_Blue_Sociology_with_Palgrave_MacMillan_)

[Felicity Picken](https://westernsydney.academia.edu/FelicityPicken)

Oceans, Space and Society: Towards a Blue Sociology Felicity Picken, Palgrave Macmillan Press. Oceans remain among the least measured, formatted and socialised spaces on Earth (Latour 2005). While marine-related sciences, archaeologists, geographers, historians and cultural theorists have become active in bringing oceans to the fore, there is no sociological work that attempts to do this. Consequently, sociology remains land-locked (Lambert et al. 2006) and subject to a land bias (Peters 2010) that has not only limited its utility in explanations of ocean space, but also denied it a laboratory with the potential to unlock established habits of thought. Theories about society are, by default, theories about the conditions of life on land and this statement appears utterly banal until a social life beneath the sea is contemplated. This book illustrates this position by demonstrating how forays into ocean space not only construct new relationships between humans and nature and new experiential dimensions, but also highlight the primacy of land-based co-ordinates in theorising ourselves and apprehending our place in the world. The book is also timely, since never before has there been such interest in, and earnest contemplation of, undersea worlds. These are now understood as serious contenders in climate change solutions, as spaces of refuge in the face of environmental extremes and uncertainty on land. This book provides an opportunity for scholars in the human sciences and educated readers more generally to familiarise themselves with a social space that is truly emergent, not yet properly invented or constructed, but gaining importance as life on a blue planet is more fully considered. As an emergent social space and field of inquiry, undersea is highly instructional in the social life of extreme environments and, conversely, the less extreme conditions of life on land that are taken for granted. The 21st century has witnessed an explosion in underwater imagery, in scuba diving as one of the fastest growing recreational pursuits and in aquaria, penetrating everyday spaces or else much bigger spaces, holding entire undersea ecosystems on land. Undersea is also host to elaborate and unprecedented levels of underwater development as the built environment is also being seeded undersea in underwater museums, art galleries and hotels. These are no longer dreams but are developing at pace in both the global north and south culminating in the construction of self supporting underwater habitats in anticipation of climatic induced transformation and need for alternative living space . All of these are forging new cultural economies, new social worlds and human experiences that have not yet been assembled in such a way that their effects can begin to be understood or anticipated. With cases spanning offshore Cancún, through to Florida Keys and the Maldives, to occupied France in WWII and Swiss engineering in Dubai, this book traces the way in which people have begun to be interpolated by life beneath the sea, with the explicit aim of composing the beginnings of a blue sociology. This book is distinctive in possessing three unique attributes: • Substantially, it is the first to undertake a sociological analysis of underwater worlds and to develop an understanding about how these are performed and their transformative capacities. • Sociologically, it is the first to investigate the importance and limitations of land to established ideas about human identity and social life. • Methodologically, by attending to social life in a forcefully interactive space like the ocean, this book draws upon contemporary social theory to provide a relationally material account of society when it cannot avoid the indifference of non-humans. These attributes form the basis of a critically constructive examination that enlivens contemporary sociological debates about human-nature relations in contemporary contexts. These debates include the place of non-humans, ontological knowledge and post-humanism in the real and anticipated threat of environmental crises; in the emergence of new cultural economies based on oceanic space and in the promises and challenges of life on a blue planet. The book produces a platform from which radical implications for both the discipline of sociology and the ‘futures’ in which it increasingly works are made evident. These will be relevant and translatable to the social sciences generally, to interdisciplinary field of oceanic studies and to the growing interest in Science and Technology Studies, as well as educated general readers. All undersea interventions are highly technological, scientific and social in dimension and this privileges new sociological approaches that treat science and technology, nature and humans symmetrically. Crucially, blue sociology can only be oxygenated by attending to the deployment of heterogeneous materials in social analysis, not least of which is the very lively, and to humans the life depleting, proposition of oceanic nature itself. This validates social investigations that aim to accommodate new members of society - an extended democracy of things (Latour 1991/1993) - where life on and within a blue planet is an entirely collaborative affair. The book employs contemporary sociological approaches including Actor-Network Theory, Post humanism, Post sociality and Relational Materialism, as described by key theorists in this field including Bruno Latour, John Law, Michelle Serres, Nigel Clark and John Urry.

AT HOME ON THE WAVES Human Habitation of the Sea from the Mesolithic to Today, 2019

Contrary to the idiom, people "at sea" have always been "at home." Since the Mesolithic and before, human communities have lived closely with the ocean, not simply as a domain to visit temporarily in order to extract resources, food, specimens, experiences, or literary analogies. Figurative depictions of seascapes as vast, unpeopled, and temporally constrained are at odds with the evidence depicted in this volume. Rather, oceans persistently constitute the principal organizing spaces through which many communities dwell in the world. In this volume, we present rich ethnographic data, both archaeological and anthropological, demonstrating the centrality of marine environments to the lives of peoples around the globe. While the majority of people experience the ocean as an alternative to a largely terrestrial existence, the voices we present in this volume tell a story with a very different emphasis. [Luciana Martins](https://independent.academia.edu/LucianaMartins14)

Journal of Historical Geography, 2006

This paper offers a prospectus for a version of historical geography that puts the seas and oceans at the centre of its concerns. This is pursued in three ways. First, via a discussion of the epistemological and historiographic perspectives that might be taken on geographies of the sea, which argues that the view from the ocean can develop geographies of spaces beyond the local and the national, and can attend to the relationships between the human and natural worlds. Second, through a consideration of the imaginative, aesthetic and sensuous geographies of the sea that contends that maritime worlds open up new experiential dimensions and new forms of representation. Finally, in a survey of the material and social worlds of the oceans that suggests that new life can thereby be breathed into current concerns with global political economy, material geographies, the relationship between knowledge and located practice, and the intersections of social and spatial difference. The paper also provides an introduction to a special issue on the historical geography of the sea.

[The Sea, Identity and History](https://www.academia.edu/116285103/The_Sea_Identity_and_History)

[Himanshu Prabha Ray](https://jnu.academia.edu/HimanshuPrabhaRay)

From the Bay of Bengal to the South China Sea, 2012

The focus of the book is the wide stretch of water- and sea-ways connecting the coasts of Bengal and Sri Lanka to the coast of Vietnam. The authors address three broad issues through an interdisciplinary perspective. The first relates to boat-building traditions and the communities who traversed these waters. The challenge is to use the ethnographic ‘present’ and mobility of the fishing and sailing groups for an understanding of the history of the sea and the extent to which knowledge of the waters was vital to the construction of identity of a maritime society. Linked to this movement across the waters, are the narratives of trans-locality inherent in memories of groups in the region. Long-distance pilgrimage and devotional networks have been consistent features of the cultural life of South and Southeast Asia. A third issue relates to European intervention, starting with the Portuguese and the Dutch. The engagement of the English East India Company with the countries of the Bay of Bengal was of a different order from that of its predecessors, as the Company sought to establish a maritime empire. The eighteenth century thus, raised a different set of issues with the colonization of large parts of South and Southeast Asia. How did notions of a maritime empire impact the study of the region’s past? It is these themes that we address in the volume thereby shifting the focus from chronological markers and national histories to communities who crossed the waters and the changes that these underwent in time.

[A critical analysis of research paradigms in a subset of marine and maritime scholarly thought.](https://www.academia.edu/8766193/A_critical_analysis_of_research_paradigms_in_a_subset_of_marine_and_maritime_scholarly_thought)

[Richard Meissner](https://csir-za.academia.edu/RichardMeissner)

The application and applicability of the humanities and social sciences are not always visible in the practical world. This is especially the case in technology-dependent areas like the marine and maritime sectors. In these sectors, control, prediction and recommendations that rely on technologies and their advancement are of the utmost importance. These sectors are, after all, those on which we rely for international trade, defence and security, sources of food and other energy requirements, like oil and gas. At first glance, it would appear that the sectors are devoid of the humanities and social sciences and that these have a minimal, if not marginal role to play. The humanities and social sciences consist of a number of subject fields like anthropology, economics, history, international relations, law, philosophy and sociology. These fields of enquiry are at times service providers to sectors when their services are needed. This relegates the fields to the cupboard of scientific investigation when long-term strategies are developed, which should not be the case. The humanities and social sciences should play a constant role in a human-dominated world. The maritime sector is, after all, human constructed. Trade routes, ports, harbours, warehouses, cranes, rail links and truck routes are not natural occurrences; neither are the technologies that constitute and sustain them. Because of the dominance of the human element, even in the marine environment, the humanities and social sciences can play a more fruitful role in creating opportunities and solving problems. What is more, it is not only the humanities and social sciences that are of importance, but also how humans view the world and react to it either through theoretical or concrete means. Here, paradigms and theories of various kinds from the humanities and social sciences also have their place. This chapter explores these dimensions in more detail. It starts by presenting a framework, called PULSE3, for analysing the role of the humanities and social sciences in the two sectors. The paradigms of the presentations delivered at the Integrated Marine and Maritime Technologies Workshop in October 2013 are assessed. This chapter reviews the abstracts of the presentations made at the workshop. The review of the abstracts is not representative of the state of research and development in the entire sector. It is only a snapshot of it. However, it provides useful insights into current thinking and practice in the marine and maritime sectors. The purpose of this assessment is to determine the type of paradigm that was dominant. Two paradigms, rationalism and interpretivism, are identified through the assessment. Rationalism views the researcher and reality as separate, with only one reality present. Research is able to control and predict this reality. This means that an objective reality exists beyond the human mind. Interpretivism, however, notes that the researcher and reality are inseparable in that realities are mentally constructed. Multiple realities exist and, as such, knowledge of the world is intentionally constituted through researchers’ lived experiences. There is no objective meaning (Wendt, 1999; Weber, 2004; Guba and Lincoln, 2005; Lincoln et al., 2011). An overview of the ethos of analytic eclecticism is provided and how it can aid the marine and maritime sectors. After this, the role of the humanities and social sciences in the public and government policy domain is presented. This is followed by the setting of a number of beacons that the marine and maritime sectors could follow to expand the role of the humanities and social sciences. The repertoire of theories plays a central role in this, the penultimate section of the chapter.

The topic of the sea and society is not new to sociology. Maritime spaces include oceans as well as coastal areas, spaces which are at risk nowadays. Ecological crises (overfishing, decline in biodiversity, climate change, eutrophication, ocean pollution), economic crises (de-industrialization, whaling moratorium, structural change, development of alternative industries) and cultural crises (destabilization of collective identities of seaside and island inhabitants, cultural practices of sharing, inhabitants’ knowledge, local traditions, transformations of maritime professions) cumulate in these maritime spaces. So far research on maritime issues is dominated by natural science disciplines. The protection of maritime natural sources and the need for sustainable development demands research on the ‘human factor’ and a sociological perspective on maritime spaces.

In the twenty-first century, human-caused changes are being recorded at an unprecedented scale in the oceans, radically transforming our perception of the oceans and seas. Initially seen as a space outside of society, the ocean has become a theatre of geopolitical, economic and environmental struggles that often treat the sea as ‘land’. At the same time, there is a growing consensus among scholars that terrestrial models of land-use planning are inappropriate for maritime governance. As oceans differ in function and space from terrestrial systems, the environmental and societal processes need a more holistic approach to conceptually grasp the oceans’ relevance to humanity. In this welcome scholarly move, anthropologists have more recently focused on exchanges and interactions between humans and the environment, oriented towards the ocean and marine ecology as a space of anthropogenic interference with natural processes. This chapter shows that maritime anthropology is no longer a marginal or peripheral niche, but an important vector in global connections and globalization, involving maritime‒marine and nature‒human dimensions, not only historically and in the present day, but from a future-oriented perspective as well. This paper calls for an opening of dialogue on the historical character of the Atlantic world between two fields. To date, historical sociologists researching the significance of intercivilizational encounters have not paid a great deal of attention to the case of the Americas. While historical and comparative sociology has assimilated the lessons of post-colonial critique, the startling histories of transatlantic colonialism have not had the impact on studies of civilizations carried out in this field that they should have. When it comes to the second field, Atlantic Studies, the paper argues that sociologists working in the first field have something to offer in their re-theorization of the character of long term inter-civilizational contacts. A fresh approach to the study of civilizations is sketched out here that reconstructs theoretical conclusions drawn in historical sociology in a way that will be of interest to specialists in Atlantic Studies. The first part of the paper examines the historical sociology of civilizations and sets out a new framework that revolves around a re-conception of radical difference and Otherness. In the second section, I explore how dimensions of the historical experience of transatlantic colonialism \*/such as mapping, place-naming and early ethnological curiosity \*/ constituted the Americas as a vital zone of the growing sense of civilizational superiority amongst Europeans between the 16th and 18th centuries. In this section, the article argues that civilizational sociology would profit from a systematic examination of this crucial historical zone. The conclusion puts out a call for further detailed inter-disciplinary research that combines the best insights of both the fields of Atlantic Studies and civilizational sociology.

# Toward a sociology of oceans

Author:[John Hannigan](https://go.gale.com/ps/advancedSearch.do?method=doSearch&searchType=AdvancedSearchForm&searchMethod=subject+click&userGroupName=anon%7E55b11cef&inputFieldNames[0]=AU&prodId=AONE&inputFieldValues[0]=%22John+Hannigan%22 "")

Date:
Feb. 2017


From:Canadian Review of Sociology(Vol. 54, Issue 1)

Publisher:Canadian Sociological Association

Document Type:
Report

Length:8,462 words

Lexile Measure:1560L

Abstract :

Despite covering around 70 percent of the earth's surface, the ocean has long been ignored by sociology or treated as merely an extension of land-based systems. Increasingly, however, oceans are assuming a higher profile, emerging both as a new resource frontier, a medium for geopolitical rivalry and conflict, and a unique and threatened ecological hot spot. In this article, I propose a new sociological specialty area, the "sociology of oceans" to be situated at the interface between environmental sociology and traditional maritime studies. After reviewing existing sociological research on maritime topics and the consideration of (or lack of consideration) the sea by classic sociological theorists, I briefly discuss several contemporary sociological approaches to the ocean that have attracted some notice. In the final section of the paper, I make the case for a distinct sociology of oceans and briefly sketch what this might look like. One possible trajectory for creating a shared vision or common paradigm, I argue, is to draw on Deleuze and Guattari's dialectical theory of the smooth and the striated. Meme s'il couvre 70% de la surface de la Terre, l'ocean a ete longtemps ignore en sociologie ou traite comme une extension des systemes terrestres. De plus en plus, toutefois, l'ocean retient l'attention, en etant vu comme une nouvelle frontiere en termes de ressources, un medium pour les rivalites et les conflits geopolitiques, et un lieu ecologique nevralgique et unique. Dans cet article, je propose une nouvelle specialisation sociologique, la 'sociologie des oceans', se situant dans l'interface entre la sociologie environnementale et les etudes maritimes traditionnelles. Apres une recension de la recherche sociologique existante sur les sujets maritimes et la prise en compte (ou l'absence de prise en compte) de l'ocean par les theoriciens de la sociologie classique, je discute brievement quelques approches sociologiques contemporaines de l'ocean ayant attire l'attention. Dans la derniere partie de l'article, j'insiste sur le besoin d'une sociologie distincte de l'ocean et je presente brievement a quoi cela pourrait ressembler. Une voie possible pour creer une vision commune ou un paradigme, selon moi, est de s'inspirer de la theorie dialectique du lisse et du strie de Deleuze et Guattari.

M A N A G E M E N T O F SHIPPING COMPANI ES

The maritime sector is dynamic and volatile, creating the need for continuous monitoring of the latest developments and their effects on the organisation, management and strategies of shipping companies. This book analyses the business environment of these companies and the approaches they adopt in organising and managing their activities. Management of Shipping Companies aims to facilitate the learning and understanding of the fascinating world of shipping business. It examines the organisation and management of companies which manage ocean-going ships, emphasising the special characteristics of the industry and the framework created by these. This textbook offers a detailed account of the companies’ processes and functions, the structural and contextual dimensions of their organisation, as well as an analysis of human resources, safety management and the outsourcing of shipping operations. Written in an easily digestible and critical manner, it includes case studies and analysis of best practices implemented by companies worldwide. This unique and accessible book is an ideal text for students in maritime studies programs as well as readers interested in learning about maritime businesses’ organisation and management. Ioannis Theotokas is Professor of Management of Shipping Companies at the Department of Shipping, Trade and Transport of the University of the Aegean, Greece.

ABOUT THE AUTHOR

Dr. Ioannis Theotokas is Professor of Management of Shipping Companies at the Department of Shipping, Trade and Transport of the University of the Aegean, Greece. He has a background of economics and has specialised in Shipping Management. He received his PhD from the University of Piraeus (1997). His research interests include issues related to management, human resource management and strategic management applied to shipping business. He has participated as scientific coordinator or as principal researcher in funded research projects related to employment, entrepreneurship, strategy and competitiveness in shipping, to maritime training and e-learning, to image of shipping and in consultancy studies. Ioannis Theotokas is the author of six books (with five as co-author) published in the UK and Greece and of 42 papers published in edited books and international journals (which include Maritime Policy and Management, Marine Policy, Business History, International Journal of Human Resource Management and Corporate Governance).

Introduction The purpose of this book is to analyse the organisation and management of shipping companies. The business environment of shipping is changing rapidly. Globalisation is a phenomenon which, although it has been a feature of the shipping industry for some decades now, manifests itself at the present time with a different intensity and in a way which directly affects almost all aspects of the organisation and management of shipping companies. The new technologies are altering the manner of operation of these companies. At the same time, the structural changes which are taking place in the world freight markets make organisational and administrative adjustments essential in shipping companies. Shipping companies are parts of wider transportation networks. Competition between companies is intensifying. Effectiveness and performance are increasingly linked with the quality of the services provided, concentration on the human factor and the response of companies to social expectations. In this context, the model of successful companies of the past, which relied upon experience and tradition, tends, in present-day conditions, to be exhausting its potential dynamism. Within this framework, the managements of shipping companies are called upon to contribute to the search for solutions for the better adaptation of companies to the new conditions and an increase in their effectiveness and yield. The content of the book is structured in such a way as to examine the organisational and management traits of shipping companies active in the international freight markets as a whole. The analysis is supplemented by studies of specific instances which have a connection with the theoretical framework to which they belong. The material of this book was developed in order to support the teaching of Management of Shipping Companies in the Department of Shipping, Trade and Transport (STT) of the University of the Aegean, in the undergraduate and postgraduate curriculum. In its basic form, it was published in Greek in a 1st edition in 2011, in a 2nd in 2014, and was re-issued in 2016 under the title Organisation and Management of Shipping Companies (in Greek) by Alexandreia Publications. For the preparation of this edition, the material has been enhanced to include recent developments in the fields which it analyses, together with additional examples and best practices from shipping companies. I would like to thank Geoffrey Cox and Despina Christodoulou for the adaptation and linguistic editing of the text for the English version. My thanks also go, for the ready approval

❚ Introduction A company is a totality of interlinked and interdependent parts which coordinate their action in order to achieve a number of aims. It functions in a dynamic environment, seeks opportunities for profitable activation, undertakes business risks, combines productive factors in order to produce products and to provide services and tries to respond to the conflicting expectations of various groups related to or affected by its operation. In order to achieve the above, coordination of the interlinked and interdependent parts of the company and, at the same time, communication and interaction with the environment in which it operates are required. Changes in the external environment may constitute opportunities or threats for the company. It is likely, then, that adjustments either in its strategy or in its manner of organisation will be called for, in order to make the best of the opportunities or to deal with the threats. In this introductory chapter, an account is given of certain basic concepts having to do with shipping, and the kinds and categories of shipping companies are described. A systems approach to the organisations is then analysed; this may contribute to a better understanding of the manner of organisation and operation of shipping companies. ❚ Basic distinctions in the shipping industry The shipping industry is not a single whole, but is made up of a totality of markets/sectors. The criteria for distinguishing the individual markets are multiple. Nevertheless, a first general distinction of the markets can be based on the type of the vessel and the cargoes which it can carry. In the light of these characteristics, the following markets/sectors can be distinguished:

A. Bulk shipping

deadweight tonnage: unit of measurement of the weight which a vessel can convey, that is, of its carrying capacity. The total carrying capacity of the vessel is measured in dwt when it is loaded to its load line, which differs depending on the zone and the season at which it is sailing. For this reason, the measurement is usually taken on the basis of the summer load line. Also calculated in the carrying capacity are the weights not connected with its productive task, such as fuels and supplies. Deadweights are, in effect, the difference between the load displacement, that is, what the vessel and the cargo together weigh, and the light displacement, that is, the weight of the vessel when it is empty of cargo.

4

Bulk shipping consists of vessels transporting bulk liquid and dry cargoes. These are cargoes which can be loaded in large quantities, the bulk of which, as a rule, occupies the whole of the transporting capacity of the ship. Included in the category of bulk cargoes are principally raw materials transported mainly in large quantities (oil, grain, iron ores, coal, phosphates, fertilisers, cement, etc.). The definition of bulk cargoes is based on their characteristics, which permit their handling and transportation in the same way and in large quantities which take up the whole of the vessel, with a view to reducing the unit cost of transporting them. The natural characteristics of each cargo determine the type of the vessel which will be used for transporting it, the equipment required for handling it and the overall structure of the transporting system. As regards the characteristics connected with loading, bulk cargoes can be divided into three basic categories (Stopford, 2009):

❚ ❚ ❚

Liquid bulk cargoes, of which crude oil, oil products (clean, principally kerosene and gasoline and dirty), vegetable oils and liquid chemicals are the main. The five basic bulk dry cargoes, that is, iron ore, coal, grain, phosphates and bauxite, which constitute basic raw materials, are the largest group of bulk dry cargoes. Minor bulk dry cargoes, that is, raw materials and semi-processed products, such as steel products, forestry products, cement, sugar, soya flour, scrap iron, coke, salt, sulphur, and chemicals.

For the carrying of bulk cargoes, differing sizes of ships are used (Box 1.1), categorised on the basis of their transport capacity in deadweight tonnage.

B. Liner shipping Liner shipping includes vessels which carry general or unitised cargoes. This, that is to say, accounts for a large number of cargoes, the majority of them products

T H E S H I P P I N G C O M PA N Y

of manufacturing, the demand for which evolves smoothly over a long period. A liner shipping vessel can transport many different cargoes at the same time, but the quantity of each of these cannot account for the whole of its carrying capacity. Included among general cargoes are goods of particularly great value or sensitivity which call for a special carrier service for which their owner prefers to pay a predetermined freight rate, rather than a freight rate in force on the free market. This characteristic of the cargoes leads to approaches on the part of liner shipping which differ from those of bulk shipping. Whereas the latter is interested in the reduction of the unit cost, the former focuses more on the reliability, speed and quality of the transportation service which it offers. For this reason, bulk shipping companies provide, as a rule, homogenised services, whereas liner shipping businesses put greater emphasis on the differentiation of their services. Liner shipping vessels transport general cargoes at times of departure and arrival which are known in advance on predetermined maritime routes (lines) at charter rates known in advance. The liner operator is responsible for the whole of the cost of transportation, whereas the terms of carriage are determined by the bill of lading. The above characteristics significantly differentiate liner shipping from bulk shipping. The transportation of many small cargoes of small volume presupposes a large and complex administrative infrastructure, while the obligation of observing a time schedule for arrivals and departures at predefined ports limits the flexibility of the company. Whereas the bulk shipping owners can apply methods which allow them to deal with the imbalance between supply and demand, such as the laying up of the vessels, liner shipping companies are obliged to observe undeviatingly the time schedule of voyages, regardless of short-term changes in demand (Stopford, 2009). In addition, liner shipping service is a link in the total supply chain. Thus efficiency in the supply chain depends on coordination and punctuality, which limits the flexibility of line operators (Strandenes, 2012). Distinguishing general cargoes, because of their great number, is based on the way in which they are handled and loaded. The most basic ways of handling and loading general cargoes are as follows (Stopford, 2009):

❚ ❚

❚ ❚ ❚

Loose cargo: This includes individual items, boxes, accessories, etc., each of which has to be loaded and stowed separately. In the past, all general cargoes belonged to this category. Containerised cargo: This is a matter of standardised containers used for the unitising of the cargo. The unit of measurement of the carrying capacity of containers is the twenty-foot equivalent unit (teu). Containers are the basic type for the transporting of general cargoes. Palletised cargo: In this category, the cargo is packaged on pallets, so as to facilitate its handling and stowage. Pre-slung cargo: This consists of small pieces, such as wooden planks, which are tied together in order to form packages of the same size. Liquid cargo: A cargo in liquid form, transported in deep tanks, containers, or drums.

bill of lading: the document by which the loading of the merchandise to be transported is certified and the obligation to transport it to a specific place and to deliver it to a specific recipient is undertaken by the ship manager.

twenty-foot equivalent unit (teu): the teu unit is employed for measuring the carrying capacity of vessels which transport containers and corresponds to a typical container with a length of 20 feet (6.1 metres), a width of 8 feet (2.4 metres), and a height of 8 feet. There are also containers of larger dimensions, of a length of 40 or 45 feet, but, as a rule, as a unit of measurement of the carrying capacity of container vessels, the teu is used.

❚ ❚

Refrigerated cargo: This category includes sensitive cargoes, loaded in a state of refrigeration either in separate holds on general cargo vessels or in refrigerated containers. Heavy and awkward cargo: Large-volume cargoes which present difficulties in their stowage, such as motors, yachts, etc., are included in this category.

C. Specialised shipping Specialised shipping includes cargoes, for the transporting of which special conditions are required, as they present particular handling problems during loading and discharging. For that reason, specific ship types for the carriage of each cargo are used. The main special cargoes are motor vehicles, forestry products, refrigerated foods, liguid gas and chemical parcels, steel products, prefabricated houses, etc. Specialised shipping has characteristics of both bulk and liner shipping (Stopford, 2009). Many of the cargoes in this category are not loaded in bulk, but in units. However, they are loaded in large quantities, and, as a rule, the principle of ‘one ship – one cargo’ applies to them.

D. Passenger shipping

derived demand: demand for a service or a product which depends upon the existence of demand for another service or another product.

The organisation of passenger shipping differs from that of cargo shipping. Nevertheless, in passenger shipping as in cargo shipping, the market is not uniform, since passengers who use its services do not share common characteristics. A first criterion which distinguishes the individual sectors of passenger shipping is the nature of the demand, or, in other words, the purpose of the voyage. In passenger shipping, demand occurs as derived and as direct. The differentiation of the purpose of the demand and the distinction of the individual sectors of passenger shipping dependent upon it also lead to the differentiation of the types of vessels which are used in each case.

BOX 1.1 Types of vessels A. Bulk carriers A large part of dry bulk cargoes is transported by bulk carriers, which may have equipment for handling the cargo. Large bulk carriers, however, do not usually have such equipment and rely for the handling of the cargo on the port facilities. The basic types of bulk carriers are as follows:

❚ ❚

mini bulkers, of a carrying capacity below 10,000 dwt; handysize, of a carrying capacity of 18,000–30, 000 dwt, which can also be used on the lakes of North America;

❚

Liquid bulk cargoes are carried by tankers, which have pumps for the discharging of the cargoes, while loading is usually carried out from tanks of the on-shore facilities. The basic types of tankers are:

ultra large crude carriers (ULCCs), the largest vessels in the world fleet, with a carrying capacity of above 300,000 dwt, while some of them may exceed 560,000 dwt;

❚ ❚

very large crude carriers (VLCCs), of a carrying capacity of 200,000–300,000 dwt; suezmax tankers, of a carrying capacity of 120,000–200,000 dwt, which permits them to pass through the Suez Canal;

aframax tankers, of a carrying capacity of 70,000–120,000 dwt; panamax tankers, of a carrying capacity of 50,000–70,000 dwt; handysize tankers and handymax tankers, of a carrying capacity of 18,000– 50,000 dwt, used chiefly for the transporting of oil products;

small tankers, of a carrying capacity of up to 10,000 dwt.

A special category of vessels transporting a liquid cargo is formed by:

❚ ❚

product tankers, which carry oil products; chemical tankers, which have many tanks for cargo and carry chemicals.

Special mention should be made of combined carriers, which can carry either oil or dry bulk cargoes, switching between tanker and bulk markets, depending on freight rates. A special category of vessels includes highly specialised vessels which have, as a rule, features which permit them to handle and transport only one category of cargo. There are many types of highly specialised vessels, but the most important of these are the following:

❚ ❚

Vehicle carriers, which transport vehicles and thus have a large number of decks; Chip carriers, which transport timber products, which, unlike iron ore, have a high stowage factor and present difficulties in handling;

❚

Cement carriers, which transport cement and for that reason have closed holds and a mechanism for its loading and unloading;

❚

Liquid Petroleum Gas (LPG) and Liquid Natural Gas (LNG) tankers, which transport petroleum gases and natural gas in liquid form, and for that reason have insulated tanks and equipment which permit the maintenance of the cargo at low temperatures and under a specific atmospheric pressure;

❚

Refrigerated ships, which transport vulnerable cargoes at a fixed temperature (meat, fruit, etc.) and for that reason have insulated holds on a number of decks, which are, in effect, refrigerated cabinets, while they have, as a rule, mechanical equipment for the handling of these cargoes;

❚

Heavy lift vessels, which transport heavy cargoes which cannot be carried by other types of vessels, and for that reason possess special equipment for their handling.

B. General cargo vessels General and unitised cargoes are carried by the following types of vessels:

❚

Tweendeckers: Classic general cargo or bulk cargo vessels, of a carrying capacity of 18,000 dwt, which have double decks so that they can carry many cargoes simultaneously, and have equipment for their handling.

❚

Containerships: Vessels of differing sizes which transport containers and have come to dominate in general cargo transportation because of their basic characteristic of speed in sailing and in loading and discharge. The largest vessels in this category have a carrying capacity that exceeds 20,000 teu, which is equivalent to that of very large bulk shipping vessels (Box 2.4).

❚

Roll on-Roll off (Ro-Ro): Ships on to which it is possible to load vehicles bearing a load, as they have a ramp instead of lifting machinery.

❚

Multi-purpose carriers: Vessels with double decks, holds with a large opening and heavy lifting machinery so as to carry many different cargoes, from bulk and frozen to large-size, together with containers.

❚

Barge-carrying vessels: Vessels which carry barges bearing a cargo, as they have special loading and discharging systems which permit them to handle them like containers.

C. Passenger ships The most basic types of passenger ships are the following:

❚

Cruise ships: Vessels specially designed and constructed which provide their passengers with organised recreational voyages.

❚

Ferry boats: Vessels which convey passengers and meet derived demand, sailing, as a rule, on routes according to a regular timetable which link two or more ports, while they can also carry simultaneously passengers and vehicles, and for that reason are also referred to as RO-RO ferries.
