❚

Catamarans: Vessels usually of limited capacity which can reach high speeds, often above 50 knots (nautical miles per hour), with a double bottom (underwater part of the vessel), which enables them to maintain better stability in difficult weather conditions. Many of them have a garage for the transportation of vehicles.

❚

Hovercraft: High-speed craft which travel on an inflated air-cushion on the sea’s surface, but are capable of hovering.

❚

Hydrofoils: Vessels, usually of a small size, with limited carrying capacity, which can reach high speeds.

D. Other types of vessels Included in this category are vessels which do not offer carrier services as they are not used for conveying cargoes or passengers, even though their activity may be connected with this. Belonging to this group are, inter alia, fishing vessels, survey ships, cable ships, research ships, tugs, salvage vessels, dredgers and ice-breakers.

BOX 1.2 A new category of size of vessel: Very large ore carriers The use of differing maritime routes for the transporting of iron ore and an increase in demand for this ore, chiefly because of the impressive economic development of China in the early 2000s, has led to an attempt to increase the carrying capacity of capesize vessels and to the introduction of a new category of size: very large ore carriers, whose carrying capacity can reach 365,000 dwt. In 2008, the Brazilian mining company Vale (www.vale.com), in order to decrease transport cost and to compete with Australian mining rivals, went even further, placing an order for the building of even larger ships, the Valemaxes, whose carrying capacity reaches 400,000 dwt. It should, of course, be pointed out that this strategic option of Vale, taken from the viewpoint of the iron ore producer, and not from that of the shipping carrier, has been the object of criticism, and doubts have been voiced as to its long-term vindication (Chan, 2012). Furthermore, until 2015, the vessels were not allowed to enter ports in China because of a ban imposed for technical and safety reasons by the country’s Transport Ministry (Mitchell, 2015). In spite of the difficulties which have been encountered, in the end, this particular type of vessel seems to be establishing itself. During 2016, moreover, it was also adopted by companies with Chinese and Japanese interests, which have placed orders for the building of a series of vessels of this category, with delivery in 2018 and 2019, with all ships destined for long-term charter to Vale (Hellenic Shipping News,

❚ The shipping company A shipping company is an economic unit which uses the factors of production for the production and provision of maritime transportation services. According to B.N. Metaxas, a shipping company is a company, an individual or a small group of individuals who take decisions on the employment (and/or otherwise) of the factors of production in the shipping world (Metaxas, 1988). The individuals who take the decisions (shipping entrepreneurs) are called upon to choose the markets/sectors which they will engage in, the types of vessels required for this and the moment in time at which the investment will be realised. At the same time, they are called upon to take decisions as to the nature of the financing to be chosen, the capital which will be drawn upon, the insurance cover of the vessel and the nature of the operation management required (Downard, 1990). The task of a shipping company is the management of ships for the supply of maritime transport services. The shipping company is an organisation consisting of two basic parts: the productive units, that is, the vessels, and the infrastructure on land, that is, the shipping office which undertakes the management of the vessels. In the case of companies which are active in the liner shipping or in specialised shipping, such as, for example, those of chemical cargoes or vehicles, the infrastructure on land includes, as the case may be, means of transport on land, harbour facilities, or terminals, or assemblage centres, or distribution centres. The areas in which the company is active, therefore, determine the manner of operation and co-ordination of these two parts. BOX 1.3 Voyage charter and time charter Voyage charter: The charterer hires the vessel from the shipping company to transport a specific cargo from one port to another. After the completion of the voyage and of the carrying job, the contract between the two sides is completed. The remuneration of the shipping company for the work of transportation of the vessel may be determined either on the basis of the quantity of the cargo which it will carry or as a lump-sum freight. In the former case, the freight is determined per ton of cargo, whereas in the latter, a fixed sum is set, regardless of the quantity of the cargo to be carried. In the case of a charter of this form, the whole of the cost of operation of the vessel is borne by the shipping company. Time charter: The charterer hires the vessel from the shipping company for a specific period of time, which may be that needed for a voyage, in which case, it is called a trip charter, or longer (period charter), which is more usual. In the case of a time charter, the shipping company assigns its vessel to the charterer, without, however, ceasing to manage it itself. The fee which it collects for the ceding of the vessel is set by the day. The commercial operations of the vessel are undertaken by the charterer, who operates within the framework laid down by the charterparty, while he can, if this has been agreed upon, sub-charter the vessel. Because of this characteristic of the time charter, the charterer undertakes certain of the vessel’s expenses, more specifically, the voyage expenses (bunkers, port charges, etc.) and the cargo handling cost. The basic operating expenses of the vessel (crew’s wages, maintenance and repairs of the vessel, insurance, etc.) are chargeable to the shipping company. Bareboat charter: The charterer hires the vessel for several years and undertakes the responsibility for its technical and commercial management. The shipping company remains responsible for the fixed cost of the vessel and the charterer for all other expenses. Under this form of chartering the charterer becomes the manager of the vessel for a great period of time, without undertaking the risk of investing in the acquisition of the vessel.

charter) or for a predetermined period of time (time charter) (Box 1.3), on terms which are shaped by the environment of competition which at the given time of the realisation of the charter prevails on the market in which it is active. An alternative form of chartering which is for an extended period of time and entails different cost allocation for the shipowner and the charterer is the bareboat charter. Another option of the shipping company for the employment of its ships is the participation in pool, which is the type of commercial cooperation where vessels of a similar type and often of a similar range owned by different shipping companies, are managed as a single entity. The earnings of the vessels are distributed by the Administrator of the pool to the owners of the vessels according to a pre-arranged weighing system. (Packard, 1989). In most of the cases, the

shipping companies remain responsible for the operation of their vessels (Thanopoulou and Theotokas, 1997). The ships are autonomous units, with their own personnel and their own organisation, which are constantly on the move far from the headquarters of the shipping company. However, for them to operate effectively, administrative/ technical support from on land on a constant basis is necessary. The organisation on land is responsible for issues relating to the general orientations, the objectives and the aims of the company, to finding employment for the vessels and to supporting their day-to-day operation on an on-going basis. The functions of the shipping company, although in their basic form they do not differ from those of other companies, are, nevertheless, more complex, as the productive units are in constant motion and may be thousands of miles from their base – the management centre, where the company is established and manages its fleet. Naturally, a shipping company may have infrastructures on land at more than one management centre, depending upon its strategy and the extent of its activities. Reference to the place of operation of the shipping company is linked to the fact that the conditions which shape the institutional framework (tax level, flexibility in operation, etc.), the availability of the factors of production (capital, human resources) and other considerations (support by the state, external economies, networking, etc.), affect chiefly the manner of organisation and operation of its infrastructures ashore.

❚ The categorisation of shipping companies Shipping, especially bulk shipping and liner shipping, is a globalised branch, made up of companies which draw resources from the world market and offer their sea transportation services in this same market. A typical example of a Panamax bulk carrier and the nationalities of the organisations from which resources or services are taken for its management, operation and chartering may be as follows (Figure 1.2): owner of ship from Greece with representative offices in London and Singapore, manager of ship from Germany with headquarters in Cyprus, ship built in Korea with finance taken from USA and classification society responsible for building and classification of ship from Japan. The ship may be registered in Panama and insured for Hull and Machinery in UK and for Protection and Indemnity in Norway. The crew might come from Greece, India and the Philippines, while the crew manager is from Greece with headquarters in the Philippines. Finally, the charterer may be from China and the sub-charterer from Brazil. These are the main entities involved. If the analysis becomes more detailed, entities of many other specialisations with corresponding nationalities may be added. Consequently, shipping companies do not face geographical limitations either in the acquisition of their resources or in making their services available. Nevertheless, the shipping industry, as has already been pointed out, is not a single one, and consists of individual markets/sectors. A first distinction of shipping companies can be based on a distinction between markets, that is, the specialisation of their fleets, and, consequently, the

kind of maritime transportation services which they offer can be used as a criterion. Thus, we distinguish between the following categories of companies:

❚ ❚ ❚ ❚

those specialising in bulk shipping, which manage vessels carrying bulk liquid and dry cargoes; those specialising in liner shipping, which manage vessels carrying unitised cargoes on routes linking different ports; those specialising in passenger shipping, which manage vessels which carry passengers; those of differentiated fleets, which manage vessels with many specialisations and which are active in more than one market.

Consequently, a first distinction between shipping companies can be made on the basis of the market in which they operate and the nature of the services which they offer. Because of the individual characteristics of each market and the differing organisational and administrative needs to which it gives rise for the companies, the organisation and the individual functions of each group of companies may differ from one another. For example, liner companies do not manage only ships, but also the lines which link various ports to one another, on which they operate their vessels. In many cases, they also manage port terminals and other infrastructures on land. This characteristic and the way in which it determines their organisation and administration will be described in the next chapter. In spite of the differences between companies, they have the basic object of the activity of every shipping company in common: the management of ships for the provision of maritime transport services. The same is true of their basic functions. This applies to a greater degree to companies which operate in the liner shipping and bulk cargo shipping market, which are those which are chiefly examined in this book. Other criteria which can be used in order to distinguish between shipping companies are the extent and degree of integration of their activities. These criteria make reference to the manner in which companies develop. There are those which choose specialisation in one activity and others which choose a wide range of activities. There are companies which are active only in one sector of shipping, others which are active in more than one sector/market of shipping and others which, besides shipping, are active in other industries. At the same time, maritime transportation services are offered by companies which are not engaged exclusively in shipping. A further distinction between shipping companies can, therefore, be based on this feature. Shipping companies can, then, be categorised by the nature of the ownership status which governs the degree of independence or integration of their activities into horizontally integrated, vertically integrated and diversified (Box 1.4). On the basis of their ownership status and the degree of integration of their activities, shipping companies can be divided into the following categories (Svendsen, 1978): a.

BOX 1.5 Vertical development in shipping Wallenius Wilhelmsen Logistics/WWL (www.2wglobal.com), which specialises in the transportation of vehicles, heavy equipment and specialised cargo, is a world carrier which provides integrated transportation solutions, since it undertakes the transporting of the cargo from the factory to the final sale point, that is to say, the responsibility for the transporting of the cargo and the organisation of all the activities in the supply chain (Figure 1.5). Included in the products and services provided are the management of the supply chain, maritime transportation, terminal services, inland distribution and technical services. BOX 1.6 The diversified China Cosco Shipping Corporation The China Cosco Shipping Corporation (www.cnshipping.com) is the world’s largest shipping company, both in terms of the number of ships of every type, and in those of carrying capacity. It was set up in early 2016 when the competing shipping companies, of state interests, of China, the China Ocean Shipping Company (COSCO) and the China Shipping Group, were merged. The merger agreement covered almost every aspect of shipping activity, given the range of activities of the two companies. The merger of the two companies was the result of 74 transactions worth 8.7 bn. dollars. The new state interests company manages the world’s largest fleet of dry bulk cargo vessels, tankers and containerships. More precisely, its fleet consists of 1,114 vessels of a total carrying capacity of 85.3 m. dwt. Included in this total are 365 bulk carriers of a carrying capacity of 33.5 m. dwt, 120 tankers of a carrying capacity of 17.8 m. dwt, as well as vessels transporting general cargo of a carrying capacity of 3 m. dwt – a fleet which is the largest in the world in terms of carrying capacity among these types. At the same time, the fleet of containerships, with a carrying capacity of 1.58 m. dwt was at the time of the merger the fourth largest in the world. After the acquisition of the Hong Kong based company OOCL, which was concluded in July 2017, the controlled fleet of China Cosco was the third biggest container fleet with more than 400 ships of 2.9 million teu. In addition to its other activities, the new company manages 46 container terminals throughout the world, through which 90 m. teu are handled (China Cosco, 2017a). The motive force for the merger was policy, and was connected, among other things, with the slowing down of the world economy and the effect of this on China, and with the exploitation of the opportunities offered by the ‘one belt, one road’ strategy. The two companies had similar operations and were competing with each other in a period of prolonged downturn in the industry. Efforts to deal with these weaknesses, to seek for synergies, to streamline and improve the efficiency of two firms, to transform scale advantage to economies of scale and to boost overall competitiveness were basic motives for the merger. The merger, then, supplied the advantages of scale and concentration on world competition. The objective of China COSCO Shipping is the alteration of traditional marketing guidelines and the shift of focus from products to customers’ needs, commitment to the delivery of quality services to customers, the fostering of green competitiveness and the implementation of activities related to corporate social responsibility (China Cosco, 2016b; Knowler, 2016; Cosco, 2017). The two companies manifest considerable cultural differences, complex organisational structures and a magnitude of activities which made the merger particularly complicated and difficult (Drewry, 2016; Liang, 2017; China Cosco, 2016a). Progress towards unification was, however, constant from the moment the decision was taken, and, in spite of the difficulties, has gone ahead.

classical theory: a combination of theories which focuses on productivity and the effectiveness of individuals. systems theory: approaches organisations as systems consisting of interconnecting parts which have a common aim. contingency theory: recognises that organisations differ and examines the factors which contribute to this.

The analysis and interpretation of the organisation and strategies of shipping companies presuppose an understanding of the framework within which they operate, that is, of the characteristics and particularities of the shipping industry. The use of analytical tools from economic theory, on the one hand, as rendered specific within the framework of maritime economics (Georgantopoulos, 1984), and from the theory of the organisation and management of companies, on the other, as specified in the context of contingency theory, can contribute to this. Maritime economics is the application of the tools of economic analysis to understanding and improving resource allocation to and within the maritime sector (Metaxas, 1983). Maritime economics examines the markets which operate within the framework of the shipping sector and the policies applied to shipping, as well as the issues relating to the administration of those companies which belong to the various markets (Heaver, 1993). Seen in this light, maritime economics is the analytical tool not only for the approach to shipping companies as regards their economic operation, but also for an understanding of the characteristics and particularities which are connected with their organisation and management. Contingency theory incorporates and unifies classical and systems theory (Luthans, 1976). Whereas the classical theory of management treats organisations as mechanical systems and concerns itself with the principles of their internal design, systems theory puts emphasis on the environment within which organisations operate, maintaining that as to their organisation, the environment must always be taken into account (Morgan, 1986). The contingency theory of organisation understands organisations (companies) as a totality of interdependent systems which, in their turn, are dependent on the environment within which they operate, and recognises that the environment and the internal sub-systems of each organisation are, in a certain way, unique, and provide the basis for the management of this specific organisation (Kast and Rosenzweig, 1985). In this context, the size (Hall, 1996), the external environment (Lawrence and Lorsch, 1990), the level of technology (Pugh and Hickson, 1989), the organisation’s culture (Handy, 1993) and the culture connected with the environment of establishment and operation (Child, 1981) are definitive factors for the organisation, manner of management and strategies of companies. The reference to systems and contingency approaches has no relation to a view as to their correctness as compared with other approaches or theories. As Morgan remarks, there are no right and wrong theories in management in an absolute sense, because each theory explains and at the same time obscures (Morgan, 2000). As will be explained in what follows, the basic characteristics of shipping companies reinforce the analytical force of these approaches as regards the study of the organisational and managerial phenomena of these companies. Nevertheless, these approaches are not adequate for a full understanding of the phenomena being studied in the case of complex organisations such as shipping companies. In any event, as the critique of these points out, these approaches manifest significant weaknesses. In particular, as regards the systems approach to organisations,

T H E S H I P P I N G C O M PA N Y

attention is drawn to the fact that it ignores the motivation and the interpretations of those who are active in them, and, whereas they are social constructs, it ascribes thought and action to them. At the same time, it fails to take into account that it is the members of the organisations who interpret what they understand as environment, introduce meanings and shared descriptions and regulate and adapt to it. Furthermore, the systems approach accepts that organisations have aims, but believes that these are not the result of agreement of their members or of the ability of the management to make their leadership prevail with its members. Further, it is accused of rejecting history and social change, in relying on the necessity of balance (Clegg and Dunkerley, 1990). From this viewpoint, within the framework of the analysis which follows, the employment of the advantages of the various theories and approaches for the analysis of shipping companies as organisations is sought. Morgan argues that we must accept the fact that each theory, each approach which we adopt in studying organisation and management, though capable of leading to valuable conclusions, is at the same time imperfect, prejudiced and potentially misleading (Morgan, 2000). By the use of metaphors, he analyses organisations on the basis of eight different types (Box 1.7), each of which refers to a dimension of the organisation which is more comprehensively explained by a theoretical approach to the organisation. The advantages and limitations of each approach can be used in parallel, thus creating a range of complementary and conflicting apprehensions. Morgan argues that if we realise that a theory is a metaphor, we shall appreciate immediately that no isolated theory will ever give us the perfect way of looking at things, or a viewpoint which will serve all purposes. We can, however, use all the metaphors together in order to produce a range of complementary and conflicting apprehensions, and learn to rely upon and employ the advantages provided by different views (Morgan, 2000). Thus, organisations are analysed as machines, organisms, brains, cultures, political systems, psychic prisons, flux and transformation and, finally, instruments of domination. In the chapters which follow, various phenomena of organisation and management are analysed by the use of theories and the analytical tools of one or more metaphorical description, in an attempt to understand various aspects of shipping companies as organisations.

BOX 1.7 Metaphors of organisations Using the tool of metaphors, Morgan (1986) analyses organisations on the basis of eight different descriptions, as:

❚

machines (they are directed and designed as machines are; they are made up of inter-related parts, each of which plays distinctly a predetermined role in the functioning of the whole);

❚

organisms (they are born, develop, decline and die, and can adapt to changing circumstances);

❚ Introduction The shipping industry requires special analysis. It consists of a series of markets – local, national, regional and global – each of which has its own idiosyncrasies. At the same time, its largest part operates as a globalised industry, within a complex and dynamic environment. The derived demand that it meets makes it a volatile and unpredictable industry. The shipping company, just as every company, is in a two-way relationship with the environment in which it operates. It is defined by it and, at the same time, contributes through its activities to defining it. It develops capabilities that enable it to exploit particular opportunities that it finds in its external environment. This chapter examines a set of subjects that relate to the external environment of the shipping industry, the analysis of which includes an understanding of the broader macro-environment, the effect of the freight market and the fluctuations that typify them, and the immediate external environment of shipping companies. The chapter then investigates uncertainty as a component of the environment, while the analysis is completed with an examination of the specific characteristics of the shipping industry and shipping companies, which also lead to the development of Management of Shipping Companies.

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

❚ Orientation of the company and its external environment When examining the external environment of a company, its prevailing feature is whether it is orientated to the global market or to national and regional markets. This feature is at the centre of the analysis, as it leads to differentiation in the extent and influence of certain factors. This is possible because, even if the regulatory framework of the shipping industry is global and has been adopted almost completely by most countries, the form of competition that companies face, dependent upon the markets in which they participate, can differ. For example, the competitive environment is different for companies that are active in global freight markets for bulk shipping in comparison with that of coastal cargo carrying companies, which are active in the inland waters of each country. This is because the institutional framework that governs maritime transportation may differ from country to country. In order for shipping companies to be able to evaluate their prospects for growth, they must assess:

❚ ❚

external environment: includes all the factors that can influence it, which are outside of it and which shape the framework in which it operates.

the prevailing conditions in their sector and the competitive environment that they create, as they are a source of both opportunities and threats; the resources and capabilities that the companies themselves have and which form the basis of both their strengths and weaknesses.

They must, in other words, evaluate the external and internal environments. A more in-depth analysis of the external environment in which shipping companies operate will be given below and the differentiating factors for each category of company will be outlined. The external environment includes all the factors that can influence a company, which are outside of it and which shape the framework in which it operates. They include forces that have an obvious direct impact on how a company operates as well as forces with an indirect impact. The external environment thus impacts upon a company in various ways (Miller, 1998), such as:

❚ ❚ ❚ ❚

it offers opportunities, which the company seeks to exploit, and poses threats, which the company seeks to face by implementing the appropriate strategies; it shapes the rules, so that the company can be competitive within its industry; it impacts upon the availability of the resources and factors of production, which are crucial for the company to operate; it impacts upon the performance of the company’s alternative investments.

The external environment is continuously changing. As a result, companies are obliged to take into account the features and factors of the external environment, to recognise and assess the day-to-day conditions and to foresee changes, so as to be in a position to respond in an appropriate way. A globalised industry such as shipping, which covers derived demand, and is therefore impacted by many unforeseen factors, operates in a dynamic and

complex environment. The global economic and political situation, the policies of different countries and their bilateral relations and the level of technology are factors that play a decisive role in shaping the operating framework of shipping. An economic recession, a regional military conflict, a technological innovation, an unforeseen natural phenomenon or a government decision may determine to a significant degree the level of demand as well as the availability of maritime transportation services. For example, in June 2008 the decision by the Chinese authorities to ‘freeze’ imports of iron ore and instead use their own reserves, while at the same time reducing industrial production and ceasing operations at many factories for environmental reasons in the run-up to the Olympic Games that were to be held in Beijing, struck a temporary blow to the dry bulk cargo freight markets. The Baltic Exchange index for capesize vessels (Baltic Cape Index) fell from a historical high of 19,867 (on 5 June 2008) to 13,960 (data for 13 June 2008) (Karageorgos, 2008a). The first level contains the factors that concern and influence all companies and organisations, the wider macro-environment, which includes the technological, political/legal, social, demographic and global environment. An analysis of the data associated with the wider macro-environment enables companies to forecast future trends and better to prepare for the challenges of the future. The second level shows the forces and factors that as a whole have an impact on the shipping industry, as they shape the framework in which all shipping companies are active. The most important of these forces are: international maritime trade; the institutional framework within which the ships and companies operate, as this is defined by the International Maritime Organisation/ IMO) (Box 2.1); the shipbuilders; the insurance companies; the flag states where the vessels are registered; the classification societies; the available capital from financial organisations and stock exchanges; and the brokers for the chartering, insurance and sale and purchase of vessels. The third level is comprised of the four shipping markets (Stopford, 2009:177) that determine the supply and demand of maritime transportation services in the short term: the newbuilding markets; the sale and purchase markets for secondhand ships; the demolition market; and, of course, the freight markets. On the fourth level is the immediate external environment of every shipping company, which includes customers, competitors, suppliers and human resources.

BOX 2.1 The International Maritime Organisation (IMO) The nature of shipping and the element of risk inherent to a great degree in its activities and operations, alongside the fact that a large number of countries are involved in shipping and it hence impacts on all of them, led early on to the realisation that the regulatory framework of shipping would be ineffective if left to the responsibility of individual countries. It was therefore necessary to create bilateral or international institutions, agencies and organisations, which would be tasked with regulating issues relating to the operation of vessels and the management of their human resources, and, through them, with the operations of the shipping industry, a regulatory framework that was to be binding and/or universal. The IMO (www.imo.org) is a specialised agency of the United Nations, which is based in London and focuses exclusively on shipping issues. Its goal is to facilitate co-operation between governments on technical issues that impact shipping and concern the safety of shipping and environmental protection. Its task is to develop a set of regulations, codes and proposals that can be adopted by the governments of its 172 member-states and three associate members (July 2017). The highest governing body of the IMO is the Assembly. The decisions taken at the Assembly are not binding, but they are often integrated into the domestic legislation of the member-states or into international law at a later stage (IMO, 2017).

T H E E X T E R N A L E N V I R O N M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

In the paragraphs which follow, the analysis will focus on the larger macroenvironment, the environment shaped by the four shipping markets and the immediate external environment of shipping companies.

❚ The broader macro-environment For all companies that are active in the global market the role of the forces of the macro-environment is crucial, as they can impact the climate within which they operate. The technological, economic, socio-cultural and politicoeconomic regime that companies have to deal with in every country in which they operate may vary. As a result, they must analyse a number of factors that may have an impact, directly or indirectly, on their plans. In the sections which follow, the technological, economic, socio-cultural and politico-economic forces of the broader macro-environment in which companies operate will be analysed.

Technological forces The technological forces are technical developments that take place in the external environment. They include areas such as inventions, techniques and organised knowledge. Technological trends and developments may not begin in the market in which the company operates, but they have a significant influence on its development (Box 2.2). They may also begin in sections of the value chain system of an industry and transform the whole way in which it operates. This happened with the introduction of shipping containers by the truck-owner Malcolm Mclean in 1956, which was the starting-point for a complete transformation of the way in which liner shipping was organised and operated, as it impacted on shipping companies, shippers, ship design, trade flow networks, the labor, investments and competition in the ports as well as the inland transport (Talley, 2000). General cargo vessels had their own cranes and could approach every port, transporting processed and semi-processed products, less important bulk dry cargoes and passengers. With the rise in global trade and the various cargoes that comprised it, transport needs were also significantly differentiated. The transport conditions offered by general cargo vessels could no longer respond to the new demands of global maritime trade. There was a long time delay in the port, which meant that the use of ships (i.e., the use of invested capital) for transporting goods was not full. Thus, economies of scale were difficult to achieve, since the use of a larger ship meant even more transport capacity remained unused. In the search for greater productivity, liner shipping companies sought means of transport that would allow unitised cargo and the development of systems and equipment that would automate the transportation process. This goal was achieved with the introduction of containers and containerships in the 1960s. The introduction of the container for cargo unitisation proved to be an especially important innovation. Within a period of 20 years, containerisation had

value chain system: the larger set of activities that include the value chains of all the companies (suppliers, producers, sellers) that are needed to create a product and for it to reach the end user.

economies of scale: the reduction of average cost through an increase in the work produced.

29

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

spread to all main and most secondary lines. Cargo containerisation was very successful in achieving its main goal, which was to reduce the time vessels spent in port. By way of illustration, a classic general cargo vessel of 22,000 dwt would remain in port for 149 days a year, i.e., 40 per cent of its time, while a containership of 47,000 dwt remains in port for only 64 days a year, i.e., 17 per cent of its time (Stopford, 2009:511). The use of containers resulted in a different form of organisation for liner shipping and in different approaches on the part of the shipping companies that participated in the transportation of general cargo. Containerisation meant that liner shipping companies could offer integrated ‘door-to-door’ transport services, and liner shipping came to be considered an integral part of the transport chain. This development resulted in a reduction in the number of companies but an increase in their size. This was so because many traditional companies were unable to respond to the new demands of the market, while others were either acquired by or merged with their competitors, so as to gain a scale that would permit them to respond more effectively and with greater results to the new conditions. Furthermore, the introduction and subsequent prevalence of containers led to the restructuring of the port industry. Technological change in shipping usually presupposes the availability of large amounts of capital and is typically associated with issues of transport capacity and/or the expansion of services. It is impacted by both internal and external developments, which create opportunities but can also entail risks. Technological

BOX 2.2 Technology and shipping’s response to climate change Technological change chiefly arises through the search for conditions that will improve cost and performance, the attempt to respond to current or anticipated social expectations/demands or the need to adapt to the available resources. Climate change and the pressure to reduce emissions are included within this category. As reported in a special issue of Lloyd’s List (Lloyd’s List, 2010) on the future of shipping and innovation, the process of adapting shipping to expectations with regard to gas emissions will not be easy and problem-free, given that a large section of the technology that is required to support the long-term targets of gas emission reduction either does not currently exist or is in a very early stage of development. However, it was contended that radical changes may emerge which will completely change the shipping market. As the article states: What will characterize innovation in shipping in the future is the faster and more forced pace of technological measures and steps. In this situation, the new age of shipping will be one where the market leaders are those that are able to rapidly adopt new low carbon of green ship technologies.

30

T H E E X T E R N A L E N V I R O N M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

change has become the main element in the growth of shipping (Frankel, 1991), given that its rate has increased. Any company that wishes to survive in a continuously changing environment must seek to keep pace with the technology that is being used in the production and distribution of its products or services (Box 2.3 and Box 2.4). As a rule, technological innovations co-exist alongside the earlier technologies that have been applied in a sector, which are not immediately abandoned. Companies are obliged to integrate new technologies into their operations if they are to remain competitive. For example, in the tanker sector, the introduction of double-hull vessels in the late 1980s was the result of technological developments in shipbuilding. This did not mean, however, that double-hull vessels immediately took over in tanker shipbuilding. Some companies adopted this technology, although most continued to build single-hull ships. Over time, however, a sequence of factors emanating from the social and political context led to the eventual prevalence of this technology (Box 2.7). This particular development confirms that those companies that first adopt new technologies, thereby taking on a greater risk, eventually enjoy the advantages of having been among the first to have made such a move. Those companies that first adopt a structural change have advantages, such as the economies of scale, the reduction of costs because of accumulated experience, building a reputation and customer relations, etc. (Porter, 1990). Those shipping companies that first invested in creating a double-hull tanker fleet did not have to deal with the immediate consequences of the European Union decision initially and that of the IMO subsequently on the mandatory phasing-out of single-hull vessels. They were thus in a position immediately to exploit the opportunities offered by the freight markets in the first period after the Erika and Prestige disasters (Boxes 2.3, 2.7 and 2.8).

BOX 2.4 The continuous trend towards larger container ships2 In 2006 the largest container vessel was the Emma Maersk (158,000 dwt, 397 metres long, 56.4 metres wide, 11,000 teu). The Emma Maersk was one of eight vessels that were ordered at the time by the Maersk Line. When the order for eight vessels became known, concerns were expressed as to whether the investment would be successful, concerns that were not, however, confirmed out since the vessel used almost 100 per cent of its carrying capacity, supplying Maersk with significant benefits from the economies of scale. Its competitors were soon rushing to build ships with a similar carrying capacity. A few years later, in 2011, Maersk proceeded with an order for significantly larger containerships, surprising the market again with this move. The order concerned the building of ten vessels with a carrying capacity of 18,000 teu, and with the option for the building of another 20 in the future at the Daewoo shipyards in Korea. The vessels would have had a length of 400 metres, a width of 59 metres and a height of 73 metres, and each one would have cost 190 m. dollars. This family of very large containerships was called the Triple E class, as its characteristics were to be Energy efficiency, Environmental performance and Economies of scale.

It was anticipated that these vessels’ fuel consumption would be 50 per cent lower that the industry average and have a maximum speed of 23 nautical miles. 18,000 teu vessels have a large draft, meaning that the number of ports they can use is limited. Maersk believed that these vessels would completely change the attitude of the shipping industry towards the size and efficiency of the specific vessels. According to Maersk, the cost of shipping a single box from Asia to Europe with new ships would be reduced by around 26 per cent (Korea Shipping Messenger, 2011). Regarding the cost per teu, estimations reveal the importance of the economies of scales in various cost categories, the fuel cost, the manning costs and the fixed costs included. The total chain cost per teu decreased from 955 euros to 585 euros when transported with 4,500 teu and 18,000 teu containerships, respectively (University of Antwerp, 2015). It has been contended that ships are not really the issue and trying to solve the cost problems by building even bigger vessels does not lead to the needed economic improvements. Today’s problems will not be solved by better ships but instead by a better transport system. Economic forces

recession: a phase in the business cycle marked by a serious drop in the level of economic activity.

business cycle: fluctuations in the level of economic activity, which cover the following phases: recession (rapid fall in total demand), trough, recovery (rise in total demand), expansion or peak (continuous rise in total demand).

34

In times of continuous growth, many companies face an increased demand, whilst there is a large pool of available capital for expansion. When the economy is in a recession, demand is reduced, unemployment rises and profits fall. Companies must continuously analyse the economic environment in which they operate in order to reduce the risks and increase the benefits from the opportunities that are created. The shipping industry covers derived demand. The demand for transport services is created by the demand for production and consumption and, as a consequence, from the global economy. It is clear that to predict the trends in the shipping market requires a full knowledge of developments in the global economy. From 2003 until mid-2008 the conditions in the freight markets of bulk shipping were positive, a factor that to a large degree was due to the impressive economic growth of the BRIC countries (Brazil, Russia, India, China). This led to a sharp increase in demand for cargo transport and a rise in freight rates. Those companies that had predicted this development and had a fleet that permitted them to exploit the opportunity from its beginning made impressive profits. The world economy is the most important single influence on ship demand, since it generates most of the demand for sea transport (Stopford, 2009). Rates of economic growth are not stable but, on the contrary, upward trends alternate with downward ones. Fluctuations in rates of economic growth have a direct impact on maritime trade and, as a consequence, on the demand for maritime transportation services. Business cycles are due to a combination of internal and external factors. External factors include sudden changes in the price of commodities, which lead to a sudden change in demand. Internal factors relate to the dynamic structure of the world economy, which leads to a cyclical, rather than a linear, model of growth.

T H E E X T E R N A L E N V I R O N M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

Political, legal and regulatory forces The political, legal and regulatory forces of the external environment can have an indirect, though powerful, impact on the operations of companies. What companies demand is stability in the politico-economic and regulatory environment. Even so, in reality, something like this does not happen often. An attempt to study and analyse this environment is, therefore, essential so that a company can be prepared if there is a change, particularly companies such as shipping companies, which operate in an international environment and, hence, in different political and legal contexts. A shipping company, for example, may have its headquarters in Cyprus, its offices in London, Piraeus, Singapore and New York, use for its ships national flags, international registers or flags of convenience, employ seafarers from Greece, the Philippines, Ukraine and Pakistan and its ships travelling to every corner of the globe, therefore obliging it to satisfy different requirements in different maritime regions. Given that it faces a complex legal and political environment, it must take care to continuously analyse it so as to anticipate any changes that may impact its operations. The choice of flag of the ships, as will be analysed in Chapter 8, contributes to the formation of their manning costs as restrictions with regard to the nationality of the seafarers in national flags limit the choices of the shipping companies, at least for certain positions of the crew. In addition, differences with regard to the implementation of safety and environmental regulations as well as with the tax avoidance contribute to the differentiation of cost conditions offered by the different categories of flags. Through their activities, many shipping companies aspire to shape favourable conditions within their external environment, which they can exploit in order to strengthen their competitive position. Illustrative of this is the case of certain shipping companies in Japan, which aimed to create favourable conditions that only they would be able to exploit (Box 2.5).

Cultural and social forces Changes in the social environment as well as expectations of the society can influence the activities of companies and the demand for their products or services. The social environment is based on people’s attitudes, convictions, desires, expectations, education and habits. Cultural and social forces can be transformed into direct forces if the companies are unable to face up to their indirect impact. In the case of the shipping industry, for example, raising awareness of environmental issues, a trend associated with the socio-economic environment, has contributed to the introduction of new rules and regulations, which in their turn have influenced the organisation and management of shipping companies. The accidents of the Exxon Valdez in the Antarctic in 1989 and the Erika and Prestige in 1999 and 2002, respectively, the great social opposition to marine pollution, in combination with the work of the environmental organisations and groups that demanded protection of the marine environment from their governments, led the USA and the European Union to take measures that have had a significant impact on the operation of freight markets and determined the way in which transportation operations would be organised and conducted on the part of the shipping companies. The passage of the Oil Pollution Act in the USA in 1990 placed certain limitations and preconditions on vessels that approach American waters and effectively strengthened the dominance of double-hull vessels. Correspondingly, the accident of the Erika led to the passing of Erika I and Erika II set of measures by the European Union in 2000, while the accident of the Prestige created an atmosphere of panic which, in its turn, obliged the European Union to speed up the timetable for phasing out single-hull vessels (Box 2.6). The measures described in Box 2.6 provided those companies that either operated double-hull oil tankers or had already ordered them and were to receive them from the shipbuilders in the near future with an opportunity. At the same time, however, it posed a threat for those companies that operated single-hull vessels, which in order to comply with the measures had to replace them immediately. To this category, for example, belongs Dynacom Tankers Management (www.dynacomtm.com), which was forced to change its strategy in order to remain competitive under the new conditions (Box 2.7).

BOX 2.6 The Erika and Prestige disasters and the mandatory phasing-out of single-hull vessels On 12 December 1999, the Malta-flagged Erika, a single-hull oil tanker with a carrying capacity of 37,283 dwt, built in 1975, broke in two and sank 40 miles out from the coast of Brittany, causing one of the greatest ecological disasters in European waters for many years. Approximately 15,000 tonnes of oil spilt out into the sea, polluting the shores for up to 400 kilometres. Almost two years later, on 19 November 2002, the Bahamas-flagged Prestige, a singlehull vessel with a carrying capacity of 77,000 dwt, which was 26 years old, sank 130 miles from the coasts of Spain. Six days earlier, the vessel had cracked down its right side. The captain requested the Spanish authorities to provide the vessel with a port of refuge so as to avoid its sinking, but the request was not approved and the vessel was removed from the Spanish coasts by tugboats. The sinking of the Prestige was a great ecological disaster, as thousands of tonnes of oil spilt into the sea, polluting the

port of refuge: port to which a ship that is in danger can divert.

coasts of Spain and France. The Erika shipwreck sparked the reflexes of the European Union and led to the Erika I and Erika II legislative packages. Subsequently, the shipwreck of the Prestige speeded up the implementation of the measures. In March 2009, the European Parliament approved of the third legislative package, known as Erika III. The Erika I package included measures for the control of vessels in ports, control of the activities of the classification societies and proposals to speed up the time taken to phase out single-hull oil tankers. The second legislative package, Erika II, contained three new measures that aimed to improve safety levels. The third legislative package, Erika III, contained measures to prevent accidents and for dealing with them when they occur. More specifically, the measures related to the responsibilities of the flag-states, classification societies, port state controls, marine traffic monitoring, investigation of accidents, liabilities and compensation for damage to passengers and the insurance of shipowners against claims. The initiative taken by the European Union, in conjunction with the corresponding initiative in the USA, speeded up developments at the IMO. The IMO eventually adopted regulations corresponding to the Erika II package, which amended the MARPOL Convention and imposed an emergency programme for the gradual phasingout of all single-hull tankers, a special inspection regime for these vessels and the mandatory transportation of the most dangerous oil products in double-hull vessels from April 2005.

❚ Shipping companies, freight markets and fluctuations

8

This section analyses the third level of the external environment of shipping companies (Figure 2.1), which includes the four markets (Stopford, 2009) that determine the supply and demand of maritime transport services in the short term. Shipping companies operate competitively in the freight markets, where they seek to have their vessels chartered, they build vessels or acquire secondhand ones in order to expand their fleets. They sell vessels so that they can either be used further or demolished. They operate, that is, in four markets, which shape an environment marked by intensive fluctuations and which impacts upon the competitiveness of the companies. Figure 2.3 shows the four markets that control shipping, the ways in which these markets operate, their interactions and the fluctuations they manifest. Various products and services are exchanged in each of these four markets. In the freight markets, maritime transport services are offered. In the sale and purchase market, second-hand vessels are bought and sold. In the shipbuilding market, transactions concern the construction of vessels and, finally, in the demolition market, vessels are purchased for the purpose of being broken up into scrap. These four markets are common to all competing shipping companies. From this perspective, they comprise the environment within which competition is created in order to attract customers/shippers and human resources, as well as for the supply of the necessary resources. Certainly, as will be analysed below, in the context in particular of the shipping markets and the market for secondhand vessels, there are sub-markets, which relate to the types of vessels. As a consequence, the specialisation of shipping company fleets is a factor that differentiates the influence that these markets exercise. The analysis that follows is, of necessity, brief and aims to provide an understanding of the basic features of the markets and the limitations that these impose upon shipping companies. Liquidity in shipping Liquidity is shaped by the inflows to shipping companies in the shipping market, as a result of the chartering of vessels by shippers. At the same time, inflows come from the demolition market, from vessels sold to demolition yards either because their technology is considered outdated or because operating them is now unprofitable. Alongside this, there are also outflows, capital that companies give to shipbuilders as payment for the vessels they have built for them. The fourth market, the sale and purchase market, does not affect the total liquidity in shipping because, in practice, this capital is recycled simply by changing hands, from the companies-buyers of the second-hand vessels to the companiessellers, and it thus always remains with the companies.

remaining markets are unified but also distinguished internally according to the type of vessel, its equipment, the cargoes it can transport and other criteria. The freight markets, for example, are comprised of many different shipping markets. The specialisation and the size of the ships the shipping companies are operating define the freight markets in which they will be active. If, for example, there are two shipping companies that operate bulk carriers, the first handysize and the second capesize, normally they do not compete with each other as their vessels do not transport the same cargoes and, as a rule, they do not use the same shipping routes. They do not, therefore, take part in the same freight markets. From the moment that their freight markets differ and given that developments in freight markets define developments in the sale and purchase markets and, to an extent, in the shipbuilding market, it is clear that the conditions that the two companies will face in these markets may also differ, at least in the short term.

Developments in the markets when demand for freight transportation exceeds the supply of the vessel capacity The freight market The freight market is the place where the supply and demand in transportation services meet. The shipping company comes to the markets offering a vessel that is seaworthy, with particular specifications, for which it seeks a charterer. The shipper or charterer has a cargo that he wishes to transport from one location to another. The role of the intermediary is undertaken by the broker. The supply of vessel capacity and the demand for cargo transportation determine the level of the freight rate. When the demand for cargo transport is greater than the supply of vessel capacity, then rates rise. In this way, the income of the companies also rises, i.e., the total shipping liquidity. The fluctuations in rates for all categories of vessel (as can be seen in Table 2.1, which shows the highest and lowest time-charter rates per day for certain types of vessel in the period 2002–2017) may be particularly strong, even over brief periods, a characteristic that is attributable to the rapid change in demand in relation to supply. The charts in Figures 2.4 and 2.5 show the lower and higher annual time-charter rates for a capesize bulk carrier and a VLCC oil tanker, respectively, for the period 2002–2017. The fluctuations for most years in this period are especially pronounced. The fluctuations in capesize rates, particularly for 2008, are such that the highest rate, which occurred at the beginning of the year, was 9.5 times greater than the lowest rate, which occurred towards the end of the year. The shipbuilding market In times of high demand for transport services and, consequently, an increase in freight rates, expectations of higher profits motivate shipping companies to decide to build new ships or purchase second-hand ones. order and the delivery, market conditions may change, as happened in the autumn of 2008, when the plunge in freight rates followed a reduction in shipbuilding prices. The option to build a ship is, therefore, a risk to a certain extent. In such periods, several shipping companies exploit the shipbuilding contracts or options they have signed with the shipbuilders in the preceding period and enter the market seeking to make a profit, selling them and making significant profits (Theotokas, 2005). A typical example of this is the successful sale of the contract for the building of a VLCC oil tanker (320,000 dwt) by the Embiricos group to Gulf Marine Management. The vessel had been ordered by the Embiricos group from Daewoo shipbuilders in Korea in early 2003 for 65 m. dollars, with a delivery date of November 2004. According to press reports, the Embiricos group sold the contract for the building of the ship in October 2004 for 125 m. dollars, thereby making a 100 per cent profit (Newsfront, 2004b). Also illustrative is the case of Transmed Shipping, which according to press reports at the time, made a profit of 250 m. dollars from the sale of contracts for eight capesize bulk carriers (Whittaker, 2010). In this case, the price of the ship that is to be delivered is significantly higher than the corresponding price for a new order as the risk is reduced for the buyer of the length of time between the placing of the order with the shipbuilder and the delivery of the vessel. The situation, of course, also exists where the order for building a ship does not prove to be a good choice because of the change of conditions in the freight markets, and so the shipowner who placed the order may decide to sell the shipbuilding contract. In this case, the buyer may benefit from the transaction. One illustrative example is that of the Samos Steamship company, which, according to details published in the press, paid 43.5 m. dollars in late 2011 for an oil tanker of 104,400 dwt that was under construction and was to be delivered four months later, which had been ordered in Sumitomo Shipbuilding in Japan by another shipping company in 2008 for 72 m. The order was subsequently cancelled by the company that placed it (Newsfront, 2011). From the data in Table 2.2, the lowest price for the construction of aframax tankers for contracts signed in 2011 was 52 m. dollars. The sale and purchase market Shipping companies that wish directly to exploit this positive coincidence in the freight market tend to seek and purchase second-hand vessels. The vessels are purchased just like goods in other markets. Companies that, for various reasons, no longer wish to operate their vessels enter the market as sellers. Companies that wish to acquire a vessel enter the market as buyers. The role of the intermediary is undertaken by sale and purchase brokers. The law of supply and demand operates here too, setting the price of second-hand vessels. The freight rate, inflation, age of the vessel and the expectations of the ship-owners are, inter alia, the factors that influence the price of a vessel. Another element must also be taken into account, however. At times of high freight rates and, hence, positive results for companies, the majority do not wish to reduce their fleets, unless they

46

T H E E X T E R N A L E N V I R O N M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

will be compensated for the profits that they would have made had they continued to operate their vessels. This means that the supply of second-hand vessels is reduced at the moment when demand is high, resulting in their prices also increasing. As freight rates rise, so do the expectations of the companies, thus leading to a rise in demand for vessels. At such moments, the prices of secondhand vessels rise rapidly (Box 2.8) and reach or even exceed the current price for ordering similar new vessels. In the sale and purchase markets for second-hand vessels, a significant number of companies chase profits, seeing the vessel as an asset and attempting to exploit price changes by buying when prices are low and selling when they are high. These companies sell when there is a marked difference between the market price and the sale price. The resulting profits can, depending on conditions in the freight markets, be impressive. Of course, the right moment is often decisive

BOX 2.8 Changing sale prices of vessels over a short-term period The following items relate to the sale of two vessels, the stated sale prices having fluctuated greatly within a short period of time. In March 2005, the Agia Eirini, a bulk carrier with a carrying capacity of 26,092/45,262 dwt which was built in 1994 (Shin Kurushima Dockyard, Onishi, Japan), was sold by the Greek shipping company Marmaras Navigation to Russian buyers for 26–27 m. dollars (Newsfront, 2005). Fifteen months later it was reported that the same vessel was sold to unknown buyers for 19 m. dollars (Newsfront, 2006). Obviously, in both the cases above the agreement was not completed. Thus in March 2007, the Agia Eirini, which had been purchased by Marmaras Navigation for 16 m. dollars in 1999, was sold to buyers from South Korea for 31 m. dollars, i.e., at almost double its purchase price, but so at a price much higher than those given in 2005 and 2006 (Newsfront, 2007b). The following two items concern the Zorbas, a Cypriot-flagged bulk carrier, with a carrying capacity of 165,133 dwt, built in 1996 (Stocznia Gdynia S.A., Gdynia, Poland) and also belonging to Marmaras Navigation. According to the first item, the vessel was sold in March of 2007 to an Indian company for 70 m. dollars (Newsfront, 2007a). Three months later, it was reported that this sale was not completed and the vessel was eventually sold to buyers from Hong Kong for 86 m. dollars, in other words, for a price 16 per cent above that given in March 2007 (Newsfront, 2007c).

47

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

for a successful outcome. The press covered the case of the bulk carrier Standard Vigor, 64,120 dwt, built in 1981, which was sold for 25 m. dollars in the autumn of 2007 to unknown buyers. Two years earlier, this vessel was to be sold for 6.9 m. dollars, but the final sale agreement was not completed (Newsfront, 2007d). The fact that this first attempt at a sale was not completed was ultimately in the interests of the company that owned the vessel, given that, aside from eventually securing almost four times the sale price, it was also able to earn extremely high profits from chartering it in a two-year period during which freight rates were at particularly high levels. Even so, these dimensions should not give the impression that high profits are the rule or that they can always easily be achieved in the shipping industry, particularly in the bulk shipping sector. As will be discussed in Chapter 3, capitalising upon the conjuncture when making decisions for the sale and purchase of vessels is not always easy. The decision-making process is complex and involves a series of factors, the impact of which it is crucial to evaluate. In any case, this is an entrepreneurial game involving two players, for each of whom the result may differ, given the cyclical character of the markets. The demolition market The mechanism of supply and demand functions in the same way in the vessels demolition market. Here, demolition prices determine the supply of vessels on the part of the companies as well as the demand for scrap from the demolition yards. During periods of high freight rates, when the whole fleet is profitably in use, companies do not sell their vessels for scrap and the supply of such vessels falls. This results in higher demolition rates.

Developments in the markets when the demand for transportation falls below the supply of vessels The markets move in the opposite direction when the demand for transportation of cargoes is less than the supply of capacity of the vessels, resulting in the freight rates showing a tendency to fall. In this case, the demand on the part of companies for shipbuilding, because of negative expectations, will be small, while, at the same time, the supply of shipbuilding (productive capacity of the shipyards) will be a given. This will cause a drop in the prices of shipbuilding. At the same time, in periods of low freights, it is a given that the liquidity of companies is reduced, a phenomenon which, in conjunction with the fact that some vessels will remain unemployed, leads many companies to decide to sell them, thus increasing the supply of second-hand ships. Furthermore, some companies are compelled to review decisions on the building of ships which they had taken at earlier periods when the freight market was at a high point and cancel orders placed with shipyards (Box 2.9). At the same time, however, most companies, because of their negative expectations, abstain from buying ships, in spite of the fact that their prices may be exceptionally attractive, with the exception, of course, of those companies which, following a ‘counter-cyclical’ (Thanopoulou, 1996) investment

48

T H E E X T E R N A L E N V I R O N M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

strategy, wish to acquire them at low prices with a view to selling them later when their prices increase. This strategy, which is also described as asset play, characterises an approach to conducting business in shipping, mostly in bulk shipping (Harlaftis & Theotokas, 2004). Greek owners have implemented this strategy repeatedly with success (Harlaftis, 1996; Thanopoulou, 1996; Theotokas, 1997). This leads many researchers and analysts to attribute the strategy of asset play to Greek owners,10 however, over the course of time many companies of different nationalities have also implemented it. The features that are crucial for the adoption and implementation of this strategy are flexibility and speed in decision making. These characteristics are mostly to be found in family firms. The case of Oldenforff, the German shipping company which is a leading dry bulk operator, is to be taken into account: from August 2003 to August 2008, i.e., the period the markets prospered, sold 92 ships at prices totaling about 1.9 billion dollars (Oldendorff, 2010). Oldendorff considers that family ownership gives them “an edge over many of our competitors, who have a more anonymous shareholding structure. Profits are not paid out as dividends, but retained for future investments, so we can pursue a long-term counter-cyclical strategy” (Oldendorff, 2017). It is contended, however, in a BIMCO study that the business model characterised by this strategy may be affected by structural changes in the markets,

BOX 2.9 Situation assessment and review of decisions on cancellations of shipbuilding orders During the closing months of 2008 and in 2009, there were many cancellations of orders for the building of ships which had been placed by companies in the period when the market was prospering, more specifically in the early months of 2008 (Figure 2.6). The cancellations were not connected in all cases with a review of the decision of the shipping companies, but may have been due to delay on the part of the shipyards, or to their inability to respond to the demands of the contracts. Those companies which decided not to go ahead with fulfilling the orders for the building of ships undertook the cost of this cancellation, as they calculated that the shipbuilding would require outflows of capital which they no longer had available, or, even if they had it, it was needed to meet other, more urgent, needs. By way of indication, the case of Genco Shipping & Trading (www.gencoshipping.com) can be cited. Genco, according to press reports, in November 2008 cancelled an order of a sum of 530 m. dollars involving the building of six dry cargo vessels, thus losing the prepayment of 53 m. dollars which it had made. Among the reasons leading to the cancellation, efforts to maintain the company’s sound liquidity in the difficult environment shaped by the conditions prevailing in the freights market are cited (Wright, 2008). In several cases, cancellations become known, but for commercial reasons, the name of the shipping company remains undisclosed.11
