especially those of dry bulk shipping. The trend for dry bulk shipping companies is to move towards increasing their size and becoming logistic providers to the commodity giants, interested more in risk management and return on capital employment. In this context, asset play will become a subsidiary benefit rather than the main business goal. In addition, the frequency of shipping cycles is expected to be reduced, a fact that will make the business model of asset play less attractive and will pose a risk to the small family-run shipping companies based on this model (BIMCO, 2016). In conditions of increased supply of tonnage and reduced demand for transportation, the prices of second-hand ships fall even further, forcing companies which want to get rid of their vessels, either because capital is needed to finance the operation of the rest of their fleet, or because they estimate that they have lost their value in technological terms, to search for buyers in the demolition market. Thus, the increased supply of such ships leads to a reduction in the scrap prices.

❚ The immediate external environment of shipping companies After the account of the broader macro-environment of companies more generally, and of shipping companies more particularly, the analysis will focus upon forces in the immediate external environment of operation of the latter, forces which exert a definitive influence both on the companies and on their competitors. The immediate environment of companies consists of four groups of factors: customers, competitors, suppliers and human resources.

50

T H E E X T E R N A L E N V I R O N M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

Customers In the case of bulk cargo shipping, the maritime transportation service is supplied from company to company. The customers are the most important of the immediate forces and the most crucial for companies. Changes in the preferences of customers prompt companies to a mandatory adjustment. A knowledge of the needs of customers and of the way in which these alter over time, of their characteristics and purchasing behaviour, is of decisive importance, and has to be taken into account in the mapping out of the companies’ strategy. For example, one of the reasons which led, in 2000, to the decision on a merger of two of the biggest shipping companies in the chemicals branch, the Greek-owned Ceres Hellenic Shipping and the Norwegian Odfjell ASA (www.odfjell.com), was their effort to respond to developments in the branch. A series of acquisitions and mergers had led to the formation of large shipper companies which were looking for carriers capable of providing international cover. Odfjell-Ceres had, at the time of the merger, a market share of 26 per cent and was the leading company in the branch (LSE, 2000). Similarly, in 2008, Maersk Tankers (www. maersktankers.com) acquired Brostrom (www.brostrom.com) in order to form a fleet which would permit the supply of services on a world basis, to achieve economies of scale, and to provide its clients with easy access to chartering offices world-wide. By the acquisition and combination of the fleets of the two companies, the largest company of product tankers, consisting of more than 130 ships (in the ownership or under the long-term management of the company) was created. The combined tanker fleet of all types consisted of 241 ships (Laurent, 2008). As to the need for adjustment on the part of companies to the requirements of customers, the development in the market of liner shipping in recent years is indicative. Economies of scale, resources specificity and frequency of transactions lead to companies to develop internally or through mergers and acquisitions, to integrate vertically and to form strategic alliances (Harlaftis & Theotokas, 2010). From the point where the extent of the activities of the customers of liner shipping companies grew, these companies have been forced to make the adjustments necessary to satisfy the demand for transportation. Understanding customers’ special requirements and responding to them by creating appropriate service are dynamic capabilities for liner shipping companies which influence their competitive advantage (Kuo et al, 2017). The need to service the worldwide demand is satisfied only by companies which are able to provide transportation solutions on a world scale. Thus, liner shipping companies seek to acquire world-wide cover and to provide their clients with integrated solutions for the transporting of their products from the locations of production to those of consumption, not only by sea, but also on land. In order to succeed in their aim, they turn to verticalisation of their activities, that is, to options for the development of their activities, as has already been described, whether these precede or follow maritime transportation, or collaborate with other companies in the branch. Thus, strategic alliances and acquisitions and mergers (Box 2.10) are foremost strategies in liner shipping.

strategic alliances: a form of collaboration between two or more companies for the pursuit of common aims by means of the employment of joint resources.

51

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

BOX 2.10 Strategic alliances, acquisitions and mergers in liner shipping Strategic alliances are a form of collaboration between two or more companies for the pursuit of common aims by means of the employment of joint resources. One of the benefits which they bring to their members is flexibility (Ryoo and Thanopoulou, 1999). There are a number of factors which favour collaboration between companies in liner shipping, such as the need for apportionment of risk, economies of scale, cost control and an increase in the frequency of the provision of services. In the late 1990s, new forces led companies to closer collaboration: globalisation of markets and the low profitability of most companies. The changes brought about by globalisation compelled liner shipping companies to follow their clients on a world scale and to provide feasible solutions for their transportation needs (Midoro and Pitto, 2000). In these conditions, companies have looked for new forms of collaboration, such as strategic alliances, which would be capable of guaranteeing coverage on a world scale and better employment of their assets. Various authors have linked the formation of global shipping strategic alliances with the achievement of objectives which have been classified as follows (Song & Panayides, 2002):

❚

financial objectives (profit maximisation, increase in shareholder wealth, capital investment sharing and risk reduction);

❚ ❚

economic objectives (cost reduction, economies of scale); strategic objectives (entry into new markets, wider geographical scope, increase in purchasing power);

❚

marketing objectives (better satisfaction of customer requirements, e.g., by higher frequency of service, flexibility, reliability, variety of routes and destinations);

❚

operational objectives (increase in frequency of services, vessel planning and co-ordination on a global scale).

In spite of the advantages offered by strategic alliances, they have proved to be less stable than expected, since their members are likely to resort to moves such as mergers with companies belonging to other strategic alliances, thus forcing the alliances to adjust their programmes. This lack of stability prevented the alliances from engaging in long-term planning and investment, with the result that they were unable to benefit to the full from the scope which they have for reducing their cost (Hoffmann, 1998). During the first half of the first decade of 2000, there have been a number of acquisitions and mergers in the liner shipping sector involving chiefly large-size companies in competition with one another, such as Nedlloyd by P&O, Sealand by Maersk and P&O Nedlloyd by Maersk (Box 5.6) and the Canadian Pacific, which

52

T H E E X T E R N A L E N V I R O N M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

expanded to global range by acquiring a series of smaller competitors (Alix et al, 1999) by Hapag Lloyd.12 In the second half of the first decade of 2000, no significant merger and acquisition activity was observable in the same sector, as major players implemented a strategy of organic growth. However, starting from 2014, a new course of M&A transactions has been noted, as will be explained in the paragraphs which follow. This new wave of consolidation is driven by the companies’ pursuit of survival in tough market conditions, while that which took place in the preceding decade was driven by a desire for growth (Drewry, 2016). During 2013, the three largest liner shipping companies, Maersk Line (www. maerskline.com), the Mediterranean Shipping Company (www.mscgva.ch) and CMA CGM (www.cma-cgm.com), whose market share cumulatively exceeded 37 per cent, announced their agreement on concluding the P3 Alliance, the purpose of which would be the exploitation of the carrying capacity of their vessels on the Asia-Europe, and Pacific and Atlantic routes. It is interesting to note that all three companies are described as ‘family-owned businesses’, a feature which in earlier studies has been linked with flexibility and speed in decision-making as well as independence from short-term pressures from shareholders (Slack and Fremont, 2008). The partners were to contribute those of their ships which were employed on these routes in a joint effort to manage the fleet independently, but continuing to have independent sales, customer service and marketing departments. The fleet to be managed by P3 would have a carrying capacity of approximately 2.6 m. teu. The three companies estimated that P3 would make it possible to offer improved services in terms of cover and frequency of schedules, would create the preconditions for a better use of the larger vessels and would have environmental benefits. A condition for the setting up of P3 was the approval of the regulatory authorities and of the companies themselves, by virtue of the final agreement. As was to be expected, this move by the three biggest companies gave rise to concerns over the future of competition in liner shipping, provoking a reaction on the part of other companies. The G6 alliance, set up in 2011 by the uniting of the Grand Alliance (GA) and the New World Alliance (NWA), thus bringing together six companies, NYK (www2.nykline. com), Hapag-Lloyd (www.hapag-lloyd.com) and OOCL (www.oocl. com) from the GA, and APL (www.apl.com), Hyundai Merchant Marine (www. hmm21.com) and MOL (www.mol.co.jp) from the NWA, decided in late 2013 to go ahead with further collaboration between its members on basic lines of action of P3, on condition of the approval of the regulatory authorities (Hellenic Shipping News, 2013). To return to the P3 agreement and the developments which followed, the U.S. Federal Maritime Commission (FMC) approved the agreement in March 2014, while the

53

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

European Commission decided in June 2014 to close the file of the enquiry into the implementation of the anti-trust legislation. There was, nevertheless, no similar approval from the Chinese Ministry of Commerce (MOFCOM), so that on 17 June 2014, it was announced that the three partners were stopping their preparatory actions for the setting up of P3 (Maersk, 2014). A few weeks later, the Maersk and MSC companies announced a ten-year vessel-sharing agreement called 2M, which would include 185 vessels on 21 routes with a capacity of 2.1 million teu, expected to start early in 2015. Since the vessel-sharing agreement did not constitute the creation of a joint company and would have a smaller market share, approvals from the authorities were not required and it could be put into effect immediately (Crouch, 2014). The third company in the P3, CMA CGM, in September 2014, signed three agreements with the China Shipping Container Lines (CSCL) company and the United Arab Shipping Company (UASC). The agreements under the name Ocean Three concerned (CMA CGM, 2014) the Asia-Europe, Asia-Mediterranean and the Asia-US East Coast maritime trades. In April 2016, collaboration was announced between the CMA CGM, COSCO Container Lines, Evergreen Line and Orient Overseas Container Line companies in the setting up of the OCEAN Alliance, which, subject to regulatory approval, aimed “to offer competitive products and comprehensive service networks covering the Asia-Europe, Asia-Mediterranean, Asia-Red Sea, Asia-Middle East, TransPacific, Asia-North America East Coast, and Trans-Atlantic trades” (CMA CGM, 2016a). One month later, in May of 2016, the setting up of a new alliance under the name of the THE Alliance by the Hanjin, Hapag-Lloyd, ‘K’ Line, Mitsui O.S.K. Lines, Nippon Yusen Kaisha and Yang Ming companies, covering all East-West trade lanes, namely, Asia-Europe/Mediterranean, Asia-North America West Coast, Asia-North America East Coast, Transatlantic and Asia-Mid East/Persian Gulf/Red Sea, was announced. The market share of the THE Alliance, when it started operation in April 2017, was 18 per cent. Also indicative of the transformations which are taking place in the liner shipping market are, inter alia, the following moves made by companies which were in the pipeline at the same period:

❚

Hapag Lloyd merged with UASC. When the merger was completed (24-05-2017), Hapag Lloyd was the fifth largest liner shipping company in the world, with a fleet of 230 vessels and a shared fleet capacity of approximately 1.6 million teu (Hapag Lloyd, 2017). Indicative of rapid changes and the realignments observable in the sector is that three years earlier Hapag Lloyd completed its merger with Chilean Compañía Sud Americana de Vapores (CSAV), becoming the fourth largest container liner shipping company in the world, with a fleet of 200 vessels and capacity of approximately 1 million teu (Hapag Lloyd, 2014).

❚

The CMA CGM and Neptune Orient Lines (NOL) companies, after discussions lasting many months, agreed on the acquisition of the latter by the former. This

54

T H E E X T E R N A L E N V I R O N M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

is the largest acquisition in the history of the French company, worth a sum of 2.4 bn. dollars. After completion of the acquisition, the managed fleet consisted of 536 vessels, of a carrying capacity of 2.4 m. teu, and a market share of 11.7 per cent, while the employees of the group were more than 29,000 (CMA CGM, 2016b). Moving further its effort to consolidate position, CMA CGM announced in June 2017 the agreement to acquire Mercosul Line from Maersk Line, subject to Brazilian regulatory approvals. Mercosul is a leading player in Brazil’s domestic container market (CMA CGM, 2017).

❚

The China Ocean Shipping Group (COSCO) and China Shipping Group merger set up the China Cosco Shipping Corporation (COSCOCS) (China Cosco, 2017). The merger, of course, not only includes the container fleet and activities, but also the fleets managed by the two companies. The container fleet of new company was the fourth largest, with a carrying capacity of 1.58 m. teu (see Box 1.6). However, after the acquisition of the Hong Kong based company OOCL by COSCO, a deal valued at around 6 billion dollars, which was announced in early July 2017, the company jumped to third position in the hierarchy of the liner shipping market, operating more than 400 vessels of a capacity exceeding 2.9 million teus (OOIL, 2017).

❚

2M agreed with Hyundai Merchant Marine to launch their strategic co-operation for a period of three years with an option for extension. The co-operation includes a series of slot exchanges and slot purchases between the partners on east-west trades (Shen, 2017).

❚

The Maersk Line concluded the agreement for the acquisition of the German container shipping line Hamburg Sud (see Box 4.5).

Tanker Management Self- Assessment (TMSA): a methodology of self-assessment of performance in specific areas of companies managing tankers. Key Performance Indicators: indicators which measure performance in operational areas which are crucial for the achievement of a company’s strategic objectives.

56

The influence exerted by customers on the organisation and operation of companies can be particularly important. This is true when specific customers account for a significant portion of demand for the companies’ products or services, in which case their negotiating power with them is considerable. For example, where a shipper covers a significant part of the supply of maritime transportation services of a shipping company, it is likely to impose specific rules on its operation in order to ensure that the carrying service supplied to it will correspond to the special standards which it lays down itself. At the same time, clients may collaborate so as to impose specific terms of operation on these companies. The instance of the oil companies which have developed standards and procedures for the inspection of vessels which they charter for the transportation of their cargoes is indicative. Companies managing tankers, that is to say, have to comply with the special requirements of specific customers for them to choose them for the transportation of their cargoes. In order to facilitate efforts to check on the standards of operation of shipping companies, oil companies have introduced the Tanker Management Self-Assessment/ TMSA programme (Chapter 9), which is used by companies managing tankers for their self-assessment in the light of a series of Key Performance Indicators. The result of the self-assessment is taken into account by the oil companies in their decision on the chartering of the vessels, a fact which prompts the shipping companies to seek to improve their performance and their internal efficiency. In an industry with marked cyclical fluctuations such as shipping, factors which have to be taken into account by the company are the reliability of the client and his ability to fulfil his obligations, even if there is a reversal of conditions in the charter markets. Indicative of this is the case cited in Box 2.11. The experience of many shipping companies in periods of crisis in the charter markets has been of a negative nature, because of the neglect of the obligations arising from the charter parties for vessels signed by shippers in a period of high freights, and in a number of cases has been the cause of their bankruptcy.

T H E E X T E R N A L E N V I R O N M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

BOX 2.11 Changes in the level of freights and creditworthiness of charterers As has been discussed in Section 2 (Figure 2.4), in 2008, the time charter freight rates for capesize vessels collapsed to particularly low levels. The falling of charter rates in conjunction with the negative expectations on the prospects of the markets created a very unstable environment. Many charterers tried to cancel chartering contracts signed in the previous period, when the charter market peaked. This led to disputes between charterers and shipping companies with regard to the validity of the contracts. In such a case, Star Bulk Carriers, the NYSE listed bulk operator, announced that it was in dispute with the charterer of one of its capesize bulk carriers, the Star Beta. The vessel had been chartered to the Ukrainian charterer Industrial Carriers until February 2010, at a daily rate of 106,500 dollars. The charterer sub-chartered the vessel to Oldendorff until February 2009 at a daily rate of 130,000 dollars and assigned the rights of the contract to Star Bulk. In the meantime, the Ukrainian charterer was reported to be asking for its inclusion under a regime of protection from its creditors (Karageorgos, 2008b). However, Oldendorff disputed the re-assignment of the contract to Star Bulk and attempted to have it cancelled. Star Bulk contended that the re-assignment was valid, so the dispute started. The dispute went to arbitration proceedings. Finally, in April 2011, it was announced by Star Bulk that the dispute was settled, with the company receiving a major portion of the amount of the claim (Star Bulk, 2011).

Competitors A company’s competitors are its ‘rivals’, as it is with them that it will compete for the sale of its products or services, and for the acquisition of resources. Similar competitors are those with the same or similar products. For example, a shipping company which manages refrigerated cargo vessels has as similar, direct competitors those companies which manage ships of the same type, but it is in competition with those liner companies which manage vessels carrying containers, which can also transport refrigerated containers, and therefore take cargoes away from it. The survival and development of companies depend to a large degree on their ability to make the first move before their competitors and in such a way that the latter are not able to imitate them. They must be first to realise what opportunities are being offered in the business environment and make efforts to exploit them, as well as to deal with the likely threats to which it gives rise. This calls for an analysis of the forces in the environment, and particularly of the competition. Companies have to analyse the strategies, the strengths and weaknesses of their competitors, in order to be in a position to foresee their moves and react in

57

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

an appropriate way. This will permit the effective use of the resources and capabilities which they have at their disposal for the appropriate exploitation of the opportunities. An instructive instance of successful analysis of competition which led to the formation of a strategy for the exploitation of the opportunities provided by the environment is that of Costamare Shipping (Box 2.12), a strategy which led to the successful penetration of and establishment of the company in the markets for the management of container vessels.

BOX 2.12 Competition over the management of container ships13 Costamare Shipping (www.costamare.com) began its activities in 1974. The purchase of aged vessels at a low fixed cost, the personal involvement of its founder, V. Constantakopoulos (1935–2011), in every aspect of the task of management and the strict monitoring of all the features of the running cost were the factors which contributed to its development. From the very beginning, it chose the exclusive use of the Greek flag for its vessels, and sought to form a team of capable seafarers to man them, whom, apart from permanent employment, it sought to provide with satisfactory working conditions. In the first decade of its operations, Costamare purchased vessels at periods of low and sold them in periods of high freight rates. This strategy supplied it with considerable liquidity and allowed it to take advantage of the opportunities which presented themselves in the second-hand ships markets. The analysis of the business environment led Constantakopoulos to conclude that the intensity of competition in markets in which the company was already active could be a source of a future threat, given that Costamare was already achieving the best possible level of cost. A further reduction could only be achieved by a change in the company’s basic approach, that is, by the registration of the vessels on registers of flags of convenience and the replacement of Greek seafarers by colleagues from low-cost countries. However, this was not within the purposes of Constantakopoulos, who thus came to the conclusion that his company would have prospects in a specialist sector, which would permit the utilisation of its capabilities in the effective and profitable management of ships, and, more particularly, container ships. The objective was not to move into liner shipping, but simply to the management of ships serving regular routes. In effect, the aim was the implementation of the basic strategy for operating in bulk shipping, that is, of the management and chartering of vessels to third parties, in a new market, the market of liner shipping. Constantakopoulos estimated that this market provided opportunities for an independent shipowner, to the extent that he would be able to satisfy the requirements of liner shipping companies in terms of frequency and reliability of provision of services. Up to then,

58

T H E E X T E R N A L E N V I R O N M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

it had been mostly German companies which had been active as independent owners who chartered for long periods the container ships which they managed to liner shipping companies. This for him was an ‘opportunity’, as he would in this way be able to enter a sector of the market in which his competitors would have greater cost than himself. Thus Constantakopoulos decided to exploit, but also to expand, the resources and capabilities of Costamare in order to enter, with the same service, the management of vessels, a new, specialist, market. This was the first Greek company to follow this strategy and the first in the world to attempt it without previously having had links with liner shipping companies. Constantakopoulos started his efforts to implement the company’s strategy by investing in the acquisition of the necessary resources. For this purpose, he took advantage of the opportunity which arose from the bankruptcy of the Greek liner shipping company Hellenic Lines. He acquired the first four container ships and engaged specialist personnel for their management. Undertaking the relevant risk, he accepted, on the first chartering of the vessels freights, 30 per cent lower than the already low freights prevailing at the given period in the market, in an effort to convince liner shipping companies in this way that a Greek company operating in bulk transport shipping would be able to support a regular line container service with its vessels in terms of frequency and reliability of service. Costamare succeeded in it and so being recognized in the market as a reliable manager. The high quality vessels and the fact that they were registered under the national flag contributed to this, together with the quality of its personnel on board the ships and in the offices ashore. In the years which followed, Costamare continued to invest in the acquisition of container ships, and began gradually to withdraw from the bulk shipping freight markets, selling off in favourable conditions the bulk carriers which it had under its management. It extended its fleet by acquiring larger and younger vessels and making moves which involved a considerable risk, but which contributed to the establishment of its reputation as a company dedicated to the management of container ships. From the mid 1990s onwards, Costamare’s development was based chiefly on building ships. It placed orders for ships of a larger size, having first ensured their chartering to liner shipping companies, thus reducing the risk of the investment. At the same time, it acquired a quality tonnage available in the second-hand ship markets. Costamare continued to derive its competitive advantage from its ability to combine quality management with cost leadership. By constant investments in the acquisition of ships and successful long-term collaborations with liner shipping companies such as Maersk Line, MSC, Cosco, Hapag Lloyd, etc., it developed its fleet and became the biggest independent company managing container ships. In the late 1990s, the

59

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

management of the company passed to the second generation of the family. The business continued its development by adjusting its strategic options. In 2010, its shares were introduced for trading on the New York Stock Exchange, while in 2013, in collaboration with the largest independent ship management company, V.Ships, it set up a joint company, whose task would be the management of Costamare’s vessels and those of third-party shipowners. In 2013 it announced the formation of containership acquisition joint venture with York Capital Management to invest jointly up to approximately $500 m. in equity for the acquisition of container vessels (Costamare, 2013). The company’s fleet in July 2017 consisted of 70 container ships with a carrying capacity ranging from 1078 to 14.24 teus, whereas its shipbuilding programme included orders for three container ships of various sizes (Costamare, 2017).

Suppliers All organisations need resources (capital, energy, equipment, services and materials) in order to produce their products and supply their services. These resources may be available in abundance or there may be a shortage of them; they may be provided by many suppliers, or by few. Furthermore, the suppliers can affect the quality, cost and time needed for production of companies. Stable and reliable collaboration with suppliers is a precondition for undertakings to function effectively. From this point of view, it is crucial for every company to be aware of the conditions prevailing in its suppliers’ markets, as well as the individual characteristics of each of them, in order to be in a position to negotiate effectively with them. The importance of a resource for the smooth functioning and performance of a company is a factor which determines the relationship which the company will choose to have with the supplier of this resource, that is, whether it will be longterm or temporary (see Chapter 10). In the case of resources which are of crucial importance and high value such as ships, companies may choose to maintain long-term relations with the shipyards. Even in this case, however, it is important for the company to seek out new suppliers capable of providing conditions which will contribute to a reduction in its operation costs. The instance of the G.S. Livanos Group and its relationship with the Hyundai Heavy Industries (HHI) shipyards is a case in point. Even before the facilities of the shipyards had been installed, the Livanos Group placed, in 1972, an order for the building of a VLCC vessel, at a price 16 per cent lower than that prevailing in the market. At the given moment, the Livanos Group was taking advantage of the wish of HHI to enter the market, while those in charge of the shipyards used the shipbuilding contract in order to secure financing for the construction of their facilities. The ship was delivered to the owners during November 1974. This mutually beneficial collaboration continued until 2002, with the Livanos Group building seven tanker ships at the HHI shipyards (Lloyd’s List, 2002).

60

T H E E X T E R N A L E N V I R O N M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

Similarly, the Angelicoussis Group, one of the biggest independent shipowners in the world, which manages tankers, bulk carriers and LNGs, has forged strong links with the Daewoo shipyards of Korea. Angelicoussis Group has built in total 92 ships, starting from 1994. When the Group placed an order for three VLCCs (April 2017), a total of 18 ships were under construction at DMSE’s shipyards in Okpo and Romania for the Group (Dong-chan, 2017). In the case of shipping companies, suppliers are a force which exerts definitive influence on their operation. The vessels are constantly on the move and travel along various sea routes. This means that the shipping company should have organised its administrative operation, linked to the supplies in such a way that it is certain not only that the necessary equipment and the necessary materials are available on the vessel when needed, but also that they are supplied on the most favourable terms for the company. It is important, therefore, that there should be a systematic investigation and record of the company’s permanent suppliers, but also of as many alternative sources of supplies as possible, so that it can at any time deal with emergency problems. A company’s scope for negotiation as regards its suppliers and an assessment of how far its operation can be affected if the latter are unable to respond to demand are two factors which must be estimated in order to assess how preferable it would be for the company to engage in the diversification of the sources of supply of the necessary resources and services, in a partial reverse vertical integration, thus seeking to produce services itself, instead of deriving them from its suppliers, or to conclude some form of collaboration with specific suppliers.

Human resources Human resources are perhaps the most crucial factor for every shipping company and are seen as the basis for the creation of a sustainable competitive advantage (Progoulaki & Theotokas, 2010). Human resources generate knowledge, capabilities and direction for the undertaking, and constitute the factor which will activate and exploit all the factors of production so that it achieves its predetermined objectives, and so have been regarded by a number of researchers as the most crucial resource for the competitiveness of every company (Lepak & Snell, 2009). But for this to happen, a company must recruit quality human resources, and it must – the company itself and its executives – be in a position to understand and take into account the factors which contribute to or determine the supply of labour by the human resources of a society. At the same time, it must be able to foresee changes (demographic, etc.) in the labour market and deal with the negative influences which these are likely to cause. It must also develop policies which permit the effective integration and management of employees (Box 2.13). The reason that HR is examined as factor of the direct environment of shipping companies is that there is intense competition in the global markets for seafaring labour, which becomes even more intense for the recruitment and retention of ship’s officers, who, as will be explained in Chapter 8, are in short

61

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

supply. In the case of companies whose activities are global and which derive factors of production from the world market, a knowledge of the conditions prevailing in various geographical regions/markets is especially important. In many cases, the potential for access to quality human resources determines the strategic options of companies. Shipping companies employ human resources in their offices on land, which may be situated in different geographical regions, and on the ships, on which employees of various nationalities may be employed. The recruitment, selection and retention of suitable human resources are crucial factors for the success of a shipping company. For this reason, the management of the human resources in a shipping company is analysed in separate chapters.

BOX 2.13 Selection of crews In the late 1990s, the shipowner Panos Laskaridis, in an interview14 with the Argo journal, commented on the quality of crews from Russia. Asked if Russian seafarers, because of their knowledge and characteristics, were sought after by shipping companies, he gave the following reply (Laskaridis, 1998): Listen, the Russians and Ukrainians, in any event, have today manned great percentage of the world fleet. But here there’s something else that we have to say. I don’t believe, personally, that there are crews with magical powers. We all cherish the highest opinion of Greek seafarers, not because they are by definition better than the rest, but because they are professionals whom we know, we’ve worked with them for years, we have rewarded them and we have been rewarded by them, and a strong and enduring bond has developed between us. But that can be said of other nationalities if you have worked with them with the same consistency. As a company, we did this for 15 years with the Koreans, as we are doing now with the Russians. If you gather up seafarers from the street, if you employ a seafarer once only, if you enlist people from different countries, some with previous service on tankers and others on passenger ships, and you put them all on the same ship, and at the same time you don’t look after them and you don’t pay them well, you’ll have the same wretched result, whether they’re Russians, or Filipinos, or Greeks. On the other hand, if you engage crews who have worked with the same company and on the same type of vessel from the day that they became seafarers, you reward them better than you are obliged to, you ensure them permanent employment, and you give them scope for career advancement, for example, to the rank of inspector, then I can assure you that there is the potential for creating a considerable number of crews who can compete with anyone in quality and performance.

From functions to processes. From profit to performance. From products to customers. From inventory to information. From transactions to relationships. Bjornland et al (2002)

❚ Introduction The basic processes are common to all companies. Nevertheless, the characteristics and particularities of the object of the activities of each company call for either the development of separate processes or the adaptation of others. This is true of shipping companies more generally. But even within the shipping industry the processes of companies can be differentiated, depending upon the nature of their activity. The needs of a company conveying passengers differ from those of one transporting cargoes. Furthermore, the kind of cargo and the organisation of its transportation are likely to give rise to further differentiation. In this chapter, the processes of shipping companies which are active in the main shipping freight markets will be defined and analysed. More specifically,

70

T H E P R O C E S S E S O F T H E S H I P P I N G C O M PA N Y

an account will be given of the theoretical framework of the analysis of the processes and systems of companies, which will then be adapted by analogy to the analysis of shipping companies. The chapter concludes with the correlation of processes and systems with departments in shipping companies.

for the analysis of the processes and systems ❚ Framework of companies ❚ The more the specialisation of the fleet which a company manages is differentiated and the more its size increases, the more the processes which it carries out are further differentiated. Regardless, however, of their specialisation or size, all shipping companies which are active in the transportation of cargoes are bound to carry out, either themselves or their external associates, a series of processes within the framework of one or more of their departments. The processes of a shipping company can be analysed with the help of the Core Processes and Systems model which is based on a systemic review of the company and permits an understanding of the relation of interdependence which exists between its processes.1 By means of the processes and resources and control support systems, resources are combined in such a way that inflows are converted into outflows (Figure 3.1). The primary processes are related to the object of the company’s business. Every company has to combine its processes in order to develop a product or a service, to create demand for that product or this service and then to meet the demand. From the point of view of the customer, the company exists in order to carry out these primary processes, which are connected with the core of its activities, the development of a product/service, demand management and order fulfilment.

primary processes: processes which are related to the object of the company’s business.

external customers: the individuals or companies which will use the product produced or the services offered by the company. internal customers: those working within the company who, directly in the production process or indirectly in the support activities, affect the quality of the product or service and the manner in which the customer is served. control systems: look at how supporting resources are allocated and used across primary processes.

72

Product development. This includes either selection from products or services which already exist in the market, or the creation of new, innovative products or services. Demand management. This starts out from an understanding of the customer’s requirements, and is therefore linked to the development of the product or service, and concludes in the creation of demand for the product or service. Order fulfilment. From the moment that there is a demand for the company’s product or service, numerous complex functions, processes and activities must be combined in order for the order to be fulfilled effectively and profitably. This group of processes may involve the ordering of supplies, assembly, planning of production, production, storage, dispatch, installation of the product, etc.

Apart from the above groups of processes, however, every company must maintain systems for the management of resources required for their implementation. These are called support systems, and include capital assets, people, and information. Support systems do not directly serve the company’s customers, but support primary processes. The ‘customers’ of the support systems are the company’s primary processes. At the same time, each process produces outflows which are inflows for some other process. By customer, therefore, is not meant only the final purchaser of the product which the company produces or of the service which it offers. For this reason, customers can be divided into external and internal. External customers are the individuals or the companies which will use the product produced or the services offered by the companies, whereas internal customers are considered to be all those working within the company who, directly in the production process or indirectly in the support activities, affect the quality of the product or service and the manner in which the customer is served (Dervitsiotis, 2005). This approach makes it possible for employees to be aware of their place in the company’s broader system and of their contribution to the final product (Evans and Lindsay, 2005). In this way, a company can be pictured as a totality of individual processes which are united in a chain with a view to the satisfaction of the external customer. Each unit is a producer which transforms inflows into outflows, in order for them to be used by the next process or the next internal customer. At the same time, the company consists of control systems which “look at how supporting resources are allocated and used across primary processes” (Miller, 1998:131). The control systems of a company may be or may not be visible, tight or loose. Furthermore, they can be organic features of the various processes, or they may function autonomously.

❚ Processes and systems in shipping companies In the case of shipping companies, the groups of processes and the resources and control support systems correspond to those shown in Figure 3.1, but the individual processes which make up each group differ, as the object of the companies’ activities gives rise to special needs which are expressed both in the definition of each process and in the activities of which it is composed. In Figure 3.2 the following groups of processes and systems of shipping companies are shown:

❚

Processes for developing the maritime transport service. The service provided by a shipping company is maritime transportation by its ships. Consequently, a process which is related to the development of the service is that of the selection and purchase of a ship. The type and the particular characteristics of the ship will determine the freight markets in which it will take part and the service which it will offer. In the case of liner shipping companies, this process is more complicated and includes the planning of the schedules and routes on which the company’s ships will be active. Similarly, the same is true of companies engaged in passenger shipping. In the case of bulk shipping, the development of the service involves the selection of the type of vessel and of the geographical region in which it will function. The company retains its flexibility, insofar as it charters its ships on a voyage basis, to alter in the short term the service which it offers, by changing either the cargoes which they

carry or the geographical region in which they operate. On the other hand, in the case of liner shipping and passenger shipping, companies lack this scope, at least in the short term, as they must respond to the commitments which arise from their planned and announced schedules. Processes for the creation and management of demand (chartering). These processes relate to the creation of demand for the company’s maritime transportation services, that is, in effect, to the search for and ensuring of chartering of company’s vessels. At the same time, for the optimal implementation of these processes, the company has to develop, and manage in an appropriate way, communication with potential customers and with the whole of the stakeholders, that is, with all the groups of individuals who have an interest in the progress of the company (suppliers, state authorities, etc.). Processes in connection with the meeting of demand (operations of vessels). When the chartering of the ships has been ensured, the company has to ensure the provision of the maritime transportation services for the customers/charterers at the appropriate time, with the quality required by them and safety for human life and the environment. In practical terms, this means that the ship which is chartered must be ready to load, carry and discharge the cargo at the port of destination on the basis of the scheduling of the charter party or the terms of the bill of lading, should have been properly maintained and observes the terms of the regulations and that the crew should have at its disposal all the necessary means for the operation of the vessel. The crew and the ship itself, as a means of production, should be insured against risks, and the operation of the ship and of the crew should be supported by systems which ensure their quality and safety. The resources systems can be divided into support and control systems. Support systems include financial management and the accounts office, the management of information and communications, and the management of the human resources employed on the vessels and in the offices of the company. However, the control systems do not necessarily involve specific processes, but activities which are diffused throughout all the other processes and have as their purpose the monitoring of the distribution and effective and profitable employment of the productive resources. For example, the assessment of the performance of the personnel in the offices and on the ships is a control system which can be adapted both to the process of management of the human resources and to each process individually by the person responsible for this process. Monitoring of the budget by ship and that of the company as a whole may be carried out by the section responsible for the control of the financial management and by the operational heads in the area for which they are responsible.

The above summary analysis cannot, of course, include every instance of a shipping company. It is a framework for analysis of the group of processes of a bulk shipping company which allows for extensions and adaptations, depending upon the particular requirements and the particular characteristics of each shipping company (corporate and business strategy, chartering strategy, size of fleet, specialisation of

ships, level of technology, quality of human resources, etc.). In any event, as is explained in Chapter 10, it is possible for a shipping company not to deploy within it the whole of the processes required, but to commission their implementation from third-party companies by the strategy of outsourcing. Furthermore, in the case of companies which manage passenger ships, for example, the processes will differ, since the service which they provide lies outside simple carrying. Similarly, they will also differ in liner shipping companies, which, as will be explained below, are expanding into developing integrated transportation solutions.

of the processes and systems of shipping ❚ Analysis companies ❚ An account is given below of the processes and systems of a shipping company, and of the departments which are involved in their implementation. The purpose of this analysis is not to give a presentation of the organisation and task of the departments of a shipping company. In any event, there is no single way in which the basic processes are organised in departments, or in which the departments themselves are organised and function. For this reason, the analysis of the basic processes of a shipping company can be organised and carried out in a different way from company to company. As will be explained in Chapter 5, a series of factors determines the way in which the company will carry out its processes. The size of the fleet which it manages, its specialisation, the organisational culture and its strategy are among the factors which can give rise to differences. However, regardless of the way in which each company organises these processes into departments, it is a given that, as we have mentioned before, these processes as a whole must be in place and must be carried out for the company to be able to provide its maritime transportation services in a comprehensive way.

Selection and purchase of ships The company’s management, when it has analysed the external environment, the opportunities which that brings and the threats which it creates, together with the internal environment, that is, the resources and capabilities which it possesses and exploits, and the gaps in the resources which must be filled, goes ahead with the taking of the basic strategic decisions, and actions necessary to implement them. The shipping company’s basic strategic decision concerns the selection and acquisition of ships, and is the responsibility of the company’s senior management. The selection and acquisition of ships involves the taking of strategic decisions in connection with the investment of the capital, and with the line of action which the company will follow. In other words, the process of the selection and acquisition of ships includes decisions in connection with the composition of the company’s fleet (and, therefore, the freight markets in which it will be active with its vessels) and the addition of new ships (by placing orders for new building or by acquisition from the second-hand market), or the sale of existing ones, as well

75

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

as the choosing of the time when the decision will be acted upon. The analysis contained in Chapter 2 above relates to various aspects of this process. The type and size of ship will also define its earning potential because volatility in the dry bulk freight markets is different for large ships than for smaller ones (Kavussanos, 1996). The largest the ship the more drastic are the responses of profitability to unexpected changes in the market (Alizadeh and Nomikos, 2010). The taking of the decision at this level presupposes an assessment of a series of factors linked to the analysis of the present situation and the estimation of the course the market in which the company is to be active will follow in the future, of the needs of its customers, of the availability of capital and, in general, of the company’s resources (Box 3.1). The capital, as any other resource the shipping companies employ, also comes from the international market. Fund-raising in shipping is based on sophisticated, innovative and complex financing instruments and tools. These include equity finance (public funding, seasoned equity offerings, retained earnings, and private equity funding), debt finance (bank lending, corporate bond issues, specialized financial institutions, shipyard finance, private debt finance) and alternative finance (lease, mezzanine finance, securitization, hybrid finance) (Syriopoulos, 2007). However, the most important source of capital for shipping are still the shipping banks. They provide capital after a credit risk analysis which is based on character and capacity of the head of the shipping company and the management team, on capital, on company, on conditions, on collateral and on cash flow analysis (Grammenos, 2010). Shipping companies in most of the case choose a combination of methods for raising the needed fund. The taking of the decision and its subsequent implementation involve executives of the company responsible for matters concerning chiefly the technical characteristics of the vessel which is to be purchased or is to be built, and its inspection prior to purchase or its monitoring during the course of building. Given that the decision on the selection and acquisition of ships determines the kind and the characteristics of the service which the shipping company will offer, its completion is linked to the functional dimension of the vessel and definitively affects the quality of the service provided. Included in this decision are, inter alia, the selection of the classification society, the choice of its register, and its insurance cover. Frequently, the process of the acquisition of a vessel may not necessarily belong within the more general planning of development of the company’s activities, but as discussed in chapter 2, it may be a move of a more opportunistic character or form part of a strategy for the use of investment opportunities on a near or medium-term horizon. This might be the objective for the investment in the acquisition of ships by an existing company or even by a new company established on purpose for that reason, such as MCP Container Ships A.S (www.mpc-containers. com), which invests with the purpose of examining how asset values will develop over a period of three to five years,3 or Tanker Investments Ltd. (Box 3.2). It is obvious then that in this case a basic motive for the purchase of the ship may not be the medium or long-term provision of a transportation service, but its low price, in relation, of course, to circumstances and future estimates as to

BOX 3.3 Choice of the timing for the purchase or sale of a ship As has already been explained, with a certain delay, the prices of ships move in parallel with freight rates. This means that when freight rates are reduced or increased, there is a corresponding reduction or increase in the prices of ships. In practical terms, this means that a shipping company, depending upon the moment that it chooses, is able to acquire ships, and to invest more or less capital for the purchase of the same ship. It would, then, seem reasonable for shipowners to take investment decisions on the basis of this practice. However, this is not the case, and here factors connected with the individual characteristics of those taking the decision enter in. But how can the person who makes the decision know when the conditions are right for exploitation? How, that is to say, is he to recognise a suitable context for the making of a decision in connection with investment or liquidation? It is a well-known fact that the period of prosperity in the shipping cycle is short. How will the decision-maker be able to identify the moment in time at which the period of prosperity is at its end, that is, to spot the right moment for liquidation? Furthermore, how will he be able to spot when the period of recession is at its end and the prices of ships are at their lowest point and that, therefore, it is the right moment for investment? Usually, the conjuncture is both negative and positive at one and the same time. This is because the fall in freight rates, that is, in the revenues and profits of the company, precedes a fall in the prices of ships, whereas an increase in the prices of ships is preceded by an increase in freight rates, that is, in the revenues and profits of the company. In practical terms, this means that the shipowner is faced with a dilemma, since at the moment when he can sell his ship at the highest possible price and make a profit, he can, at the same time, also derive high profits from its commercial exploitation, since freight rates are at a high level (on fluctuations in the prices of ships and freights, see Tables 2.1 and 2.2). Is it, then, the optimal option for him to sell the ship when he can charter it at a high freight rate? Moreover, if high freight rates persist for a length of time, is he not taking on the risk of the loss of profits by the company? This dilemma is described in striking terms by the shipowner Michalis Peratikos (Peratikos, 2001:56):5 My son Costis was the first to come to me, in 1986, at the end of the crisis, and say, “It’s time we bought a ship”. He was talking about a 1980 vessel, six years old, of 45,000 tons, which, at Costis’s suggestion, which appealed to me, was bought for 5,600,000 dollars. We put in 2,200,000 in cash and the rest was a loan. But I

79

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

said to him: “Promise me that when there is 100% and more profit, we’ll sell it”. Because, you know, the mistake the Greeks make is that often they don’t sell when they should . . . “OK”, he said to me. “But to ask a good question: how much is 100%?” I asked him. “Around 11 million”, was his answer. “No, Costis”, I told him. “We’re putting in 2,200,000. If the ship manages to sell at 7.5 or 8, that’s where you have to think about selling it. Not 11, because it’s 11 together with the loans. Don’t expect to make a profit out of them.” At the same time, when freight rates and, in all probability, the company’s profits, are at their lowest point, the prices of ships are very low. Is it in the shipowner’s interest to tie up capital in order to buy ships when this capital can be used for his survival in the market in the event of his company being faced with a problem of liquidity? In either case, the shipowner takes on the risk. For this reason, the decision to be taken depends upon the personality and the values of the shipowner, his attitude towards the risk, his perception and intuition and, naturally, the information which he has on the basis of which he will make his forecasts. A shipowner who is concerned to establish his place in the market will prefer to continue with the exploitation of his vessels and will not engage in their sale. Such a shipowner, that is to say, will not take on the risk of reducing his fleet. For him, not selling is behaviour opposed to risk. A shipowner who prefers immediate profits will choose to sell his ship when this will bring in the greatest profit, and therefore the highest selling price, and will not risk losing the high yield. For him, the sale of the ship is behaviour opposed to risk. It is clear, therefore, that the problem is perceived in a different way.

Companies have to choose the line on which a vessel will be active and the ports of call, as well as planning the schedules which will be known to potential customers, so that they can plan the timely transportation of their cargoes (Box 3.4). They will have to plan their service taking into account that it will be a link of the total transport chain of goods from the place of production to the place of consumption (‘door to door’). Consequently, this is a composite process, in the planning and implementation of which almost the whole of the organisation is involved. This means that the efficiency of the supply chain depends on co-ordination and punctuality to a much larger degree than for bulk shipping, which limits the flexibility experienced by the individual shipping operator (Strandenes, 2012). BOX 3.4 The planning decisions of liner shipping companies Liner shipping companies operate networks of lines. Mulder and Dekker (2018) discuss the problems liner companies are trying to solve in their effort to optimise their operation, and examine the relevant decisions they make at three planning levels, i.e., the strategic, the tactical and the operational, as follows: At the strategic planning level, they are called upon to make decisions which refer to:

❚

Fleet size and mix of it, i.e., how many ships, of which type. Companies’ objective is to benefit from the exploitation of the economies of scale and at the same time to avoid the risk of not using the full capacity of the ships.

❚

The trade lines to participate in, a decision that sets the context for the operational requirements that should be met.

At the tactical level, their decisions to solve problems related to the service network adjustments, which, for example include:

❚

The network design, which concerns the ports that are approached and the order of the approaches and the arrival-departure times for each port, the ships that will be deployed on each route and the frequency of call at each port and the optimisation of the sailing speed.

81

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

❚ ❚

The pricing of the transportation of containers. The repositioning of empty containers which is a need due to trade imbalance between exports and imports. Companies re-allocate empty containers to solve the imbalance with the minimum transport cost.

At operational planning level problems that occur in the operation of the lines, such as:

❚

Cargo routing so that the cargo flows over the network are decided in a way that satisfies capacity constraints related to ships’ allocation, minimises transporting cost by optimising the number of unloading and transshipment operations, avoids penalties for demand not met and minimises transit time constraints.

❚

Disruption management, i.e., the efforts to get back on schedule with minimum cost when a disruption occurs.

❚

Revenue management by varying prices according to available capacity between a port pair.

❚

Stowage planning of containers during the loading of the ship, taking into account the constraints (ship stability, ports of loading and unloading, position on the ship, etc.).

There are also problems that can be considered at two planning levels, such as:

❚

Sailing speed and bunkering optimisation, taking into account environmental issues, sailing speed optimisation and the bunker refuelling plan, which might be adjusted accordingly owing to fluctuations in prices or consumption.

❚

Robust schedule design, which is a combination of scheduling problem at tactical level and disruption management at operational level using as input the order in which ports are visited to determine the planned arrival and departure times and the actions to perform in case of delays.

In the light of the above analysis, the development of the service in the case of a bulk shipping company is a process which has to do chiefly with the vessel which will supply it, whereas in that of a liner shipping company, it has to do with a series of factors which may lie outside its limits. The liner shipping companies, in their effort to control as many of these factors, as possible, have expanded their activities in order to provide the integrated service the customers require. Thus, the expansion of liner companies to logistics services is a response to the increasing demand of shippers for integrated supply chain solutions which derives from the widespread importance of just-in-time and make-to-order production-distribution systems. Liner companies, to respond to this demand and be able to differentiate their services, to increase their source of income and to tie their customers to the upstream and downstream logistics services, thus increasing customers switching cost, have expanded vertically and horizontally (Haralambides & Acciaro, 2010).

82

T H E P R O C E S S E S O F T H E S H I P P I N G C O M PA N Y

However, in parallel with the trend of liner shipping companies to expand to the logistics sector, providing integrated services to respond to the requirements of their customers, there is another trend noted, which is related to the customer side. In early 2016, Amazon China received approval from the U.S. Federal Maritime Commission to provide ocean freight services; this was perceived as an indication of Amazon’s plans to expand its logistic business for its own retail business and to provide third-party logistics services to other industries (Saito, 2016). In January 2017 Amazon begun coordinating ocean freight shipments from merchants in China to its warehouses in the U.S. (Stevens, 2017). This move of Amazon, according to industry actors, could easily be the first step towards it leasing vessels, just as it already does with aviation space, which could be a first move in the transformation of the traditional shipping model (McCarthy, 2017). Turning to the organisation of the businesses in liner shipping and the logistics sector, there are companies vertically and horizontally integrated with involvement in terminal operations and inland logictics, as well as conglomerates, as, for example, NYK, with the liner business forming only part of their activities (Notteboom, 2012). Using as a criterion the provision of bundles involving ocean transportation and logistics services, four major groups of players have been identified (Haralambides & Acciaro, 2010):

❚ ❚ ❚ ❚

Pure liner companies Integrated liner companies Third-party logistics service providers connected to a liner company Pure third-party logistics service providers

The trend, however, is for the expansion of players of the first group to the logistics sector, in response, as already pointed out, to market demands. There is evidence that supply chain integration adds value and leads to an increase in performance in liner shipping companies (Panayides et al, 2012). For a liner company to be able to provide integrated ‘door to door’ services, it has to combine production factors and means of transport at sea and on land for the development of intermodalism, i.e., “the movement of goods (in one and the same loading unit or a vehicle) by successive modes of transport without handling of the goods themselves when changing modes” (European Communities, 2003). It must combine the use of large vessels (mother ships) for the transportation of containers between two transhipment ports (hubs) with the use of smaller vessels (feeders) from the transhipment port to the port of final destination (spoke) (Figure 3.4), and maritime transportation with transportation on land in a unified network which will include taking delivery of the shipment from the point of origin and its transportation to the port, loading on to a vessel and transportation to the

Employment for the vessels (chartering) The process of ensuring employment for the vessels in the fleet of a shipping company engaged in bulk cargo transportation is the equivalent of the process of sales in a business on land. The choice of the form of chartering, that is, voyage charter or time charter, which, as has already been explained, are the two basic forms of chartering for companies active in the freight markets of bulk shipping, is a process of a strategic character, connected with the company’s attitude towards the risk caused by fluctuations in the freights markets (Box 3.5 Shipping and derived demand), as well as with its aspiration for development.

BOX 3.5 Shipping and derived demand A basic characteristic of the sectors of the shipping industry which serve international maritime trade is that they meet derived demand. This means that the ships provide transportation services where there is a demand for production and consumption. As demand alters, because of various factors (wars, economic crises, political decisions, weather conditions), the demand for the provision of maritime transportation services changes accordingly. This characteristic intensifies the fluctuations in freight rates in the basic freights markets. The periods of equilibrium between supply and demand are of shorter duration than the periods in which imbalance prevails in the market. This gives rise to a major fluctuation in freight rates, which lead to fluctuation in the revenues of shipping companies. A shipping company can for a short period operate its fleet with high profits and for a long period with low profits or with losses, though the cost of its production and the ton miles of cargo shipments may be the same for both periods. It is reasonable that this characteristic should influence the organisation, management and strategies of shipping companies. An idea can be gained of, for example, the change in the revenues of a company which manages panamax bulk carriers if the course followed by freights for these vessels in the period 2002–2017 (Figure 3.6) is

84

T H E P R O C E S S E S O F T H E S H I P P I N G C O M PA N Y

noted. The highest time charter rate for a panamax bulk carrier reached in 2002 was 11,500 dollars, whereas in 2004 it had more than quadrupled and reached 49,000 dollars. After a continuous drop in the years 2005 and 2006, it jumped in 2008 to 84,000 dollars, the highest price recorded in the period under examination. In the years which followed, freights showed a sharp downwards fluctuation, falling in 2016 at the lowest level of 4,250 dollars, that is, almost to 1/20 of the corresponding figure for 2008. At the same time, in the light of published data for companies listed on the New York Stock Exchange, the average daily operating cost of ships of the panamax size, including expenses for wages and salaries, cost of insurance, maintenance and repairs, spare parts and supplies and other relevant expenditure, was, for 2007, approximately 5,000 dollars, whereas in the light of data from Moore Stephens for 2015, the operating costs for the same type of vessel was 6,046 dollars, that is, more than the lowest time-charter freight rate (Greiner, 2015). Fluctuations also make their appearance in the freight markets for tankers, which do not always move in parallel with those in the freight markets for dry cargo or general cargo. The different course followed by freights for suezmax tankers, particularly in the period after 2012, is indicative (Figure 3.7). The marked fluctuation in freight rates forces companies to adopt a balanced strategy in chartering, with a view to creating a portfolio of charters which will effectively exploit the conditions, but, at the same time, will reduce the risks which may arise from a sudden drop in the demand for transportation services (Box 3.6 and Box 3.7). At the same time, the marked fluctuation leads companies to strategies of diversification of their activities, either within shipping or in other business sectors. As the freight markets may move in a different direction, the diversification of activities by participation in different markets makes it possible for the company to reduce the risk.

A correct assessment of the market data and the selection of the appropriate form of chartering by a reliable charterer are necessary preconditions for the survival and development of the company (Box 3.6). Among the factors which the shipowner must take into account when deciding on the form of charter for his vessel, that is, whether it will be chartered by a voyage charter or a timecharter, particularly in periods of high freights, are the company’s loan obligations and liquidity needs. Where the ship is not burdened with obligations to banks or other creditors, the option of a voyage charter in the spot market makes it possible to exploit the whole of the opportunities which fluctuations in the freight markets may offer.

BOX 3.6 Chartering strategy The decision on the choice of the type of charter for the vessels of a fleet in the medium term is likely to be taken in conditions of uncertainty, that is, in a state of the absence of information in connection with the probable impacts of the choice between voyage chartering and time chartering. This is the case not only because there is the imponderable factor of ‘demand’, a change in which cannot be foreseen with accuracy, but because a number of unforeseen factors may have a positive or negative influence on the freight markets. The choice between a time charter and a voyage charter is a function of, inter alia, the more general attitude of the shipowner towards risk. In accordance with a more rational behaviour, shipowners, to the extent that they manage fleets with more than two ships, create a portfolio of charters in which are included both voyage charters

86

T H E P R O C E S S E S O F T H E S H I P P I N G C O M PA N Y

and time charters. In this case, the proportion of each kind of charter is determined by an assessment of the prevailing conditions in the freight markets and of the course they will follow in the future, as well as by the degree of undertaking of risk which is desirable for each shipowner. The chartering strategy which favours either time charters or voyage charters exclusively may lead to undesirable outcomes, particularly when the conjuncture in the freight markets manifests extreme changes in the level of demand, and therefore of freight rates. In any event, of course, the option of a time charter may not give rise to the maximum profitability for the company, but it is one of low risk and can only rarely lead to undesirable situations. Some indication is given of the importance of chartering strategy and of the risk which results from it by the case of two important Norwegian companies, Hilmar Rekstens Rederi and Sig. Bergesen d.y., which during the 1980s followed differing chartering strategies and, of course, had a completely different fate (Tenold, 2000). For Reksten, the choice of voyage chartering was the only desirable one, in the light of the estimation that it would have low risk. Hilmar Reksten aimed to have the ships in his fleet always free and available in the market in order to exploit the wrong estimates of the oil companies in their calculation of future demand for tankers. He believed that the high revenues and the capital reserves which he could accumulate during this culmination in the freight market would allow him to cover possible losses in phases of recession. This belief in the correctness of his strategy was such that he did not hesitate to tell those in charge of charters for the oil companies that “the reason he was so rich was that they were so wrong in estimating future tanker demand” (Tusiani, 1996:83). For Bergesen, on the other hand, time charters were a low-risk choice, so its management sought the time chartering of its vessels with long-term contracts (Tenold, 2000). Developments in the liquid cargo freight markets in the 1970s vindicated the latter and not the former company. For the 1970–1973 period, when there was a steep increase in freights resulting from a constantly increasing demand for oil, the choice of voyage charters seemed to involve minimum risk. From charter to charter, the level of the freight rate increased for the shipowner. However, the first oil crisis of 1973 caused a sharp reduction in freight rates. The drop in demand for the transportation of oil cargoes coincided with a constantly increasing supply of capacity, as the ships which owners had ordered during the phase of prosperity continued to be built and delivered. The result of reduced demand and the increased supply was a continuous drop in freights. For those shipowners who followed the Reksten strategy, this meant that

87

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

their ships would be chartered at a low freight rate which might not cover their cost, or would remain unemployed and would be laid up. On the other hand, for those owners who followed the Bergesen strategy and chartered their ships with a time charter, this meant that they would continue to collect the freights previously agreed, which would be significantly higher than those in force in the market at that particular time. It is instructive that in 1976 nearly 55 per cent of the Bergesen fleet was employed with time charter contracts which expired after 1981, and 30 per cent with time charter contracts which expired in the years between 1976 and 1980. In the case of Reksten, on the other hand, only 20 per cent of the vessels were employed with voyage charters and the remaining 80 per cent were laid up (Tenold, 2000). This difference in developments is one of the factors which explain the way in which these two companies developed. While Reksten was faced with bankruptcy at the end of the 1970s, Bergesen has continued up to the present to be one of the biggest shipping companies in the world, operating, however, under a different name and ownership after its acquisition in 2002 by World-wide Shipping. The company Bergesen Worldwide was established in 2003 and rebranded BW (www.bw-group.com) in 2005.

In the case of companies which manage diversified fleets, it is possible for them to create a portfolio of charters which will include voyage charters and time charters, depending on the different type of vessel (see charts in Figures 3.8 and 3.9). By means of this strategic option, companies are in a position to make use of the positive conditions in the freight markets, having, however, secured liquidity by the time charters. In order to reduce the risk caused by fluctuations in the freights markets, many shipping companies engage in forward agreements, such as forward freight agreements and forward contracts.6 The companies’ forecasts as to future developments in the freight markets will also determine the way in which they will act in forward agreements. The search for and ensuring of employment for the ships is the task of the chartering department, in which are employed chiefly personnel whose duties correspond to those of shipbrokers. As, however, the transportation agreement is a composite transaction for the completion of which many skills and much knowledge are required, ship-brokers have, as a rule, apart from being wellinformed about the market, the necessary specialist skills and knowledge (knowledge of the legislation, information, technical knowledge, negotiating skills, etc.) for the negotiation of the terms of the charter party (Box 3.8). Usually, a chartering department is to be found in medium and large-size companies, whereas in those of a smaller size there are personnel and/or the owner himself who manage the issues concerned with the chartering of the vessels which are commissioned from shipbroking companies.

BOX 3.7 Balanced chartering strategy A balanced chartering strategy is one of the preconditions for the long-term survival of shipping companies, particularly in the bulk shipping freight markets. Below a summary account is given of the approach of two of the biggest companies in the fields of dry and liquid bulk markets, respectively. The Star Bulk company (www.starbulk.com) manages a fleet of 68 bulk carriers of various sizes. As stated on its website, the company “uses a moderate, adaptive and flexible strategy of employing a portion of the fleet on longer period charters and the rest of the fleet at spot employments or short period time charters” (Star Bulk, 2017a). In the light of the data included on the company’s relevant web page (7-72017), the portfolio of charters of the company includes spot charters, time charters, short term charter, index-linked voyage charter7 as well as participation to pool for several ships of its fleet (Figure 3.8).
