As will be discussed in Chapter 5, digitalisation will cause structural changes in the shipping industry. The way the businesses are conducted will be affected and professions will become obsolete. The shipbrokers’ profession is one of those which will be affected, taking into account that online auction systems for transporting cargoes have been introduced in the large dry bulk sector of the industry. These systems allow for the direct and digital negotiations of cargo owners and vessel operators, bypassing the role of broker. Although industry actors contend that in sectors with complicated market dynamics the old process will remain the norm, the case is that shipbrokers will need to make their service more value-added for their customers (Shen, 2017). Consolidation in major shipping sectors is the second important change that will affect the industry model for shipbrokers. Bigger shipowners will want to deal directly with larger customers for major commodities on the major trade routes. However, as is stated in a study by Bimco, shipbrokers who add value to a deal, broadening the commercial offering and seeing themselves more and more as advisers, will always be in demand for niche trades and for minor trade routes (BIMCO, 2016).

In the case of companies working in the liner shipping market which employ their vessels on the basis of ‘one ship, many cargoes’, that is, they do business with many different customers simultaneously, the process of finding employment is more complicated (Frankel, 1982). It is usually organised by a department responsible for marketing and sales, in which a large part of their personnel is employed. These companies, that is to say, are systematically concerned with the process of creating, communicating, delivery and exchange of outflows which have value for their customers and associates, and society as a whole, as prescribed by the definition of marketing (AMA, 2014). The head office usually retains the responsibility for market research, the development of new services and their advancement and promotion, research into customer satisfaction and the co-ordination of the company’s sales network. It handles relations with the company’s major shippers, while a significant part of the transportation contracts are concluded by the network of agents/representative offices/branches which it maintains in the various ports of call. Furthermore, the advertising of the network of services offered and the promotion of the company’s image is of particular importance. For that reason, companies working in liner shipping invest heavily in communication through their Internet sites or the means of social networking. In connection with the former, the use of a special web page (www.countonmol.com) by Mitsui OSK Lines for the posting of information on its performance, on the basis of certain Key Performance Indicators (KPI) which the company sets for the services it provides (Figure 3.10) is indicative. For each of the KPIs, the company presents the target and the actual achieved performance. For example, the Emission Reduction indicator measures performance on reduction of Carbon Dioxide (CO2) per teu-mile, on reduction of Nitrogen Oxide (NOx) and on reduction of Sulphur Oxide (SOx). It is, then, obvious that the organisation and the demands in terms of resources, particularly in personnel and information, differ as between bulk cargo shipping and liner shipping. According to M. Stopford, bulk cargo ships give rise to a small number of transactions for the management company. Given that they carry out few voyages each year, carrying one cargo on each voyage, their annual revenue depends on the corresponding number of negotiations per vessel. This means that the management of information in connection with the transaction is of lesser priority than the information in connection with market research. A shipping company concentrates on market research in order to derive data and information useful for its negotiations with the shippers. By way of contrast, in the case of liner shipping, the transactions having to do with its revenues may fluctuate between 10,000–50,000 per vessel on an annual basis, since the number of different cargoes carried on each voyage is large. With so large a number of transactions, the company relies chiefly on prices known in advance and, to a limited degree, on negotiations (Stopford, 2002). The estimations on the number of transactions refer to a period where the largest container vessel was of approximately 6.000 teu. As analysed in Box 2.4, the carrying capacity of container vessels has been increased to exceed 21.000 teu. Thus, the number of transactions has been increased accordingly. Operations management of vessels As has already been explained, a characteristic which intensifies the complexity of the organisation of shipping companies is the distance between the productive unit and the office on land. The smooth operation of their ships is based on the existence of personnel on land for the co-ordination and execution of the necessary activities. This is one of the most important processes undertaken by shipping companies, since it has to do with the core of their activities, the production of transportation services for the effective and efficient performance of the ships’ charter party, in the case of bulk shipping, or the fulfilment of the terms of the bill of lading in that of liner shipping. Its effective fulfilment determines to a large degree the level of competitiveness of the shipping companies. Activities relating to the day-to-day operation of the ship are carried out by the ship operators, who are personnel in the operations department. This department is in many companies the basic link between the management, the rest of the company’s departments, the suppliers and other individuals or organisations. The operator is usually an ex ship’s officer, for preference a captain, and has, consequently, a first-class knowledge of the particularities of the ship and of the conditions which the crew is likely to encounter during the course of a charter. He knows the ship very well as a productive unit, has a very good knowledge of the charter party and of any special terms which it may contain, and constantly monitors its execution, applying the policies, rules, and procedures foreseen by the company’s management system, and providing additional instructions or advice where necessary. It is his responsibility to be aware of the problems which may arise during the chartering of the vessel, and either to make decisions for their resolution or to co-ordinate the efforts of other personnel involved in it. One of the greatest challenges for the operator is effective communication with the captain and, through him, with the members of the crew. In order for him to be effective in this, he must be capable of putting himself into the position of those who receive the instructions and are called upon to carry them out (Kennedy, 2001). He must, therefore, have a good knowledge of the organisation of the ship, and of the duties and roles involved in every job. In view of the fact that a large percentage of ships nowadays have crews of various nationalities, the operator must be able to manage the difficult matter of cultural differences (see Chapter 8). The group of processes related to the operations management department can be divided into the following areas (Goulielmos, 1996):

❚ ❚

external processes, that is, the relations with the ship’s agents, the suppliers of fuels, the charterers and the rest of those involved; on the ships, whose movements it follows closely and supports;

Voyage schedule. On the basis of the charter party, the operator suggests the ports of loading and discharging, the order of call for each port and the sea route which the vessel will follow. For liner shipping companies these are decisions concerning a given period of time of employment of ships which is taken at a tactical level (see Box 3.4) and not on a voyage basis. Appointment of agents (Box 3.9) and stevedores. In the event of the company not having offices to represent it in the ports of loading and discharge of the vessel, which is usually the case with bulk cargo shipping, the operator looks for agents in the port of call to handle the ship’s affairs, and, depending on what is laid down in the charter party, stevedores for the handling of the cargo. It is the responsibility of the captain and the crew to work together effectively with the agents on all matters for the support of the ship, and with the stevedores on those matters which concern the cargo (state of the shipment, conditions for loading and discharging), informing the operator of any issue which raises doubts. Arrangement of details of loading and discharge. On the basis of the charter party, the operator will be responsible for either the details of the procedure, or the punctual arrival and preparation of the ship for loading or discharge in the ports specified. BOX 3.9 The ship’s agent From the moment that the chartering of the ship is agreed, the agent of the shipowner or of the charterer intervenes in the process. He acts on behalf of his principal in such a way that the latter remains legally responsible for all the acts carried out by the former by reason of the agency relationship. The agent is the representative of the shipowner and the captain in a certain port. His obligations include giving notice of the arrival and departure of the vessel, the anchoring and berthing of the ship in a suitable position, the handling of the procedures of loading and discharge, the collection of the bills of lading and the freight, responsibility for its supply with fuels, stores and other materials, the carrying out of the necessary port procedures and documentation, responsibility for the carrying out of the necessary drydocking and repairs, the filling of gaps in the personnel and the repatriation of the seafarers and other related tasks (Branch, 1996). His remuneration, the agency fee, is usually calculated on the turnover of duties which he performs.

❚

discharge, as any delay will give rise to additional claims of the charterers against the company. Arrangement of issues of bunkering. With his knowledge of the particular characteristics of the vessel (capacity of the tanks, kind of fuels, consumption at full and optimal speed), the fuel reserves and the sea routes which it will follow (bunkering ports and prices, deadweight of the cargo and the load line zones for the specific season on the sea route which it will follow, etc.), the operator will arrange matters having to do with the supply of fuels. Monitoring of the suitability and quantity of the fuels and the supplying of information to the operator on the vessel’s fuel reserves are the responsibility of the captain. The cost of fuels is a basic constituent of the voyage cost of the ship. Consequently, the choice of the optimal speed is a crucial factor for the effective management of the vessel, as well as for the reduction of fuel consumption and emissions. For a ship with a service speed of 15 nautical miles, a 15 per cent reduction of speed means a reduction of fuel consumption of approximately 25 per cent and, consequently, a reduction in carbon dioxide emissions. More specifically, a VLCC travelling at a speed of 15 n.m. consumes approximately 100 tons of fuel per day. By reducing speed to 13 n.m., consumption is reduced to approximately 66 tons (Surveyor, 2010). The contribution of the cost of fuel to the shaping of the operating cost leads companies to monitor it systematically. Box 3.10 illustrates the use of the main indicators of performance of Maersk Line for this purpose. Provision of voyage instructions to the captain. The operator supplies in writing a series of instructions which provide information, guide and assist the captain in his task and deal with the voyage (port, times of loading, voyage plan, time of discharge), the cargo (nature of cargo, special characteristics of loading, transportation and discharging, matters concerning the bill of lading), the agents (particulars and means of communication with the operator’s representative at each port), communication with the office, the speed at which the vessel should sail, the places and times for the supply of fuels, the charter party and its terms and other crucial issues. All the above instructions should not, of course, be contrary to the powers and responsibility of the captain or call these into question as regards matters to do with the safety of the vessel. Cost control. The operator also takes responsibility for the economic results of the voyage by monitoring its cost (cost of fuels and of handling of the cargo, agents’ commission, harbour dues and costs), and for the details of the operating cost of the vessel. Insurance claims. The operator and the operations management department more generally are called upon to handle any insurance claims against the company for loss or shortfalls in the cargo.

BOX 3.10 Key performance indicators and fuel consumption at Maersk Line The use of KPIs for the performance of ships by Maersk has contributed to the cost savings in many areas, one of these being the energy costs. The following article of Digital Ship discusses the approach of Maersk Line on the issue (Digital Ship, 2012)10: Maersk Line has reported that it has saved almost US$90 million through the use of its Maersk KPIs (Key Performance Indicators) system. The company says that, in just three years, it has saved this enormous amount in energy costs by measuring the performance of individual vessels and raising awareness on fuel consumption. Maersk KPIs have been used for a number of years to measure the performance of the company, however, it is only since 2009 that the KPIs have also made their way on to the vessels in a bid to boost performance. The vessel KPIs have evolved through close cooperation between the vessels, Maersk Line Vessel Management and Maersk Maritime Technology, which is in charge of the Maersk Ship Performance System (MSPS). Today the overall scorecard consists of four parameters – energy, safety, daily running costs and cooperation (best practice sharing). At this stage a monetary value for savings has been applied to the energy KPI only. After implementation of the programme by the crew and fleet groups, the company has been able to pinpoint some of the savings made over the last three years. For example, Maersk Line notes that 160,000 tons of fuel have been saved as a result of higher propulsion efficiency, contributing to the US$90 million figure, a total which does not even include savings from other energy initiatives such as trim optimisation or basic load reduction. “If you can’t measure something, you can’t control it. If you can’t control it, you can’t improve it. It is essential to realise that the scorecards are only a valuable tool if they help facilitate decision making amongst stakeholders”, said Anup Rajan, performance manager at Maersk Line Vessel Management.

In the case of many shipping companies, the operations management department undertakes responsibility for freight collection and the handling of disbursements, that is, of the payments made for support services for the ship in ports. Freight collection involves the collection of the revenues earned by the ship on the basis of the charter party. Furthermore, among the operator’s activities is the part which he plays in the selection of the ship’s officers and the crew for the deck department, and the assessment of their performance. The operator is also responsible for the ship meeting the requirements of the company’s safety and environment management system, and the provision of all

T H E P R O C E S S E S O F T H E S H I P P I N G C O M PA N Y

the information and instructions required to its officers in such a way that they are able to deal with unforeseen events and circumstances. It is expected that developments in technology will affect definitively both the manner of operation of the ship itself and the way in which it is managed from the office on land. For example, a study has recently been completed which shows encouraging results for the development of technological applications which will permit the operation of the vessel by automation from on land (MUNIN, 2017). According to the Maritime Unmanned Navigation through Intelligence in Networks (www.unmanned-ship.org/munin/) study, vessels which carry cargoes on the open seas, with the exception of the time when they are arriving at or departing from ports, will be able to operate and be controlled by means of technological systems on land. Various organisations, for example, classification societies and technological universities, implement projects aimed at further fostering the prospects of the introduction of the unmanned ship in the future (Maritime Executive, 2017). In parallel, an indication of the interest of industry actors in the issue is that the IMO has agreed to add the issue of the autonomous ship to the discussions agenda of the Maritime Safety Committee meeting (Digital Ship, 2017b). The idea of the autonomous vessel is one of long duration, and presupposes the fulfilling of a whole series of technical and legal conditions, such as, for example, concerning the role of the human factor, as that is determined in a number of international conventions. Cyber security is also a challenge to be met.11 Seeing the issue from the human resources management point of view and taking into account the fact that the sustainable competitive advantage is based on human resources, as will be explained in Chapter 8, the questions to be answered are many. In any case, the role of human factor, at least at the moment, is considered by many actors in the industry to be irreplaceable: We will still have human beings on board because I – and I try to be a little bit provocative here – I don’t see the autonomous vessel being replicable for the 60,000 seagoing vessels around the world, so we will still have the human element on board.12 Regardless, however, of how far these conditions will be met and the plan proves realistic, it is a fact that the speed at which technological solutions for the distant control of the productive units are developing and being put into action may lead to a further reduction in the number of their crew, raising, of course, questions relating to safety at sea. At the same time, the management of a vessel will constitute one more field of drastic changes as to the organisation of shipping activities. In any event, of course, operations management is one of the most important processes in a shipping company, not only because its task is the effective management of the vessel, but, in addition, because it receives information from and distributes it to the other processes and its appropriate departments, draws up the plans for voyages, and reports on its future positions, and, at the same time, co-ordinates the work of other departments in solving problems which the ship may encounter. Furthermore, in order to fulfil his duties, the operator must have at his disposal well maintained, seaworthy and suitably manned vessels, and

An autonomous ship need not mean an unmanned ship, although the work on board will be different; a shipbroker will still be required to advise clients on getting the best deal; partnerships of skills will be brought together to work on specific projects; senior managers will still be asked to set challenging goals (Clayton, 2017).

must work closely with the personnel involved in the corresponding processes. In addition, since he maintains contacts with the customers and others who do business with the company, exchanging information, he also contributes to the shaping of its image. The series of activities carried out by the competent personnel of the company at the office, following the chartering of the vessel, are the preparation of the ship for loading of cargo (cleaning of tanks or of holds), the appointment of the agent and the provision of information to the charterer. After the loading of the ship, bunkering follows, and the transporting of the cargo starts. The ship proceeds to the discharging of cargo and the freight invoice is issued. The next stream of activities includes the appointment of an agent at the discharging port, the preparation of discharge, the documentation, the provision of information to the charterer and the freight income generation. After the discharge of cargo, the ship travels in ballast to the next loading port, and a new round of activities starts. The analysis above describes, to a greater degree, the operations management in a bulk cargo shipping company. In the case of liner shipping, because of the particular characteristics which have already been described (e.g., maritime transportation as a part of the total transportation process, reliability and frequency of links, unitisation of the shipment), operations management has a number of constituents. This is an additional reason why for liner shipping companies operation capability is considered the most important capability dimension, followed by that of costumer service. Attributes of operation capability that contribute to the increase of the companies’ competitive edge include terminal operations, loading and discharging efficiency, service routes planning, container positioning and space control (Lu, 2007).

Technical management of ships

technical department: the department responsible for the technical management of the fleet. superintendent engineer: the member of the team responsible for the technical management of the vessel.

100

In order for a vessel to operate as a productive unit it must be seaworthy and provide safe, quality transportation services. This means that it must conform to international regulations and the operating and maintenance standards that are laid down by the classification society of the class to which it belongs. The maintenance of a seaworthy condition and the minimisation of the time in which the vessels remains out of operation (off-hire) are preconditions for its effective management. Responsibility for ensuring the seaworthy condition of the company’s fleet, in other words its technical management, lies with the technical department. Technical management is one of the most important functions of the shipping company as its effective performance contributes significantly to the productive capabilities of the vessel, as well as to determining the cost of the services offered (Box 3.11). It also covers a large range of duties, such as the scheduled maintenance and inspections of the vessel, and repairs and dry-docking. Responsibility for the technical management and operation of the vessels belongs to the superintendent engineer, who is a technical specialist with knowledge of the technical characteristics of the vessel, and who is assisted by staff who perform the related management duties (Goulielmos, 1994).

BOX 3.11 Technical department contribution to value creation The Director of the Technical Department of Norden A/S, Jens Christensen, describes how the department contributes to value creation in the company (Norden News, 2016a)13: First and foremost by delivering a safe, reliable and price efficient technical operation of the owned fleet. The vessels must be on-hire and meet the customers’ requests and requirements, and together with the other departments, the Technical Department must also assist in making sure that NORDEN is leading within fuel efficiency. Next, we must make sure that the technical and maritime knowledge is brought into play also when it comes to the part of the fleet which is chartered and which is daily managed by the commercial departments. With a chartered fleet of approximately 225 dry cargo and product tanker vessels, there are plenty of daily challenges where a technical or maritime perspective could very well provide the best solution. Furthermore, the Technical Department can contribute to value creation in NORDEN by quickly getting to inspect the vessels which we consider buying and by maintaining the current fleet in a permanent good state ensuring that any vessel can be sold if we for some reason would want to change our fleet composition.

The main functions of the technical department are (Downard, 1990):

❚

❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚

the maintenance and management of the vessel and equipping it on the basis of the demands of the flag state and classification societies, as well as the standards established by the company, so that the vessel can respond to demands and perform effectively for the company; designing work plans and budgets, and ensuring the anticipated results are achieved; improving the vessel’s operational capacity; ensuring that the vessel has all the necessary certifications to sail; preparing the vessel’s attendance reports, which provide information on its performance and safety but also its adherence to regulations, in which damage reports are also included; receiving offers from ship repair yards for scheduled maintenance work and necessary repairs, compiling budgets, co-ordinating technical support for the work being done on the vessel; the supply of suitable parts, equipment and services for the vessel, collecting and distributing documents and technical information on the vessel; keeping an archive of documents to be processed but also for legal purposes, which includes data on the characteristics and performance of the vessel, a diary of maintenance and repairs, inspections and checks, machinery and equipment, etc.;

101

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

❚ ❚ ❚

the supply of advisory services and dealing with emergency incidents among the engine-room staff, as well as guidelines for maintenance work; co-ordination with the vessel’s operator and the rest of the management for the safe, effective and economical operation of the vessel and making sure it is in a seaworthy condition; supervising the vessel’s technical staff.

The technical department is also responsible for ensuring that the vessel, particularly from a technical point of view, responds to the requirements of the management system for safety, quality and environmental responsibility. The members of the technical department visit the vessel on a regular basis, make inspections and check its operations and the degree to which the crew responds to its duties and obligations. Aside from the above, the technical department is responsible for checking the technical performance of the vessel and planning corrections if divergences are recorded. It also collaborates with the classification societies for inspections to the vessel, as well as with other departments in the company, offering advice and guidelines on technical issues, checking fuel quality, participating in the selection of engine-room staff, assessing their performance, etc. The technical department’s role in determining the specifications of the vessels is also important, as is the technical monitoring of the shipbuilding process. The technical departments of certain companies employ teams of specialists to conduct technical monitoring of the vessel. For example, Norden Shipping has created the roving safety officers team, which consists of three experienced employees who have worked as senior officers on board the company’s vessels. They constantly travel around to visit the vessels that Norden owns and is in charge of their technical operation, to point out safety breaches and to instruct the crew. During the visits, they test the ship’s equipment and systems, focus on the planning of a voyage (the vessel’s planning and execution of the current voyage) and inspect the vessel’s overall condition (physical condition of ship, discussion of safety behaviour with the crew, assessment of the crew’s ability to work together and function as a team) (Norden News, 2016b). Thus, they focus on the safety performance of the ship as a whole, i.e., the equipment, the systems and the people on board. There are also companies which employ teams of specialists to conduct technical works on the vessel. The existence of such a team ensures that the shipping company can deal with any technical problems that may arise during the operation of a vessel in a timely and effective way. For example, the technical department of Stealth Maritime Corporation, according to information published on its website, employs a permanent team of specialised technicians which functions as a mobile repairs unit and boards any vessel that requires repair work or upgrading, thus securing the economical and effective execution of the necessary work (Stealth, 2017). The work of the technical department is facilitated by the application of modern technological tools which, on the one hand, allow the control of the maintenance process of the ship, while, on the other, allowing maintenance cost optimisation. For example, MOL LNG Transport Europe, an affiliate of the

102

T H E P R O C E S S E S O F T H E S H I P P I N G C O M PA N Y

MOL Group, agreed with ship engine manufacturer Wartsila ten-year engine maintenance, which covers online remote support and scheduled maintenance activities for the vessels’ engines. The agreement, which was signed for two LNG carriers, concerns the use of Wartsila’s Condition Based Maintenance (CBM) system to plan relevant activities, basing maintenance on actual engine condition and not on a calendar-based schedule. The CBM monitoring system collects data about engine operations and transmits the information in real time to the Wartsila service centre for technical expert analysis (Digital Ship, 2017a).14 It should be noted that condition-based maintenance instead of the typical planned maintenance schedule is considered a best practice in technical management (Fraunhofer CML & GL, 2013).

Support for the operation of the vessel For the secure and smooth operation of the vessel as a productive unit, the necessary equipment is required (spare parts, engine supplies, lubricants, paints, etc.) together with the services foreseen. At the same time, its performance depends upon the performance of the human resources that work on it. Providing the necessary supplies to the officers and crew is a basic concern of the company. Responsibility for supporting the operation of the vessel through the provision of equipment and supplies lies with the companies’ supplies department. The main purpose of this department is to ensure that the vessel is sufficiently provided with equipment and supplies so that there is no impact on the execution of the voyage and that no danger arises due to a lack of supplies, either to the crew of the vessel or to its cargo. At the same time, in many companies the supplies department, in collaboration with the crews department, ensures the transportation of the crews to and from the vessels. Furthermore, the task of the supplies department is to seek the purchase of supplies for the vessel at an advantageous cost, with the expected quality and at an appropriate time. The equipment and supplies reach the vessel at the moment that they are needed, as supplies are not stored on the vessel beyond an economically acceptable level (Downard, 1990). The supplies department therefore monitors and controls each of the company’s procurements, from the moment that the need arises to the purchase and delivery of the item to the vessel. This, of course, requires collaboration with other departments in the company, particularly the operations and technical management departments. Carrying out the work of the supplies department presupposes that certain tasks are done, such as market research to select the most suitable suppliers, placing the orders and monitoring their development, confirming orders and cross-referencing the details of invoices with the delivered goods and checking the specifications of the supplies.

supplies department: the department responsible for supporting the operation of the vessels by providing equipment and supplies.

Staffing of vessels and offices Human resources are the most important factor for the long-term success of a company. It provides productive services by its skills, knowledge and reasoning,

103

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

crew department: the department responsible for the managing of human resources of ship.

and by the decisions which it takes (Grant, 1998). The competitiveness of a company depends to a significant degree upon the capabilities and commitment of its human assets. This, naturally, also applies to shipping companies, which operate in an environment which has a number of peculiarities. Issues concerning the manpower of offices and vessels are examined in greater detail in Chapters 7 and 8. It is essential that the premises/offices of the company on land should be staffed with employees with a knowledge of the particularities of the shipping industry and the demands made by shipping management. A ship is an item of property worth many millions of dollars, it provides transportation services at a great distance from the offices of the company on land and the way it performs depends on the knowledge, capabilities and performance of the individuals who work on board it. It also depends upon communication between and the ability to co-exist, understand each other and work together of the members of the crew in conditions of day-to-day functioning, as well as in emergencies. It is therefore essential that the group of people who live and work on board the ship should possess not only the qualifications specified by the regulations, but also the cohesion to render them a single unit when this is necessary. The ensuring of this characteristic becomes even more essential when crews of different nationalities are employed on the vessel. The above considerations make the staffing of a shipping company particularly important. Of course, in speaking of staffing, we are not referring only to the process of selecting and engaging personnel, but to the whole of the actions which relate to this. In practice, included in the duties of those who carry out staffing are all the practices of the human resources management which safeguard the company’s performance, that is, the analysis and design of work, human resources planning and recruitment, the selection and placement of candidates, the training and development of these human resources, performance management, employee retention, remuneration and labour relations (Noe et al, 2008). All the above actions are applied to the management of the personnel on land and on board the vessel, with minor variations or adaptations either by a unified administration or by separate departments of the company. As to the manning of the vessel in particular, the task of managing the human resources is the responsibility of the crew department. A ship is a work environment with important special characteristics which must be not only known, but also be taken into consideration in the planning and execution of decisions which concern the vessels’ personnel. Shipping companies are in competition in the labour market, in an effort to attract and retain capable staff for their ships. In the case of well organised companies, the crews are not treated as casual labour, but as their basic resources. Included in the duties of the crewing department are:

❚ ❚ ❚

104

the selection of the nationality of the crews; the attraction, selection and placement of capable and well qualified seafarers; the determination of the needs of the seafarers for training (and its provision in collaboration with the competent departments of the company) and the planning of their careers;

T H E P R O C E S S E S O F T H E S H I P P I N G C O M PA N Y

❚ ❚ ❚

responsibility for maintaining good labour relations on board the vessel; the regulation of any issue concerning the ship’s crew on the basis of the requirements of the company’s management systems; the keeping of detailed records on the qualifications, training and experience of the crews, and the part played by them in the assessment of their performance, in collaboration with the operations and technical management departments.

Given the international character of the labour market for seafarers, it is not always possible for companies which employ seafarers of different nationalities to carry out themselves all the processes of the management of crews (e.g., selection and engagement). This task can be carried out by external associates/ specialist crewing agencies. The crewing department is responsible for the company’s collaboration with agents who undertake the relevant task.

Insurance cover against risks A ship is an asset of great value, exposed to the natural elements, and its fate depends upon its seaworthiness and proper maintenance, as well as on the performance of the human resources working on it. During its operation, the vessel is likely to encounter a number of unforeseen situations which may endanger both the life of the individuals who work on it and the ship itself as a productive unit. In order to avoid these risks, shipping companies seek insurance cover against them, that is, protection against financial risks arising from unexpected and uncontrollable situations. The handling of the shipping company’s insurance cover is undertaken by the special insurance department, which is responsible for ensuring the protection of the shipowner (an individual or a business) from financial risks by means of the insuring of the vessel. The vessel as a productive unit and asset is insured against total loss (e.g., by sinking or destruction) or partial loss (e.g., destruction of an engine). This insurance is known as hull and machinery. Additionally, it is insured against its liability for damage caused to third parties (e.g., accident to or death of crew, destruction of cargo, pollution, etc.). This insurance is called protection and indemnity, and is provided by protection and indemnity clubs. Furthermore, the owner can choose to insure his vessel against other risks which lead to loss of income, such as loss of hire insurance (Box 3.12), delay insurance, war risks insurance or pirate loss of hire and kidnap. The negotiation of the insurance policies and their annual renewal, as well as the determination of premiums which are reasonable for the company, are the responsibility of the insurance department. The amount of the premium can differ from ship to ship and from company to company, as the charge is made in the light of the insurance history of each shipping company. Furthermore, the amount of the premium is a function of the cargo carried by the vessel, the maritime regions in which it operates, the flag which it is under and its age and general condition. At the same time, the monitoring and processing of claims which may arise for the company, as well as collaboration with and the provision of specialist

insurance department: the department which undertakes the handling of insurance cover of the shipping company.

105

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

BOX 3.12 Insurance against loss of income The shipping company’s option of insuring against risks over and above the usual ones, that is, hull and machinery and third-party risks, can prove essential in the mutable environment of the shipping markets. The different effect which the Korea Line financial problems of 2011 had on two companies because of their differing options in the matter of insurance is indicative of the importance of insurance cover against risks, such as loss in the face of the insolvency of a charterer. In early 2011, the Korea Line shipping company announced that it would seek bankruptcy protection as the reduced freights in the bulk freight markets had caused operating losses for the company and had meant that it was unable to service its loans. A number of shipping companies had chartered their vessels to Korea Line. Among the company’s charterers were Navios, Genco and Eagle Bulk. In the case of Navios Maritime Partners (www.navios-mlp.com), whose revenues from the chartering of the vessels to Korea Line accounted for 15 per cent of its total revenues at the given period, there was cover against possible losses because of the company’s policy of insuring its freights. In that of Eagle Bulk (www.eagleships.com), which had chartered to the Korean company 13 of the 39 vessels which it was managing at the time, the risk was significant. According to press reports of the period, Eagle had similar insurance cover against losses arising from insolvency of the creditor, but decided not to renew it in 2010 (Eden, 2011). In the end, Eagle Bulk suffered major losses (Eagle Bulk, 2012).

instructions to the competent departments and to the captains of the company’s vessels are tasks of the insurance department. In connection with claims, it is of crucial importance that the hull and machinery insurers or the protection and indemnity club should be informed immediately when the claim arises and that the necessary data and details should be assembled at once.

Management of information and communications A basic factor for the success of every shipping company is information. The existence of the information required is a precondition for the taking of decisions and its smooth and effective management and operation. Information concerning, inter alia, the level of freight rates, present and future demand, the supply of a workforce and conditions in the various ports is crucial for the functioning of a shipping company. The acquisition of the information required from the right person at the right time and at the minimum cost can substantively increase competitiveness. As has already been pointed out, the market in which the company is active determines the number of transactions which must be completed with customers on an annual basis. Bulk shipping companies carry out a small number of transactions, whereas liner shipping companies carry out thousands of transactions

106

T H E P R O C E S S E S O F T H E S H I P P I N G C O M PA N Y

per ship per annum (Stopford, 2002). This leads these companies to make investments in the development of systems which will facilitate transactions and at the same time reduce cost (Box 3.13). A comparative examination of the web pages of leading shipping businesses in bulk shipping and in liner shipping demonstrates that, whereas the former provide information, the latter supply enhanced services, permitting users to carry out a large number of transactions for reasons which are connected both with the reduction of the cost of transactions and with the quality of the services provided and customer satisfaction. At the same time, the complexity of the organisation of shipping companies makes the locating, dissemination and processing of information, as well as

BOX 3.13 The Hapag Lloyd information system Hapag Lloyd, a liner shipping company founded in 1847 in Hamburg, manages 230 modern ships, employs more than 13,000 personnel in more than 360 offices operating in 125 countries and on its vessels (11,000 in the offices, 2,100 on the ships). It maintains a world network of 130 regular lines which link all the continents (Hapag Lloyd, 2017). As it believes that the utilisation of the latest technology to the greatest possible extent leads to the effective and punctual transportation of cargoes and conveying of information, it has gone ahead with the development of technological solutions which are based on standardised processes and advanced systems and are capable of adaptation to the needs and requirements of its customers (Hapag Lloyd, 2016a). The company’s information system is constantly being reinforced and adapted so that it can respond to the changing needs of its clientele and operate in a unified way in order to support all the needs of the company. The entering of the details of a shipment is carried out once only, and it then can be monitored in real time from any point. This ensures the avoidance of errors resulting from double entries, enhances the quality of the service by means of good documentation and reduces the cost for those involved. Hapag Lloyd provides a variety of e-business solutions, supporting the effective processing of data throughout the transport chain. The customer can, by use of the web page, access in the company’s operating system all the information concerning the transporting of his cargo at any moment, and exchange information between his own information system and that of the company. Furthermore, he can access the systems of other associated companies regarding issues concerning the transportation and receive updates by electronic means on its crucial points (Hapag Lloyd, 2016a). All steps in the transport chain, which include the schedule, the tariffs, the booking, the shipping instructions, the bill of lading, the tracing, the import and the invoice, can be processed via several electronic communication channels provided by Hapag Lloyd (Hapag Lloyd, 2016b).

communication between the various departments and vessels, crucial factors. For this reason, shipping companies invest considerable sums in: information system: an operating system which processes data from both the internal and the external environment of the company and provides the management with information so that correct and sound decisions can be quickly taken.

Information Technology department: the department responsible for the development and operation of the company’s information technology and communications systems.

❚

❚

information technology and the development of information systems which process data from both the internal and the external environment of the company and provide the management with information so that correct and sound decisions can be quickly taken; the development of simultaneous communication both within the infrastructures on land and on the ships.

The needs of companies at this point are also determined by the type of vessels which they manage. For example, ships which carry dangerous cargoes are governed by a strict regulatory framework, a factor which leads to the need for a more rapid and more frequent exchange of information regarding their operation management and the monitoring of their functioning by the company’s offices. Many shipping companies have developed systems which make possible continuous connection by satellite and the transmission of an image and sound between ships and their offices. Of course, the cost of this kind of communication, though it is rapidly being reduced, continues to be high – a factor which limits its implementation. The responsibility for the development and operation of these systems is that of the Information Technology department, among whose basic duties are the following:

❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚

the co-ordination and operation of its equipment and operating system in view of the requirements set by its safety, quality and environmental responsibility systems; responsibility for effective communication between its vessels and offices; the development of software or the selection of ready-made solutions which will respond to the needs of its processes and systems (operations of ships, finance, control etc); monitoring of the keeping of the records of the operating system; provision of assistance to its personnel; the solving of technical problems.

The more a company makes use in its organisation and operation of the new communications technologies, the more developed the process of the management of information and communications and the department responsible will be. It is reasonable that a company which invests in the creation of a paperless working environment, where everything is handled electronically, will commit a workforce and capital to this field. Furthermore, the investment of companies in this sector and the development of dynamic systems which permit their linkage to the systems of companies with which they collaborate, thus expanding the value chain of all the collaborators, can boost their competitiveness and permit the forging of stable and long-term collaborations with their charterers.

T H E P R O C E S S E S O F T H E S H I P P I N G C O M PA N Y

Financial management (Finance and accounting) The object and aim of financial management is the handling of the company’s finances and the maximisation of its total value. The achievement of this aim is pursued by means of the support system relating to the decisions on investment and financing, financial analysis and programming (Artikis, 1996). More specifically, financial management includes the shaping of the investment and financial policy of the company, the management of its capital, economic programming and its credit policy, management of cash resources and its accounting process, receipts and payments and the monitoring of credits. The above processes apply, of course, to businesses as a whole. In the more particular case of the organisation of the financial management of shipping companies, especially as regards the accounting process, their special characteristics connected with spatial dispersion and with the need for monitoring in terms of accounting of the ships’ operation must be taken into consideration (Goulielmos, 1994). Belonging, usually, within the finance department of a shipping company is the accounts department, whose responsibility it may be, apart from the management of expenditure and income, to collect the freights of the vessels. The finance department of a shipping company is responsible, inter alia, for:

❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚

the drawing up of the budget; monitoring of expenditure at every level of the company; management of relations with banks, or, where the company is listed on the stock exchange, management of relations with the supervisory authorities and the shareholders; management of the insurances and insurance claims account, and of financial issues involving crews; monitoring of the operation of the company and of the vessels in accounting terms; management of the company’s cash resources and the carrying out of audits; monitoring of the monthly general accounts of the ships’ captains (Masters’ General Accounts), accounts which include those ship’s expenses incurred on the responsibility of its master.

finance department: the department responsible for the organisation of the company’s financial management. accounts department: the department responsible for the management of the company’s financial data.

The financial management of a shipping company also includes its long-term investment plans, the forecasting of income for each activity of the company, the assessment of needs for investments in the future and the sources from which capital will be drawn, as well as other processes relating to the company’s competitors, and the markets in which it takes part (Branch, 1996).

Communication with customers/charterers and other social and business partners In the rapidly changing environment in which companies operate today, where social responsibility is a condition for their acceptance by the public to which they address themselves, it is an additional duty that they should concern themselves with their image and the way in which this becomes known to the shareholders,

109

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

communications department: the department responsible for the management of the company’s relations with the mass media, and, more generally, with those groups involved in its operation.

the customers/charterers, and to their other social and business partners (suppliers, employees, local community, government, labour unions, etc.). In order to succeed in this, an increasing number of shipping companies are setting up a special communications department, which handles their relations with the mass media and, more generally, with those groups involved in their operation. Communication is a crucial factor for the progress of companies not only in normal conditions, when their aim is to convey their image and work to the various groups mentioned above, but also in crisis conditions (e.g., accident at sea, leakage of fuels into the sea), when developments can be so rapid as to invalidate the effectiveness of even the best organised publicity and communication programmes. For this reason, many companies incorporate their communications departments into the planning for dealing with emergencies (crisis management), assigning to them the special responsibility for planning, co-ordinating and implementing the actions for communication with the mass media and with any other group of individuals involved in the situation. In the case of those companies whose shares are listed on some stock exchange, the process of communication also extends to their relations with shareholders. Because of the need for frequent and reliable briefing of investors and shareholders, the process of communication extends as well to support for the companies’ share through the promotion of their image.

Safety, quality and training

International Safety Management Code/ ISM: focuses on the implementation of standards and processes by shipping companies with the basic aim of the safety of human life and the avoidance of the causing of harm to the marine environment and to property.

110

Shipping companies are required to comply with a number of rules and regulations in connection with the protection of human life and of the environment and the ensuring of the quality of the services which they provide (Chapter 9). The process of the management of safety, quality and environmental responsibility is connected with the efforts of the company to develop, operate and maintain management systems which safeguard its scheduled performance in these areas. The training of staff, of employees on land and of the crews of the ships is nowadays a basic concern of every company which organises its processes on the criterion of the need both for constant improvement of the standards of its services and for the development of the skills of its personnel. As will be explained in Chapter 9, while safety management is, in accordance with the International Safety Management Code, mandatory for shipping companies, quality management and its certification, as well as the management of environmental responsibility and its certification, are an option for companies. Certification can be carried out on the basis of the ISO 9001 and ISO 14001 standards, respectively, which concern the safeguarding of quality and environmental responsibility. ISO 9001 was not designed exclusively for shipping, but it can serve as a supplement to the ISM Code. This leads many companies in most instances to opt for the development and certification of the quality management systems by the same organisation as certifies their compliance with the ISM Code. As regards the development of the system of management of environmental responsibility, certification is carried out in the same way. In Box 3.14 there is an account of the policy on safeguarding quality of a specific shipping company.

T H E P R O C E S S E S O F T H E S H I P P I N G C O M PA N Y

BOX 3.14 Quality assurance policy of a shipping company15 The shipping company is committed to providing professional ship management services at a competitive cost, in order to satisfy agreed requirements of its customers, as well as to fulfil its duties towards its employees, society and the environment. Its objective is the provision of quality services by means of:

❚ ❚ ❚ ❚

satisfaction of its customers’ requirements, as those are defined in the charter party; development and implementation of controlled processes; constant improvement in its operational response; compliance with the requirements of ISO 9001:2000, ISO 14001:1996 and the ISM Code, as well as constant improvement of its management system;

❚

formulation and dissemination of measurable and consistent objectives and performance aims for the personnel;

❚

compliance with applicable statutory requirements, international legislation and requirements of classification societies;

❚

operation of vessels under its management without accidents or incidents which endanger its employees, the environment or property in its care;

❚ ❚

compliance with current health and safety legislation; development of its employees’ skills and the increasing of their contribution through their effective training.

All those employed in the company, either on land or on its vessels, are responsible for the implementation of the company’s policy on quality safeguarding. In order to meet these objectives, the company has developed and applies a management system which, as a minimum, must satisfy the requirements of ISO 9001-, ISO 14001and of the ISM Code. The management of the shipping company is responsible for monitoring and revising the quality policy at regular intervals in order to ensure that it remains relevant and effective.

In today’s world, quality is seen as a constituent part of services for every well organised shipping company and an important factor for its success. Shipping companies place strong emphasis on quality considering it as an important valueadding attribute of their services (Lagoudis et al. 2006). Academic research into this subject is limited (Thai et al, 2014). Nevertheless, accounts given by companies confirm the benefits arising for them from the development of quality management systems for cost, internal efficiency, reputation and competitiveness. Certification on the basis of Standard 9001 can contribute to an increase in the company’s competitiveness and yield; this has been confirmed by a series of studies (Dimara et al, 2004). Of course, in view of the particularities of the shipping industry, a condition for this to apply is, as has already been pointed out, the ability of the company to be competitive in the market and to offer services at a lower cost than its competitors.

111

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

Factors of particular importance for the successful development and certification of quality management systems are the following:

❚ ❚ ❚ ❚

safety and quality department: the department responsible for the management of those matters which relate to the company’s safety and quality management systems.

It is worth noting that as regards certification and the development of the systems, these are not ends in themselves, but are designed to serve the aim of the company of providing services of a higher quality, and constant improvement, which results from small and gradual or major changes, and depends upon what is learnt. Within this framework, the development and certification of such systems must be combined with the development by the company’s human resources of all the skills and competences which will contribute to the achievement of the aim. Here it can be pointed out by way of indication that during an audit of the Shell oil company to a Norwegian shipping company, approximately 2.5 hours were needed for the examination of the crewing department, with the inspection concentrating on matters concerning the management of skills and assessment of actual capabilities of the staff, and only 45 minutes for the quality department. And this is only one of the many examples which confirm the adoption of such an approach by charterers (Bordal et al, 2002) and demonstrate the contribution made by the human resources to the implementation of the quality management systems and the crucial nature of training, which should not be carried out within the framework of a traditional approach focusing on the familiarisation of the personnel with the systems and the processes and their better observance, but on the development of skills and competences which will also permit the better application of these systems. The process of the system of management of safety, quality and training is carried out in large companies by a special department, whereas in certain others, mainly of a large size, an independent department for the organisation and carrying out of the training of the human resources is in operation. The tasks of the safety, quality and training department are as follows:

❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚

112

commitment of the senior management and its participation; training and provision of authorisation to the personnel; briefing on quality; measurement of performance and concentration on the customer (Cheng & Choy, 2007).

the planning, application, monitoring of the implementation and the assessment of the safety, quality and environmental responsibility management systems; the monitoring of the regulatory framework of operation of shipping companies in relation to safety and the protection of the environment, and the necessary updating of the systems; the carrying out of checks and inspections on the ships in order to ensure their compliance with what is stipulated in the systems; the constant upgrading of the systems by means of the improvement of the rules and processes which these include; the recording, analysis and assessment of data relating to accidents and other incidents of non-compliance, and the planning of corrective actions which will avert a repetition of them;

❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚

the training of the personnel and their familiarisation with the company’s system by means of the planning and carrying out of training programmes for the employees on land and for the crews, either on land or on board the ship; the planning and carrying out, in collaboration with the captains, of exercises and drills, in order for them to deal with emergency situations; the ensuring of a full and adequate understanding on the part of the persons involved of the framework within which the system of safety, quality and environmental responsibility management operates; the co-ordination and checking of the carrying out of the activities of other departments of the company with a connection with the implementation of the system; the co-ordination of actions to deal with emergencies (accidents, etc.) by the staff belonging to the team of responders to emergencies.

Simply stated, the modern shipping firm needs to have its own top-notch organization in place. This means it needs to possess the relevant base of know-how, it needs to be able to change, it needs to have a penchant for keeping things simple and focused, and it needs to be the lowest possible cost provider. (Lorange, 2005:142)

❚ Introduction The contribution of organisation to the on-going efforts of the company to remain competitive is essential. Organisation is the process by which the relations between activities, resources and employees are determined. Adaptations having to do with the external environment of each company, as well as with factors such as its size, strategy, technology, etc. are required. In this chapter, the manner in which organisation determines the structure of the company will be analysed. The structure initially is designed to serve the company’s strategy; thereafter, however, as it functions as a framework for the taking of decisions, the undertaking of actions and the manifestation of behaviours on the part of the individuals of which it is composed, it leads to the necessary adaptations of the strategy. The purpose of this chapter is the analysis of the organisation of a shipping company. After a discussion of the importance of organisation, the organisational structure and the systemic nature of the organisations are analysed, with special reference to the shipping company as a system. The structural dimensions of the organisational structure are then examined and a separate account is given of formalisation, centralisation and complexity.

118

T H E O R G A N I S AT I O N O F T H E S H I P P I N G C O M PA N Y

❚ The importance of organisation Senge informs us that he has often put the following proposition to groups of executives (Senge, 1996:414): “Imagine that your organisation is an ocean liner on which you are ‘the leader’. What is your role?” Most of them assigned to themselves the role of captain, others that of the chief engineer and some that of the person responsible for social activities. Senge points out that these roles are indeed leadership roles, but that it is the designer whose role is really the leading one, because he has the greatest influence on the operation of the vessel. And it is true that it is not worthwhile to be a leader in an organisation which has not been designed properly. The same is true, by extension, of the captain of a ship which has not been designed properly. The organisation and organisational structure of companies and organisations are not fixed, but are affected by the variation of factors which determine them, variation which may be due either to internal factors (development, alteration of strategies, change of leadership, change in the philosophy of management, etc.) or to external factors (new regulations, intensity of competition, reduction of demand, etc.). Given that effectiveness is one of the aims of an organisation, the question arises as to whether there is a specific model of organisation which leads to greater effectiveness and is of general application, that is, holds good for all companies, as has been maintained by classic management theory. The principle of the ‘one and only best way’ of organising companies has predominated in the thinking on organisational structure from the early twentieth century (Mintzberg, 1996). However, a series of failures at the business level has made it clear that organisations differ, and that what is applicable to one company is not necessarily applicable to another. Thus, management theory has departed from the principle of the ‘one and only best way’ and has headed towards the ‘all depends’ principle, also known as the ‘contingency’ approach, according to which the state of the organisation, meaning its age and size, the type of the system of production and the degree of complexity of the environment in which it operates must be reflected in the organisational structure. Mintzberg draws attention to the need for the organisation to take everything into consideration (Mintzberg, 1996). In line with this approach, “effective organization depends on developing a cohesive set of relations between structural design; the age, size, and technology of the firm; and the conditions of the industry in which it is operating” (Morgan, 1986:56). The term ‘organisation’ is used to describe a totality of individuals who are, in a co-ordinated manner, pursuing the realisation of the same objectives, but, at the same time, the process by means of which the relations and manner of interaction between the individuals are determined. Shipping companies are organisations with the dimension of a social entity or of a totality of individuals who pursue a specific aim. Similarly, each ship belonging to a shipping company constitutes an organisation with the dimension of a totality of individuals who pursue a specific aim. But for a totality of individuals, an organisation as a social entity, to function effectively, it must, internally, implement the process of organisation.

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

❚ Organisations as systems Organisations can be analysed as open socio-technical systems which are in interrelation with their environment. They consist of the following interrelated sub-systems (Figure 4.1) (Kast and Rosenzweig, 1985):

❚

❚

❚

❚

Organisational Goals and Values sub-system: An organisation takes many of its values from the socio-economic environment. In accordance with a basic principle, the aims of the organisation as a sub-system of society must be imposed by the system to which this organisation belongs. The organisation carries out a function for society, and, if it wishes to receive inflows from it, it must comply with its demands. Technical sub-system: The technical sub-system is concerned with the knowledge which is necessary for the jobs, including the techniques, used for the transformation of inflows into outflows. It is determined by the requirements of the organisation as regards its task and differs depending upon that task. Psychosocial sub-system: Every organisation has a psycho-economic subsystem which consists of interacting individuals and groups and is shaped by the behaviour and motivation of the individuals, the relations determined by their position and roles, the dynamic of the groups, the emotions, values, attitudes and expectations of the individuals. Structural sub-system: The structural sub-system includes the ways in which the task of each organisation is divided up and co-ordinated. In the typical

Figure 4.1 Organisation’s sub-systems Source: Based on (Kast and Rosenzweig, 1985: Exhibit 3.5).

120

T H E O R G A N I S AT I O N O F T H E S H I P P I N G C O M PA N Y

❚

form, the structure is defined by the organisation chart, the positions and their description, the rules and the processes. Managerial sub-system: This sub-system links the organisation with its environment, sets its objectives and plans and designs the structure and the mechanisms for its monitoring.

Each sub-system is related to one theoretical approach to organisation and management which focuses on the relevant aspects of organisations. The structural and the managerial sub-systems are related to the view of the classical theory, the psychosocial sub-system is related to behavioural theory, and the technical sub-system is related to the management science approach. Under the system approach, all sub-systems are considered as interrelated parts of an open sociotechnical system. Seeing organisations from this point of view allows the analysis and understanding of the interrelations and the dynamic dimension of the parts and the whole.

The shipping company as a system A shipping company can be analysed as a system of interrelated and interdependent parts (Figure 4.2). The management of it is complex since it includes the handling of units of high capital value scattered in distant regions of the world, which makes the day-to-day monitoring of their operation difficult (Frankel, 1982). The factor of spatial dispersion renders such an approach essential. As will be explained later in the chapter, spatial dispersion is the dispersal in space of activities and personnel. In such a case, an organisation can carry out the same processes with the same division of labour and the same hierarchical structuring at many points simultaneously (Hall, 1996). Spatial dispersion is one of the basic characteristics of a shipping company (Goulielmos, 1996), as its ships carry out the same task with the same organisation and the same hierarchical structuring even though they are located in different ports or are travelling along different sea routes. In spite of their apparent similarity, however, each vessel is a different organisation. Given the differences in the human factor, the vessels differ from one another at least as regards the psycho-social sub-system and the sub-system of organisational goals and values. For example, even in the best co-ordinated systems, it is difficult for two groups of crew made up of seafarers of differing nationalities to show complete identity as to the manner of operation and the dynamic which develops from the interaction of their members. At the same time, the offices on land support the operation of the vessels. The performance of the ships is influenced by physical factors, as well as by acts or omissions of the crews on board them, and of those working in the company’s offices on land. This means that there is a need for careful co-ordination and collaboration between the different departments and the vessels for the achievement of the common objective. Furthermore, an assessment of the direct and the indirect impacts of specific decisions or actions undertaken by a vessel or a department upon the rest of the company’s vessels and departments is required.

For example, a ship/sub-system of the company may be more efficient in the execution of specific processes which is likely to be due to the approach the captain and the rest of the crew adopt. In this event, the company which operates as a well co-ordinated system will seek to make adjustments on all the other ships/sub-systems, in order to make them operate in an equally effective way. This, naturally, presupposes the existence of mechanisms for identifying the difference in effectiveness and the reasons contributing to this and for the dissemination of the way of carrying out the specific processes to all the company’s other ships. In this context, communication between the various departments and the vessels which constitute the sub-systems of the shipping company’s system is a condition for the effective operation and the achievement of the aims both of each sub-system and of the company as a whole. Shown in Figure 4.2 is a relatively simple instance of a system in which there is an infrastructure sub-system on land and 10 ships/sub-systems, each of which also functions as a system consisting of the five sub-systems described above (goals and values, technical, structural, psycho-social, managerial). All these sub-systems have to achieve co-ordinated and effective operation, both in their interior and in their relations with the other sub-systems, in accordance with the planning and objectives of the company. In such a system, there are formal lines of communication between the office and each ship, but also informal lines which permit communication between the vessels. The complexity of a system
