which includes a series of sub-systems/infrastructures on land in various shipping centres all over the world and some dozens of ships/sub-systems, moreover, of differing types, in constant operation at different geographical points worldwide, can be imagined.

❚ Organisational structure For the realisation of any activity engaging many people and different tasks, two basic and opposing preconditions must be fulfilled: the division of labour and the co-ordination of the individual tasks. Thus, structure is the whole of the ways in which the division of labour is carried out and its co-ordination is achieved (Mintzberg, 1996). Organisational structure is intended to produce organisational outputs and to achieve organisational goals, is designed to minimise or regulate the influence of individual variations on the organisation and provides the settings in which power is exercised, decisions are made and activities are carried out (Hall, 1996). The organisational structure is the result of organisational design, that is, of the process during which the different dimensions and the different constituent parts are combined in such a way that they form a single system. These processes can be classified as structural and contextual (Daft, 2010). The structural dimensions refer to the internal characteristics of the organisation. The three most basic structural dimensions are formalisation, complexity and the putting in place of the process of the decision-taking in the hierarchy of the organisation. The contextual dimensions, otherwise known as defining factors, characterise the whole organisation and concern the framework which influences and shapes the structural dimensions. Included in these are the external environment, strategy and objectives, size, technology and the organisational culture of the organisation. The organisational structure is reflected in the organisation chart, that is, the visual representation of the range of activities and processes of an organisation.

❚ Dimensions of the organisational structure Every organisational structure is characterised by:

❚ ❚ ❚

formalisation: the degree to which expectations in connection with the means and the final result of the task are specified, written down and imposed upon the members of the organisation; centralisation/decentralisation of power: the degree to which the taking of decisions remains at the top levels of the management or is decentralised to other levels; complexity in the division of labour: the degree to which there are different kinds of tasks and, consequently, complex organisational and management problems.

organisational structure: the arrangement of the departments (the constituent parts) of an organisation or of a company. organisational design: the process of combining the dimensions and constituent parts of the organisation in such a way that they form a single system. structural dimensions: refer to the internal characteristics of the organisation’s structure which create the basis for its comparison with other organisations. contextual dimensions: characterise the whole organisation and concern the framework which influences and shapes the structural dimensions. organisation chart: visual representation of the range of activities and processes of an organisation.

In the analysis which follows these three dimensions are examined.

123

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

Formalisation formalisation: written specification of expectations in connection with the means and the final result.

Formalisation has to do with the degree to which expectations in connection with the means which will be used in the task and the final result are specified, written down and imposed upon the members of the organisation (Donnelly et al, 1995). It also has to do with the existence of the rules, procedures and policies designed to determine the way in which tasks are carried out which are recorded in handbooks, and govern the action of individuals. From this point of view, formalisation is the most important dimension for the personnel of the organisation, since the degree of it determines their behaviour and action (Hall, 1996). The dimension of formalisation influences the organisation and manner of operation of the organisation at many levels, since, inter alia, it is linked with control, the approach to the human resources, and the knowledge management. The extent of the formalisation of an organisation is indicative of the manner in which the individuals who take the decisions approach its members. If the members of the organisation are regarded as capable as regards their judgement and self-control, then the degree of formalisation will be low. If they are not thought capable of taking decisions, and rules are required to guide their behaviour, the degree of formalisation will be high (Hall, 1996). The degree of formalisation of an organisational structure has a significant relation with its organisational culture. In organisations with a strong and cohesive culture, formalisation is more limited, since the culture serves as a substitute for it, as it guides the attitudes and behaviours of the employees. An additional characteristic of formalisation is connected with the scope it provides for an organisation to manage knowledge more effectively in its interior. This applies particularly to explicit knowledge, which, through formalisation, with the help of software, can be rendered specific, recorded and disseminated to the whole organisation (Box 4.1). The recording of explicit knowledge in handbooks and the formulation of rules and procedures are connected with the characteristics of formalisation which contribute to the minimising of influence from changes in personnel and economising on resources. Each shipping company develops systems in which the jobs, competences and responsibilities of each employee, the policies, rules and the procedures which determine the manner of action of the individuals and narrow the margins for any arbitrary behaviour on their part are explicitly described. For each post, the following are precisely defined:

❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚

the title of the post; the person to whom the holder of the post reports; those who report to this person; his replacement; the qualifications of the holder of the post; his responsibilities and competences.

Within this framework, which is determined by international regulations, the degree of formalisation of the jobs carried out by the personnel of the offices on

124

T H E O R G A N I S AT I O N O F T H E S H I P P I N G C O M PA N Y

land and by the crews of the vessels has increased. At the same time, of course, a high level of formalisation in certain cases acts as a discouragement to the taking of initiatives, particularly among the crews, because, as will be explained in Chapter 8, a series of factors has contributed to the criminalisation of the seafaring profession as a consequence of the way in which the authorities deal with seafarers in the event of accidents and other occurrences. The following extract from an article describes this change in the attitude of seafarers because of the criminalisation of their profession. In the past, you knew that your seamen would, in difficult situations, invent solutions and take initiative to solve problems. Today, it seems people are increasingly afraid to take actions that are not prescribed in the manual, fearing that if they do, and something goes wrong, they will suffer for it. (Korkodilos, 2010)

BOX 4.1 Knowledge and its management The knowledge management approach contends that the creation of a competitive advantage relies upon resources which are based on knowledge, that is, on the intellectual capabilities and the knowledge of the employees, and on their ability to learn, and thus acquire new knowledge. Companies which are able to create organisational knowledge, that is, new knowledge, to convey it to the whole of their organisation, and incorporate it into their products, their services and their systems fulfil all the conditions for success. Knowledge can be divided into explicit and tacit. Explicit knowledge can be formulated in language, and can be rendered in grammatical terms, mathematical formulae, in handbooks and by other means, in such a way that it can easily be communicated from employee to employee. Tacit knowledge, on the other hand, is difficult to formulate in language; it is personal knowledge, rooted in personal experience, and includes indefinable items, such as personal convictions, a specific point of view and systems of values (Nonaka and Takeuchi, 2001). In the light of the above, explicit knowledge can be standardised, codified and conveyed. Tacit knowledge, on the other hand, resides in experience and is difficult to express; individuals possess this knowledge, but cannot in all circumstances convey it. Tacit

explicit knowledge: can be formulated in language and communicated from individual to individual, officially and easily. tacit knowledge: cannot easily be formulated in language; it is personal knowledge, rooted in individual experience.

knowledge manifests itself only at the moment when it is applied, and provides the basis for the development of a competitive advantage which competitors are not able easily to copy (Lubit, 2001). According to the basic hypotheses of the knowledge management approach (Grant, 1997):

125

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

❚

knowledge is the most significant productive resource in terms of strategic importance and its contribution to added value;

❚ ❚

different types of knowledge vary in their transferability; it is individuals who create knowledge, and, in the case of tacit knowledge, it is they who possess it;

❚ economies of scope: achieved when the cost of the supply of two different goods by the same company is comparatively smaller than the cost of their separate production.

most knowledge, particularly explicit knowledge, is subject to economies of scale and economies of scope.

For the company, it is not the acquisition of specific knowledge, which will rapidly lose its value, but the acquisition of knowledge which is difficult to copy, and the development of mechanisms which will facilitate its acquisition, which are important. Knowledge is not always easy to diffuse in an organisation. If it exists and is not disseminated, it remains a possession of those who have it and has limited influence on the ability of the company to create value. At the same time, however, knowledge which can be diffused in the interior of the company, that is, explicit knowledge, can also penetrate its boundaries and become the possession of competitors, so that it cannot serve as a basis for the creation of a sustainable competitive advantage. Tacit knowledge, because of its subjective and intuitive nature, is not amenable to processing and is not easy to communicate. A condition for its communication and diffusion is its conversion into words and numbers which can be understood. What results from the conversion of tacit into explicit knowledge is organisation knowledge (Nonaka and Takeuchi, 2001). Tacit knowledge is conveyed through action, often requiring an extended period of social interaction (DeNisi et al, 2003). Usually, tacit knowledge is not shared or communicated, so that it is lost when the employee leaves the company. This is one of the most important restrictions on shipping companies in their efforts to manage knowledge in their interior. In the case of those working on board ships, the process of the communication of knowledge, particularly of tacit knowledge, encounters limitations which have to do with the suspension of the employment of seafarers for a certain period and the alteration in the composition of the teams on each vessel. At this point, a factor of special importance is the stability or otherwise of the employment of the members of the crew (Chapter 8). Thus, a factor that can facilitate the dissemination of knowledge is the strong ties between the employees and the organisation, which are supported by the appropriate organisational systems. There is a feature of the shipping industry which might create hindrances to the organisations’ effort to disseminate the knowledge their members possess, which, as will be explained in Chapter 8, is related to the form of employment of their crews. If the seafarers are employed by crewing agencies and not by the shipping companies which operate the ships and if they know they will not continue

126

T H E O R G A N I S AT I O N O F T H E S H I P P I N G C O M PA N Y

employment on the same ship or even with the same company in their next sea service, it is possible that their engagement will be minimum. Thus, in case of minimum commitment and loyalty of employees, the dissemination and integration of the knowledge they possess encounter difficulties (Theotokas et al, 2008; Fei, 2011). Apart from the distinction made between tacit and explicit knowledge, one is also made in the bibliography between market-specific and firm-specific knowledge (Berdrow and Lane, 2003). In the case of shipping companies, market-specific knowledge includes information about the shipping industry, information about customer demands on a company’s services and information about competitors, while firm-specific knowledge refers to operational technology, employees’ expertise and organisational know-how (Lee & Song, 2015). Research carried out among Korea’s shipping companies shows that these two categories of knowledge, combined with absorptive capacity, i.e., the learning capacity of the companies and their ability to make best use of the knowledge, contribute to the maximisation of organisational innovation and logistics value of the companies. It is thus argued that the managers of shipping companies should recognise the significance of the knowledge asset and their internal capacity, in order to not only improve their own competitiveness but also contribute to the greater integration and higher performance of the whole global logistics chain in which they belong. (Lee & Song, 2015:231) Wilh. Wilhelmsen is a company noted for its innovative approach and its place in the global logistics chain to which it belongs. Its approach to the matter of knowledge and the ability of the company and of its employees to make best use of it is fully described by its CEO: We have 48,000 hands on deck. We would be nothing without our dedicated employees. Their knowledge of the industry, how our products and services can improve and make our customers’ operations more efficient combined with sound knowledge of regulatory requirements, give us a unique position to deliver high quality services. (Wilhelmsen, 2016)

127

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

BOX 4.2 Knowledge share in Teekay Teekay (www.teekay.com) makes a significant investment in ways which ensure that knowledge is shared and developed. On the basis of the idea that there is in the company a wealth of knowledge and a variety of abilities which could become the possession of those working in the company, it has promoted the concept of ‘learning from each other’. In July 2013, Teekay Vancouver embarked upon the ‘Knowledge Share’ programme, which has as its objective the diffusion of experience and ‘learning from each other’. This started out on a voluntary basis with personnel who made a presentation of and discussed subjects relating to Teekay, but also to the shipping industry more broadly. The programme was subsequently extended to other regions. By means of the programme, the personnel of the company share knowledge on issues on which they are working when they visit other offices belonging to the company. As the company’s web page states about the Knowledge Share programme: “It’s a simple way of sharing and teaching each other new things, while learning more about the different aspects of our business and the maritime industry” (Teekay, 2015). Moving in the same direction, another initiative of the company which aims at knowledge dissemination is the One Tanker Team, a series of videos which aims at projecting for members of the company the Teekay Way. Each video shows the modus operandi of certain member-groups from the various processes of the company (Teekay, 2017).

Formalisation exerts a significant influence on the organisation of a shipping company, and particularly on the ships, which constitute organisations in which alternation of the personnel is frequent, as the period of service of the officers and members of the crew frequently extends to seven months. Furthermore, the crew usually consists of seafarers of different nationalities who speak different languages and, naturally, have differing cultures. Each change, then, is likely to give rise to a temporary disruption of the system, at least until the new crew member comes to understand the environment to which he belongs, as well as the nature, the content and the interdependences of his job. Formalisation reduces the impacts from the frequent alternation of individuals in the organisational structure of the vessel and, apart from anything else, permits the smooth transmission to new arrivals on the vessel of the explicit knowledge which has been acquired from its operation in the past. The existence of rules and procedures, and of handbooks dealing with the performance of various tasks on board the ship, in language intelligible to everyone, permits the more rapid adaptation and better performance of these individuals in the new working environment. Moreover, formalisation by means of rules and procedures which determine the way in which activities are carried out contributes, among other factors,2 to an increase in the control range, and, consequently, to the creation

128

T H E O R G A N I S AT I O N O F T H E S H I P P I N G C O M PA N Y

of organisational structures with few hierarchical levels. Control range refers to the number of subordinates whom a supervisor can manage within a specific time frame. Given that in the case of companies which are active in the international bulk and liner shipping markets, the environment to whose demands they must adapt may differ, particularly as regards safety and requirements in the operation of the ships, formalisation has to provide the framework for the adaptation of the processes and actions to these demands. This factor will be discussed further in the next chapter.

control range: the number of subordinates whom a supervisor can manage within a specific time frame.

Centralisation – decentralisation of power Centralisation – decentralisation of power concerns the sharing out in the interior of the organisation of power and the right of decision-making, that is, who has the right to take decisions, and the nature of those decisions. Centralisation is the degree to which decisions are taken at the high levels of the organisation, while decentralisation is the degree to which the power of decision-making is transferred to the lower levels of the organisation (Daft, 2010:93). A series of factors contributes to the definition of the degree of centralisation/ decentralisation in an organisation, such as formalisation, the cost of decisions, the culture of the organisation, the skills of its personnel, the conditions of the business environment and the policies and mechanisms of control applied in the organisation. In the case of shipping companies, a series of decisions are taken by personnel at the lower levels of the hierarchy, but these are in essence, in normal conditions, strictly scheduled, and the scope for initiative on the part of the personnel is predetermined by the rules, procedures, policies and handbooks of the company. This characteristic also applies to the company’s ships. In the past, when the technologies and communications systems did not favour constant office-ship communication, the degree of decentralisation of decisions to captains was greater than it is today. The degree of centralisation/decentralisation serves as an indication of the way in which organisations treat their employees. In organisations with a high degree of centralisation in the taking of decisions, the employees are in need of close monitoring, whereas in others, with a low degree of centralisation, the employees are able to monitor themselves. The degree of centralisation/decentralisation in the taking of decisions is related to a number of factors, such as the size of the organisation, the level of technology, the training of the personnel, the culture prevailing in the organisation and the environment in which it operates.

Complexity The complexity of an organisation, that is to say, the existence of different kinds of work and of complex organisational and management problems, has a significant influence on the behaviour of its members, on the structural conditions,

centralisation – decentralisation of power: the sharing out within the interior of the organisation of power and of the right of decision-making.

complexity: the existence of different kinds of work and of composite organisational and management problems.

129

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

horizontal complexity: the division of the task carried out by the organisation.

vertical complexity: the division of the hierarchical levels of the organisation.

130

on the processes which are carried out in its interior, and on its relations with its environment (Hall, 1996). In order to measure the degree of complexity of an organisation, various criteria are employed in the bibliography. The three most important of these are horizontal complexity, vertical complexity and geographical distribution or spatial dispersion. Horizontal complexity describes the division of the task carried out by the organisation, and can be estimated by measuring the number of different jobs which are carried out. One measure for the estimation of horizontal complexity is the number of departments there are in the organisation. The more departments there are, the greater is its horizontal complexity. Shipping companies are organisations with relatively large horizontal complexity, which, of course, is also a function of the size of the fleet which they manage, as well as of the degree to which they carry out within their own interior the whole of the management processes and do not outsource them to third parties. On the other hand, ships as organisations show smaller horizontal complexity. Vertical complexity describes the division of the hierarchical levels of the organisation and can be estimated by measuring the posts between the managing director and the employee who is lowest in the hierarchy. The greater the vertical complexity is in a company, the greater the difficulty of monitoring, coordination and communication will be (Hall, 1996). The specialisation of the fleets they operate affects the vertical complexity of the shipping companies. Bulk Shipping companies can be regarded as businesses with small vertical complexity. This is owing to the fact that whereas carrying out their managerial task requires a relatively large number of operational specialisations, the number of personnel employed is small. As a rule, the ratio of employees working in the company’s offices on land to each vessel fluctuates between 0.5 to 1 and 4 to 1. The larger the ratio, the more the operations that are organised in-house by the shipping company (Theotokas, 1997; Mottram, 1999; Stopford, 2009).3 This ratio is a function of the company’s strategy and the degree to which it incorporates the new communications technologies into its operation. A company which manages ten ships may employ a staff of 25 in its offices on land. In such a company there is a number of departments, in the interior of which a small number of staff is employed, and there are usually three levels in the hierarchy. Within an organisation, the degree of complexity of its different divisions or departments may vary. A large shipping company which manages a fleet of 60 ships and employs more than 150 individuals may have more than ten departments, each with a different degree of complexity. The legal department may be staffed by a lawyer and his two assistants, whereas the technical department can have more than 30 employees and include three or four hierarchical levels. The organisation chart shown in Figure 4.3 is indicative. Liner companies, on the other hand, as has been discussed in Chapter 3, are organisations with large and complex organisational structures which employ many employees, especially within functions related to the marketing and sales and the operations. Thus, vertical complexity will be higher, with more hierarchical levels (see Box 4.5).

Figure 4.3 The structure of the technical and the legal departments of a large bulk shipping company

Spatial dispersion can include both horizontal and vertical complexity, that is, the activities and the personnel may show a geographical distribution according to either their horizontal or their vertical dimension in the light of the way in which their centres of power or their tasks are divided up. Spatial dispersion is particularly important for an estimation of the complexity of an organisation if it carries out the same processes, shows the same division of labour and maintains the same hierarchical levels in many different locations. It goes without saying that a shipping company belongs within this category. It therefore manifests a large degree of spatial dispersion, as its vessels operate in all the seas. Nevertheless, there are also reasons connected with the strategy or the operation of the company which may call for the existence and functioning of offices, and therefore of organisational structures, in different parts of the world (Box 4.3).

spatial dispersion: the dispersion of activities and personnel according to their horizontal or vertical dimension.

BOX 4.3 Spatial dispersion and strategy at TORM In June 2007, TORM (www.torm.com) and Teekay Shipping Corporation (www.teekay. com) completed the joint acquisition of the OMI Corporation, with the former acquiring control of 24 ships (product tankers) from the OMI fleet. The combined smart move of purchasing OMI took the tanker market by surprise (Mathews, 2007). After the acquisition, TORM created a fleet with more than 100 ships, chiefly tankers. The aim of both purchasers was to acquire a high quality fleet and to enhance their position in the consolidated tanker market. For TORM this move contributed to its strategic aspiration to become a global company, increasing its organisational capacity and scope. The increase in size meant the introduction of new roles and responsibilities, new offices in international maritime centres and a new organisational setting. The challenge for the company was to make sure that the quality of services delivered to customers would continue to be of the same high level (Torm, 2010). To

131

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

succeed in this, it was crucial to bring together the different cultures of TORM and of the part of OMI that came in and meld them into one cohesive firm. After the acquisition, TORM implemented a cost-saving programme, introducing changes to its organisation (focus on process standardisation, better use of IT, decrease of operating cost, optimising the company’s land based organisation) with the aim of improving its efficiency (Tanker Operator, 2009).

The spatial dispersion of the organisation of shipping companies is connected with their size. In the case of a large-size shipping company with a fleet of dozens of ships and representative offices in many countries, co-ordination is a constant objective. In Figure 4.4, the world-wide activities of Odfjell, a company active in specialised shipping for transportation and storage of bulk liquid chemicals, acids, edible oils and other special products, are shown. Odfjell operates a fleet of 80 chemical tankers whose carrying capacity ranges from 4,000 to 75,000 dwt, and two LPGs of 9,000 cbm. These ships are dispersed in the oceans, carrying specialised cargoes, having to deal with complex conditions as all tankers have (see Box 4.4). Thus, the need for proper support from the offices ashore is constant. The Odfjell group consists of 25 companies registered in various countries, and it operates offices in Bergen, Tokyo, Shanghai, Manila, Singapore, Melbourne, Durban, Mumbai, Dubai, Buenos Aires, Sao Paolo and Houston. In parallel, its network includes owned terminals in eight ports in Europe, Asia and North America, and terminals party owned by related parties in many ports of Latin America. At the end of 2016, it employed 2,890 employees in the network activities. For such a complex organisation, horizontally, vertically and spatially, co-ordination is a constant objective, for which an organisational structure supporting clear lines of communication and accountability, and rules for delegation of authority that specify responsibility, have been established (Odfjell, 2017a).

As the Surveyor, in which the speech was published, notes, these demands, at the time of the Erika accident (Box 2.7) increased significantly. More specifically, Capt. The ship’s personnel are expected to cope with all these visitors while, at the same time, dealing with the operations of discharge, security and safety. In what other industry would this state of affairs be tolerated, in the context of an operation of the magnitude and risk that attends the discharge of a crude oil tanker? Enough is surely enough.

As has already been pointed out, every shipping market manifests its own particularities and makes specific organisational and management demands on the companies. Companies which work in the liner shipping markets, for example, are required to have a different organisation, for example, from those of bulk shipping. In the former case, the companies are concerned with the management of vessels which carry general cargo on a regular basis, serving many ports at one and the same time, on routes known in advance, where transportation is organ-

134

T H E O R G A N I S AT I O N O F T H E S H I P P I N G C O M PA N Y

ised and carried out on a ‘one ship – many cargoes’ basis, whereas in the latter, the companies manage vessels which, as a rule, are carrying one cargo on each occasion from one port to another port, a cargo which, moreover, belongs to one shipper. While a bulk shipping company can find employment for its vessels with the aid of a charter broker, who may be its associate exclusively, or an independent/external associate, where a liner shipping company is concerned, the finding of employment for its vessels is a considerably more complicated process. Liner shipping companies transport cargoes which may be in small quantities, but belong to many different shippers. For this reason, as has already been explained in Chapter 3, liner shipping companies engage in marketing, relations with clients, the reservation of cargoes, programming and many other activities with which bulk shipping companies are concerned only in a limited way. Thus, it is necessary for them to have a more highly developed organisational infrastructure on land, not only at the company’s headquarters, but also at the basic ports where their vessels call, as well as an extensive sales/brokerage network at various geographical points. At the same time, as they carry chiefly finished or semi-finished products, either for further processing or for consumption in the final markets, it is vital that they should ensure frequency and regularity for their customers – necessary preconditions for their unimpeded operation. Shippers prioritise time related and responsive services, over their prices, i.e., reliability and speed over value, which confirms that differentiation of services based on time attributes add competitiveness (Yuen and Thai, 2015). On the contrary, for bulk shipping companies time-related factors are considered as less important compared to those of quality, cost and service (Lagoudis & Theotokas, 2007). Furthermore, as has already been pointed out, it is essential that they should supply their customers with integrated solutions for the transportation of their products from the place of production to the place of consumption (‘door to door’) at prices which are known in advance. In order to achieve this, liner shipping companies resort to options involving the vertical development of their activities. The complexity of the organisational structure and the needs of coordination within such a company can be made clearer by the instance of Maersk, an account of which is given in Box 4.5.

BOX 4.5 Mergers, acquisitions and organisational complexity In 1999, the Danish liner shipping company Maersk, which is part of the A.P. Moller – Maersk group, with diversified activities in many sectors acquired the American Sealand company. The same year it had acquired the Safmarine Container Lines, which it chose to retain as a separate branch. Maersk/Sealand was at that time the first liner shipping company, of a size almost double that of the second; it employed a staff of 10,000, had 325 offices in 100 different countries, operated 25 container terminals throughout the world and managed a fleet of 250 vessels (600,000 teu), 110 of which were in its own ownership.

135

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

A few years later, Maersk made an even more striking strategic move: in August of 2005 it acquired the Royal P&O Nedlloyd5 liner shipping company for 2.8 bn dollars. At the time of the acquisition, Maersk was the largest liner shipping company in the world, while Royal P&O Nedlloyd had the third largest shipping company fleet. The two companies together employed 35,000 personnel, managed nearly 500 ships and operated containers of a size of over 2.5 m. teu. Maersk maintained offices in more than 125 countries and P&O Nedlloyd in more than 146. The importance of spatial dispersion in the organisation, co-ordination and monitoring of the personnel and of the new business pattern which resulted can be easily understood. The acquisition led to the creation of the biggest line in history, at a period when the industry was growing, globalisation still evolving and the prospects for development very strong. In this environment, organic growth was not an easy choice because of the lack of capacity (Drewry, 2016). The merger of the two companies began in early 2006. The fleet of some 170 vessels of P&O Nedlloyd was merged with the approximately 340 of Maersk’s fleet, thus creating a network of lines with greater cover and frequency of service, under the name of Maersk Line. As was expected, the amalgamation gave rise to costs arising from the merger of the two organisations, the termination of contracts, the halting of current activities and the starting of new ones, etc. The synergies connected with the acquisition of P&O Nedlloyd made their appearance to a moderate degree during 2006, but the preconditions were created for them to be present and made use of in the years immediately following. It is interesting that in its annual report for 2006 (A.P. Moller Maersk, 2007), Maersk stated that Maersk Line had lost a market share chiefly because of the difficulties arising from the implementation of a number of information systems at the same time as its amalgamation with P&O Nedlloyd and the merger of the global network of services of the two companies were taking place (Drewry, 2016). The simultaneous realisation of these two activities was the cause of major unexpected problems. The loss of a market share led to a reduction in the extent of the exploitation of the carrying capacity of the vessels and an increase in the unit cost. In early 2008, Maersk Line made public the details of the new strategy which it had planned, by which it would seek to reverse the direction it had taken up till then and return to sustainable profitability (Logistics Inside Asia, 2008). This long-term strategy, to which it turned after an analysis of its progress and feedback from its customers, was to lead to the simplification of the company. The four basic features of the strategy were:

❚ ❚

136

the loading of the vessels with profitable cargoes; the provision of the more reliable product sought by clients;

T H E O R G A N I S AT I O N O F T H E S H I P P I N G C O M PA N Y

❚

the provision of services which were rapid and responded to the requirements of clients;

❚

a reduction in the complexity and operating cost of the company.

As regards the last item, the company was going to reduce the number of jobs by 2,000–3,000, as well as the size of the offices representing it, and would decentralise the taking of decisions in them, so that they would be taken in closer proximity to customers. At the same time, it would embark on re-organisation with a view to reducing bureaucracy and improving processes, to the benefit of internal effectiveness and of clients, while, in order to simplify its organisational structure, it would reduce the number of distinct regions which it included from 14 to 11. The rest of this new strategy would be undertaken by a new managerial team, to be made up of executives from other industries, a move which would mark a change in the company’s longterm policy on the promotion and optimal employment of personnel (Sorn-Friese & Iversen, 2008). In the years which followed, the company continued to develop, adopting the approach of growing market share organically. Changing conditions in the market led to seek for cost leadership advantage and search for synergies among its operational units. Within this framework, it pursued economies of scale, an improvement in the way that vessels were employed and, through this, the reduction of cost per container carried, the re-negotiation of chartering contracts for vessels and charter parties and a reduction in the number of personnel (Skou, 2017). It was, however, hesitant about engaging in another major acquisition after the difficulties in amalgamation which followed the acquisition of P&O Nedlloyd (Milne, 2016). Nevertheless, in September 2016, it announced that its new strategy would divide its activities into two sectors: – that of transport and logistics, which will include the Maersk Line, APM Teminals, Maersk Container Industry, Svitzer and Damco companies. As scheduled, operational integration started in early 2017; – the oil-related business sector, which will include Maersk Oil, Maersk Drilling, Maersk Supply Service and Maersk Tankers. The companies in this sector will continue to operate individually, with a view to the maximisation of value for their shareholders. The aim of the new strategy is for the group to achieve a revitalisation of its activities and to find new sources for the development of revenues, so that it is not reliant on the saving of cost and an improvement in effectiveness (Rasmussen, 2017). Its growth strategy would be based on organic growth, digitisation and inorganic growth. Within the context of this strategy, in late 2016 it was announced that Maersk Line had reached an agreement with Oetker Group on its acquisition of the fleet of the German Hamburg Sud liner shipping company, provided, of course, that the details were

137

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

agreed and the necessary approvals received from the regulatory authorities. Hamburg Sud at the time of the agreement was the world’s seventh largest liner shipping company, managed a fleet of 130 container vessels with a container capacity of 625,000 teu, employed nearly 6,000 personnel and maintained more than 250 offices throughout the world. The company held a leading place in North-South trade. According to the CEO of Maersk, Soren Skou, Hamburg Sud complements Maersk Line, and together they will provide their clients with the best possible services, first of all on the North-South lines. Hamburg Sud, within the framework of the agreement, will continue to operate as a separate brand and continue to serve customers through their local offices (Maersk, 2016). This option differentiates this acquisition from the previous ones, given that it does not involve the merging of the companies, and therefore reduces the attendant risks. After the acquisition of Hamburg-Sud Maersk Line came to an agreement with CMA CGM for the selling of Mercosul Line, a company with leading role in Brazil’s container shipping market. Maersk had acquired Mercosul Line in 2006 (Maersk Line, 2017). After the completion of the acquisition, Maersk Line has consolidated its leading position in the liner shipping market, as it will possess a container capacity of around 3.8 million teu and a 18.6 per cent global capacity share. The number of its employees will increase to 35,460, while its fleet will consist of 742 container vessels, with an average age of 8.7 years (iContainers, 2016).

❚ Introduction The organisational structure of a company is the result of the simultaneous influence of many factors, and for that reason can be compared with a building (Hall, 1996) which has been designed to answer to the needs of the people who will live in it, to their activities and the requirements of the environment, while it has been constructed externally and internally in such a way as to facilitate the technological applications which will be used by its residents. Organisations are designed by people who do not always agree with one another over this planning. They may have structures similar to those of other organisations or take forms which are usually those prevailing at a specific period. Just like buildings, which may be adapted to the needs of their residents and be in harmony with their environment, so organisations may be re-designed and adapt their structures to the demands of their internal and external environment. In the designing and redesigning of an organisational structure, if it is to be effective, the limitations imposed by certain factors connected with the framework within which it will operate have to be taken into account. One major category of factors affecting structure is contextual factors, which describe the situation in which an organisation is operating (Hall, 1996). According to the above approach, the environment in which a company operates and its internal structure are unique (Kast & Rosenzweig, 1985). There is no best way of organising a company, but it is a function of the factors, endogenous and exogenous, which affect its operation (Lawrence & Lorsch, 1990).

141

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

Figure 5.1 Contextual factors of the organisational structure Source: Compiled by the author.

In the case of the shipping industry, companies must possess the flexibility and the scope to adapt to the conditions of the environment and, of course, to adapt their strategy and their organisational structure accordingly. Factors such as the uncertainty of the environment in which the company operates, its aims and size, the stages of its life-cycle, its ownership status, the level of technology and the strategy which it implements contribute to the determination of its organisational structure (Donnelly et al, 1995). At the same time, the influence of the culture both at the national level and at that of organisation is of importance (Child, 1981). Other factors, such as the kind of service which the shipping company provides or its relations with the charterers of its ships, also affect their organisational structure (Frankel, 1982). In the units that follow, the contextual factors of organisational structure are examined.

142

C O N T E X T U A L FA C TO R S O F O R G A N I S AT I O N A L S T R U C T U R E

❚ The ‘size’ factor Size is one of the factors connected with the determination of the organisation of and the manner of managing a company (Kast & Rosenzweig, 1985; Hall, 1996). There is no criterion common to all for the classification of companies on the basis of their size. In the analysis which follows, the number of vessels which shipping companies have under their management is used as a criterion for their classification on the basis of size. Nevertheless, this criterion cannot function in an absolute way, since it does not reflect with accuracy either the magnitude of the capital which has been incorporated into each company or the number of persons whom it employs. This is owing to the scope for making use of economies of scale and to the generation of different needs at a level of organisation by different types of vessels (Box 5.1).

BOX 5.1 Ratio of those employed on land per ship The doubling of the number of a company’s ships does not necessarily lead to a doubling of its personnel in its offices on land. On this point, the economy resulting from scale is applicable (Metaxas, 1988). It is argued that a company which manages effectively a fleet of four vessels does not need many more staff to manage a fleet of ten ships with the same effectiveness (Spruyt, 1990). Of course, the number of staff is a function not only of the number of vessels, but also of the individual characteristics and strategies of the company (e.g., emphasis on the quality of the services or on cost, implementation of models of management, emphasis on purchases and sales of ships, etc.). With the development of the technology of communications, a considerable number of land jobs have been scrapped, chiefly because of the new approach called for in shipping companies by the regulations on the safety and quality of the services provided. It has already been pointed out that in the case of dry bulk shipping companies, the ratio of a ship to the number of those employed in their offices ranges from 1:0.5 to 1:4, depending on the strategy of the company with regard to outsourcing of activities (see Chapters 4 and 10), the emphasis on issues related to quality and the use of modern IT in its operation. Studies show that the number of employees per ship differs in different categories of companies (Theotokas, 1997; Mottram, 1999).

In the light of the above, it will be obvious that the organisational structures of dry bulk shipping companies include fewer employees compared to structures of other types of shipping companies. A company which manages 40 vessels may, on certain conditions, operate effectively with 100 employees in its offices.

143

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

The extent of the organisational structure is connected, then, with the size of each company, which, in its turn, is closely connected with efforts to achieve efficiency and a reduction of cost by means of economies of scale. Is there an optimum size for a shipping company? The answer can take the form of a question: for what kind of company? This means that there are no prescriptions, but that the specific characteristics of each company must be taken into account. There is a series of factors which determine what is the optimum size for each company. Included among these, inter alia, are the philosophy and strategy of the senior management, the quality of the workforce and the extent of the undertaking of risks. In addition, those factors which are connected with the external environment and the conditions which this imposes, as well as the special characteristics of the markets in which the shipping company is active, are also definitive. The following views of the shipowner Thanassis Martinos and of Jens Martin Jensen, Financial Director of Frontline, are instructive in this connection: East Mediterranean’s philosophy is that the size of the company should be reasonable to have certain benefits of scale, such as good services and better prices from suppliers. But on the other hand, we do not want to be too big. (Lloyd’s List, 2000:28) Some companies tend to hire more people when the market is good; we have never done that and we have always remained lean and mean so to speak. In a high and low market we try to offer the same service. Keep costs and the organisation under control. (SMI, 2010:43) The size of companies may change over time. This is true chiefly of shipping companies which operate in an environment which provides opportunities for a rapid increase or reduction in their size, that is, of the number of their ships. A change in their size, however, does not always lead to a change in their organisational structure. A company can, that is to say, in its efforts to exploit the opportunities offered by circumstances, either increase its fleet by acquiring ships at low prices and selling them after an interval in order to make capital gains, or reduce its fleet by selling ships at higher prices than the acquisition prices, until prices come down again and it can acquire new ones. Of course, structural changes in shipping, particularly in the freight markets for liquid cargoes, have as a consequence a reduction in the weight brought to bear by this strategy. At the present time, one of the crucial factors for success is the continuous presence of the company in the markets and the maintenance of an acceptable level of management, mainly as regards its qualitative characteristics (Box 5.3). The frequent and major increase or reduction of the size of the fleet may have a negative effect on the level of management and its performance, particularly in areas which are assessed by the charterers of its vessels (on the TMSA programme, see Chapter 9).

144

C O N T E X T U A L FA C TO R S O F O R G A N I S AT I O N A L S T R U C T U R E

BOX 5.2 Fleet size, asset play and organisation of the company Evangelos Pistiolis, Managing Director of Top Ships (www.topships.org), a shipping company listed on the Nasdaq stock market, in an interview on the issue of buying or selling ships, stressed that, while in general the approach is to buy ships when the market is lowering and to sell when the market is good, the application of such a strategy has limits, which are due to the fact that there is a need for the shipping company to have a continuity in its operation. Charterers, especially those in the tanker sector, are seeking to work with companies controlled by owners devoted to the market, who can be trusted, and not companies adopting opportunistic approach, based on the prices of ships. For Pistiolis, thirty ships drop to 25, but 15 don’t drop to ten, because then you become small, and you lose the name you’ve been building for so long, in order to earn 5–10 m. dollars. And the charterers become even more difficult and demanding. (Pistiolis, 2004)

A small company which is active in the dry bulk markets, maintaining departments which have to do with its basic processes, particularly operation, technical matters and accounting, is able, usually at the expense and through the creativity of the owner, to answer to the needs of its operation (Theotokas, 1997). Small companies manifest a series of particularities which relate to their organisation and management. At the stage of setting up and survival, the owner of the company concerns himself with the whole of its jobs and activities (Churchill & Lewis, 1983). This is because of the fact that in most cases the owner of a company is experienced in the object of its activity. In bulk shipping, there are companies which were set up either by merchant marine officers, or by executives of shipping companies, or by members of shipowning families who have decided to branch out on their own.1 The owner may be a single source of management until the company has more than ten employees. From that point on, it has to take on personnel for the co-ordination and control of the work of others. The ownership and certain aspects of the day to day management then become separate (Keasy & Watson, 1993). The ratio of the size of the company to the extent of its organisational structure results from the organisation charts shown in the Figures 5.2 to 5.6, which follow.2

From a careful observation of the above organisation charts, it can be concluded that as the size of a company increases, so does the horizontal and vertical complexity of its structure. A shipping company with a fleet of four ships (Figure 5.2) is seen to maintain only departments which deal with basic processes of the management of the ships and with financial management. A shipping company with a fleet of 12 ships (Figure 5.3) has departments which handle almost all the processes which were analysed in Chapter 3, as does the company which manages 19 ships and has ten departments (Figure 5.5). And a shipping company which manages 114 ships (Figure 5.6) shows greater horizontal and vertical complexity. The size of the fleet and the variety of its activities lead to the grouping of the departments into divisions, a measure which adds one further hierarchical level to the organisation chart.

❚ Level of technology – Specialisation of fleet technology: the productive process of an organisation which includes work procedures and mechanical equipment.

Technology is the productive process of an organisation which includes work procedures and mechanical equipment (Daft, 2010). Technology involves “the acting on and/or changing an object from one state to another. . . The object can be living being, a symbol, or an inanimate object” (Goodman et al, 1990). Research interest in the relation between technology and the organisational structure stems from the works of Joan Woodward (Pugh & Hickson, 1989). The observations on which her analysis was based were derived from research into industrial units, but the basic relations which emerged raised the issue of the relation between the level of technology and the organisational structure and of the need to combine the two, which was subsequently confirmed by later research. Service companies such as shipping companies, which provide maritime transportation services (or door-to-door integrated transportation services in the case of liner shipping companies), manifest important differences from industrial companies as concerns their structural and technical characteristics. In the case of service companies:

❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚

the product is non-material; production and consumption take place simultaneously; the role of the labour and knowledge of the workforce is important; there is interaction with the customer; the human factor plays a very significant role; the quality of the service is easily perceived but difficult to measure; a rapid response to the customer’s requirements is considered essential; the location of establishment is particularly important (Daft, 2010).

Of course, in the case of shipping companies, which belong within the category of companies providing services, there are features and characteristics in their operation which refer us to the secondary sector of the economy, as, for example, the high formalisation and the emphasis on technical skills of the human resources. The ship itself, as a productive unit, shows correspondences with a production factory. It is a management pyramid with many levels of hierarchy. Traditionally, the structures on ships are hierarchical and remain such because of the nature of the work on board. In spite of the fact that the organisation of the ship is small in size,3 it is marked by the existence of many hierarchical levels. If, then, we look at the shipping company as a system, we can suppose that the infrastructure subsystem on land shows chiefly the characteristics of service companies, while its ships manifest the characteristics and the corresponding technology of companies producing products. At the same time, the technology which the ship embodies, that is, its type, its equipment and the nature of the cargo which it can carry, is likely, in each case, to increase the complexity of its organisational structure by including additional specialisations for the handling of specialist jobs. The technology of vessels managed by a company, like the technology which it incorporates into the operation of its infrastructure on land, to some extent determines both the size and the basic dimensions of its organisational structure.

148

C O N T E X T U A L FA C TO R S O F O R G A N I S AT I O N A L S T R U C T U R E

The complexity of an organisational structure which is designed to support the operation of specialised vessels, such as those transporting chemicals or liquefied gases, differs from that of a shipping company which manages the same number of bulk carriers (Box 5.4). The level of automation of each type of vessel, which is determined by factors such as safety requirements and advanced functionality, as well as the level of logistics required by its management, which involves factors such as supply chain management, regularity requirements and extension to land-based transport service, can serve as criteria for the analysis of the shipping industry markets (Rensvik et al, 2003) (Box 5.3). Segments with high automation and logistics

BOX 5.3 Technological specialisation and maritime markets The maritime market may be characterized into low-end and high-end market segments dependent on complexity in automation and logistics and ship management (see Figure 5.7). The automation complexity is defined in terms of number of input and outputs, advanced functionality, safety requirements, etc. Similar the logistics and ship management complexity are characterized by supply chain management, size of fleet, regularity requirements, customer relations and trade, cargo handling, extension to land-based transport service, etc. Within logistics and ship transport it is a trend that traditionally low-end players expand their business to total integrated logistics chain (door-to-door services). It is also seen that the automation degree and the introduction of on-board software systems increase (Rensvik et al, 2003:10–11).4

complexity are characterised as those which involve a high number of inputs and outputs and complicated operations. It appears to be the case that the more specialised a market is (on the basis of the type of ship which it includes), the higher is the level observable as regards automation, logistics and the management of the vessels. It is to be expected that these characteristics are taken into account in the organisation of the company. Bulk carriers and tankers belong in the markets with a lower level both in the matter of automation and in that of logistics and management. Vessels transporting containers are listed low down as regards automation and high in relation to logistics and management. Specialised vessels, such as those carrying chemicals, belong in the middle in terms of automation and high up in relation to logistics and management, whereas even more specialised vessels transporting liquefied gas (which also operate on the principle of ‘one ship – one cargo’) are high on the list as regards automation and low in terms of logistics and management.

BOX 5.4 The transition of a traditional company managing tankers and bulk carriers to the specialist market of LNG vessels GasLog (www.gaslogltd.com) entered the liquefied natural gas (LNG) market by way of its collaboration with British Gas (BG), undertaking the management of two of the latter’s ships (under the name of Ceres Hellenic Enterprises). This was the first Greek company to enter this specialised market, which is the strictest in matters of safety (Theotokas & Harlaftis, 2009). To begin with, the vessels were included in the company’s fleet, the first two being manned by experienced officers from Spain who were already serving on them under the company which had been managing up till then. Subsequently, the company chose experienced officers who had been serving up to then on its chemicals vessels and used Spanish officers to train them. At the same time, the people at Ceres collaborated closely with those of the BG group on the improvement of the processes and procedures, and on the response to the demands of managing LNGs. They realised in the course of this that this effort would bring better results if they created a new company whose exclusive task was the management of the specific vessels. This is how Ceres LNG Services resulted. The process of the transition of the company from the mentality and logic of the traditional manager of bulk carriers and tankers to being a leading manager of LNG vessels is of interest. This process, as described by Theodoros Katemidis, the company’s Managing Director (Surveyor, 2008), called for careful planning, which included the re-training of the personnel and a change of mentality throughout the organisation, from the lowest-ranking crew member to the technical staff and the top management on land. In parallel, the collaboration of the Ceres LNG people with the British Gas group was yet another means for the acquisition by their side not only of

150

C O N T E X T U A L FA C TO R S O F O R G A N I S AT I O N A L S T R U C T U R E

the different technology for the management of the LNGs, but of the different mindset which this calls for. As regards the latter, what is required is a change in the way of thinking, with dedication to the rules and good planning. The ship’s handbook covers every operational detail. Every time that the vessel heads to port for loading or discharging, a compatibility study is drawn up so that the officers and crew know exactly what the terminal expects of them and of the vessel. This is a market in which good preparation and the avoidance to the greatest possible extent of surprises are required. On the ship itself there are important specialisations, such as the LNG cargo engineer and the electronic engineer, which have to do with the need for special handling of the cargo and of the technology (electronics, computers, automations) which the vessel embodies in its operation. One of the company’s ships may have on board approximately 4,000 sensors and 20 computers which monitor the pressure, the temperature and the conditions inside the individual cargo tanks, in the engine-room and in the system for handling the cargo. The vessel’s extra demands bring the company to the implementation of an extensive training programme for its seafarers, which includes two months of training during the building of a vessel, and annual programmes and 40 days’ training on board the ship before taking up their duties (Surveyor, 2008). The approach which has been followed by the company in order to maintain high operational and safety standards includes, apart from training, incentives and career prospects, which have enabled the company to achieve high rates of retention of its employees, which for 2016 were 95 per cent for senior sea-going officers, 98 per cent for other sea-going officers and 99 per cent for shore staff (GasLog, 2017a). In the course of events, GasLog has expanded from the management of LNG vessels into ownership. In 2012, its shares were introduced on the Nasdaq stock market. In recent years, it has developed further by the purchase of LNG vessels, creating a fleet (April 2017) of 12 vessels in its own ownership, five ships on order, 10 ships belonging to partners of the company and three which are under its management (GasLog, 2017b), which are employed mostly on Long-Term Charters (up to ten years). Continuing the tradition of the Livanos family in innovation,5 the ships on order include innovative characteristics (DNV-GL, 2014). It has some 1,500 employees on board the ships and in its offices in New York, London, Monaco, Athens and Singapore, as well as in the new buildings site in Busan – South Korea (GasLog, 2017a).

The technology which a shipping company incorporates into the operation of its offices on land may vary the extent and basic dimensions of its organisational structure, as well as the profile and the capacity of its personnel. The installation of high-technology equipment and the relevant systems on board its vessels and in its offices permits the assignment of jobs to the personnel of the vessels and reduces the need for staff in the offices, since some of these jobs

151
