“In a Fourth Wave shipping company I can well imagine a team of these bright maritime analysts developing sophisticated numerical tools and iPhone style apps which improve every aspect of the company’s maritime transport operation. It’s cheap compared with the $90m cost of a VLCC” (Stopford, 2015).

digitalisation: the integration of digital technologies to all aspects of shipping companies’ operation.

“Some describe digitalisation as a change of mindset and corporate culture, rather than IT investments. That’s why only those who are willing to adopt a new mentality have a better chance of surviving. That might be the most difficult part. Human behaviours only change gradually” (Lin, 2017).

152

are now carried out electronically. It has in this way contributed to the reduction of their complexity, as it leads to the creation of more flat organisations with fewer hierarchical levels and a greater depth of authorisation for the taking of decisions in personnel of lower rank. Information can be available to the whole of the personnel, a factor which facilitates the taking of decisions and increases the range of control of management executives. At the same time, the IT infrastructure favours team work, while better access to communications by the use of satellites and the Internet reduces the complexity resulting from the geographical dispersion of the companies’ activities. The level of technology may differ within each company, as there are differing needs and requirements in the individual processes. For example, the processes of a shipping company which have to do with accounting and the management of supplies can be regarded as being based on jobs with low variation, clear rules and specific procedures. The organisation of the departments which carry out these processes may be marked by high formalisation, a broad extent of control, centralisation and vertical communication. On the other hand, processes which are connected with the technical and operation management of the vessels may be marked both by high variation, since the frequency of new and unexpected events which may be encountered is great, and by lower formalisation. The organisation of the corresponding departments is characterised by horizontal communication, a limited range of control and a lesser degree of formalisation and centralisation.6 The tendency towards digitalisation in maritime transport, i.e. the integration of digital technologies to all aspects of shipping companies’ operation, which has been encouraged by inter-state authorities such as the European Commission, will increase the importance of the technology factor, as a contingency factor which affects the organisational structures of firms. This development will affect the shipping industry overall, but it is estimated that its implementation will have greater intensity in liner shipping, where the number of interactions which involve documentation that could be digitalised is considerable and includes many different organisations throughout the length of the transport chain (Porter, 2017). Digitalisation is not a new trend. Many companies years ago used digital technology to transform their business models. However, the low freight rates and the big losses that many liner and dry bulk companies suffered in recent years, especially during 2016, were strong motives for transformations based on digital technologies, initially within container shipping and at a later stage within bulk shipping (Lin, 2017). Digitalisation is a part of the transformation which is expected to take place in liner shipping, which will affect the existing models of organisation of companies. It should be noted, nevertheless, that digitalisation should not be examined as an IT project, but as a change which will require of companies «to embrace process management as the core way of governing the business» (Jensen, 2017). Digitalisation is described as a change of mind-set and corporate culture and not simply as IT project, which means that it demands the adoption of a new mentality. In that sense, it will be a long trial-and-error process, with some jobs becoming obsolete and new ones emerging (Lin, 2017). This change will require changes in the nature and organisation of jobs and,

C O N T E X T U A L FA C TO R S O F O R G A N I S AT I O N A L S T R U C T U R E

consequently, in the organisation of the companies itself. Recently, market reports and announcements show that the major liner companies are going ahead with collaborations with specialised companies with a view to being in a position to respond to the challenges of the new technological environment, as, for example, Maersk,7 which collaborates with online platforms for marketing space on its ships or with IBM in order to use blockchain technology to digitalise paperwork related to the global supply chain process, to improve efficiency and optimise costs, and CMA CGM, which set a venture capital fund to spend 2 m. euros a year on investing in digital start-ups related to container shipping business (Porter, 2017). Indicative of the organisational transformations that companies pursue to become ready to respond to the challenges of digitalisation is the decision of Wilh. Wilhelmsen to launch a digital trainee programme, which has been designed to strengthen the Group’s digital capabilities and focus, by recruiting and training digital talents for a period of 18 months (Wilh. Wilhelmsen, 2017). Digitalisation at the level of industry raises the issue of digital trust, which is unaddressed in the industry, as well as the issue of learning how to share information and how to do business out of it. Investments in gaining information is one issue and sharing this information and making use of the results is another (SMI, 2016). In the view of Martin Stopford, the digital technology necessary for the transition to digital shipping already exists to a satisfactory extent and is expected to improve constantly over the coming years. He attributes the problem, however, “to the business model running through shipping cycles”, which is not suitable a platform for the introduction of the new technology, and proposes three ways for it to change, all based on technology. These are smart ships which use technology to manage the operation and maintenance of sophisticated systems, smart fleet management, i.e., the transition from independent small business units to a large integrated company, and smart global logistics which integrate door-to door transport (Stopford, 2017).

blockchain: a shared ledger for recording the history of transactions – that cannot be altered (IBM, 2017).

❚ The ‘external environment’ factor The dimensions of an organisation are in a state of composite interdependence both with one another and with the conditions of the environment. There is an important relationship between the degree of certainty and variation of the environment and the degree of differentiation and integration of an organisation (Lawrence & Lorsch, 1967). Companies have to examine and analyse the degree of uncertainty and change in the environment and adjust accordingly the dimensions of their organisational structure. Burns and Stalker distinguish two types of environment, the mechanistic and the organic, and the corresponding structures in the companies which operate in each of these (Figure 5.8) (Burns, 1990). Mechanistic structure is based on rules, policies and procedures. In this, techniques for taking decisions are predetermined, while the control systems are

mechanistic structure: structure based on rules, policies and procedures.

153

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

organic structure: structure that facilitates innovation and adaptation to the changing demands.

skilfully structured. As a structure, it compels management to place emphasis on the usual procedures, in the pursuit of efficiency. Comprehensive mechanistic structures resemble machines. Organic structures, on the other hand, are not formalised, and are based less on procedures. They seek adaptation, and for that reason, jobs within them are interdependent and alter continually. Communication is vertical and horizontal, and includes briefing and consultancy. Organic structures resemble living organisms, because they are innovative and can adapt to the changing demands of the environment. Because of their flexibility, they do not manifest stability – the predominant characteristic in mechanistic structures which allows them to carry out the usual jobs effectively. In a shipping company, structures which match the mechanistic and the organic structure co-exist, with the former being implemented on the ships, and the latter, chiefly, in the companies on land. On the vessels a relatively strict structure is adopted, with high formalisation, which, as has already been pointed out, results in the introduction of rules and the regulation of matters connected with safety and the protection of the environment, but also of centralisation in the taking of decisions, of the hierarchy, of roles, etc. In the company’s offices on land, the structure may show fewer hierarchical levels, with less difference between them, the taking of decisions may be less centralised, interaction may not be vertical, co-ordination may not stem from the hierarchy, etc. In companies

Figure 5.8 Characteristics of mechanistic and organic structures

154

C O N T E X T U A L FA C TO R S O F O R G A N I S AT I O N A L S T R U C T U R E

of this type, the organisation is characterised not by the position of the individuals of whom it is composed, but by the nature of the task which they are called upon to carry out. Of course, this does not mean that the mechanistic structure is not applicable to the organisation of the companies on land, since many of these are organised on the basis of the specific structure, thus favouring centralisation and hierarchy. As will be explained below, in these companies organisational culture, which cultivates such characteristics and is reminiscent more of bureaucratic organisation, prevails. The organisational structure of a company may not be describable as totally mechanistic or organismic, but as positioned at any point on a mechanistic/ organismic structure continuum, combining features of both (Carrell et al, 1997).

❚ The ‘strategy’ factor From the point where a company has determined its aims and objectives, the next step is to define the strategy which it will follow for their realisation and the way in which it will organise its processes. The strategy of a company can be examined on three levels (Figure 5.9):

❚ ❚ ❚

strategy: the definition of the basic long-term aims and plans of action, for the achievement of these aims.

on the corporate strategy level: this strategy involves the company’s aim and the choice of the activities (the business portfolio) which it will develop to realise it; on the competitive or business strategy level: this concerns the company’s competition in specific markets in which it is active; on the functional strategies level: this concerns the way in which the different processes of the company contribute to the corporate strategies and the competitive strategies.

Each strategy should be supported by the appropriate organisational structure and the internal systems of the company. The extent of the company’s activities and the various markets in which it participates with its vessels determine the level of complexity in its operation and give rise to the need for a clear definition of the strategy, the structure and the processes which have to do with each activity (Lorange, 2005). In the event, for example, of a company implementing a complex strategy which concentrates on many markets, good co-ordination and speed in decisionmaking – that is, centralisation of final decision making – are necessary (Lorange, 2005). As will be explained in the next chapter, these are characteristics which are encountered in more traditional organisational structures (Johnson, 1985). Views on the relation between strategy and organisation and the degree to which the one precedes and determines the other differ. The prevailing view is that the organisational structure should follow the strategy and support it. After the company has chosen the strategy which it will follow, it has to take decisions on roles, responsibilities and the allocation of resources, decisions which will

155

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

Figure 5.9 The levels of strategy Source: Compiled by the author, based on the analysis of Box 4.5.

contribute to the implementation of this strategy (Chandler, 1962). In the light of the above approach, the strategy (what the company plans to implement), the structure (the way in which the different parts of the company are organised in order to apply the plan which arises from the strategy) and the systems (the internal lines of communication which transmit information) complement each other in shaping the capabilities of the company. These capabilities determine the kind of opportunities which arise by way of the environment and of which the company can take advantage (Boyce & Ville, 2005). When the strategy of the company changes, corresponding changes must also be made in its structure. Given, however, that the environment of companies (including shipping companies) is dynamic and complex, the view that strategy constitutes a rational plan of the managing executives cannot be regarded as predominant. On the contrary, strategy should be seen as arising from the company’s efforts to remain competitive. In practice, the structure of the organisation

156

C O N T E X T U A L FA C TO R S O F O R G A N I S AT I O N A L S T R U C T U R E

Figure 5.10 Strategy and structure

can also contribute to the creation of the strategy (Brooks, 1999). There are also cases where the strategy and the structure can change simultaneously in order to adapt to the demands of the company and of the environment in which it operates (Grant & Cibin, 1996). In the end, a reciprocal relationship develops between the strategy and the structure in which the structure is derived from the strategy, or follows it. Nevertheless, from the time of its creation, it may influence the implementation of the strategy, as well as its future course (Figure 5.10). The case of the Maersk Line shipping company (Box 4.5) is indicative of the dynamic relationship between strategy and structure. Maersk, adopting a strategy of expansion, went ahead with the acquisition of P&O Nedlloyd. A new organisational structure resulted from the amalgamation of the two companies. An assessment of the effectiveness of this new structure led to the development of a new strategy, which, in its turn, led to a re-articulation of the structure of the unified company. The restructuring included a reduction in the number of jobs, the decentralisation of decision-making, the optimisation of procedures, the simplification of the structure and the creation of a management team with personnel from other industries. The restructuring was followed by a period characterised by organic (internal) development, and internal improvements. Through the assessment of investment challenges, Maersk arrived at the adoption of a new strategy, a basic feature of which is the combination of organic and inorganic growth, re-organisation and the search for synergies across the transport sector of the company (Figure 5.11). The successful course followed by a company is a function of the degree to which it achieves strategic fit with its environment and supports its strategy with appropriately planned organisational structures and management processes (Miles & Snow, 1990). Strategic fit is a dynamic process by which the alignment of the organisation with its environment and the arrangement of its resources in its interior to support this alignment are sought. The basic mechanism of alignment is the strategy, while internal arrangements are the organisational structure and the management procedures. Even though strategic fit seems self-evident, it is, nevertheless, difficult to achieve. Strategies at various levels (i.e., corporate, business, functional) give rise to the limitations on the design and implementation of structure. One can easily think of the influence that alternative choices for investment strategy or for chartering strategies may have with regard to the horizontal and vertical complexity of the structure. In the case of large diversified groups, the approach adopted may be different for different organisational or business units, taking into account that other dimensional factors may be also different for each unit. In particular units, the strategy implemented may require structure that supports cost efficiency, control and formalisation, while for others flexible horizontal communication may be critical.

organisational culture: a pattern of basic assumptions, invented, discovered or developed by a group as it learns to cope with its problems of external adaptation and internal integration.

❚ Organisational culture One of the factors which determine the organisational structure of a company is the culture within it, which results from values, convictions, hypotheses, norms and ways of interpreting and understanding situations. It provides a kind of identity for the members of the organisation, reinforces their commitment to the realisation of its aims and makes clear the models of behaviour which will predominate among them. Various levels of culture, which interact, can be identified (Figure 5.13). First there is the individual culture, that is, the characteristics shared by the members of a group which forms a sub-total of a broader group. Second there is the professional culture, that is, the characteristics of the individuals who practise a profession and who have usually received a similar training. Third is the organisational culture, that is to say the totality of individuals belonging to an organisation.

Figure 5.13 Levels of culture Source: Compiled by the author, based on (Brooks, 1999: Figure 9.2).

160

C O N T E X T U A L FA C TO R S O F O R G A N I S AT I O N A L S T R U C T U R E

Fourth is the culture of the sector, that is, the norms prevailing in it and contributing to the determination of reactions to its organisations. The fifth level of culture is the national culture, that is, the cultural characteristics of individuals of the same nationality and the sixth is the supranational culture – the characteristics shared by individuals from different national cultures. Organisational culture is a pattern of basic assumptions, invented, discovered or developed by a given group, as it learns to cope with its problems of external adaptation and internal integration, that has worked well enough to be considered valid and, therefore is to be taught to new members as the correct way to perceive, think, and feel in relation to those problems. (Schein, 1990: 111) In the shaping of the culture of an organisation, the role of its founder predominates. Usually, the person who takes the initiative of setting up a company chooses associates with whom he shares the same views, thus creating its nucleus. The principal associates have a shared vision, invest in it and work for its realisation. As the company advances, new people join it and adopt the approaches and behaviours of the dominant group, thus creating the common culture (see Box 5.6). The organisational culture can be analysed in the light of (Robbins & Coulter, 2002):

❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚

attention to detail: the degree to which employees are excepted to show accuracy, skill in analysis, and attention to detail; orientation towards the result: the degree to which the managers concentrate on the results or the outflows, and not on the procedure or the way in which these results are achieved; orientation towards individuals: the degree to which the managers, in taking decisions, assess the impacts which these will have on the individuals in the organisation; orientation towards the team: the degree to which work is organised on the basis of teams and not individuals; aggressiveness: the degree to which employees are more aggressive and competitive than co-operative; stability: the degree to which decisions and actions as to organisation stress the importance of maintaining the status quo; innovation and risk-taking: the degree to which employees are encouraged to be innovative and to undertake risk.

The extent to which each of the above characteristics makes its appearance defines the form which each dimension of the organisational structure will take. For example, a company orientated towards the team, innovation and risktaking will have a low degree of centralisation and formalisation. Conversely, a company with a culture orientated towards detail and stability will have an organisational structure with a high degree of centralisation and formalisation.

161

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

Types of organisational culture Various typologies have been proposed for the classification of companies on the basis of their organisational culture. In terms of the typology of Harrison and Handy (Handy, 1993), companies can be divided into four categories in the light of the structural characteristics which predominate in their culture: power culture, which is symbolised as a web; role culture, symbolised as an ancient temple; task culture, symbolised as a matrix, and person culture, symbolised as a hive (Figure 5.14). Each type of organisational culture differs as to the characteristics described above. Power culture places greater emphasis on people and the outcome, role culture on detail and stability and task culture on the team, innovation and outcome. In the world of companies, the power, role and task cultures appear with greater frequency. Each form of organisational culture supports and is supported by a specific organisational structure and departmentalisation. Power culture is often observable in small companies and is dependent upon one central source of power. Companies which are based on this are capable of moving quickly and of reacting successfully to threats. The basic factor of this culture is the strong leader who takes the decisions and controls their implementation. Role culture is frequently likened to bureaucracy, in which the role of the individuals is more important than the individuals themselves who perform it. The holding of a post is the basic source of power, while rules and procedures are the main methods of co-ordination. Role culture is not quick to apprehend the need for change and does not change easily. Task culture is orientated towards work or the task. It seeks to assemble, together with the necessary resources, suitable individuals to complete the task. It has exceptional adaptability, is suitable for companies which operate in flexible and changeable markets and favours change and innovation. In the case of person culture, the organisation exists to serve the individuals of whom it is made up.

Organisational culture and dimensions of the organisation What form the culture of a company will take is a function of its size, ownership and aims, as well as of the environment in which it operates, and of the people who staff it. For example, the fact that the power culture is prevalent in most small and medium-size dry cargo bulk shipping companies is owing to their family character, the leading role played by their founder in their organisation and management and to the extensive employment of family members. The model of the family business has an important or predominating role in the shipping companies of numerous countries, particularly in those which have traditional structures. As has already been pointed out, in a company or an organisation there is a dominant culture, which is the totality of the values shared by most of the members, and one or more sub-cultures (Luthans, 1995), each of which is a totality of values shared by a few members. A sub-culture may be shaped in the efforts of a group to handle effectively the problems which they are faced with. The existence of a sub-culture is usually connected with factors which unite some members of a company, such as their education or specialisation. In the case of shipping companies, for example, the specialisation of those working in the offices may lead to the embracing of different values and, by extension, different attitudes and behaviours in the workplace. This is observable at many levels, each of which involves groups of employees. A first level is that of the previous work experience and concerns employees coming from the seafaring profession and those who have experience in other branches or have no previous experience. If we go one level further down, we shall see that in the ranks of former seafarers a distinction is observable between deck officers and engine-room officers. Among non-seafarers, there may be distinctions which have to do with what the subject of their studies was. The professional culture of each employee, that is to say, determines to some extent the terms on which he is integrated into an organisational culture. The different sub-cultures are likely to shape specific conditions in the co-ordination and interaction of the individuals associated with them; that is, at the level of departments, there are likely to be differences as to organisation. The technical department, for example, in which numerous personnel who have trained as engineers are employed, may have co-ordination mechanisms (see p. 173) which differ from those of the accounts office, where personnel who have studied economics are chiefly employed. Many shipping companies intervene to facilitate the formation of the dominant culture using various available tools, which at the same time facilitate knowledge dissemination within the organisations. In this category belongs the company’s magazine, which provides members with news about the company and advances the image the company wishes to pass to its members. These are publications addressed mostly to seafarers. In most of these magazines there are sections with news about the company’s activities and fleet, new approaches to managing ships, employees’ achievements, employees’ profiles and interviews, new arrivals and relocations of employees, as well as other topics of interest, mainly for seafarers. Companies that publish magazines at regular intervals are,

for example, Odfjell (Odfjell Quarterly Magazine), Wilh. Wilhelmsen (WWWorld), Maersk (Maersk Post), Danaos Shipping (The Danship News), the MOL Group (Open Sea), Rickmers (aroundtheglobe) and Norden (Norden News). Others, for example, Maersk (Maersk, 2017), MSC (MSC, 2017) and Vroon (Box 5.5), make explicit statements published on their websites of the culture that prevails in their organisation. Know-how is linked to the professional culture, that is, to the training and professional experiences of the employees. In a successful shipping company, it is necessary that there should be four categories of know-how/professional culture: market culture (ability to understand the functions of the shipping markets), operations culture (know-how for executing processes necessary for delivering an efficient transportation service), business analysis culture (know-how for pursuing niche-based strategies) and financial culture (ability to manage financial issues) (Lorange, 2005). Where the predominant culture is strong, even those members who espouse values of the sub-culture also espouse values of the predominant one. Otherwise, the sub-culture may undermine the effectiveness and cohesion which result from the predominant culture. The stronger the organisational culture is, the greater will be the cohesion of the company. Nevertheless, culture is not imposed, but, rather, develops during the process of social interaction (Morgan, 2000). The more the company grows and develops, the more its culture is modified. Organisational culture does not change easily, because it exists on many levels, penetrates all the organisation’s activities, is regarded as self-evident and given for its members, has deep historical roots, possesses a political dimension, and has to do with the distribution of power in the organisation, shows pluralism, since it is made up of many sub-cultures, and gives rise to interdependences with its structure and systems (Pettigrew, 1990). In Box 5.6, Stelios Haji-Ioannou speaks of the difficulties which were encountered in the efforts to change the manner of operation of a company well-established in the market, with a dominating position in its branch (Troodos Shipping), whose founder continued to be a leading figure. This difficulty in making the change rendered the organisational culture a crucial factor for the success of mergers or acquisitions, since where there is no compatibility of culture between the companies involved, the effectiveness of their consolidated operation is undermined. For this reason, a factor which is taken into consideration before the taking of the decision on acquisitions, mergers or collaborations is their compatibility at the level of organisation and organisational culture (Box 5.7). Furthermore, culture is one of the factors which can contribute to the successful implementation of a new strategy, or can, alternatively, undermine it, especially when it conflicts with basic assumptions and values held by important figures in the company.

BOX 5.5 Statements of the company’s organisational culture Many shipping companies make statements of the culture that prevails in their organisation. This kind of statement makes known to stakeholders what are the core values that characterise each company’s organisational culture, and at the same time commit the members of the company to respect and comply with those values as they carry out their duties. The Vroon Group B.V. (www.vroon.nl), for example, which operates and manages a diversified fleet of approximately 200 vessels and is active in many business segments, publishes on its website the company’s culture, i.e., the values that guide the “Vroon way” (Vroon, 2017):8 Safety culture We believe safety is a ‘way of being’ and a mindset. We commit to safe practices in everyday actions, more than compliance to form-filling. The Vroon way is to show our duty of care for wellbeing and safety of our colleagues and those we serve. Team Culture We believe we are stronger together. The Vroon way is to involve others – as equals – in working through challenges and get them implemented. We role-model the Vroon culture and will keep each other accountable for this. Trust and communication culture We believe everyone has a right to be treated with respect. It is NOT the Vroon way to bad-mouth or embarrass colleagues intentionally or unintentionally. We will each do our best, give our best and expect the best from each other. Performance culture We believe in operational excellence, whilst keeping things simple and sober. The Vroon way is to recognise, encourage and celebrate each other’s small daily wins – so together we achieve more. Culture of discipline We believe each one of us is accountable for delivering what we say we will. No excuses! The Vroon way is to know our business and numbers inside out. We get and provide accurate information. We set clear goals, learn from our mistakes and continuously improve the way we work. Customer-partnership culture We believe in caring about our customers and business partners. In a world of ‘sameness’ and uniformity this gives us an edge. The Vroon way is to build lasting relationships. We demonstrate this in our day-to-day contacts.

BOX 5.6 An established mode of operation and efforts to change it Stelios Haji-Ioannou (1998:20) describes in an interview9 with the Argo journal his experience of playing a part in the Troodos Shipping family company. This experience led him to the decision to withdraw from it and set up his own company – Stelmar Shipping, which operated successfully until 2004, when it was acquired by the OSG company (www.osg.com). What were your first steps in your father’s business like? For a young man who was starting out working in the business of his father – and, moreover, of a successful and dynamic father, one who was, I would say, a little absolutist in management – life was not easy. In these cases, you have to prove your worth to him and his associates. As you realise, this was a company which was already 30 years old, there were people who had been with my father for 30 years; in terms of time, they knew him better than I did; they had the same mentality and followed the same way of working. For example, there was a total absence of computers in the company – and we must have been at that time the biggest company which didn’t use computers not only in Greece, but in the whole world. I can remember that at the offices in B’ Merarchias St, there was a floor filled with accountants who kept the books by hand. A company with 40 ships keeping its books by hand! In spite of all that, it made a profit. The British say that ‘You can’t argue with success’. If someone is making a profit, you can’t tell him he’s getting it wrong – just that the ideas are outdated. How did you react to this environment? I tried to bring to it something new. It is true to say that my father gave me the scope, I had the title of Chief Executive, I can’t say that I had anything to complain about, but at some point I realised that it was impossible to change the company’s profile as radically as I would have wished. And what did you do then? I considered that it would be easier for me, and for the success of my efforts, to start something off from a new beginning. It was my belief that it would be easier to create a company on new foundations than to change the existing situation at Troodos, given that my father felt that he still had what it takes and wanted to go on controlling the company. So there was a generation gap, which made it necessary either to moderate my radical views and become simply my father’s employee, taking orders, or to do something of my own from the beginning.

One of the factors which facilitated the decision of the two companies was compatibility in their organisational culture. In the words of Lennart Simonsson, Managing Director of Brostrom, there is a perfect match between our two companies. ❚ Introduction

departmentalisation: the division of the totality of the work into specialised jobs and the organisation of those jobs into separate departments.

The need for co-ordination of tasks leads to their grouping and to their enrolment into departments. This chapter examines the process of the departmentalisation of activities as a basic method of co-ordination and of organisation, and analyses its basic forms and its application in the case of shipping companies. Departmentalisation is the division of the totality of the work into specialised jobs and the organisation of these jobs into separate departments. The objectives sought by departmentalisation are effective management, co-ordination, the achievement of synergy and economies of scale, the effective use of resources, co-operation and job satisfaction on the part of employees.

BOX 6.1 Co-ordination mechanisms of the organisational structure Co-ordination of activities and integrated operation are requirements for any organisation. The issue of co-ordination and the mechanisms by which it is achieved are at the very centre of interest of the configuration approach (Mintzberg, 1996). Every organisation consists of six basic parts. At its base is the operating core,

172

D E PA RT M E N TA L I S AT I O N I N S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

consisting of the individuals who carry out the basic work of the organisation, that is, the process of undertaking the production of the product or the provision of a service by the organisation. At its top is the strategic apex, the individuals responsible for the strategic management and the co-ordination of the rest of the organisation’s parts. In the middle is the middle line, the personnel who mediate between the strategic apex and the operating core and are responsible for the implementation of the strategy and co-ordination at operational or departmental level. As the organisation grows and becomes more complex, individuals enter it who are concerned with the standardisation of the organisation’s procedures in its various processes and provide specialised services to the strategic apex and the middle line, though they lie outside the line of the hierarchy (engineers, researchers, etc.). Technological equipment and employees in this team make up the technostructure or technical support and help the organisation to change and to adapt to the demands of the environment. Innovations derive from this category of personnel. At the same time, there is one further category, outside the line of the hierarchy, which supplies support services to the rest of the organisation: the support personnel. Included in the support services are the secretariat, legal support, human resources management, public relations, etc. Every active organisation also has a sixth part, which does not consist of people: culture/ideology. This is what makes the organisation distinctive, while it surrounds and at the same time penetrates all its teams and imbues its structure. The co-ordination of the various teams is achieved by the following mechanisms (Mintzberg, 1996): 1. Mutual adjustment. This achieves co-ordination by the simple procedure of informal communication. Individuals interact in order to co-operate.

co-ordination mechanisms: the mechanisms which permit the co-ordinated operation of the basic parts of the organisation.

2\. Direct supervision. Comes into operation when a minimum number of individuals have entered the organisation, so that it is no longer possible for mutual adjustment to operate effectively, and it is necessary for one employee (supervisor) to co-ordinate the others (subordinates). 3. Standardisation of work processes. This involves the definition of the content of the job, and of the procedures which are to be followed. Each job is standardised, and its relations with the rest of the jobs are regulated and co-ordinated by procedures and regulations. 4. Standardisation of outputs. The definition of the output which is expected to result from the job or from working teams and departments. In this way the intercorrelations between the jobs and the expected outflows are defined.

173

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

5\. Standardisation of skills. This is a looser means of achieving co-ordination. Here the standardisation involves the employee and the knowledge which he applies in his work. This standardisation takes place outside the organisation (at a university or professional association) before the employee embarks upon his job. 6. Standardisation of norms. The employees share a totality of convictions by means of which the co-ordination of organisation is achieved. These mechanisms keep the organisation united. As the organisation operates, it becomes more complex and there is a transition from the more simple co-ordination mechanism to the more complex. However, it cannot be based on only one co-ordination mechanism. On the contrary, all the co-ordination mechanisms will co-exist, though one of these will predominate and characterise its organisation. The predominant co-ordination mechanism may differ at the different stages of the life of the company. In a small company, mutual adaptation can function effectively. When a company develops in size and in terms of the number of its employees, it is likely that mutual adaptation will predominate in the interior of its specific departments, but overall in the company co-ordination is achieved through direct supervision. When it expands into new activities, each of which requires its own particular organisation, standardisation of outflows may predominate in the company as a whole, whereas within each activity, depending upon size and other characteristics, mutual adaptation or direct supervision may be predominant. Shipping companies are organisations which, by reason of the nature of their activities, rely on different co-ordination mechanisms at one and the same time. Ships, for example, rely upon hierarchical organisation, but their effective operation cannot be achieved in all conditions by direct supervision only. In each particular instance, other co-ordination mechanisms may prove useful. The organisations of the companies on land, depending upon the process which they carry out, may also be based on different co-ordination mechanisms.

The basic criteria for the departmentalisation of a company are: a)

b)

174

Interdependence of jobs. The carrying out of the task of a job is dependent upon carrying out of the task of another. Jobs and processes or activities with a high level of interdependence are grouped in the same department so that their co-ordination is achieved. Specialisation of jobs. Jobs with the same or related specialisation are placed in the same department so that their effective management is achieved and synergies are created. This also contributes to the development of a shared culture within the departments.

D E PA RT M E N TA L I S AT I O N I N S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

c)

Best use of resources. In order to avoid the squandering of resources, grouping is carried out in such a way as to favour the creation of synergies and the avoidance of overlapping between departments.

For purposes of the definition of the form of the company’s structure, it is necessary to take into account the framework within which it will operate and the demands to which it will have to respond. The form taken by departmentalisation determines the manner in which, in making use of the unity of command, the employees will position themselves together in groups to carry out their work. Design must be carried out in such a way as to facilitate horizontal and vertical communication between the personnel and the departments, and take into account the mechanisms of co-ordination which prevail in the organisation (see Box 6.1). In practice, there are traditional and innovative approaches to departmentalisation reflecting different ways of making use of unity of command (Daft, 2008). In traditional structures which are based to a greater degree on the chain of command for the definition of departmental groupings and reporting relationships, in the hierarchy are included the functional, the divisional and the matrix structures. Structures based on teams and virtual networks have emerged to meet organisational needs in the changing global environment. The above framework is also obviously applicable to shipping companies. Nevertheless, the type of organisational structure which each shipping company chooses depends upon (Frankel, 1982):

❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚

the kind of service which the company provides, as that is defined by the type of vessels which it manages and the markets in which it is active; the degree of ownership of the vessels possessed by the company, that is, if it is the owner or has been entrusted only with their management; the relations which the company has developed with the ships’ charterers; the method of management which it implements; the technology which it incorporates into its operation; the factors in the external environment which make specific demands or impose certain restrictions.

The existence of an organisational structure which facilitates the development and use of know-how, the change, the simplicity in operation and the cost control is a precondition for success for shipping companies (Lorange, 2005). In the units which follow, the forms of departmentalisation which have greater application in the shipping industry will be examined in detail. It must be borne in mind that the vessels of a shipping company form part of its organisational structure, since its basic aim is the support of their operation. Consequently, the organisational structure and the departmentalisation of the company involve the whole organisation, with the organisational structure on land and the ships forming the sub-systems of a unified system.

❚ Traditional approaches to structuring organisations Functional structure functional structure: grouping of activities on the basis of their functional specialisation.

176

In functional structure, activities are grouped on the basis of their functional specialisation. Jobs which are related to the same process (analysed in Chapter 3) are located in the same department. Functional structure is practised in companies which take part in markets with a stable external environment and develop a few services which do not change over time. It allows for central control, the clear definition of competences, an in-depth approach to operational issues and the development of specialisation in the personnel. Overall, it contributes to an increased effectiveness of companies. At the same time, however, it has disadvantages which have to do with the concentration of the personnel on the aims of their department at the expense of the overall objectives of the company, the emergence of problems of horizontal communication, the employees with high specialisation who cannot have overall supervision of the company’s processes and difficulty in adaptation to changes in the environment. In the case of shipping companies which implement functional structure, there are departments which correspond to the processes and functions, which were described in Chapter 3. A typical form of functional structure is shown in Figure 6.1. The extent of departmentalisation depends upon the size and, consequently, the volume of work and the number of the personnel of the company. In the case of large-size companies which implement this structure, each function is organised in an independent department. The organisation chart of such companies is to be expected to take the same form as that in Figures 5.3 and 5.4 (Chapter 5). In companies with smaller fleets, some functions are likely to be amalgamated with others and to be carried out within the same department. In this case, the departments which are in the end set up are fewer in number than the company’s processes in Figure 5.2 (Chapter 5). Functional structure is the traditional form of organisation in shipping companies also (Smith & Roggema, 1980), and is encountered more in bulk cargo shipping companies. It is possible in some cases that it deprives the company of the necessary dynamism and pursuit of change, leading it to keep its mode of operation and its activities unaltered. It has been said in this connection that many traditional shipping companies continue to keep their organisation and activities unchanged in order to retain their employees in their offices and on board their ships (Lorange, 2005).1 It is argued that this operating model, which places emphasis on stability in the workforce, leads to the implementation of strategies “that in the end made little sense from an evolutionary commercial shipping view” (Lorange, 2005). Given, however, that a shipping company is required to be simultaneously a centralised and decentralised organisation, as it presupposes the taking of specialised decisions in technical areas of high specialisation, as well as a high degree of unification of all these decisions, the traditional functional structure can be effective because it brings together personnel with the same specialisation, ensuring at the same time their unification at the top level of the hierarchy

(Frankel, 1982). Nevertheless, it is likely to lead to the development of the departments in such a way as to cause a lack of concentration on the very object of their activity – the ship (Downard, 1990).

Divisional structure Departmentalisation by division, often referred to as either product or service departmentalisation, or departmentalisation by strategic units of business activity, groups processes and functions on the basis of the organisation’s outflows (Daft, 2010). In the case of the shipping industry, divisional structure is adopted by companies which manage diversified fleets, with a large number of ships. The fleet is divided into groups, depending upon the specialisation of the vessels, and each group constitutes a division. Within each division, those processes which involve the management of the ship and require specialisation in relation to each type, such as technical and operations management, are organised in a department, while the support processes are organised in independent departments which serve all divisions. Figure 6.2 shows an indicative form of divisional approach based on the types of vessels managed by the company. Three divisions are set out: tankers, bulk carriers and LNGs, and within each there are the same departments. The logic of this form of departmentalisation is based on the fact that different sectors (types of ships) require different knowledge and skills (on the particularities of the management of LNGs, see Box 5.4). Each division is organised in the light of its needs, using entrepreneurial resources in common with other divisions. The advantages of divisional approach are many, as it makes possible rapid changes in an unstable environment, permits each division to engage in the necessary modifications in order to adjust to these changes, facilitates co-ordination between the various processes within each division, contributes to personnel development by overall supervision, promotes control, reinforces the concentration of management on strategic issues and encourages initiative and autonomy in employees. Among the disadvantages are the increased cost of implementation, as it abrogates economies of scale in the line departments and leads to the existence of a large number of employees with the same duties, the probability of poor coordination and conflict between the divisions over claims on the entrepreneurial

divisional structure: grouping of processes and functions on the basis of the organisation’s outflows.

Figure 6.2 Divisional structure

resources, the addition of a further level of management and the elimination of in-depth knowledge and technical specialisation on the part of employees. Its full implementation in the shipping industry is observable only in companies with very large fleets, each of which is made up of a large number of ships.

Matrix form of organisation matrix form of organisation: the combination of functional and divisional structures.

unity of command: an employee should report to one superior only.

178

The matrix form of organisation is the combination of the forms of functional and divisional structures. While those two structures presuppose vertical lines of command and communication, the matrix form includes vertical and horizontal lines simultaneously. On the vertical axes are the various processes and functions of the company and on the horizontal the different divisions or the different business activities which the company carries out (Carrel et al, 1997). In this way, a system of dual command and responsibility is created, and each employee reports to two superiors at the same time: to the head of the division of his specialisation and to the person responsible for the function at which works. The dual hierarchy is opposed to the principle of unity of command, according to which an employee should report to one superior only, but its adoption in the matrix form of organisation may be effective when the company which implements it has to use rare resources in common in different business activities. Furthermore, it is subject to pressures from its environment to two or more outflows of crucial importance and operates in a complex and uncertain environment (Daft, 2010).

D E PA RT M E N TA L I S AT I O N I N S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

The matrix form of organisation is suitable for innovative companies which offer a wide range of services and change over time. It increases their flexibility and permits their adaptation to any rapid changes in the conditions in their external environment. Among its advantages are the good use which is made of specialised personnel and equipment, motivation, flexibility and adaptability, encouragement of co-operation and the exchange of ideas and knowledge, a limited need for hierarchical control, personnel development, the creation and diffusion of knowledge and the encouragement of innovations. Its disadvantages include the existence of a system of dual command and responsibility, the possible causing of confusion among employees, time-consuming procedures in the taking of decisions and in the resolution of disputes and the effort which is called for to retain a balance of power. The advantages of the matrix form of organisation render it suitable for bulk cargo shipping companies, which operate in a dynamic and uncertain environment. As has already been pointed out, shipping management calls for an organisational structure which is at one and the same time centralising and decentralising, as it needs special decisions in technical areas of high specialisation, such as technical management or the programming of ships’ movements, as well as a high degree of unification of all these decisions. Some of the most crucial decisions in the management of a shipping company which contribute to the determination of the cost of operating the vessels depend upon effective co-operation between employees of differing specialisations. For this reason, the role of matrix organisation is substantive in shipping, as it allows for the co-ordination of specialisations both at the level of operations and at that of activity (Frankel, 1982). Moreover, matrix departmentalisation orientates to a greater degree the whole organisation towards the ship and favours decentralisation by means of entrusting more decisions to its personnel (Downard, 1990). The matrix organisation is implemented by companies which manage a diversified fleet. There are two considerations which have led many shipping companies to apply it. The first has to do with the size and diversification of their fleet, that is, with the fact that these are large-size companies which have fleets with many types of vessels, and the second is connected with increased competition, which calls for greater efficiency as regards cost and the quality of services (Smith & Roggema, 1980). Figure 6.3 shows an indicative form of matrix organisation, from which it can be seen that grouping is on the basis of the type of vessel and the departmentalisation of processes. Depending upon their experience and knowledge, individuals work in teams, in order to carry out specific tasks.

Geographical structure Geographical structure is a form of departmentalisation based on the region in which a company operates and is active. It is based on the logic of concentrating all the activities within a region in one structure. It is indicated for the organisation of companies which maintain activities at many geographical points, the

179

Figure 6.3 Matrix approach

central co-ordination of which is not always effective. Belonging to this category are liner shipping companies, which, as has already been explained, maintain extensive networks of agents in many of the world’s ports. By implementing this form of organisation, they are able to gain a better knowledge of the particular characteristics of each market to which they address themselves, thus increasing their effectiveness. Figure 6.4 shows an indicative form of geographical departmentalisation. An example of company which adopts a similar approach is Pacific Basin (www.pacificbasin.com). It manages a fleet of over 200 handysize and supramax bulk carriers and operates a network of 10 commercial offices and three technical/crewing offices around the world in order its chartering and operations’ staff to be positioned close to customers and markets (Pacific Basin, 2017). Basic advantages of geographical departmentalisation are the better co-ordination of activities and the scope for the decentralisation of decisions. Nevertheless, its application precludes the employment of economies of scale, since it leads to the existence of many employees with the same duties in different geographical regions.

❚ Modern approaches to structuring organisations As was explained in the introduction, the structure which is based on teams and that which is based on the network are modern approaches to the organisation of companies. One of the reasons for the increased adoption by companies of these approaches is the recognition by many companies of the limits set by traditional vertical structures in the rapidly changing business environment (Daft, 2008). Modern approaches are based to a greater extent on horizontal co-ordination than on vertical control, and permit companies to operate with more flexibility and adaptability.

Team approach Team approach is called for by the need for a shift from vertical structures which manifest the weaknesses described in functional departmentalisation to horizontal and more flexible structures, which reduce the hierarchy and the limits on the functional departments. Organisation which is based on teams is considered to increase the adaptability of the organisations, improve the co-ordination between the operational specialisations and contribute to the appropriate utilisation of the human resources (Daft, 2008). For the co-ordination of the tasks, working teams are needed which are interdepartmental, include employees of differing specialisations and are composed

team approach: the creation of teams which are made up of employees of different specialisations.

181

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

Organization still matters and many ship managers move away from a pure vertical organization to a more process-based one. The best known example is the so-called fleet teams, where technical, purchasing, crewing and/ or accounts experts sit together in one organizational unit to serve a certain number of vessels. (Fraunhofer CML & GL, 2013:13)

in such a way as to respond to the needs of the internal customers, that is, the ships. The working teams retain the power to make decisions on the problems which they are called upon to resolve. The setting up of teams consisting of employees of different specialisations has significant advantages, particularly in relation to the development of new knowledge and its diffusion, while it contributes to the good co-ordination of activities within each team. Nevertheless, it is likely to give rise to an increase in complexity in the organisation of the company, particularly if it includes many small-size teams, resulting in difficulties in control. For this reason, organisation by teams, in parallel with emphasis on the operation of each team separately, must also be concentrated on the way in which the teams can be organised in order to achieve their unification (Mullern, 2000). The horizontal orientation of team structure contributes to decentralisation in decision-making, to the production of specialised personnel who have overall supervision of the task of management and to an increase in the flexibility of organisation. Furthermore, it reinforces effectiveness, as it orientates employees towards the outcome, that is, towards the services provided by the ships. Each team is co-ordinated by the fleet manager, and undertakes the management of a group of the company’s vessels. Team-based organisation is seen as suitable for shipping companies (Box 6.2) (Korres & Thanopoulos, 2005). The creation of a cross-functional team is proposed as one of the tactics that can help container shipping companies to unleash their full potential (Glave et al, 2014). This structure, which allows organisations to move away from vertical organisation to a process-based one, is also called fleet teams. It is considered as best practice in ship management, as it “reduces interfaces and waiting times, gives clear ownership and accountability of results” (Fraunhofer CML & GL, 2013:13). The implementation of teams-based structure, of course, must be combined with the use of modern systems to support its functioning. The case of the Thenamaris (Ships Management) Inc. (www.thenamaris.com) company, an operator of diversified fleet of 89 ships, which implements a similar approach, is indicative. According to the company, the functioning of inter-departmental teams is supported by evolved business procedures and the corresponding

BOX 6.2 Organising the department in groups The operator of tanker and dry cargo vessels Norden A/S has adopted an approach similar to the one described by the team-based structure (Norden News, 2014). The technical department of the company (which is responsible for technical management of the fleet, crewing, safety and environment, inspections and purchase and newbuildings) consists of four vessel groups, with each one of them including vessels of the different types of the Norden’s owned fleet of vessels managed internally (Norden, 2017). Each vessel group is in charge of the technical operation of the vessels, which means that members of the group hold the responsibility of managing all issues for their vessels, either themselves or by asking for the assistance of support functions of the department which they need. Thus, for every need they have, the crew of the ships contact their own group. Norden decided to move to re-organisation of the department when it realised that factors such as the increased legislative complexity and the demand from customers put the existing organisation under pressure. It adopted this approach after conducting an extended study which identified the key action areas for the technical department, 79 in total. The key actions were grouped in the following five areas (Norden News, 2014): 1. Close co-operation with and around all vessels 2. Successful HR management of the seafarers 3. Close relationship with the commercial departments 4. Better use of knowledge 5. Effective and sustainable implementation of solutions The re-organisation of the department allowed the company to make better use of existing competences of employees and of other resources.

systems, which permits the teams to carry out difficult tasks and manage complex problems (Thenamaris, 2017). Figure 6.5. shows an indicative form of team structure. Each team consists of a fleet manager and employees from different departments connected with the vessel. Each department provides those members taking part in the teams with the support required for the fulfilment of the team’s duties. In effect, each team consists of employees with specialisations which correspond to processes having to do with the order fulfilment (Chapter 4, Figures 4.1 and 4.2).

Network structure Network structure is a form of organisation which extends the importance of co-ordination and collaboration beyond the boundaries of the company. It is

network structure: the company maintains a small-size structure which is linked to other companies, to which certain of its processes are entrusted.

183

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

implemented by companies which entrust to other companies in the same group or to external associates many of their management processes and retain a smallsize structure which has a co-ordinating role. This form of organisation provides the company with the maximum flexibility and makes it possible for it to concentrate more effectively on the processes and functions which it carries out itself or which it considers crucial for its competitiveness. This option is connected with the knowledge and know-how which the company possesses as regards the various processes which are necessary for its activation. This, of course, is likely to lead to a reduction of the control over the processes which it commits to external associates. Among its advantages are a reduction in cost, and increased flexibility and innovation, while the disadvantages include difficulties in coordination and control and possible competition from external associates who may expand their activities. Figure 6.6 shows an indicative form of network departmentalisation. A crucial factor for the effectiveness of network departmentalisation is the electronic interconnection between the partners, as this permits their control and co-ordination by the central structure. The development of independent ship management companies and the implementation of the strategy of outsourcing (Chapter 10) have given rise to the conditions for the implementation of this form of departmentalisation in shipping also. The demands which shipping companies must meet are constantly evolving, either because of changes at a macro-economic level, or because of rapid change in customers’ needs with regard to the transportation of their cargoes. Consequently, shipping companies have to seek that change which allows them to adapt constantly to the givens of the situation. Organisation which is network-based supports the operation of a company which is orientated towards dynamism and change, while prompting it not to continue to retain activities which do not justify their operation in terms of effectiveness. At the same time, it orientates the company towards cost control by means of a search for effective options in terms of cost and towards the avoidance of overlapping in its internal operation (Lorange, 2005).

Figure 6.6 Network structure

184

❚ Combination of forms of structure In practice, the structures described in the preceding units are rarely applied in their pure form. It should be noted that large companies in particular often use a hybrid structure which typically combines features of different approaches in order to meet their strategic needs. Hybrid structures which permit the use of the positive characteristics and the avoidance of the weaknesses of each approach in the organisation are implemented particularly by companies which are active in rapidly changing business environments, as they allow them to retain the necessary flexibility (Daft, 2010). The same is true of shipping companies. A number of shipping companies implement a form of departmentalisation which is very close to one of those which have been described in the preceding units. Most, as has already been explained, practise functional structure of some form. The other forms, particularly matrix organisation, are effective for companies with specific characteristics. Since, however, they show significant advantages, many companies which do not have the characteristics suitable for their full implementation attempt to make use of features from these forms in developing their own form of structure adapted to their individual strategic directions, their resources and their operational needs. They thus create ‘mixed’ forms of structure. It can be concluded from an analysis of shipping companies that the basic combinations implemented are the following:

❚

❚

❚

❚

The combination of structures by function and by division. It is implemented by companies which manage a diversified fleet. Usually, the operations and technical management departments belong within divisions corresponding to the types of vessels of the company’s fleet, while in the rest functional departmentalisation is practised. The combination of structures by function and matrix organisation. This is implemented by companies with a medium and large-size fleet, diversified or otherwise. Usually, there is one maritime division in which matrix organisation is applied and to which the departments which have to do with the ship’s management (operations and technical management, quality and safety, crew) and the fleet teams belong. The rest of the departments are included in a second division, in which functional departmentalisation is implemented. The combination of structures by division, by function and by teams. This is usually adopted by large companies with diversified fleets and sectors which relate to each type of ship which they manage. Within these sectors, team structure is followed. In the case of the rest of the line and staff departments, departmentalisation by function is practised. The combination of structures by function, by division and by geographical region. Large companies are likely to have offices in different centres of management, each of which can undertake specific processes for a certain type from among the vessels which they manage.

Over and above these combinations, of course, many others are also observable, since, as has already been pointed out, each company can plan and implement the form of departmentalisation which meets its individual needs. Human Resource Management in shipping companies Put another way, unless we can persuade people of shipping’s value, it is hard to attract investors and bring the best talent to our industry. And if we don’t have the best people, we have much less chance of creating value beyond the steel. (Sohmen-Pao, 2005:15)

❚ Introduction The ability of a company to create value and stand out depends to a great extent on its human resources. Its employees can improve the value created by its capital assets, although this is not enough to make a company or an industry distinctive. For human resources to be able to perform in the anticipated way, it is essential that the shipping company be properly managed. This is the point at which Human Resource Management (HRM) plays a decisive role. The particular features of the shipping company make HRM an interesting subject for analytical investigation. While in all other companies HRM systems are unified and adapted to the specific characteristics of each group of employee (specialisation, training, etc.), in the shipping company there are two groups of employee: those who work on shore and those who work on the ships, with their corresponding HRM systems. This approach stems from the fact that the working environment, the characteristics and the needs of each group are very different. At the same time, however, there is a need to be consistent and effective, and therefore to have systems that unify their individual aspects, incorporating them into the strategic goals of HRM. The effective management of human resources of the shipping companies contribute to the creation of sustainable competitive advantage (Progoulaki & Theotokas, 2010). In this chapter, the function of HRM and its application in shipping companies’ employees

192

H U M A N R E S O U R C E M A N A G E M E N T I N S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

ashore are examined and analysed. In the units which follow, issues connected with categories of shore-based staff and their qualifications as well as their recruitment and selection are examined.

❚ HRM for personnel of the offices of the shipping company The human resources of the shipping company can be divided into:

❚ ❚

personnel in the shore-based organisational structure, personnel on the company’s ship or ships.

The organisational and management needs of each organisational structure differ. The management of each of these groups must adapt to certain particular features that are closely connected with the nature of the work that they do, with the place where it is done and with the particular characteristics of the individuals who perform it. For this reason, different departments of the company are responsible for the management of each of these groups. HRM for the employees of the shore-based facilities of a shipping company can be performed either by the corresponding department or by employees who also have the responsibility of other management positions. This choice is made according to the size of the company, which is determined by the number of its ships. The greater the number of employees, the greater the need to create and staff a special department. As is noted in the bibliography on the subject, in 2015 the correlation between staff members with responsibility for Human Resource Management and the total number of employees was 1.1 per 100 (Bloomberg BNA, 2015). This, of course, relates to companies with a large number of employees and does not mean that companies with fewer than 100 employees have no staff dealing with HRM. This rule also applies, naturally, in the case of shipping companies too. In very small companies, HRM or certain activities related to it (selection, compensation, etc.) are carried out by administrative staff who also have other duties. Only in large shipping companies with many employees or in companies with a specific approach on the human resources (see Box 9.7) the management of whom represents such a workload that it cannot be handled by line staff, are special HRM departments set up. Of course, the way the HRM department is organised also depends on the company’s strategy and the approach followed for its HRM. It is possible that the employees working in HRM do not comprise a special department but operate within other departments. Norden (www.ds-norden.com) has opted to move the HRM department so as to be close to the employees by physically locating it within the company’s various departments. In this way, the company claims, HRM plays an important role and functions “as a sounding-board for employees and managers” (Norden, 2017). As a rule, in large companies which employ a large number of staff on shore as well as in companies with a long-term approach to human resources issues, regardless of size, there are well-organised departments or divisions (see Box 7.1)

193

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

and developed systems, practices and procedures that contribute to effective HRM. For this purpose, tools are used to perform surveys measuring the effectiveness of the systems that are applied and for the design of corrective actions, such as the annual employee satisfaction or employee engagement surveys, which are used by a series of companies, such as Maersk (A.P. Moller Maersk, 2017; Maersk Line, 2017), Wilh. Wilhelmsen (Wilh. Wilhelmsen, 2017a), Neptune Lines (Neptune Lines, 2015), EST (EST, 2017), Odfjell (Odfjell, 2017) and Torm (TORM, 2017) (for Torm employment satisfaction surveys, see Box 7.2).

BOX 7.1 The organisation of HRM in large diversified companies In companies that employ thousands of people, HRM may be a division within the organisation of the company which has individual departments that are responsible for specific HRM functions or categories of employee. The structure of the divisions and the departments they include is evidence of the priority given by the companies for certain aspects of HRM. For example, the MOL Group has a Human Resources Division (see Box 6.3) which includes the Human Resources Management Group (for land-based personnel), Seafarers and Labour Relations Group (seagoing personnel), Human Resources Planning Group, Counselling and Aid Centre-Human Rights Co-ordination Centre, Global Human Resources Unit, Diversity Management Unit,
