BOX 7.2 Human Resource Management assessment tools. Employee satisfaction at TORM (TORM, 2010) TORM, which employs around 280 members of staff in its offices, in an attempt to create a quality work environment carries out employment satisfaction surveys. The results of these surveys provide information that is used to improve the level of employee satisfaction and the contribution of employees to the company’s performance. For the years 2008 and 2009 in the company’s offices employees from Denmark, the USA, India, Singapore and the Philippines were working. In 2009 the satisfaction level was 69 in comparison to 64 in 2008.

195

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

On the basis of the 2009 results, a working team was formed with representatives from all the departments in order to identify areas for improvement. According to the company, the restructuring of the previous years, which resulted in organisational and administrative changes, was responsible for the decline in employment satisfaction. In the light of the assessment carried out by the working team, the company made some moves to ensure that employees had greater access to top management, the best possible way of communicating its goals and direction. The informal ‘lunches with the boss’, where management and workers discussed all issues of common interest, was one such measure. There were monthly meetings at its offices in Copenhagen so that members of the top management team could explain developments relating to the company’s activities. The presentations given at these meetings were available to the employees working in the offices abroad, where similar meetings were held with the local management.

The degree to which the functions of HRM have been developed is also related to the limitations or demands that the charterers of the ships may impose on the companies. For example, companies which operate tankers are called upon to assess for themselves the degree to which the management systems they apply conform with the Tanker Management Self-Assessment (TMSA) programme, proposed by oil companies (Chapter 9), so that their systems achieve acceptable performance levels. These companies must respond to the demands of key performance indicators and apply best practices, corresponding with those given in Box 7.3. For this purpose, they must develop HRM systems in their offices and on their ships with the features outlined in Box 7.3.

BOX 7.3 Tanker Management Self-Assessment (TMSA) and HRM in shore-based facilities According to the TMSA programme, employees of the company ashore have a crucial role to play in the effective management of the fleet and in supporting its safe operation. For that reason it focuses on their training needs, so that they have the necessary qualifications in order to perform their duties. The companies must plan and apply procedures which will (OCIMF, 2004): – favour retaining staff, with an emphasis on retaining and developing staff in key positions; – secure the employment of an adequate number of personnel so that all the ships of the fleet will be given full oversight;

196

H U M A N R E S O U R C E M A N A G E M E N T I N S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

– confirm that the personnel are capable and have the necessary qualifications in order to perform their duties; – determine the abilities of the staff as regards their technical and general training, their skills and experiences; – confirm, with respect to health issues, that the personnel fulfil all the preconditions, both during the assignment and during the performance of their duties; – include an assessment system that covers all the criteria for promotions and the requirements in relation to the preconditions for finding successors to senior staff; – ensure that data are held on the qualifications, experience and training of the personnel; and – determine the preconditions for updating staff training and maintain data on seminars, courses and conferences attended. As for recruiting and managing shore-based staff, in order for the fleet to be supported by people capable of undertaking and performing the related responsibilities and duties in their totality, the performance indicators include (OCIMF, 2008): – The existence of procedures for orientating new recruits – The existence of a system for verifying the qualifications and experience of candidates, and a system for verifying them for those who have been selected for staff – The existence of an employee assessment system which is used at least on a yearly basis – Ensuring that skills gaps are identified and covered through training – Maintaining a database on staff experience and training – Ensuring that the necessary sources are made available for the implementation of the company’s safety system – Encouraging staff to undertake higher education courses – Transferring senior staff from the ships to the offices in order to gain experience.

The staff of the shipping company, whether in the offices or on the ships, must have the qualifications and skills that will allow them to respond to the demands of managing unexpected situations. The shore-based offices must operate with the purpose of serving and supporting the ship. Efficient communication and good working relations are preconditions for the effective operation of ship and office. An understanding of the requirements of every working environment is needed and of the fact that something that might be considered important and urgent in, for example, the office may be seen as a bureaucratic detail for the ship (Seahealth, 2011). It is, consequently, crucial that the office staff have a direct knowledge and sense of the conditions that prevail on the ship and the working environment of the seafarers, so as to be in a position to understand their demands and needs at any given moment. This, as will be analysed below,

197

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

also explains the goal of shipping companies to recruit people with previous experience in the shipping profession. At the same time, the office staff must be able to make judgements, to take initiatives, have the ability to take decisions (e.g., the management of a crisis that demands an immediate decision). For this reason, some companies use special techniques for the selection of their staff, which reveal if they possess such skills, such as stress interviews during which the candidate’s resilience and response in situations of intense pressure are measured.

❚ Categories of shore-based staff The staff needs of the shipping company are to a great extent determined by:

❚ ❚ ❚ ❚

❚

The size of the fleet. The relationship between the size (fleet) of a company and the number of its members of staff is influenced by the ability to achieve economies of scale, as will be explained below. The specialisation of the fleet and the complexity of the task of management. The more specialised the company’s ships are, the greater the complexity of operating them and, consequently, the greater the need for personnel. Communications technology. The degree to which the shipping company uses new communications technology in its operations impacts upon the number of its employees as well as their qualifications. Goals and strategy. The shipping company may apply a competitive strategy based on cost, the differentiation of its services or its capacity to respond to the needs of specific clients (Sletmo & Holste, 1993). The number of employees as well as the skills and the abilities they should have may differ in each of the above cases. Management style and culture. The desired level of control and the organisational culture within a company is another factor that may determine the quality as well as the quantity of the human resources needed.

An important part of the shore-based work and activities concerns the ships themselves and their operations, which require personnel with knowledge of the ship and technical skills equivalent to those of the profession of seafarer. At the same time, a significant part of the work and operations requires analytical skills and knowledge of the shipping market and its particular characteristics, as well as specialised knowledge of different market sectors, such as ship insurance, communications, etc. In general, there are three groups of management tasks in shipping companies, or, in other words, three management areas (Moreby, 1983): 1. 2.

198

the group that focuses on planning the company’s policy and strategy; the group that focuses on expenses, namely, supporting the operation and management of the ships from onshore and which includes the operations, the technical department, the crew department and the supplies department;

H U M A N R E S O U R C E M A N A G E M E N T I N S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

3.

the group that focuses on the company’s income and includes the sectors of chartering, freights collection and insurance claims.

In family companies, the main administrative positions are filled by members of the family which owns the company or by individuals who have worked in it for years. Even in companies with multiple stakeholders, senior management is selected, as a rule, from among personnel with long experience in shipping, either within the company or from the broader market. The lower positions in the hierarchy of the first group of employees (and, to a lesser degree, of the third group) are filled by individuals who may also have experience in other sectors of industry. The second and third (to a lesser extent) groups employ primarily individuals with experience only in shipping and, especially, ships. This limits the viewpoint from which different situations can be approached. Most of these employees are ship’s officers who, after a successful career at sea, take up positions in the shore-based offices of a company. The management personnel of the first group normally are more open to new ideas and suggestions, while the personnel of the second group often dedicate time and energy to defending traditional practices (Moreby, 1983). This can lead to the adoption of a negative attitude towards change and be one of the reasons why changing the management structure of a shipping company is sometimes difficult. This distinction places certain limitations both on the shore-based management and staff as well as on the organisational structure itself, as it impacts upon its flexibility and ability to change and adapt to developments in the shipping sector. This happens because of two co-existing aspects within the company’s culture: the culture of those with no seafaring experience and the culture of the ‘seafarers’. Between these two groups it is possible that disagreements may arise from time to time over issues crucial for the operation of a company. One such issue was the introduction of regulations to improve safety in shipping. The culture of the ‘seafarers’, which attaches more importance to tradition, had been, generally speaking, more hesitant towards adopting concepts such as logging operations, procedures and rules because, aside from anything else, they led to an increase in the workload. In contrast, for those who had no seafaring experience, this was not only essential but a means for understanding and overseeing the work of the ‘seafarers’ (Theotokas, 1997). It must of course be stressed that the working environment in the shipping industry itself is changing, given the introduction of a series of regulations. These have been adopted either out of obligation or choice, made necessary by developments in the market. The analysis in Chapter 9 of safety management focuses on this point in greater detail. One result of these changes, as analysed in Chapter 4, is the increase of formalisation in organisation of shipping companies. This creates a need for a larger number of personnel who may not have a good knowledge of the specifics of a ship, but do, however, know and can use modern tools of management and are also able to comprehend the specifics of shipping management. Alongside this, there is another trend as regards the supply of and demand for labour, which is associated with the aim of an increasing number of ship’s officers to gain an academic specialisation in shipping or management, in

199

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

order to work in the offices. Certain shipping companies even encourage their personnel to take such initiatives. In any case, as can be seen from Box 7.3, this is one of the TMSA indicators, on the basis of which the company’s management is assessed. Of course, it is evident that the second and, in part, the third of the groups mentioned above will always be to a certain extent staffed by personnel with mainly seafaring previous experience. Since these job positions presuppose direct contact with the ship, all those who occupy them must have, aside from a good knowledge of the ship as a means of production, also a knowledge of the specifics of the seafaring profession, must be able to communicate with seafarers, understand their problems and support them through their work. This is an additional reason for the implementation of the traditional model of employing ex-ship officers in job positions ashore. Although certain positions of these categories are filled nowadays by university degree holders, the combination of skills and tacit knowledge of ship officers favours their transition from the ship to the offices (Theotokas, 1997; Theotokas et al, 2008; Pallis & Ng, 2011). Very few women work in these groups, given that the shipping profession is male-dominated. At this point, it is worth noting that shipping as a whole is dominated by men. This reality is associated with the fact that in companies that come from countries which have a culture in which values related to the feminine prevail, as opposed to those linked to the masculine, i.e., caring for others, the quality of life or people versus assertiveness, acquisition of values and things, such as in Scandinavia (Hofstede, 1980), where there is a larger percentage of females in the industry in comparison with other countries, it is acknowledged that the presence of women, in particular in top management positions, must increase.1 Maersk and the AP Moller Group cite research showing that businesses perform better when they have diverse teams, and mention that while diversity encompasses much more than just gender and nationality, for these two areas the groups have set targets (A.P. Moller Maersk, 2017). The relevant goals are also included in the companies’ social programmes, for example those of MOL, which has as its goal for 2015 to “continue to support women employees and to encourage their success and career development”. This resulted in the establishment of “a division responsible for promoting diversity, \[which\] presented diversity promotion seminars, and publicized its initiatives in the in-house magazine”, while the target for 2016 was “to continue to support women employees to encourage their success and career development, toward achieving the target of 8% of women in management ranks in 2020” (MOL, 2017a). According to a study carried out on shipping companies based in OECD countries, although using a relatively small sample, the percentage of those who had been in the seafaring profession and who worked in jobs involving fleet management reached 50 per cent (OECD, 2003). As is shown in Figure 7.3, 15 per cent of companies held the view that the percentage of former seafarers will increase in the future, 55 per cent that it would stay the same and 30 per cent that it would fall. When the question was limited to seafarers from OECD countries, the results were differentiated, with a percentage of 35 per cent believing it would increase and 65 per cent believing it would stay the same. One issue

One interesting study in Great Britain showed that in the period 1996–2003 almost all the companies which employed former seafarers did so in positions for which their maritime experience was a main requirement or advantage. The study also showed that the demand for employing former seafarers on dry land had reduced slightly, a reduction that appears to be related to the difficulty in finding qualified people (Pettit et al, 2005). From the OECD research, it also appears that it is deemed essential that former seafarers should be employed in specific positions in the shore-based offices. As can be seen in Figure 7.4, 98 per cent of companies considered it essential that former seafarers be employed in the operation and technical management of the fleet, while for operations relating to safety the percentage reaches 86 per cent. A significant percentage of shipping companies, even for the first of the three categories analysed above, would prefer individuals with seafaring experience, namely staff with multifaceted qualifications. The employee with previous seafaring experience knows the object of the work very well and can appreciate the effect that his/her work has on the other staff on the ship. For this reason, many companies provide training systems for their shore-based staff on their ships. Correspondingly, the large companies have a programmed system of transferring officers from the ship to the office, selecting those with specific qualifications and offering them motives to gain further knowledge through education and

This method for selecting staff functions as a mechanism for transferring to the shore-based staff the rich tacit knowledge that they have gained during their seafaring experience and, vice versa, as a mechanism for enriching and expanding their already accumulated knowledge. Subsequently, the enriched knowledge is transferred to the company’s seafarers in a planned manner, during the visits to/inspections by office staff members of the seafarers on the ships and in an un-programmed manner when dealing with emergency situations. Indeed, in the past few years, practices such as the programmed transfer of captains or engineers to office positions for a specific period of time is being increasingly adopted by companies operating tankers, in their efforts to achieve an even higher assessment in the Tanker Management Self-Assessment (Box 7.3). This is because these practices are considered best and must be adopted in order for a company to achieve the fourth stage in the self-assessment of the practices in recruiting and retaining staff on the ships. ❚ Recruiting and selecting shore-based staff The way in which those shipping companies which are active in the international freight markets participate in a global market from which they draw their productive factors has been analysed. In the case, however, of shore-based staff, the labour market to which they turn has specific geographical limits. While a small number of senior staff can be recruited from the global market, either through special hiring agencies or through advertising in the shipping press (Lloyd’s List, Tradewinds, etc.), for most staff the market is geographically limited to the base of the company. In any case, the feature that makes an administrative centre attractive and makes shipping companies select it as their base is the supply of specialised human resources, from which they will draw their personnel. Recruiting candidates is the process of identifying and recruiting suitable individuals (both from within and outside the company) in order to fill vacant job positions. Correspondingly, the selection of candidates is the process of collecting information and assessing the candidates in order to select the most suitable ones to fill specific positions. These are crucially important processes, and the quality of the company’s staff depends on them being effective. The quality of the company’s staff is a factor which, in turn, determines its overall assessment, particularly if it has many stakeholders and shares listed on a stock market. Investor decisions on the purchase of shares depend upon a company’s ability to recruit talented individuals (Jackson & Schuler, 1999).

Recruiting staff can be done both through sources that are external and internal to the company. Each type of source has its advantages and disadvantages. For companies that operate within a stable and predictable environment, the internal market is more suitable because it raises the morale and acts as a productivity incentive. On the other hand, this method leads to the hardening of ideas and to a small possibility for change and adaptation, and, consequently, is not suitable for companies operating within a changing environment. Furthermore, it cannot support the pace of fast-growing companies. The combined use of recruitment sources, based on the needs of each job position, leads to the best possible results. In the shipping industry, internal sources for recruitment are mostly encountered. This is because of the fact that, as explained in the previous unit, the employees in shipping companies are, to a great extent, former seafarers. It is worth noting that, according to Figure 7.6, most companies which took part in the OECD study (OECD, 2003), in response to the question as to which source they draw their shore-based staff from, answered former company seafarers (78%) and former non-company seafarers (59%). It is accepted that the key to assessing the incentives of an employee is appreciation of his/her values. If the employee shares the same values as his or her employer, then it is much more likely that he or she will commit to achieving the same goals (Carrell et al, 1997). Staff selection is one of the main methods for preserving and strengthening the organisational culture within a company (Luthans, 1995). The recruitment and selection of staff who share the cultural values of the company means the employment of dedicated personnel who are committed to achieving the company’s goals. These characteristics and the values of loyalty and commitment can more easily be sought in employees who come from the internal labour market, which in the case of shipping companies is primarily made up of the officers on their ships. This is, of course, observed primarily in large companies, which have greater personnel needs and more job positions available so that they can offer such a career trajectory. Those officers who are transferred to the offices then become themselves the promoters of these values and contribute to reproducing the company’s organisational culture. Even so, this aspect changes as the structures of the companies change. At the same time, companies become increasingly aware of the importance of human

resources for their success, which then contributes to abandoning or modernising traditional practices of selecting personnel. The development of safety and quality management systems and, through these, the analytical description and specification of every job position as well as the processes for recruiting and selecting staff lead to the search for employees with qualifications that respond to the positions that they will fill. For companies with good organisation, then, the recruitment and selection of personnel are based upon carefully designed procedures. This does not, of course, mean that they do not make use of the skills that the internal labour market offers them, but that this choice, when it is made, is based primarily on the job specifications and the particular characteristics and strengths of the employees who will be considered and eventually selected. Furthermore, the commitment of the employees to fulfilling the company’s goals is supported by the design and development of HRM systems that enable employees to observe their personal development in parallel with the growth of the company. A company’s mode of operation may also define to a great degree the sources from which it can recruit personnel. Even so, the fact that shipping companies are organisations which are active in international markets and in several cases have operations in more than one country means that they employ staff with different nationalities and that there may be a high degree of diversity among their personnel. If to the shore-based office personnel we add the personnel on the ships then the level of diversity, as will be discussed in the next chapter, is extremely high (see Box 7.4). This creates a need for actions which will enable this diversity to be managed and used, which will in turn increase cohesion within the company and allow it to operate from a unified platform, incorporating and making the most of local conditions. One illustrative case of this is the Wilh. Wilhelmsen company, which in its Annual Report notes the following: Despite a staggering presence in 125 countries around the world, with over 125 nationalities employed, our company embraces one culture. We revolve everything around our Code of Conduct and five core values: empowerment, teaming and collaboration, stewardship, learning and innovation, and customer centred (Wilh. Wilhelmsen, 2016)

BOX 7.4 Cohesion based on teamwork culture Oldendorff (www.oldendorff.com), which operates approximately 600 privately owned and chartered ships, is the world’s leading dry bulk operator, with offices not only in Germany, where it is based, but in 16 different countries. It employs over 670 members of staff in its offices, of whom approximately 300 are in Germany. In total, it employs over 51 different nationalities in its offices and, in a particularly noteworthy move, on its website provides details of all its employees in every office throughout the world (Oldendorff, 2017a). Oldendorff describes itself as a ‘self-propelled’ global network of commercially driven managers and seagoing personnel. It appears to seek internal cohesion by adopting a horizontal organisation, with a culture that is dependent upon teamwork, mutual support of its members and the common utilisation of knowledge and resources. It uses technologies which make communications between its different offices around the world easier, maintaining open lines of communication through sound and image throughout the day. Oldendorff has also adopted a modern approach that takes into consideration the abilities of the employees who are called upon to become part of an open working environment that has no dress code and does not discriminate on the basis of job position or seniority (Schoer, 2015). The employees are encouraged to take ‘ownership’ of their business. This functions by decentralising decision-making to an extraordinary extent, thus facilitating speedy implementation (Oldendorff, 2017b). To recruit staff, the company uses the Internet and social media, publishing information on all available positions as well as video and audio material, making the company an attractive prospect for potential applicants.

The human resources of ships – the crew – make up a special category of employees for reasons which have to do with the nature of the work which they do. Furthermore, whereas in the case of the human resources of the offices, the labour market necessarily has geographical boundaries, this does not apply to crews, since the market for the seafaring workforce is world-wide. In the units which follow, issues connected with the special characteristics of the seafarers’ profession, the management of crews, the practices which are implemented by companies and the particularities which arise from the world-wide nature of the seafarers’ labour market are examined.

The profession of seafarer is also at the same time a way of life. The seafarer does not merely work on board the ship, he lives in it for the length of time that his engagement lasts (Korres, 1978). Consequently, during the course of the examination of seafarers as a category of employees, certain characteristics which shape a particular approach and behaviour on their part must be taken into consideration. Inter alia, some of these characteristics are:

❚

Seafarers are for a great length of time far removed from their family and social environment, and contact with it is not always easy, even at the present time, when there are many more accessible means of communication – at the relevant cost, of course. The findings of a study of the working life of seafarers

209

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

❚

❚ ❚ ❚ ❚

are of interest, as they reveal that they regard as a basic plus of a career at sea the high remuneration, as compared with that for jobs on land. Nevertheless, it is reported that the transition from the ship to shore is the most difficult period in the work cycle, as the seafarer attempts to re-adjust to life on land and his/her partner in order to function again as part of a couple. The seafarers stressed that a significant length of time is needed to get over the stress of working life on board ship, while they also spoke of the difficulties in adjusting to the day-to-day routine of their family (Thomas, 2003). Between the members of the crews a series of differentiations are observable, including those of hierarchy, specialisation and nationality. Differences in training is also another level of differentiation among members of the multicultural crews (Progoulaki et al, 2006). To the extent that the appropriate systems do not exist or the required practices are not implemented, these differentiations can undermine the effectiveness and cohesion of the team. It is essential that they should live together efficiently with the other seafarers on board, even where they differ as to nationality, culture, language, knowledge, apprehensions, etc. The limited working space is at the same time their social space for the whole of the term of their service. They remain confined in this, though remaining at the same time with bonds to their families (Box 8.1). The physical conditions in which they provide their labour may change, as the ship is constantly on the move and travels to different geographical latitudes and longitudes. The working conditions and the givens created by the rules and regulations for the safety of the vessel and the limits on the time that the ships remain in port, in conjunction with the difficulties involved in going ashore, increase

BOX 8.1 Communities of seafarers on board ship The market for labour in shipping is world-wide, and states take part in it on competitive terms, given that the restrictions on the ethnicity of the crew hold good in part only for registers of traditional flag states. Features of the seafarer’s job, such as competition for work and work far from the family environment, form the basis for a shared identity, which has led to a virtually ‘cosmopolitan’ behaviour among seafarers of all nationalities. As reported in a study on relations between migrants and seafarers, “though they cannot be described as migrants, and quite often cannot establish a kin-based community while living on board ships, they still remain strongly connected with their families at home by regular communication with them and by sending remittances” (Borovnik, 2004). This scope for maintaining regular communication with the family and the social environment by means of the communications technology which is now available at affordable cost is also a basic incentive for remaining in this occupation (Papachristou et al, 2015) (see also Box 8.13).

❚

❚

the sense of isolation felt by seafarers. Those working on board ships, particularly lower-ranking crews, feel that what in the modern world of technology (and particularly in shipping) has led to the shrinkage of space and time for themselves functions as a restriction and not as liberation, as it affects the terms of their access to the wider world. Seafarers, particularly on container vessels, are rarely able to go ashore, while their direct contact with ‘outside’ for the duration of their engagement is limited (Sampson & Wu, 2003). Issues concerning their working conditions and with the procedures which they are called upon to follow are regulated by other employees who are located in the office and who may lack a direct awareness of the realities which seafarers experience on board ship and may never seek their opinion as to the way in which these matters are regulated (Boxes 8.2 and 8.6). Thus, seafarers perceive a lack of understanding of many of the office’s staff for their working condition onboard (Sampson et al, 2016). Characteristics having to do with both the range of duties and the prestige of the ships’ officers, particularly of captains, and with the organisation of work on board the ship and the relations between ship and office, have undergone significant changes (Box 8.2).

BOX 8.2 Organisation of work onboard and relations between ship and office The ship must be in constant contact with the office. This means that the captain can be monitored as to every detail of his task or can be subject to interventions as to the way in which he carries this out. Today, the captain is an employee who implements the practices and instructions of the company. At one time, ships remained in port for many days, whereas today they remain for a few hours. New regulations, restrictions by the terminals, demands on the part of the charterers, port state control inspections, requirements of the market – all these things put pressure on the crew. As soon as they adapt to the demands, some new condition comes into play (Surveyor, 2010). Shipping companies in the past were decentralised organisations (Stopford, 2002). At the present time, however, the control which is exercised by the office has reduced the captains’ scope for taking substantive decisions in connection with the management of the vessel’s operation. The same technology which made possible contact between ship and office, the transfer of decision-taking power to the office and greater control of the ship and the crew can, as Martin Stopford proposed in 2002, be used in the reverse direction, so that the management of the vessel can gain access to the same systems as are used by the office management so that it can manage every aspect of its operation, from the chartering of the vessel to its supplies, crew and drydocking. The ship can become a centre for revenues. Certainly, this will

211

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

influence the interaction of ships and officers/crews with the rest of the company (Stopford, 2002). Technology, then, permits this change. What is required is a change of culture and a shift to a business model which permits employees to be effective as members of a unified team with better and more profitable career opportunities for the young and with a more unified process between ship and office (Stopford, 2015). Such a change will lead to an improvement in the satisfaction of crew members, will enhance working life and will be able to function as an incentive for the recruitment and retention of seafarers, both from developed and from low-cost countries, in the profession.

❚

Seafarers are migrants who, unlike workers who migrate to a foreign country, migrate, essentially, within the global market. Nevertheless, if we take into account the countries which have not ratified many conventions of the International Labour Office, we shall reach the conclusion that the world market is no substitute for national markets as regards social policy and the laws which govern labour (Gerstenberger, 2002). In the case of maritime labour, particularly in the sector of flags of convenience, a lack of regulation is observable (Alderton & Winchester, 2002). In an effort to create minimum conditions of protection, the International Labour Organisation/ILO has introduced the Maritime Labour Convention (Box 8.3).

BOX 8.3 The Maritime Labour Convention1 The Maritime Labour Convention/ MLC was finalised by the International Labour Organisation/ILO following discussions which lasted four years. In February 2006, it was approved by 900 representatives of maritime labour unions, shipowners’ associations and governments from 100 countries. The MLC provides a consolidated overview of the reciprocal rights and obligations of seafarers, employers and the state, and is regarded as the ‘fourth pillar’ of the regulatory regime of shipping. The other three are the IMO Safety of Life at Sea (SOLAS), Standard of Training, Certification and Watchkeeping (STCW) and the International Convention for the Prevention of Pollution from Ships/MARPOL (Box 8.4). The MLC defines the rights of seafarers to decent working and living conditions, and contributes to the creation of conditions for fair competition for companies. It consists of five chapters which deal, respectively, with the minimum requirements for seafarers to work on a ship, with conditions of employment (employment agreements, wages, hours of work and rest, etc.), issues concerned with the accommodation, recreation facilities, foods and catering for the crew, health protection, medical care issues, welfare and social

212

MANAGEMENT OF HUMAN RESOURCES OF SHIPS

security protection and matters pertaining to compliance with the terms of the convention and its activation. By virtue of the convention, those crew management companies (or crewing agencies) which operate in countries which have ratified it must possess the appropriate certification, while the responsibility for monitoring must belong to the authorities of these companies. If shipping companies employ seafarers from companies which operate in countries which have not ratified the convention, the obligation to comply with its requirements is incumbent on the companies themselves. The MLC is regarded as the charter of seafarers’ rights. It is argued that it will contribute to a change in the image of shipping into one of a modern and socially responsible field of employment which will provide decent working and living conditions for seafarers. It includes clear rules which permit the checking of vessels by port state control inspectors to establish that the provisions on the work and living conditions of the crew members are being implemented. Thus it will contribute to a reduction in the number of employers who indulge in exploitative practices as to seafarers, particularly those coming from low-cost countries. According to the content of the convention, in the case of vessels of above 500 register tons which engage in international voyages or in voyages between foreign ports, the existence of a Maritime Labour Certificate and a Declaration of Maritime Labour Compliance is required. Owners must certify a system of labour management corresponding to the safety management system stipulated by the ISM code, and captains are responsible for the observance of the plans and the provision of suggestions as regards their compliance with the system. The states/flags inspect the owners’ plans in order to confirm and certify the existence of and compliance with what is foreseen in the system. The MLC was brought into force on 20 August 2013. The data from these checks in port, carried out in the first year that it was in force within the framework of the Paris MOU, showed that 113 ships were detained for MLC-related deficiencies, which corresponds to 17.4 per cent of the total number of ship detentions. Furthermore, of the total number of deficiencies on ships which were inspected (46,798), 7.4 per cent related to the implementation of the MLC. Among the deficiencies which led to the detention of ships, those which occurred with the greatest frequency were in connection with ‘payment of wages’, ‘manning level of ships’, ‘health and safety and accident prevention’, ‘food and catering’, and ‘accommodation’ (Paris MOU, 2017). The benefits of the MLC for seafarers and shipping companies, according to the ILO, are presented in Figure 8.1.

The seafarer occupation involves dangers to the safety and life of crews, dangers from natural causes or from unlawful/criminal acts such as piracy, which in recent years has shown an upsurge in certain geographical regions. According to the latest data of the International Maritime Bureau, the number of pirate attacks on ships, though reduced because of the significant reaction of the shipping community world-wide, continues to be large, particularly in specific regions such as South-East Asia and Somalia, while the violence committed in the course of them is intense (ICC IMB, 2017) (Figure 8.2). The evolving nature of piracy has a negative effect on the welfare of seafarers, who, when they fall victim to piracy, find themselves faced with unprecedented violence, especially in cases of Somali pirates (Galani, 2016). There have been a number of cases where the holding as hostages of seafarers by pirates has lasted for months or even years, in conditions described as inhuman, as in the case of the 26 seafarers from the Naham 3, who were held hostage for nearly five years by Somali pirates. The profession of seafarer was criminalised after accidents which caused pollution of the environment or other events the consequences of which were borne by seafarers, even in cases where they had done everything in their power to avert them or to minimise their impacts. This is a feature which has had a negative effect on the attractiveness of the occupation. Research conducted in 2010 on criminalisation of seafarers revealed that 92 per cent of the respondents worried about criminalisation of the maritime profession, while 66 per cent of them declared that the fear of criminalisation has an impact on their feelings about working at sea (Nautilus International, undated). Seafarers like to feel that the company management and their colleagues in the office recognise what they do, they appreciate them and take their opinion into account – something which research confirms is not happening to a desired degree. There have been cases of shipping companies which have managed to survive long periods of crisis in the charter markets, with the smallest possible losses, with the help of the seafarers on their vessels who were not merely the addressees of orders on cost-cutting, but, with the consent of the companies, came to understand the problems and took part in the taking of decisions which concerned themselves.

Research conducted with seafarers members of Nautilus International has shown that 66 per cent of those interviewed believe that they are not given sufficient opportunities to influence the company’s policy which affects their employment and has a direct connection with it, while 95 per cent believe that they should be given such opportunities (Nautilus International, 2010). It should be noted, though, that the percentage of those who believed they are provided with sufficient opportunities increased from 23 per cent in 1991 to 28 per cent in 2001 and 34 per cent in 2010. When asked whether they believe that they are treated by their employers as members of the management structure of the company, 50 per cent of those questioned answered in the affirmative and 50 per cent in the negative, while 89 per cent think that they should be members of the management structure of the company.

❚ The ship’s working environment The ship’s working environment, as has already been pointed out, is also a social environment for as long as the seafarer is engaged. As such, it shows similarities

215

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

with (as well as substantive differences from) total institutions, as those are described by Goffman (1961), as places for work and living where a large number of individuals find themselves in physical confinement away from their broader (family-social) environment, where they carry out their activities, which are organised in a bureaucratic way (Box 8.5). This characteristic calls for intervention on the part of the companies which aim at the effective elimination of these features by way of dealing with their impacts on the working and social life of seafarers, such as, for example, by the provision of communication free of charge, the promotion of a group culture, careful selection of the team, etc.

BOX 8.5 The confined space of ships Ocean-going ships manifest characteristics which resemble features of the organisations described by the sociologist Erving Goffman as total institutions (Poole, 1981; Forsyth & Bankston, 1983; Davies, 1989; Encandela, 1991; Oltedal & McArthur, 2011; Theotokas et al, 2014), given that:

❚

the details of the (social, working) life of these individuals for the length of time that they are members of the crew are enacted in the same place and in the same power structure, controlled by the captain;

❚

the personal activities of these individuals are organised and carried out in conjunction with the activities of other individuals with whom they co-exist;

❚

the time schedule for the carrying out of jobs is tight and is faithfully followed, and under the pressure of a multiplicity of regulations;

❚

the jobs form part of a unified rationalised plan whose aim is the fulfilment of the objectives of the organisation.

Nevertheless, the intensity of the above characteristics varies, given that the arrival on and departure from the ship of the members of the crew are not subject to limitation, while there is scope for mobility in the hierarchy for those who possess the necessary qualifications. The ship, consequently, operates as a contributory open system. A further point of differentiation has to do with the structure of the vessel, in which, in spite of the sharp distinction made between officers and crew, the lines of hierarchy are in some cases blurred, particularly those relating to social life on board the ship (Davies, 1989). The shared experiences of the members of the crew affect and contribute to the creation of a sense of community among them, with respect for the hierarchy (Encandela, 1991). The findings of research show that the seafarers themselves prefer the ship’s organisation to function more as collegial with emphasis on consensus and teamwork, and themselves, as members of the team which pursues the realisation of

216

MANAGEMENT OF HUMAN RESOURCES OF SHIPS

a strategy and objectives, rather than as a formal organisation based on explicit rules and policies (Shea, 2005). Furthermore, the improved possibilities of maintaining on-going communication with the family and social environment add to the characterisation of the ship as an open system.

The working environment on a ship itself gives rise to conditions which differ significantly from those in workplaces on land. The findings of a study in which a comparison is made between the work models and satisfaction of those working in a car factory on land and those of seafarers on a car carrier vessel are indicative of the particularities of the seafarer’s profession, particularly at the present time, with the fierce competition in the world maritime labour market (Box 8.6).

BOX 8.6 Work models and job satisfaction In order to demonstrate the stress and intensification of maritime labour, and particularly the working conditions which prevail on ships, Erol Kahveci (2003) examined two working environments in the same value chain: a car factory and a car carrier vessel. The intensive nature of maritime labour can be seen from the data which he examines and cites, since a car carrier vessel in the 1970s was manned by a crew of 30, and remained in port, on average, 270 hours, whereas in the 2000s the crew had been reduced to 16 and the time spent in port to 13 hours. All the crews whom he studied worked on closed contracts, the term of which was for the officers from four to seven months and for the ratings nine months, without any guarantee of re-engagement. The length of working hours ranged for 23 per cent of the seafarers from 61 to 70 hours, whereas for 77 per cent it was in excess of 70 hours, per week. The corresponding length of working hours in the case of those employed in the car factory ranged from 37 to 40 hours per week. However, the longer working hours had no effect on the remuneration of the seafarers. The wages for 56 per cent of them reached 284 dollars per week, when only 18 per cent of those working in the car factory earned no more than that sum. And yet 73 per cent of the seafarers stated that they were very satisfied or satisfied with their remuneration, regarding it as significantly greater than what they would receive if they worked on land in their country, and appeared to be loyal to their company in a higher percentage than those working in the car factory. Indicative both of the management model and of the Human Resource Management practices implemented in the shipping industry is the fact that 41 per cent of the crews stated that they had never been asked by the company for which they worked about changes in working practices, and 64 per cent had not been asked about issues of crew selection.

217

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

Technological developments in the design, construction and equipment of ships have significantly reduced the number of members of crew required, just as they have at the same time intensified working conditions for seafarers. In the last decade in particular, with the introduction of a series of regulations concerning the operation of the vessel, the workload of seafarers has increased significantly at the same time that the crew numbers have been reduced, a factor which causes fatigue, which in its turn affects aspects of the ship’s operation which have to do with the safety of the vessel and the health at work of the seafarers (Smith et al, 2006). Attention should be drawn in particular to the negative experience of seafarers arising from the fact that the bureaucratic load which they have to handle is especially large, complex and time-consuming. Seafarers believe that they have to devote a great deal of time to jobs which can be regarded as administrative burdens and that a series of jobs which they carry out on board ship, such as, for example, the completion of port and pre-arrival documents, are more complex and time-consuming than is necessary (Danish Maritime Authority, 2013). The regulations seek to focus on the human factor and to rule on issues connected with training and safety, but, since in their drafting factors concerning the workload of seafarers have not always been taken into consideration to the necessary extent, they do not fully succeed in this aim. As regards the International Safety Management Code (ISM), which will be discussed in Chapter 9, the example given in Box 8.7 is typical.

BOX 8.7 The ISM Code and seafarers The purpose of the ISM Code is the regulation of the effect of factors having to do with the way in which work on board ship is organised and carried out. Nevertheless, both on the ship itself and in the company’s offices on land there are points which give rise to a need for more careful design of the safety management systems. Brown and Haugene (1998), for example, propose a model for the assessment of the effect of organisational and management factors on the risk of grounding of a tanker, and draw attention to high risk factors which are not taken into account by the ISM Code. In their study, they cite a series of organisational and management factors which can affect this risk, and conclude that the most important of these are the workload, the organisational culture, the quality of life, formalisation, the evaluation of performance, personnel turnover, personnel selection and benefits. They compare these factors with those which are considered important according to the ISM Code, that is, organisational culture, safety culture, organisational learning, formalisation, co-ordination of work, communication and personnel selection and training process, and observe that the workload, which in the model which they propose is considered the most important factor, is not taken into account by the ISM Code. They also note that the quality of life, benefits and turnover, factors which lead to a limited knowledge and experience of the vessel on which they work, are not taken into account with clarity.

At the same time, the search for low-cost seafarers has led to the existence of multicultural crews, but not always to concern with the socialisation of the members of the crew, and this has resulted in the creation of conditions of alienation. This, in conjunction with the introduction of regulations such as the International Ship and Port Facility Security Code (ISPS Code), which regulates issues in connection with an improvement of the safety levels on ships and in ports, imposes a series of new restrictions on their working environment, and cuts back on seafarers going ashore in ports. It gives rise, as has been aptly pointed out, to conditions which can be likened to ‘house arrest’ on land (Horck, 2005) (Box 8.8).

BOX 8.8 The ISPS Code and the seafarer occupation In January 2005, the ITF carried out research by means of a questionnaire with the purpose of assessing the effectiveness of the ISPS Code and the impacts on seafarers from its implementation. The ISPS Code lays down a series of regulations on the improvement of security levels in the shipping sector. Among them is the introduction of security systems for ships, the appointment of security officers and of a management executive on land with similar duties, the equipment of vessels with the necessary means and security systems and the appointment of officials responsible for security in ports, the taking of measures for the control of access and of the activities of individuals both on board ships and in ports. Taking part in the research were ITF inspectors and representatives of trade unions from various countries. It emerged that the basic issues as regards seafarers and the implementation of the Code were (ITF, 2005): – the increase in the workload and its negative effect on seafarers' performance; – the increase in responsibilities, but without a corresponding increase in wages; – defective education; – the reduction in shore leave from the ship; – problems in connection with obtaining permission to enter the USA; – the difficulties which union representatives have when they board ships to provide their services to the crew.

❚ The seafarers’ profession and gender Shipping, as has already been pointed out, is a male-dominated sector. This applies to an even greater degree to the seafarers’ profession. It is observable that even companies which have a positive attitude to diversity and where the percentage of women among those working in the offices is fairly high, where the seafarers’ profession is concerned, historical reasons and the tradition of

219

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

shipping lead them to the employment of a low proportion of women as crew members (Norden, 2017a). There is, however, a relatively small number of companies which implement a policy which favours the employment of women and, moreover, establish quotas with regard to the development of the relevant magnitudes and to pay, as well as to the provision of equal opportunities in general.2 There are, also, significant difficulties in overcoming the problem of discrimination against women in countries with a major share in the world maritime workforce, such as, for example, the prevalence of practices in education which reproduce prejudice against women, or have a different course structure or tuition fees (Acejo & Abila, 2016; Zhao et al, 2017). Women joining the seafarers’ profession, though it remains a slow and hesitant process, is now a reality which increasingly attracts the interest and attention of international organisations and groups interested in shipping. The percentage of women seafarers in the profession as a whole is estimated to fluctuate around 2 per cent (ITF, 2017b), with significant differences from country to country and with the majority of women working at sea employed as ‘hotel’ staff on passenger and cruise ships (ILO, 2003). Statistics from the European Maritime Safety Agency show that in 2014 the figure for women officers was 2.09 per cent, though with significant differences as between countries. They also show that in some countries there are no women seafarers, while in the countries of the European Union a different framework is in force as to the employment of women seafarers (EMSA, 2016). On the other hand, the BIMCO-ISF report, which for the first time includes data on women seafarers, shows that as a percentage of seafarers as a whole, they account for 1 per cent, but it also shows that the prospects for an increase are positive, as 6.9 per cent of officers in training were women (SMI, 2016). Research into the employment of women in the seafarers’ profession examined the experiences of male members of the crew of the forms of co-operation they already had with their women colleagues on ships as well as their attitudes and apprehensions as to the employment of women on board ships (Theotokas & Tsalichi, 2013). According to the basic hypothesis of the research, the main factor for the determination of the prospects for employment of women in a male-dominated profession such as that of the seafarer is the acceptance which they are accorded by their male colleagues, particularly if it is borne in mind that to a large extent managers of crews of shipping companies who take decisions on the attraction, selection and employment of seafarers are former captains. The research findings confirm that while male seafarers are initially influenced by the stereotypes and prejudices prevalent in the branch and appear to believe that women cannot follow the seafarer’s profession, when they have had the opportunity to co-exist with women on board ship, or, even more so, when they have had experience of working together with a woman colleague, they adopt a positive stance (Figure 8.3). The younger officers appear to be more positively disposed towards the prospect of collaboration with their women colleagues. This, in conjunction with the high percentage of women cadets recorded in the BIMCO-ISF study, gives rise to optimism that as the younger generations advance in their career, the positive attitude towards women will improve. A precondition

❚ The Crew Department of shipping companies As has already been explained, particularly as regards the personnel on the vessels, the task of Human Resource Management is the responsibility of the Crew Department. This department carries out all the processes of Human Resource Management which concern crews, in collaboration at all times with the other departments of the shipping company. The task of the Crew Department is the selection and placement of capable and qualified individuals, their training and the planning of their career, as well as the management of any related issue. The contribution of the Crew Department to the effective and smooth operation of the vessels is of particular importance. The basic activities of the Crew Department have to do with (Downard, 1990):

❚

the composition of the crew, that is, the number, the specialisations and the qualifications of the seafarers and the qualifications required for the manning

221

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

❚

❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚

of each vessel in accordance with the requirements of the flag state where the vessels are registered; the seafarers’ pool, that is, the seafarers required for the manning of the company’s vessel, both those who are already serving and those who are awaiting to replace others who are interrupting their engagement for various reasons (rest, illness, training, etc.); the recruitment and selection of seafarers needed for the formation of the pool; the conditions of service of seafarers; the signing of employment contracts; the placement of seafarers on the vessel; changes in the composition of the crew of vessels; seafarers’ accounts; secondment of seafarers for education and training; labour relations and relations with the maritime labour unions; the search for information, warnings and maintenance of discipline in the event of problems; the dismissal of seafarers; respect for the laws on the employment of seafarers; the insurance cover and pensioning of the seafarers; the personal development and well-being of the seafarers; the provision of advisory services to the seafarers; the keeping of budgets of the cost of the crew of each ship; the keeping of archives with information on the careers of the seafarers.

The need for the existence of a separate Crew Department is due to the fact that even in the case of small companies, the number of the crew and the particular characteristics of maritime labour call for the existence of personnel in the offices concerned with the management of their labour issues. If in the offices of a shipping company which manages five ships 15 staff are employed, the crews can exceed 140 in number, engaged and awaiting engagement. Given that the crew of each ship forms a team which lives and works far from the headquarters of the shipping company, the selection of its composition must be made in such a way as to ensure its cohesion and effectiveness, as well as its ability to operate with good results and safely both in normal and in emergency conditions. The ensuring of the latter characteristic becomes even more difficult in the case of ships with crews of differing nationalities, for reasons which will be explained below. Furthermore, what is typical of the working environment on board ship is the high turnover. As the average length of time of the service of each seafarer may be more than four months in the case of the officers and more than seven in that of the ratings, provision must be made for the recruitment and selection of seafarers for the timely replacement of those who are leaving. At the same time, the replacement must be carried out in such a way as to avoid disturbing the cohesion and effectiveness of the team on each ship. In the case of companies with large fleets which employ large numbers of seafarers, the aim is the creation of teams who will be employed regularly on the same types of vessels, so that, on

222

the one hand, they gain a better knowledge of the working environment and can be more productive, and, on the other, they derive more satisfaction from their work. In the light of the above analysis, it will be obvious that the staffing of the Crews Department rely mostly upon former seafarers, that is, on people who have had experience of working on ships, know the code of communication of seafarers, and are in a position to understand their attitudes and behaviours and to be aware of their needs. As can be seen from Figure 7.5 the basic specifications for the post of Crew Manager is his experience in the position of captain and the ability to organise and plan. At the same time, however, the Crews Department is concerned with the Human Resources Management of the ships. Consequently, it is essential that the personnel of the Department should possess skills relating to Human Resources Management, so that they can effectively carry out processes such as training and development, assessment and dispute resolution.

❚ Human Resources Management practices on ships As was explained in Chapter 2, among the basic characteristics of the shipping industry is the ability of companies to derive resources from the world market. The maritime labour market is global, and its boundaries are determined by a company’s strategy as regards the manning of its vessels. Seafarers from various countries of the world which enter the market on competitive terms, seeking to claim a market share, play a competitive role in it. The countries with the largest overall supply of seafarers are China, the Philippines, Indonesia, Russia and Ukraine; in the case of officers, however, India is included among the basic countries (Figure 8.7). In the light of the BIMCO/ISF data, the supply of the maritime workforce in 2015 amounted to 774,000 officers and 873,500 ratings. Compared with the relative demand for these categories, the data show a deficit (16,500) in the supply of officers, and a surplus (119,000) in the supply of ratings (Figure 8.4) (BIMCO/ISF, 2015). therefore, great, allowing companies, as will be explained below, to make combinations which better satisfy their objectives in relation to cost, quality, etc. For this reason, in many companies, seafarers of more than two nationalities are employed (Box 8.9). On the other hand, the competition of shipping companies for the recruitment and retention of officers is very intense because of their shortage, a fact that is reflected at the level of their wages. Recruitment and selection is a global problem compounded by the negative public image3 of the seafaring profession and the attraction of land-based careers (McLaughlin, 2015). Naturally, the choice of nationality of the crew is determined to a considerable extent by the regime laid down by the state whose flag the vessel bears. In the case of the traditional flags, there is usually provision for composition which includes a minimum number of seafarers of the nationality of the state/flag. On the other hand, in the case of open registers or flags of convenience, there is no restriction as to the nationality of the crew. In any event, this is one of the basic reasons for choosing them on the part of shipping companies. As regards the composition of the crew, a shipping company may choose from different combinations of nationalities for ship-officers, officers of high or low rank and ratings. The world maritime labour market consists of individual markets whose existence renders possible discrimination between groups of seafarers and the payment to them of different wages (Leggate & McConville, 2002). As can be seen from charts in Figures 8.8 and 8.9, in which comparative data for seafarers’ and captains’ remuneration from selected countries are given, on the basis of the ranking carried out by the ICS-ISF in the mid 2000s, which with minor deviations continue to hold good,4 the differences between countries are particularly large. These differences, moreover, are not only between ‘traditional’ and developing countries, but are also observable within each group. In any event, the breadth of the differences between low and highly paid seafarers, both in the case of able seamen and in that of captains, is striking. In order for shipping companies to exploit these differences, they must be in a position not only to identify the individual markets, but also to recruit and engage quality seafarers from them. If the people who make the decisions do not possess the necessary knowledge and information to make this selection, and, at the same time, the cost of acquiring them is great and outweighs the potential

Rewards and compensation Shipping companies seek to attract and employ quality seafarers. Of course, in a competitive labour market, not all companies have the same scope where this is concerned, even if they have the resources and have developed the relevant capabilities. Thus the policies which they implement differ from company to company. Factors which contribute definitively to the differentiation of the Human Resources Management policies and practices for the seafarers are the size of the company and its scope for employing economies of scale. Small companies do not have the same capacity for offering their crews the prospects for career development and permanent employment which large-size companies can, so that their range of options is limited. At the same time, the policies implemented by companies are not in all cases uniform for the whole of the crews. The differentiations have to do with nationality and hierarchy, and the specialisation or the type of vessel on which they are engaged. More particularly as to the last, it is a fact that the remuneration package differs as between liquid cargo vessels, particularly highly specialised ones such as LNGs or Chemicals, and dry cargo vessels. The cost of crews is the sum of the direct and indirect cost of the seafarers. The direct cost includes expenditure directly in connection with the seafarer wages, as well as travelling and catering expenses, whereas the indirect cost includes expenditure on the benefits which are specified in the company’s agreement with the seafarer (Drewry, 2006). Within the framework of what is possible for it, each company seeks to attract and retain quality seafarers by offering a series of incentives which involve remuneration (monetary incentives) and more general conditions of employment (non-monetary incentives). The conditions in the freight markets and the levels

Pay (basic, overtime, allowances, etc.). In a number of countries, the level of pay is determined by the employment contracts agreed between employees’ and employers’ unions, or is linked to these. This applies to seafarers who are employed in their national shipping. In the case of foreign seafarers, particularly those from low cost countries, pay is determined in most cases by the agreements signed between the International Transport Workers' Federation/ ITF (Box 8.11) and employers’ representatives after bargaining, which are considered the most significant case of transnational union bargaining coordination in existence (Lillie, 2004). Extra-ordinary allowances. These are related to the performance of the specific seafarer or of the vessel during a certain period. Rest or waiting pay. This is usually paid to the ships’ officers, as a rule to captains and engineers, as an incentive/commitment for them to be re-engaged on a vessel of the company after their rest period. Its sum corresponds to a small part of the salary which they receive during the course of their engagement, and is paid at the time of their re-engagement on one of the company’s ships. Profit-sharing with the provision of shares and bonuses (for ship officers) (Box 8.12). Pension plan.

BOX 8.11 The ITF employment contracts The ITF contracts are of three categories. More specifically (ITF, 2017a): – The ITF Standard Agreement, which sets the standard terms and conditions applicable to all seafarers serving on any ship in respect of which a special agreement between the Union of seafarers, an affiliate of the ITF and the owner/ agent of the ship. – The ITF Uniform Total Crew Cost (TCC) Collective Agreement on crews of ships under flags of convenience, which sets the standard terms and conditions as regards all seafarers who serve on ships as to which a special agreement is in force between the ITF and their owner or manager. The TCC contract is regarded as a model in the shipping industry. – The ITF International Bargaining Forum (IBF) Agreement which sets the standard terms and conditions that are applicable only to seafarers serving on ships owned or operated by companies that are members of the Joint Negotiating Group in respect of which there is in existence an IBF Special Agreement.
