BOX 8.12 Incentives systems In mid 2007, the Danish TORM shipping company announced a programme of incentives designed for its personnel on land and for the officers on its ships – for 365 employees in total – which included bonuses and the possibility of acquiring shares (TORM, 2007). On the basis of the programme, the total sum of the bonuses would be determined by the annual yield on TORM’s invested assets in relation to their budgeted yield. Seventy-five per cent of the bonuses would be in cash and the remaining 25 per cent in shares, which the employees would acquire on condition that they worked in the company for the next two years. One part of the bonus for each employee would be determined by his pay and another part by his performance. According to the announcement, if the company achieved the budgeted yield on its invested assets, the sum of the bonuses would amount to 9.5 m. dollars. As regards the possibility of acquiring shares, 50 per cent of these could be bought at a predetermined price, and the other 50 per cent at a price which would be determined in TORM’s annual report after a period of three years, that is, the price of the shares for 2007 would be set in March 2010. All these shares could be sold by the employees three years after their acquisition and at the latest within a period of three years; that is, shares acquired in 2007 could be sold after 2010 and no later than 2013. Similarly, at the same period, the Tsakos Group decided to distribute shares of the New York-listed Tsakos Energy Navigation-TEN (www.tenn.gr) company, of a value of 14 m. dollars, to its employees, including seafarers, to whom it would make available 100,000 shares (Newsfront, 2007).

Non-monetary incentives

❚

❚ ❚ ❚ ❚

The possibility of permanent employment, that is, re-engagement on the company’s ships after the seafarer’s rest period. As will be obvious, small shipping companies cannot always provide such an incentive, as the size of their fleet does not give rise to as many jobs as would be required for all its seafarers to have permanent employment. This incentive is most important for ratings. The possibility of development for the officers, that is, the provision of a career plan. The possibility of personal progress by means of the provision of incentives for training and updating of their skills and knowledge. Provision of a job on land after the completion of their career at sea. Better working conditions (modern ships, benefits on board, etc.) (Box 8.13).

229

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚

Prestige and position in the company. The possibility of access to the Internet and of communication with the family and social environment. Facilities for recreation. Provision of services to the families of seafarers to help them to deal with emergency needs. Employment on the same type of vessel. Benefits after the completion of their career.

BOX 8.13 Access to communication as an incentive to attraction A large number of shipping companies have, during the past decade, been investing in the installation of systems to provide constant access to the Internet for the whole of their fleets, not only for reasons of operational effectiveness (better communication, control of the vessels from the office, provision of services on board ship, training of the crew), and of better relations with customers, but also in order to attract trained seafarers (Brostrom, 2008). In the case of some companies, the installation of these systems forms part of the corporate social report in the ‘welfare of seafarers on board’ section. The MOL company, for example, set as a target for 2015 to “further improve on-board welfare programmes”, and for the achievement of this aim, “installed FBB (\*3) or VSAT (\*4) fleet broadband with in-house chartered vessels, and confirmed the establishment of an environment in which broadband service is freely available to seafarers” (MOL, 2017). The retention of seafarers in the profession in general, and with the same employer, is one of the greatest challenges for world shipping, given the shortage of qualified seafarers. Studies have shown that there is a series of factors which contribute positively or negatively to remaining in this occupation and to satisfaction with it (Figure 8.10). As is to be expected, the provision of the possibility of communication on an on-going basis, with the external environment of the ship, is a factor which affects both the conditions on board and job satisfaction. This is particularly true of the younger generations of seafarers, since it contributes to dealing with what is put forward as a basic weakness of the profession by this category of seafarers: absence from family and friends (ITF-Intertanko, 2009; Hult & Ljung, 2012). This explains the increased interest in the issue of communication, both at a practical level, and at the level of research.5 The staffing of ships nowadays relies on generation Y (and soon will rely on the generation Z), for whom the new technologies and social networks form an integral

230

MANAGEMENT OF HUMAN RESOURCES OF SHIPS

Figure 8.10 Factors influencing retention in the seafaring profession6

part of their everyday life. They communicate, are informed, are educated and research by the use of the Internet and social media. It is, therefore, unrealistic to expect this generation to remain on board ship for months without communication with the outer environment (McCarthy, 2012). It is important that they are positive about themselves paying the cost of communication where this is not provided by the company in a significantly larger percentage than their older colleagues (Papachristou et al, 2015). Thus, the provision of the possibility of access to the Internet and of communication with the family and social environment is a basic precondition for their motivation and for them to remain in the profession, and it is also a crucial factor for selection of the company for which they will work. According to recent study, while 88 per cent of those responded were provided Internet access, the percentage of those who had access to video calling, to social media and to personal emails, which are essential parts of maintaining contact with family, were 6 per cent, 34 per cent and 57 per cent, respectively (Nautilus International, 2017). The importance of the existence of conditions which ensure communication with the external environment is also stressed by the Maritime Labour Convention,7 where it is clearly stated that: “Consideration should also be given to including the following facilities at no cost to the seafarer, where practicable” and “reasonable access to ship-

It is clear that for the new, and even the not so new, generation of seafarer – and would-be seafarer – internet access at sea is considered a necessity and can no longer be considered to be a luxury (North, 2017:8).

231

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

to-shore telephone communications, and email and Internet facilities, where available, with any charges for the use of these services being reasonable in amount”. Results of recent study on the issue, however, show that a great percentage of seafarers (67%) who responded is not aware of this recommendation (Nautilus International, 2017). The facilitation of communication with the external environment, and particularly with the family, is, in the light of the above, an essential part of the seafarer’s welfare and well-being, and from this point of view constitutes a best practice for companies. Communication is one aspect of welfare. The other is the facilitation of being informed and of recreation by means of the provision of special tools and packages which make it possible for companies to provide simultaneously all the ships in their fleet content involving news, sports, entertainment which seafarers can follow by means of the mobile phone, tablet or the televisions on board the vessels (Digital Ship, 2017). A considerable number of companies supply their seafarers with such packages, and it is expected that the number will increase significantly in the immediate future. The welfare of seafarers, at the same time, is also the strongest incentive for investment in communications technologies which will permit a better connection between ship and office (Fraunhofer CML & GL, 2013).

A large number of references to companies which have followed the same strategy of developing communications technology in order to take advantage of the benefits which this brings both to the effective management of the ship and to the satisfaction of the crew could be cited. What is certain is that with the passage of time, the fulfilment of the technological conditions and a reduction in the cost of connection, the provision for those working on board ship of the conditions for unimpeded and constant communication with the external environment will be the rule for well organised shipping companies which seek to recruit and retain quality crews, given that, as research has shown, this serves as a criterion for the owner’s selection for the new generations of seafarers (Papachristou et al, 2015; Futurenautics, 2015).

❚ Crew management and competitive advantage It has already been explained that Human Resources Management which concerns crews may be carried out either within the shipping company, or in part by the appropriate department within it and in part by an external associate, or may be commissioned in its entirety from an external associate. In the second and third instances, the company outsources processes which traditionally it would have carried out itself. The outsourcing of an activity involves risks and entails cost in the transactions, which, as the case may be, may exceed the cost which the activity incurs if it is carried out within the company. The decision to

232

MANAGEMENT OF HUMAN RESOURCES OF SHIPS

outsource an activity is bound up with the recognition of the company’s core functions, that is, the functions connected with its competitive advantage. As will be explained in Chapter 10, in which the issue of outsourcing in shipping will be discussed, the decision on outsourcing is linked to the answer to the following questions (Arnold, 2000):

❚ ❚ ❚

Is the activity specialised? Is the activity strategically important? Is the activity a constituent part of the company’s competitive advantage?

Obviously, each shipping company gives a different answer, always relating to the Human Resources Management of the ships, to these three questions. This also explains the fact that there are differing options in the matter of whether to outsource or not the relevant process (Papadimitriou et al, 2005). In the light of the answers to the three questions above, companies which regard Human Resources Management on ships as a core competence treat their crews as a source of distinct skills which contributes to the reinforcement of their competitive advantage. Where this is the case, specific systems and policies which lead to this are designed and implemented. The objective is the creation of long-term relations of collaboration with the seafarers and of conditions suitable for the cultivation of a sense of security at work. Even if they choose to commission a part or the whole of this process from a third company, they seek to retain control, that is, they continue to have in their organisation a mechanism which allows them to monitor the way in which the third company carries out the duties which have been commissioned from it, particularly in relation to training, assessment and the cohesion of the team. This undoubtedly gives rise to hidden cost, which, however, is necessary if the likelihood of opportunism on the part of the third company is to be reduced. Companies which treat the crews as a source of distinct skills select their personnel not on the basis exclusively or chiefly of the cost, but also calculate the prospects of their contribution to their competitiveness, as they examine their qualitative characteristics, which are what in the end shape their productivity and effectiveness. This treatment of them is the product of a long-term approach which leads to the development and implementation of Human Resources Management systems, and which recognises the individual identity of each seafarer, and provides:

❚ ❚ ❚

a system of incentives for the improvement of his performance and his retention with the company, a career plan, and training and development which ensures, on the one hand, the improvement of his performance and, on the other, his personal progress. This is considered one of the best practices applied in crew management (see Figure 8.11).

Within this framework, particular attention is paid to the careful planning of the composition of the team of seafarers who serve on each ship, so as to achieve

233

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

the greatest possible cohesion and to create the conditions for co-ordinated work, both in normal conditions and in crisis situations. It should be noted that, as proposed by a recent study, many shipping companies re-insource crewing activities to gain more control and quality than with purely “temporaly workers” provided by crewing agencies (Fraunhofer CML & GL, 2013).

BOX 8.14 Fleet development strategy and crews Thenamaris (Ships Management) Inc. (www.thenamaris.com) is a traditional operator of the bulk shipping industry which adopts a long-term approach to the management of human resources at the offices ashore and on board the ships, aspiring to be a preferred employer. According to the Executive Director of the company in the period in question, the availability of qualified personnel of both groups was historically a crucial factor for the decision to invest in new ships and for the definition of the total number of ships under its management. “We have been very-very cautious and conservative on this issue. We have learned over our lifetime of around 40 years as a corporation that our most valuable assets and most critical assets are our people” M. Vordonis, in Mayer (2010:52).

Companies which consider that Human Resources Management is not a core process usually regard their crews as an item of cost. Obviously, in this case, what is sought is the outsourcing of a part or of the whole of the processes of the Human Resources Management of the ships, either to independent ship management companies or to crewing agents, and the selection of seafarers from the competitive maritime labour market, whose employment permits a reduction in payroll cost. Such an approach, which is of a short-term character, frequently gives rise to objective weaknesses in the organic integration of these seafarers

234

MANAGEMENT OF HUMAN RESOURCES OF SHIPS

into the Human Resources Management of the company, weaknesses which are likely to undermine the team’s spirit and dynamic. Furthermore, it will perhaps lead to the opposite results to those desired, as it may serve as a counter-incentive not only for those seafarers who do not know whether they will continue in the future to work for the same company, but also for the ships’ officers who are called upon to manage groups with characteristics which do not favour a team spirit, the dissemination, reproduction and broadening of knowledge, or commitment to the realisation of the ships’ objectives (see also Box 4.1). In the last analysis, even if an employee is paid low wages, and, therefore, is regarded as cheap, he/she is in reality expensive as he/she does not have the knowledge, experience or the commitment required for the company to be profitable. On the other hand, a well-paid employee can be very effective from the point of view of cost (Pfeffer, 1997). This observation explains the choice of shipping companies from traditional maritime nations (i.e., Norway, Denmark, Germany, Greece) of the employment of seafarers from their country of origin whose payroll cost is higher than that of seafarers from the new shipping countries (Figures 8.8 and 8.9). This happens even when the flags which they choose for their ships do not set limits as to the nationality of the seafarers (Theotokas & Progoulaki, 2007b). All the above shows that an examination of the competitiveness of seafarers in terms of cost alone is totally restrictive, as it overlooks their qualitative characteristics, which determine their productivity. Seafarers are an item of cost with a soul and not a supplement to the ‘ship’ as a technical system (Gerstenberger, 2002). A company’s ships may have the most perfect systems and be supported by similar systems on land, but they only operate effectively if they are manned by capable and satisfied human resources. Various structural changes in shipping in recent years, in conjunction with technological development, have led gradually to the establishment of a policy which concentrates on the ships’ officers and puts the lower crews in second place or ignores them. Companies focus on the qualifications, but, above all, on the experience of the officers, but not to the same extent on those of the ratings (Gerstenberger, 2002). Nevertheless, the safe and effective operation of the vessel depends upon the technical skills and capacity for team work of the crew as a whole, regardless of hierarchy (Commission of the European Communities, 2001). Apart from pay, there are other factors which lead to superior performance. Seafarers wish to feel that the company’s management and their colleagues in the offices recognise what they do, appreciate them and take their opinion into account. Individuals are interested in the outflows (remuneration), but they are equally interested in the process which generate these outflows, and wish to know that their opinion is listened to, even if it is not adopted. It has been established that managers who believe that the company’s processes are fair show high levels of trust and commitment, and, consequently, provide their collaboration willingly. This leads to the conclusion that individuals are likely to trust the systems and to collaborate without reservations when they are aware that the processes are fair, regardless of whether they themselves lose or gain by them.

235

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

A series of studies has shown that fair processes favour an increase in commitment to the organisation, of performance at work, of service to customers, of job satisfaction, and encourage the appropriate behaviour. Of course, if individuals feel that, in the end, with the implementation of fair processes they are never the winners, then their effectiveness is doubtful. In any event, however, the absence of fair processes, particularly when it is combined with unsatisfactory outflows on the part of the company to the employees, has a disruptive effect and undermines the cohesion of the team and, eventually, its effectiveness. While dissatisfaction is noted among crew members over inequalities in pay (Wu, 2004), in other studies (Kahveci, 2003) it is pointed out that in spite of the major difference in remuneration between seafarers from the traditional shipping countries of the West and those from the developing countries, the latter are satisfied because this pay is much higher than what they would receive from work on land if they were employed in their own country. More specifically, in a study concerned with the importance of collective bargaining for seafarers who work on ships with flags of convenience, it is reported that the remuneration which seafarers receive from certain low-cost countries can be up to 22 times greater than that received by industrial workers in their countries of origin (Lillie, 2004). However, even where remuneration is satisfactory, the absence of satisfaction arising from a sense of fair treatment can have a negative effect on the cohesion of the team. Consequently, the development of Human Resources Management systems which will be addressed to the whole of the personnel of a company and will give rise to the conviction that everyone is treated in the same fair manner by it is called for. It must, of course, be stressed at this point that fair processes in cases where crew management is entrusted to another company or to crewing agents is a matter largely for the latter company. This, of course, does not mean that the commissioning company is not called upon to take action to ensure that fair processes are in force. In the case of the Maersk company, for example, we were told that in 2017 they “will be launching an awareness campaign across all our businesses for procurement and HR functions about third-party labour responsibilities” (A.P. Moller Maersk, 2017). It should be noted that monitoring of crewing agents by the shipping company is one of the key performance indicators of the TMSA. In the light of the above analysis, a series of crucial questions arises which every company should take into account to the extent that it is seeking to derive a competitive advantage from the crews of its ships. By way of indication, some of those questions could be the following:

❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚

236

Are the skills of all its ships’ crews recorded and known? Are these skills made use of to the greatest possible extent? Do they invest in the personal development of the members of their crews? Do they provide incentives for higher performance? Do they create a member’s identity and commitment in each member of the crew? Do they seek by means of training and other systems which they implement the transformation of seafarers into agents of the company’s organisational culture (Box 8.15)?

MANAGEMENT OF HUMAN RESOURCES OF SHIPS

BOX 8.15 Training of crews on board ship with Mitsui OSK Lines In order to support the training of its crews, Mitsui OSK Lines (MOL), apart from the operation of training centres at different points throughout the world, purchased the Ginga Maru, which had been the training ship of the naval schools of Japan for many years. MOL bought the ship in 2007, and, after modernising it, renamed it MOL Spirit and began its operation as a training vessel. As it states in its report on corporate social responsibility, this was the first time a shipping company had acquired what was exclusively a training ship. It operated until 2013, and more than 2,200 cadets were trained on it (MOL, 2017). During the course of the programme, the trainees, who came from various countries, acquired a basic knowledge of the seafarers’ profession and learnt the standards and practices of the company, particularly in relation to safety. MOL’s aim was that its seafarers should receive the same education as Japanese students. By means of the training on board the ship, in parallel with the development of their skills, the cadets shared their experiences with others and were able to gain an understanding of the different cultures and to take on the identity of a MOL seafarer. After 2013, MOL embarked upon the operation of a training programme for cadets by means of which it provides practical training on active vessels of the company which are equipped with training infrastructures and installations for cadets (MOL, 2017).

❚ ❚

Are feedback mechanisms and procedures which result in an increase of devotion and commitment of crews to the realisation of the company’s aims implemented? Do the Human Resources Management practices favour the development of relationships of trust?

Some of the above questions are taken into account in the self-assessment which companies managing tankers carry out for their ranking in one of the four levels of the TMSA in the third area, that is, the attraction and management of the personnel of the vessel, which consists of two parts. The aim of the first part is the manning of all the vessels with a competent crew which fully understands its roles and responsibilities and can work together as a team, whereas the aim of the second is, by means of strong leadership, the promotion by the management of the company of the concepts of safety and the protection of the environment at all its levels.

237

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

seafaring labour market, multiculturalism ❚ Global and Human Resources Management of vessels ❚ Traditionally, shipping companies have sought to employ crews with a shared national origin, and therefore with a common culture and common values, thus achieving the greatest possible cohesion on every vessel. Gradually, this reality has been changing, just as the conditions in the maritime labour market at world level have been changing. In developed countries, the seafarer’s occupation has ceased to be as attractive as it was in the past, given that the opportunities for employment on land have multiplied, while the cost of employing seafarers from the traditional countries has increased. At the same time, the supply of the maritime workforce has increased, since seafarers from low cost countries are entering the labour market. In order, initially, to meet the needs of manning their vessels, and then to reduce the payroll cost, shipping companies began to employ seafarers from low cost countries. Thus, world shipping gradually shifted from the single-nationality to the multicultural crew, with seafarers of two or more nationalities. In the SIRC study it is reported that on almost 65 per cent of merchant vessels strategies of manning with a multinational crew are implemented (Kahveci et al, 2001). Data from studies of Nautilus International in 2001 and 2010 reveal an increase in the scale of multinational crews onboard the ships the respondents of the survey were serving (Nautilus International, 2010a). While in 2001 24 per cent of the number of ships had crew of single nationality and of 26 per cent of two nationalities, in 2010 the percentages decreased to 13 per cent and 10 per cent, respectively. On the other hand, the percentage of ships with

Figure 8.12 Different nationalities onboard ships Source: Compiled by the author based on data of Nautilus International (2010a).

238

MANAGEMENT OF HUMAN RESOURCES OF SHIPS

three or more nationalities increased substantially. The percentage of ships with six or more nationalities increased from 13 per cent to 30 per cent (Figure 8.12). The new reality of multinational crews has led gradually to changes in management in the offices of companies on land. Moreby wrote in 1990 that a probable scenario for the future would see ships manned completely by foreign crews, and technical managers in the companies’ offices on land of the same nationality as the foreign seafarers (Moreby, 1990). This is today the actual situation. For reasons which have been explained previously, the employment of low cost seafarers has been combined with the outsourcing of the processes of Human Resources Management dealing with these seafarers, thus contributing to a reduction of payroll cost for companies, but giving rise to a series of particularities and problems of an operational character. The commissioning of the relevant processes either from management companies or crewing agents may have entailed limited scope for control by the shipping companies over the Human Resources Management practices which they implemented. Although the real employer of the seafarers is the crewing company, all the efforts which might be made by the shipping companies meet with limitations, since the seafarers’ job satisfaction is chiefly defined by their relationship with their employer. A series of bad practices, accompanied by the exploitation of crews, the impacts of which are felt by both the seafarers and the companies which employ them on their ships, have been observed (Box 8.16). It is proposed to shipowners to be very careful about those who ‘provide crews’, particularly those who make their appearance in low cost countries supplying seafarers (Drewry, 2006). The existence of ‘sub-standard’ crewing agents negatively affects the public image of shipping and of the seafarer’s profession (Papadimitriou et al, 2005). Thus, efforts are being made for the responsibility for bad practices to be blamed on the shipping companies themselves, so that they have an incentive to take action to avoid them. One such effort is the introduction by the TMSA of indicators as regards the relation between the shipping company and crewing agents, such as the annual inspection of the crewing agents used by the shipping company, so as to ensure that their practices are consistent with the recruitment and selection practices of the ship’s manager. Many shipping companies have been faced and are still faced with cultural differences as an unavoidable effect of their pursuit of payroll cost-cutting by the

BOX 8.16 Bad practices in crew management As regards bad practices in crew management, the following statement by a Chinese seafarer is typical (Wu, 2004:77): Now I am working for a Taiwan company through a crewing agency. As 3rd officer, the company pay me $1200 monthly and I can receive $850, or 70% of my salary. The rest is taken out by the current agency every month.

239

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

selection of crews from low cost countries. In practical terms, two categories of seafarers have resulted from this approach: seafarers from the company’s own country, and foreigners. Different Human Resources Management may be applied as to each category. However, it is necessary to develop Human Resource Management systems in which the factor of cultural origin is taken into account, and not simply the minimising of the impacts of differentness is sought, but the employment of features which can, under certain given conditions, serve as an advantage for the company (Progoulaki, 2008). Among the instructions on best practices of the International Shipping Federation it is stated that most of those employed on ships have better working conditions and are better paid than they would be on land in their own country. One special paragraph entitled ‘Discrimination and ill-treatment’ states the following (ISF, 2001):

❚

❚

Any physical, social or mental abuse, whether caused by discrimination on the basis of race, colour, religious affiliation or gender, or simply because one person takes a dislike to another and pursues that dislike actively and in manner which causes distress to the other person, should be prevented. Employers should adopt and implement a policy designed to prevent discrimination and abuse of all types.

The avoidance of discrimination and ill-treatment results from systematic efforts on the part of companies. Just as the creation of a safety culture within the company is not a question of the implementation of the ISM Code and other related regulations (see next chapter), but of commitment of the management, training of all the personnel and of a change of perceptions, which require time to yield results, so the transition from the stage of the rejection of differentness to the stage of unification presupposes commitment on the part of the management, training of those involved, supporting action and time. With regard to training on discrimination issues, however, lack of it is reported.8 According to a study of multicultural crews on ships in Greek ownership, the investigation of the actual conditions which prevail on the ships, and not the taking of decisions on the basis of stereotypes and ideas derived from tradition, is of particular importance (Theotokas & Progoulaki, 2007a). In addition, it is stated that the choices of Greek seafarers as regards the seafarers of different nationalities who are easiest to work with differed significantly from the corresponding choices of the executives of the shipping companies. More specifically, while the managing personnel of companies thought that seafarers from the Philippines, Russia and Ukraine could work well with Greek seafarers, Greek seafarers who worked with these nationalities were in agreement as regards the Filipinos, but disagreed about the Ukrainians and Russians. Given then that, at the present time, apart from specialisation and the hierarchy, a factor for differentiation in the team is cultural origin, it is essential that this factor is taken into account at the stage of the selection of the members who make up the multicultural team. For this purpose, a knowledge of the ranking of

240

MANAGEMENT OF HUMAN RESOURCES OF SHIPS

different nationalities as to specific dimensions, as, for example, those defined by Hofstede (1984) in his studies, is of use. A culture can be analysed in the light of:

❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚

power distance, individualism vs. collectivism, uncertainty avoidance, masculinity vs. feminity, long term orientation, indulgence vs. restraint.

It is to be expected that the ability to co-operate of individuals of different cultures is connected with their approach to each of these dimensions. This analysis, of course, calls for special attention, as the dimensions of culture were derived from research carried out by Hofstede within the context of those working for a company world-wide who differ as to their work profile from seafarers. In any event, however, it can be used for the assessment and understanding of the likenesses and differences between the various nationalities (Theotokas & Progoulaki, 2007a). In the light of the above analysis, the handling of cultural difference presupposes an enhancement of companies’ Human Resources Management systems with systems which will eliminate its negative dimensions. At the same time, it calls for managing personnel in the offices and of captains on the ships with capabilities in the management of cultural difference. These executives should be marked by cosmopolitanism and should not be ethnocentric but adapted to the different cultures, should speak a number of languages, respect the convictions of others and think in universal terms. Captains in particular need to possess abilities which permit them to act as the link between the company and its customers and other partners, and to be at the same time line managers and managers of the human resources; they should bear in mind the fact that seafarers with differing cultural origins should in the shortest possible time when they are on board the ship overcome stereotypes and negative ideas, and be at the stage of unification, as this is required by the cohesion of the team and its effectiveness in normal conditions and in emergencies. It is, therefore, necessary that they should know the stages of awareness of difference (Box 8.18) and adapt their leadership behaviour in the light of the classification of members of the team. The development of these skills requires training, which, however, is not always provided to the necessary extent (Box 8.17). It has been noted that the training of officers and captains in soft skills, in which are included leadership, decision taking, human resources management, dispute resolution, etc., is defective (Clayton, 2012). They are trained in developing navigational skills, safety, etc., but not to develop skills which are crucial for the complex situations which they frequently encounter (Corbett, 2014). Various companies choose to follow different paths with regard to this. For example, Rickmers Shipmanagement is a company which has chosen to provide its officers with training in leadership. Training of this kind has led to improvements in a number of fields, particularly in relation to the crew, the management of knowledge and communication with third parties (Corbett, 2014).

“The ship Master is the key person for the effective and efficient operation of the ship. In his/her portfolio of activities are included almost all tasks of senior managers. In parallel, they are the leaders of the team, the persons who can inspire, engage, motivate, train and reward people. This important role of the ship Master has not received the expected attention of research on HRM in shipping” (Theotokas et al, 2014). “… shore-based managers who are in charge of a budget of a few thousand dollars get training in management and leadership but not officers and captains who are in charge of crew, assets and cargo worth tens of millions of dollars.” Bjoern Sprotte, Head of Maritime Services of Rickmers Shipmanagement, in Corbett (2014:12).

241

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

BOX 8.17 Power to captains At this point, it is worth pointing out another dimension which is connected with the prestige which shipping itself assigns to captains and officers generally. It was mentioned in a previous unit that in the past the master – captain – had more powers and prestige than is the case today. The performance of the ship as a productive unit depends to a large degree on the performance of the vessel’s captain and officers. And while in the selection of managing executives on land the owner of the company himself is often involved, this is rarely the case where the choice of the captain is concerned, even though he manages an asset worth many tens of millions of dollars. There are some owners who take part themselves in the choice of the master, but they are regarded as ‘old school’ and backward, rather than forwardlooking (Clayton, 2012). While the knowledge and experience of the captain and officers are adequate for them to manage the vessel, they are not sufficiently made use of in companies. This observation also brings out the importance of the different approaches to the running of the shipping business and permits an understanding of the fact that in a world-wide industry with a marked tendency towards consolidation, the old school of owners, encountered chiefly in small to medium-size companies, continues to make use of the knowledge and experience created on the ship and to operate successfully particularly in countries in which the family model remains strong.9

BOX 8.18 The stages of awareness of difference The stages which intervene between low and high awareness of difference in an individual are as follows:10 Denial. The individual develops localist approaches to the world, has no awareness of cultural differences and in extreme cases openly undervalues other cultures. Defence. The individual feels that his own perception of the world is threatened, adopts negative stereotypes and believes that his own culture is superior. Minimisation. The individual conceals or undervalues cultural differences and concentrates on those who are like himself in a group. Acceptance. The individual accepts differentness in behaviours and values while recognising the validity of other ways of thinking and perceiving the world. Adaptation. The individual begins to understand others with different cultural characteristics and transposes himself from the one cultural approach to the other. Integration. The individual adopts a multicultural attitude which permits the incorporation of the differences and adaptation to a level of knowledge and behaviour.

242

MANAGEMENT OF HUMAN RESOURCES OF SHIPS

❚ Management of cultural diversity The employment of multicultural crews has advantages which, however, cannot be exploited by the use of the management practices which prevail in many companies or with the limited awareness of culture which many managing executives have. Included among the advantages are the provision of a diverse range of responses and input from crew members who tend to use different intellectual processes and patterns, the improvement of crew members’ knowledge of the world, the ability to recruit excellent staff from a larger pool of seafarers, the ability to make use of crew member’s culture or language in doing business with people of the same nationality as the crew member and the ability to build on the positive characteristics of each crew member’s culture (Horck, 2005). In research conducted at sea into multinational crews, it is stressed that these are not only viable – when, that is, they are effectively supported – but can be exceptionally successful (Kahveci et al, 2001). It has been concluded, moreover, that co-operation and the degree of unification of the crew increases as the number of nationalities of the members of the team increases. Shipowners link the advantages of multinational crews chiefly with training and learning, as well as with discipline on board, whereas, for their part, seafarers consider that the motivation for working together with seafarers of other nationalities creates an environment which protects and facilitates the development of healthy personal and working relations. In the study, a series of changes are proposed which will lead to the maximisation of the advantages arising from the employment of multinational crews, changes which concern the ensuring of a good knowledge of English (or of another shared language) in the case of officers and lower crews, the adoption of policies which encourage the retention of crews, the promotion of social activities at sea, the development of Human Resources Management skills in captains and first engineers, the adoption of anti-racist policies and practices and the avoidance of the use of material which encourages the development of stereotypes. On the other hand, of course, studies with specific national contexts produce different results, according to which cultural diversity has a negative effect on the job satisfaction of seafarers, or is treated as a negative side of the occupation (Guo et al, 2005). An analysis of the subject in respect to Greek shipping shows that multiculturalism does not constitute a problem. What is a problem is the way in which it is managed both on the ship itself and by the offices on land (Theotokas & Progoulaki, 2007a). How far a shipping company will be able to make use of the advantages provided by the employment of a multinational crew and manage in an appropriate way the issues from which weaknesses arise is a function of the approach which they are going to follow. For the management of multiculturalism in such a way that it is a fundamental skill for a shipping company, a framework which relies on the resources based view of Human Resources has been proposed (Progoulaki, 2008). In using this framework, the attitude of the company towards cultural differences, the degree to which the criteria for the achievement of a sustainable competitive advantage, the short-term charges on the increase of the cost of crews and the long-term impacts on the company’s competitiveness

243

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

must be taken into consideration. The options proposed as regards the management of cultural differences are four in number, each of which has different impacts in the short and the long term (Progoulaki, 2008, 2011; Progoulaki & Theotokas, 2016). In the case of the first, the company ignores cultural differences and seeks to make use of the benefits which result from the contribution of the multicultural crew to the reduction of cost resulting from its lower wages. In this case, the short-term charges are low, but the long-term impacts on the company’s competitiveness may be negative. If it chooses the minimising of the negative impacts resulting from cultural differences, the shipping company intervenes on the ships or in the offices by the appointment of mediators or of support groups. The mediators may either be of the same nationality as the foreign seafarers and be placed in the company’s offices, or come from the country in which the company has its headquarters and be placed on the ship. The latter instance involves an officer with special training and special duties. The support groups may consist of individuals of a different culture and educational/professional background and provide on-going support on the vessels and to the company as a whole. If a company maintains a more active stance on the matter of culture, it sets additional criteria for the selection of personnel, in an effort to deal with possible problems, and to contribute to the development of skills which relate to the management of cultural differences on the ships and in the offices, satisfying the criterion of rarity. If a shipping company chooses the minimisation of the negative impacts, but undertaking a more active role in the matter of the management of multiculturalism, it must choose the systematic development of skills in all the personnel of the vessel and the personnel on land who are involved in the management of the ship, by means of the provision of on-going special training. In this way, it will respond to the criterion of imitation (proposed by the Resource Based View of HR), since the human resources will have developed skills which, even if they are copied, will not be able to have the same results. This will lead in the short term to an increase in cost, and in the long term to the development of a competitive advantage. In the case of the fourth alternative, the company seeks to utilise human resources’ cultural diversity by developing a multicultural corporate culture and an integrated system of strategic HRM. A condition for the implementation of this strategy is the support of all the employees by the company’s organisation and the systems which it has at its disposal. This allows the company to meet the criteria of Value, Rareness, Imitation and Organisation of the VRIO model on HRM (Barney, 1995). This strategy, which is found in very rare cases (Progoulaki, 2011), requires time, effort and cost; however, it represents a longterm approach that contributes to a company’s effort to achieve a sustainable competitive advantage (Progoulaki & Theotokas, 2016).
