Management of safety, quality, environment, health and energy – The integrated approach

252

272

The safety of human life and property and the protection of the environment are aims which lie at the very centre of interest of the international shipping community, and have done particularly for the last 25 years. In that period, regulations, codes and conventions have been brought into force by the International Maritime Organisation, the implementation of which was mandatory for shipping companies, such as the International Safety Management Code (ISM) Code. In addition, various states or different unions of states have unilaterally proceeded to the taking of measures, such as the USA in the introduction of the Oil Pollution Act of 1990 and the European Union in the case of the Erika packages, with a view to the improvement of the levels of safety of vessels in their territorial waters. At the same time, apart from safety, the quality of services provided and the protection of the environment have been placed at the centre of interest of the shipping industry by means of the adoption by shipping companies of standards ensuring quality (ISO 9001) and environmental management (ISO 14001). Moving in the same direction, certain shippers set specific demands in connection with the performance of the management systems of shipping companies in the fields of safety, quality and environmental responsibility. Unlike the regulations which are concerned with safety, the adoption of the other standards of management is not compulsory, and for this reason they are not applied by the whole of shipping companies. In any event, however, they contribute to the creation of an increasingly more structured and strict framework of operation for shipping companies.

S A F E T Y M A N A G E M E N T I N S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

In this chapter, issues connected with the management of safety and, more specifically, the way in which this is regulated by the ISM Code, the contents of the Code, positive points and weaknesses, as well as organisational parameters in its implementation are examined. An account is then given of the other standards implemented by shipping companies.

❚ Shipping industry, quality and safety In the contemporary operating environment, shipping management has entered upon the stage of standardisation, as various regulations and codices favour the development of rules, procedures and checks on their implementation, in order to ensure the response of shipping companies to the demands which they make. Each company is proceeding with the development of safety, quality and environment management systems which reduce the scope for arbitrary action by individuals. Of course, the view has been repeatedly expressed in various quarters that safety and quality in the shipping industry do not depend exclusively on the practices followed by shipping companies. Quality can be assessed, and this assessment must, as with all products and all services, be a function of supply and demand. Neither shipowners nor regulations can impose quality if it is not demanded by the users of shipping services (Haralambides, 1998). The transportation operation can be treated as a chain with many links. Maritime transportation is one of the links in the transportation chain. Concentration on prevention or suppression in only one link in the chain is of limited effectiveness. From this point of view, the introduction of rules and regulations which concern almost exclusively the manner of operation and practices of shipping companies does not have the expected results, as it focuses only on one part of the transportation chain. For example, efforts to reduce the phenomenon of sub-standard ships, that is, ships which do not comply with the international requirements and international standards as to the hull and the engines, the safety and environmental protection equipment and the training and skills of the crew, will not bring about the expected results if they do not include among the aims a check on the incentives with which the market forces provide the owners of such vessels. As long as there are charterers who seek to transport their cargoes at the lower freight, even if this means with lower quality specifications, there will be owners who will supply such vessels. At the same time, the control mechanisms can never be adequate enough and effective enough to deter the appearance of undesirable practices on the part of shipping companies or other agencies involved in the transportation operation. Each time that a new regulation is introduced and the mechanism for the monitoring of its implementation becomes stricter, it is likely that new opportunities will present themselves for those who do not wish to comply, but are seeking easy profit by indulging in unacceptable practices. Competition in the world of flags of convenience and the entry of new flags into this market confirm this observation. International regulations and the wish of established flags of convenience to respond to their demands have led to the creation of a gap for those

sub-standard ships: ships which do not comply with the international requirements and international standards as to the hull and the engines, the safety and environmental protection equipment and the training and skills of the crew.

253

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

vessels and their owners which cannot or will not undertake the cost of their implementation (Alderton & Winchester, 2002). An effort is being made by the new flags of convenience to attract on particularly flexible terms ships and their owners which are not accepted by the established flags. It seems, that is to say, that the efforts at regulation at a world level have led to the creation of a new area in the world of flags which is less regulated than the previous one. It could be pointed out by way of indication that in the latest edition of the Flag State Performance Index (2016–17) of the ICS and ISF, which contains the recent statistics on the assessment of the flags of 117 states, only 19 flags are shown as receiving a positive assessment in all areas (ICS, 2017). The assessment concerns chiefly the areas of safety, protection of the marine environment and of the working conditions of seafarers. There are several flags of states whose performance is assessed as unsatisfactory in almost all the areas included in the assessment. In the light of the above, if the culture of all the links in the transportation chain (owners, managers, shipyards, registers, insurers, flags, charterers, etc.) favour the adoption of practices which lead to an improvement in safety levels, only then will the objectives pursued be achieved to the greatest possible extent.

❚ Safety management in the shipping industry The systematic concentration on safety and quality occurred initially in the field of industries which involve a high degree of risk in their operation, such as chemicals vessels, and gradually extended to the whole of the shipping industry. Nevertheless, certain accidents at sea which occurred demonstrated that these aims could not be achieved only by their adoption and implementation by shipping companies on a voluntary basis, as there are some which put their private benefit first and are indifferent to the safety and quality of the services which they offer. This is because the automatic mechanisms of the market have failed to minimise the ever increasing difference between the private and social cost of production of goods and services, and consequently there is a need for regulation (Metaxas, 1988). Thus, while the rate of increase in accidents at sea gives rise to a need for regulation by the introduction of new rules, competition prompts many maritime actors to put profit before safety, which imposes limitations on or invalidates the regulation process (Størkersen, 2015). The move from regulator responsibility to companies taking total safety responsibility has not yet taken place in the shipping industry (Størkersen et al, 2016). There is, nonetheless, a trend present for a transition from regulation to self-regulation, the reinforcement of which, however, presupposes support from administrative bodies and co-operation between companies. There are positive examples such as the Container Ship Safety Forum (CSSF) (Box 9.1), or the action of companies such as Shell and the holding by it of annual safety workshops, attended by 500 associated companies at world level (Lappalainen, 2017), which, however, are not the rule at the moment. Within this framework, the International Maritime Organisation introduced the ISM Code, whose basic aim is the implementation of the concept of safety

254

S A F E T Y M A N A G E M E N T I N S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

BOX 9.1 Voluntary co-operation for the improvement of safety. The Container Ship Safety Forum The Container Ship Safety Forum (CSSF) was set up in 2014 and is a world-wide co-operation network on a business-to-business basis, with the aim of improving performance in the field of safety and of management practices in the container transportation industry (CSSF, 2017a). The vision of the Forum is the operation of the industry with high levels of safety, without harm caused to people, ships, cargo and the environment. Its members work together to promote the constant improvement of a culture of safety, reporting and benchmarking, sharing best practices, and engaging key stakeholders to develop durable solutions, respecting, within the framework of their co-operation, all applicable anti-trust and competition laws. The initiative is addressed to companies a large percentage of whose activities is owning or managing container ship vessels. Motives for participation for members are the definition of improvements in the system of safety management, participation in the development of shared standards of safety, indicators of performance and assessments of risk, access to predetermined performance indicators and the ratification of their own performance networks, the exchange of best practices and the discussion of challenges to the operating safety standards and compliance with these. The Forum represents more than 40 per cent of the TEU capacity of the global containership fleet, while many shipowning and ship management companies and liner operators are members (CSSF, 2017b).

in the shipping industry. For the first time, a set of regulations with the purpose of improving the levels of safety did not concern only the ship, but also extended to the shipping company on land. The ISM Code, as regulations of a mandatory character, is applied by all shipping companies. It was adopted by the International Maritime Organisation in 1993, and in 1994 was incorporated into the international convention on Safety of Life at Sea/SOLAS.

❚ The ISM Code The ISM Code focuses on the implementation of standards and procedures by shipping companies, and has as its basic aims “to ensure safety at sea, prevention of human injury or loss of life, and avoidance of damage to the environment, in particular to the marine environment, and to property”. In this framework, a shipping company has to provide a safe working environment and ensure the implementation of safe practices, make an assessment of all the identified risks to its ships, personnel and the environment and ensure the appropriate protection, while it must seek the constant improvement of the skills of the personnel ashore and aboard ships for safety management. To use the terminology of the

255

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

risk: the combination of the frequency and the severity of the consequence.

Safety Management System: structured and documented system for the effective implementation of the safety and environmental protection policy.

IMO, we define risk as the combination of the frequency and the severity of the consequence, whereas frequency is defined as the number of occurrences per unit time, while consequence is the outcome of an accident (IMO, 2002). The Code is divided into two parts. The first describes implementation and contains 12 units (Figure 9.1): the first of these includes the definitions, aims, field of application and functional requirements for a system of safety management. The second unit includes the policy on safety and the protection of the environment, and the third company responsibility and authority. The fourth deals with the Designated Person Ashore, the fifth with the master’s responsibility and authority and the sixth with the resources and personnel which the company must secure. The seventh unit covers shipboard operations, the eighth emergency preparedness, the ninth the reports and analysis of non-conformities, accidents and hazardous occurrences and the tenth the maintenance of the ship and equipment. The first part includes also in the eleventh section documentation, and in twelfth section the company verification, review and evaluation. The second part includes four sections which analyse certification and periodical verification, interim certification, verification and the forms of certificates, respectively. Each shipping company must set up, implement and maintain a Safety Management System/SMS which ensures the company’s compliance with the

Figure 9.1 ISM units

256

S A F E T Y M A N A G E M E N T I N S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

Figure 9.2 Functional requirements of the SMS

regulations and that the whole of the codes and directives applicable to shipping in connection with safety are taken into account. The SMS contains a series of functional requirements, directives and procedures in connection with (Figure 9.2):

❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚

the company’s policy on safety and the protection of the environment; the safe operation of the ships; the established levels of authority and lines of communication in the offices on land and on shipboard; accident and non-conformity reports; the response to emergency situations; internal checks and reviews.

The development and implementation of the SMS lead to a Document of Compliance for the shipping company and to Safety Management Certificate for the ship; these have force for five years and are subject to constant re-assessment. The certificates are issued by the administration of the flag under which the ships are registered or by another relevant organisation authorised by the administration of the flag for this purpose. The ISM Code attempts, to a certain extent, the standardisation of the management process and the reduction of the field of application of practices which are on the edges of legality, both on board the ships and in the infrastructures of the company on land. The basic idea on which the development of the ISM Code was based is connected with the role of the human factor in the prevention and avoidance of undesirable situations. Since in the majority of accidents at sea the human factor is involved, a system of rules and procedures which will determine its actions and will restrict arbitrary action on the part of those who have to do with the management of previously identified risks could lead to a reduction in the occurrences of undesirable situations (Box 9.2).

document of compliance: the document which certifies the compliance of the company with the requirements of the ISM Code. Safety Management Certificate: the document issued for the ship to certify that the shipping company and its shipboard management operate in accordance with the SMS.

257

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

BOX 9.2 Accidents and the human factor As in the case of every system, so on board ship, the human factor is involved in every malfunction, without this necessarily meaning that it also is responsible for it. Nevertheless, it could be said that the easy solution is to attribute responsibility to the human factor. “In the case of every accident, laborious investigations are carried out, and in the end it emerges that the human factor is responsible. But this could have been said before the investigation even started” (Krishnamurthi, 2004:3). Ships are complex socio-technological systems, a combination of people, technologies and structures, which operate in a given external environment. These four factors function in a state of interdependence, and when one of these changes, the others are affected. The introduction of new technology sometimes permits the reduction of the number of the crew of ships. Any reduction, however, constitutes a change which must be seriously examined and its consequences checked before it is implemented. If the change is not an appropriate one, it is likely that there will be undesirable impacts in connection with the vessel’s safety (National Research Council, 1990). Furthermore, the procedures which are designed for dealing with risks cannot be comprehensive or provide for every detail. This means that in some cases a strict application of the procedure may take away the flexibility necessary to avoid a difficult situation. The gaps which may exist are filled in by the users, those who carry out the procedures. The crews of vessels are very frequently forced not to implement precisely certain procedures in order to adapt to the functional requirements created by complex situations with which they may be faced. In this case, where an accident takes place, the divergence between the procedure and the actual reaction will be deemed an anomaly in the process of causation analysis (DMAIB, 2016). The undesirable impacts will be attributed to the human factor, without, however, this always having any connection with reality.

More particularly, as regards the defining of levels of authority and lines of communication, factors which refer to the organisational structure of the company, their effectiveness depends to a significant degree upon the approach on which the company relies. The implementation of the ISM Code has led many companies to the designing of organisation charts and the description of roles and competences for the personnel (MCA, 2006). The manner in which this is carried out and becomes known to everyone working in the company influences the effectiveness of the structure which is designed, and the system of safety management and performance. If the organisational structure of the company and the corresponding organisation chart are treated as a mere form which has to be created, together with roles and competences for the personnel, without this having been developed with their contribution, and without their being notified of them or their having been revised from time to time in order to increase their effectiveness, they will be of limited usefulness. Similarly, if the

258

S A F E T Y M A N A G E M E N T I N S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

organisational structure is not designed with the aim of supporting the SMS, an improvement in performance in this area is not to be expected (Box 9.3). This calls for an examination of the question of the participation in the design of a system of those involved in the implementation of it. Studies which examine the implementation of the ISM confirm the importance of the participation in the design of the system of those involved. In an examination of the role of employment and social conditions, a distance was observed between the perceptions of seafarers and of managing executives on the implementation of the ISM and a noteworthy gap between expected outcomes and outcomes and practice. Managers perceive the ISM as an administrative tool which seafarers are called upon to implement, whereas the latter see the Code as a regulation which does not make it possible for them to take part in the planning of the manner of managing the risks with which they are faced (Bhattacharya, 2012).

BOX 9.3 Organisational design and safety Organisational design contributes to the definition of an organisation’s reliability. This means that when organisations re-design or change through either the addition or subtraction or the outsourcing of processes, the pathways to their reliability are also altered. It is, then, essential that every change at the level of organisation should be accompanied by a re-assessment of the levels of its reliability. Each change should be carried out only when there is an adequate understanding of the way in which the system will behave afterwards. Therefore those who will implement the systems should take part in their design. If those who are going to implement the processes which are followed take no part in their design, the phenomenon of non-conformity will be observed when they realise that opposition and conflict are present within the context of their work (Bourrier, 2005). In judging the above within the framework of a shipping company and of the introduction and implementation of the ISM Code, it has been shown that a factor crucial for the effectiveness of the SMS is the lack of participation of crew members in workplace health and safety management, which demonstrates that in shipping the crucial working and social conditions which support self-regulation are not present. Within this context, a dimension which should be taken into account is that having to do with stability in work and the security which this creates in seafarers. The seafarer who feels insecure about his job and the future renewal of his contract functions more on the criterion of the expectations of managers as to his behaviour as regards safety issues (Bhattacharya, 2012).

259

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

The aspects of the ISM Code All the aspects of the ISM Code can be grouped into those which concern management, people, the ship and its equipment and procedures (Chauvel, 1997) (Figure 9.3):

Figure 9.3 The aspects of the ISM Code

A. Management

Designated Person Ashore: the person who has direct access to the highest level of management, designated to ensure the safe operation of each ship and to provide a link between the company and the people on board.

A substantive point of the ISM Code is the commitment of the management to the duties and responsibilities which it imposes. The management is responsible for the development, implementation and maintenance of an effective system of safety management on land and on board the ships which will put into practice the company’s policy. The system includes procedures, plans and directives which are constantly updated and are accessible to the whole of the personnel of the company. It also includes a written description of the responsibilities and authority of each individual in connection with the policy on safety and the protection of the marine environment, as well as the definition of the Designated Person Ashore (DPA), who acts on the basis of the general management of the company and oversees the implementation of its policy. Often, the DPA is regarded as responsible to the company for whatever relates to the SMS. In actual fact, however, he is responsible for monitoring and confirmation of the implementation of the SMS. For this reason, he must have direct access to the highest level of management to ensure the provision of the necessary resources and necessary support ashore for its implementation. This means that the DPA enjoys independence and also has the authority to ensure the promotion of the attitudes and behaviours which favour a safety culture (Box 9.4) at all levels of the company (see Figures 6.8 and 9.6 for the placement of the DPA on the organisation chart) (Chawla, 2008). B. People The SMS places emphasis on the qualifications which the personnel must possess in order to respond to the requirements of the SMS. It requires from the company that it should ensure that the captain has the qualifications to manage, and that each ship is manned with qualified and healthy seafarers in accordance with international standards. Emphasis is also placed on communication between the

260

S A F E T Y M A N A G E M E N T I N S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

members of the crew, and on ensuring that orders are conveyed to those involved in a suitable way. Important factors are the existence of procedures for the familiarisation of new personnel with their duties, the understanding by the whole of the personnel involved in the implementation of the SMS of the rules, regulations and directives, the recognition of the training needs of the personnel and the provision of the relevant training and the provision of information to the seafarers in connection with their job in language which is intelligible to them. C. Ship and equipment The ship is the working environment for seafarers. The company must design procedures to ensure the maintenance of the vessel in accordance with the regulations and specifications which it lays down itself, and within this framework ensure that inspections are carried out at appropriate times, non-conformities are recorded and their causes given, corrective action is taken and entries are kept for each relevant action. The company must also define the equipment and systems whose operation is crucial for the ship, and the measures which it takes to ensure the reliability of these systems. D. Procedures Procedures are the means for the transmission of experience. They are enhanced by acquired experience and contribute to the avoidance of the repetition of errors (Chauvel, 1997). The term ‘procedures’ is to be found in almost all the units of the ISM. More specifically, the company must develop procedures to ensure the safe operation of ship and protection of the environment, for the familiarisation of new personnel, for the training of the personnel, for the crucial processes on board ship, for the response to emergency shipboard situations, for the reporting of nonconformities, accidents and hazardous situations and the relevant corrective actions, for internal audits and management reviews, for the maintenance of the vessel, for the checking of the documents and data relating to the SMS, for the periodical assessment of the SMS and for the required verifications.

BOX 9.4 Concentrating on safety culture There is no reference in the ISM Code to the concept of safety culture. Nevertheless, the development of a safety culture is one of the most crucial factors for the successful implementation of a system of safety management. Safety culture is defined as “shared values (what is important) and beliefs (how things work) that interact with an organization’s structures and control systems to produce behavioural norms (the way we do things around here)” (Uttal, 1983). The Maritime Safety Committee of the

safety culture: culture in which there is considerable informed endeavour to reduce risks to the individual, ships and the marine environment to a level that is ‘as low as is reasonably practicable’.

261

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

IMO defines safety culture as a “culture in which there is considerable informed endeavour to reduce risks to the individual, ships and the marine environment to a level that is ‘as low as is reasonably practicable’” (IMO, 2003). This definition gives emphasis to practical considerations. Safety culture is a sub-component of corporate culture, which means that the dominant corporate culture and its context will influence its development and vice versa, and that safety culture affects and is affected by other operational processes or organisational systems (Cooper, 2002). The ideal safety culture propels the system towards the achievement of the aim of maintaining the prevention of operational risks, regardless of the personality of the individuals who form the leadership, or commercial interests (Reason, 1998). Of crucial importance for the creation of a safety culture are (ISF, undated): – the recognition that all ‘accidents’ are avoidable and in normal conditions occur only when safe practices are not implemented or correct procedures are not followed; – constant bearing in mind of safety; – the constant definition of aims with a view to continuous improvement. A critical parameter for a safety culture to prevail is the creation of an informed culture, that is, of an environment in which those who manage and operate the system have the necessary knowledge of the factors (human, technical, organisational, environmental) which determine its safety and are aware of the various ways in which the defences of the system can be violated or circumvented. When this is achieved and there are no longer significant accidents in the organisation, for it to be maintained, a reporting culture is necessary to gather every item of information in connection with safety (incidents, near misses, etc.), which is, however, not simple because it requires people to report their own mistakes. A third condition, which is connected with the trust which individuals should have in the organisation, is the existence of a just culture, and a precondition for that is an awareness of where the boundary lies between unacceptable behaviour, the necessary disciplinary action and where the punishment is not appropriate and is no help in the promotion of safety (Reason, 1998). In the light of the above, safety culture presupposes participation in the system of safety management and a knowledge of the parameters which define it on the part of the people who are involved in its implementation, a willingness to learn and constant improvement by means of observation, recording and analysis, and confidence of individuals in the system. Within this framework, the role of management and its commitment to respect for the above principles is essential and a precondition for the necessary change in behaviour of individuals.

262

S A F E T Y M A N A G E M E N T I N S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

In organisations in which there is a safety culture, all the members think and function with safety as a criterion. Every time that there is a conflict between safety objectives and commercial aims, the former take precedence. Written statements and the procedures contained in the handbooks do not ensure the existence of a real safety culture if there is no commitment of all the members to this. A study made of occupational health and safety in Chinese shipping is of interest; it examines the interaction between managing executives in the office and the crew, and notes that every time there is a conflict between the speeding up of production and safety issues, given that the management is in a position to fully control the ship, the managing executives give orders which are in favour of the speeding up of production, and the captains, tacitly, follow them. It links this reaction with the high power distance and the strict hierarchy involved in ship management. This divergence is seen as affecting the successful implementation of the ISM Code, the existence of which, however, is believed to have led to executives being subtle in giving orders and exerting dominance (Vue et al, 2017). Clearly, factors connected with the organisational culture of companies and the priorities of the managements of the companies which were studied lead to the prevalence of behaviours which are not compatible with the safety culture.

Experience of the implementation of the ISM Code Compliance with the requirements of the ISM Code is a condition for companies to ‘remain’ in the market. Furthermore, the ISM Code prompts them to concentrate their efforts on issues connected with their effectiveness and, at the same time, to ensure their safe operation. The ISM is described as a step ahead for setting the quality standards in the shipping industry, in spite of the fact that it is a maritime regulation with limited scope (Karahalios, 2015). Of course, only companies which respond fully to the requirements of the ISM Code and go beyond compliance with minimum requirements of legislative standards combine effectiveness with safe operation. Nevertheless, the statistics of the Port State Controls (Box 9.5 and Figure 9.4) show that deficiencies in connection with the ISM Code recorded during inspections of ships continue to be numerous, which means that for large numbers of shipping companies, substantive and complete implementation of the Code is still something to be desired. The implementation of the ISM Code has undoubtedly led to an improvement of the levels of safety in the shipping industry; this is confirmed by a series of studies in various national contexts (Tzannatos and Kokotos, 2009; Lappalainen et al, 2014; Størkersen et al, 2016; Lapallainen, 2016). Moreover, benefits have been recorded as arising from the implementation of the ISM, such as, for example, improved co-operation and communication between the shore and the shipboard personnel and between the personnel on board, the definition of roles and responsibilities, and the existence of unified and systematised procedures on

Port State Control (PSC): mechanism monitoring the compliance of ships with international rules and safety regulations by means of the carrying out of inspections.

263

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

BOX 9.5 Inspections of ships and Port State Control A ship, from an operational point of view, is obliged to comply with international regulations which concern the safety of those on board, the protection of the marine environment and the working conditions on it. Its compliance is confirmed by certificates issued by classification societies authorised by the state authority, and by the state authority itself. At the same time, monitoring of compliance is carried out both by the shipping authorities and by special inspectors in various ports (Port State Control/PSC). PSC is the basic mechanism for the monitoring of the compliance of vessels with the international rules and international safety regulations, and serves, essentially, as an international control network based on shared standards and serving common aims. Inspections are carried out in order to ensure that the ships under a foreign flag which put into a port possess the necessary documents and certificates proving their compliance, that is to say, that the hull and the machinery are suitable for the kind of voyages which it is carrying out, that they give rise to no danger of sea pollution and that they provide the crew with a safe working environment. PSC functions on the basis of the Memorandum of Understanding/MOU, which is signed by the states taking part. Each MOU sets aims in connection with the percentage of ships entering their ports which are to be checked, chosen on a series of criteria. By way of indication, the relevant procedure of the Paris MOU lays down the framework for the inspections of ships of the Paris MOU is based on a risk-based approach to targeting ships for inspection. The objective is the recognition of quality shipping and the rewarding of good owners with fewer inspections, while at the same time, ships with a high risk profile will receive detailed inspections more frequently. In order to facilitate the choice of ships which will be inspected, the inspectors ‘feed’ the central data base of the Paris MOU with detailed data. The data base (THETIS) informs national PSC authorities of the ships that are due for inspection and provides data on ships’ particulars and reports of previous inspections in the Paris MOU region. Every ship which is recorded on the information system is assigned a ship risk profile (SRP) which determines its priority for inspection. For the definition of the risk profile, the type of the vessel, its age, the flag, the recognised organisation, company performance, the deficiencies and the detentions within the previous 36 months are taken into account (Paris MOU, 2016). A similar procedure is implemented by the Tokyo MOU (Tokyo MOU, 2014). On the basis of the framework for selecting and monitoring vessels, the periodical inspections are determined by their risk profile (Paris MOU, 2014). In the case of ships with a High Risk Profile (HRP), the periodical inspection takes place five to six months after the last Paris MOU inspection, in that of ships with a Standard Risk Profile (SRP),

264

S A F E T Y M A N A G E M E N T I N S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

the period increases to 10–12 months, while for those with a Low Risk Profile (LRP), the period increases still further to 24–36 months. Naturally, unforeseen factors may lead to an additional inspection of the vessel in the intervening periods, which alters the time of the next periodical inspection accordingly. The pattern for the selection of a ship for inspection includes two categories. In Category I, the ship must be inspected if that is determined by the length of time since that last inspection on the basis of its risk profile or if there is an overriding factor. In Category II, a ship may undergo a periodical inspection because it is within the time limit specified for its category, or because the port state considers an unexpected factor warrants an inspection (additional).

board. Nevertheless, in confirmation of the data in Figure 9.4, a series of studies shows that significant weaknesses have also been recorded (see also Box 8.7). Factors such as the safety climate (Bhattacharya, 2015), failure to define requirements and the manner of their implementation clearly (Lappalainen, 2017), comprehension of the philosophy of continuous improvement, lack of uniformity in implementation and excessive bureaucracy (Lappalainen at al, 2014), contribution to a bureaucratic culture (Størkersen et al, 2016), employment and social conditions which do not support effective implementation of self-regulation (Bhattacharya, 2012) and high priority given to speeding up production to the detriment of safety (Vue et al, 2017) have led to the observation of a divergence between expectations and results from the implementation of the ISM Code. A typical comment is that there appears to be a mismatch between the intentions of the regulators for the ISM Code and the industry’s adaptation to these regulations (Størkersen et al, 2016). Whereas among the regulators’ intentions were self-regulation, continuous improvement and the safety culture, the industry response seems to be compliance-driven, the minimum standards for safety and administrative structures, respectively. It is also worth noting a point which is connected with concentration on and dedication to the procedures, which it is essential to treat not as a safety barrier, but as aid or guidance. In practice, the observance of the procedures is incumbent to a large degree on the ships’ crews. Although a key purpose of the ISM Code is to ensure that the responsibility for safety management is evenly distributed between the ship and the company, undesirable outcomes are often projected as instances where procedures are not followed by the seafarer (DMAIB, 2016). One of the negative factors which has been noted in the course of the implementation of the Code was the negative attitude expressed by employees as a result of the fact that they have been the addressees of a plan which has been loaded on to them without having been prepared or trained for this and without their involvement being requested (Anderson, 2004). This is a point which has

265

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

Figure 9.4 ISM-related deficiencies in the Paris MOU and Tokyo MOU regions, 2002–2016 Sources: Compiled by the author based on data contained in Tokyo MOU, Annual Reports, various years; Paris MOU, Annual Reports, various years.

in various ways been noted as a negative aspect in the implementation of the Code up to the present. As has already been noted, seafarers perceive the SMS as a regulatory exercise which does not give them space to take part in the management of the risks to which they are exposed on a daily basis (Bhattacharya, 2012). The fact that the safety culture is associated with compliance with developed safety measures, without how far these measures are appropriate to the specific situation being examined, leads on many occasions to the crew being called upon to implement measures which in many cases are not suitable (Oltedal, 2011). They are called upon, that is, to implement a system with a large amount of bureaucracy and a large number of reports and check lists, which to some degree invalidates the professional skill of the seafarers (Størkersen et al, 2016). A better incorporation of the experience and specialisation of the operating personnel can lead to a change in this situation, since in some cases the safety measures are developed by personnel without experience at sea, and this shows that this weakness is connected with the office side of the company on land (Oltedal, 2011).

266

S A F E T Y M A N A G E M E N T I N S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

❚ Factors favourable to the development of the SMS A particularly important point in the implementation of the ISM Code is the manner of planning and implementing the SMS. As noted in a study by the Nautical Institute, carried out six years after the coming into force of the ISM Code, which lasted two years and had as its aim an investigation of its effectiveness, a considerable part of the shipping industry continued to have problems in its implementation because of dysfunctional SMS. In the light of the findings of the study, a number of negative – but also some positive – factors emerged, a number of which were referred to in the preceding paragraph, which were raised by employees involved in the implementation of the SMS. The existence of an environment which favoured participation in the planning of the system of those involved in its implementation was mentioned as one of the factors most critical for the success of the SMS. On the basis of an analysis of the positive points in the implementation of the ISM Code, and of the deficiencies and weaknesses which have been noted, the substantive steps to be taken for the creation of a Safety Management System in accordance with the requirements of the ISM Code are the following:

❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚

❚ ❚

Commitment of the management to the changes and improvements which must be made in order to cultivate a safety culture in the company at all levels of the organisation; Participation of the personnel in the planning of the system, which will lead to a fuller definition of the risks and to the development of ways of managing them; Securing of the resources necessary for the implementation of the system; Training of the personnel in the cultivation of the skills which will be required for the successful implementation of the SMS, with particular emphasis on soft skills such as the development of leadership and decision-taking skills; Given the multicultural composition of crews in a large percentage of ships, the provision of training both to crews and to the office staff on the development of diversity management skills (Progoulaki et al, 2013); Effective communication between ships and office and on board each ship; Clear definition of the role and competences of all involved in the implementation of the system; Establishment of an approach which leads to the adoption of the practice of continuous improvement; Development of mechanisms for the handling of knowledge and the adoption of practices which have a supportive function, such as, for example, rotation of ship and shore staff and frequent meetings of the on-shore and ships’ personnel for the discussion of safety issues; Encouragement of reports of incidents and near-miss occurrences and establishment of just culture in the personnel; Systematic dealing with factors which lead to differentiation among the members of the team, such as, for example, national culture, which is related to safety culture (Havold, 2007);

267

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

❚

❚

Support for co-operative relations and mutual trust between office personnel and ships, given that the factor of interpersonal trust (employees to managers, among seafarers) has a positive influence on safety-related organisational outcomes (Gausdal & Makarova, 2017); Ensuring of the existence of a collegial environment on board ships in which seafarers feel themselves to be members of the team, as this has a positive influence on the safety culture of the ship (Shea, 2005).

management and the organisational structure ❚ Safety of a company ❚ The role of the organisational structure in the effective implementation of the SMS by a shipping company is particularly important. In any event, one of the concerns of the ISM Code is the definition of the competences and the responsibilities of those involved in its implementation, as well as of the procedures which must be followed. Organisational structure, however, can be an obstacle if no effort is made on the part of the company to deal with the possible problems arising from its manner of operation, but also to make use of features which may favour the effectiveness of the structure as regards safety. In a study of the influence of organisational structures on safety, obstacles and favourable factors which interact with the organisational structure and affect its performance in the safety field also, are discussed (MCA, 2006). Included among the obstacles which have a negative effect on the effectiveness of the structure, particularly in relation to safety, are:

❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚

the existence of unsuitable resources; internal conflicts between safety and commercial effectiveness; large or excessive workload; the absence of career prospects and reduced experience on the part of the crews; lack of respect for cultural differences; inappropriately planned re-organisation; expansion leading to erroneous management; non-objective selection of personnel; idiosyncrasies of the owner and priority of profit over safety; major reliance on automatic measures and check lists; issues concerning the designated person on land; lack of training on matters of management; ship management companies with low performance.

Among the favourable factors which have a positive effect on the effectiveness of the structure, particularly in relation to safety, are:

❚ ❚ ❚ 268

clear and effective communication; consistent leadership which demonstrates commitment to safety; clear policy and strategy on safety;

S A F E T Y M A N A G E M E N T I N S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚

safety culture; an effective system for reporting accidents and incidents; respect for cultural differences which exist between the members of the crew; reliable systems for measuring the effectiveness of the structure; the cultivation of a spirit of dedication in crews; support for career prospects; selection of crews; encouragement to display responsibility as regards matters of safety; effective management of new ships; owner-manager relations; development of skills in crews.

The type of organisational structure which the company will choose interacts with the obstacles and the favourable factors. Its organisational structure may be hierarchical, flat or flat and decentralised. Each type corresponds to one or more of the forms of departmentalisation analysed in Chapter 6, while manifesting complexity, centralisation and standardisation of a differing extent. The hierarchical structure corresponds to a standardised and centralised structure, described by functional departmentalisation. The flat organisational structure corresponds to a structure with less standardisation and centralisation, described as matrix organisation, while the flat and decentralised structure is described by departmentalisation by teams. Naturally, the identification of structures with forms of departmentalisation is indicative, since, as has already been pointed out, in most companies forms of departmentalisation respond more fully to the specific needs of each of these.

❚ The Tanker Management Self-Assessment programme Until recently, oil companies which chartered tankers belonging to independent owners placed emphasis chiefly on the procedures for the inspection of the vessels (vettings), on the basis of the results of which they took their decision on whether or not to go ahead with the chartering. In order for the inspections process to be more effective, the members of the Oil Companies International Marine Forum (OCIMF), the union of oil companies (www.ocimf.com), undertook in 1993 the setting up of the Ship Inspection Report Program (SIRE) and in 1997 the introduction of a single inspections procedure, on the basis of a uniform inspection protocol that is predicated by the Vessel Inspection Questionnaire (VIQ), the Barges Inspection Questionnaire (BIQ), the Uniform SIRE Inspection Report, the Vessels Particulars Questionnaire (VPQ) and the Barges Particulars Questionnaire (BPQ), which is used by all its members (OCIMF, 2017a). The results of the inspection and any comments are entered on the Ship Inspection Report database, to which all members of the OCIMF have access. The inspections, however, give a picture of the state of the vessel at one particular moment, a picture which does not permit the assessment of a risk which may stem from the way in which the shipping company manages it.

Oil Companies International Marine Forum (OCIMF): union of oil companies interested in the transportation and storage of cargoes of petroleum oil and its derivatives, chemicals and gas. vetting inspection: part of a screening process to assess acceptability of a vessel by oil and chemical companies. Ship Inspection Report Program: a very large database of up-to-date information about tankers and barges.

269

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

Since 2004, the members of the OCIMF have applied the Tanker Management Self-Assessment (TMSA) programme, a guide to good practices in the management of ships, which, as the OCIMF states, provides the means for the assessment and definition of the quality management of ships. The TMSA adopts approaches which concern standards as regards safety, quality, the environment, health and safety at work. It has been revised twice – in 2008 and 2017. In order for shipping companies to improve their systems of organisation and management, they use the TMSA. This guide encourages them to estimate and assess the level of safety of their systems in relation to the predetermined Key Performance Indicators/KPI; that is, it provides them in this way with a measure of best practices. Each manager carries out his own internal assessment in the light of more than 260 performance indicators in 13 areas of operation and grades performance and progress in each area (OCIMF, 2008). These areas and the objectives pursued in each are shown in Figure 9.5. The TMSA is based on the principles of the ISM Code, and encourages managers of tankers to check the procedures which relate to safety, quality and the protection of the environment, in pursuit of their continuous improvement. It introduces four levels of assessment, with Level 1 being the lowest and Level 4 the highest. A company which fully implements the letter and spirit of the of the ISM Code should be able to self-assess itself at Level 1. Given, however, that the aim of the TMSA is to encourage the managers of tankers to seek continuous improvement, even if a company is at Level 4, it should not cease to seek the means of further improvement of its processes. Of course, the TMSA is based on the ISM Code, but it includes actions on units which are not connected only with safety management, but also concern quality management in the company, as an assessment of the improvement which has been achieved, procedures for the human resources management systems, the management of changes, etc. are included. At each stage of the grading of the company’s performance, key performance indicators are specified, while for each definitive indicator, the relevant best practices are proposed. The TMSA is based on the certainty that the managers of tankers will themselves review the procedures concerning safety, quality and the protection of the environment, with a view to proving their constant improvement. Furthermore, it is based on the supposition that every manager will use self-assessment in order to implement plans for the improvement of performance. At the same time, the TMSA encourages managers to set higher standards of performance and constant improvement in relation to best practices, even if they are at the highest level of self-assessment. For this purpose, companies should develop appropriate internal systems and suitable training procedures, supported by the relevant software tools. If a company does not succeed by its performance in satisfying the expectations arising from its ranking at a certain level of the TMSA, its credibility will be called into question. Information drawn from the investigation of any accidents, the port state controls or inspections of any kind may confirm, or otherwise, the reliability of the results of the self-assessment. On the basis of the above, the implementation of the TMSA obliges shipping companies which manage tankers to engage in organisational and administrative adaptations. It is worth noting that the introduction of the TMSA has given rise

270

S A F E T Y M A N A G E M E N T I N S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

to questionings and concerns similar to those which were current in the period of the introduction of the ISM Code in connection with the impacts on operating costs, particularly of small companies. The introduction of the ISM Code, which also led shipping companies to significant organisational and administrative adjustments, was regarded as a threat to small-size companies, while at the same time, their ability to respond to its demands from the point of view of cost and organisation was called into question. In the course of events, however, it became apparent that companies which approached the implementation of the ISM Code with the necessary seriousness of purpose were able to meet its requirements and make use of the advantages which it created. The TMSA provides companies with the performance indicators, but not with the way in which performance will be measured. Its implementation calls for the collection, processing and analysis of a great deal of information and data, which will then support corrective action and the pursuit of continuous improvement. The infrastructure of information systems which the company has at its disposal greatly contributes to this.

271

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

The implementation of the TMSA comes at a cost, but it provides companies with a number of benefits as it contributes to an awareness of the processes crucial for their performance, permits assessment of the risk which may arise in these areas, contributes to a safety culture and to efforts to achieve continuous improvement of the systems which ensure it, favours the improvement of the knowledge and skills of the personnel by means of planned training and permits the company to be informed of its performance, and to compare this with that of its competitors. With the assistance of the appropriate information system, self-assessment can contribute to the recording, dissemination and enhancement of the knowledge which the company possesses, thus strengthening its competitiveness.

of safety, quality, environment, health ❚ Management and energy – The integrated approach ❚ The improvement of safety constitutes a central goal, which is, however, more completely achieved when, in parallel, objectives are attained which have a connection with the quality of the services, the management of the environment, health and safety at work, as well as energy performance. Studies have confirmed the compatibility and advantages of the joint development of safety and quality systems in the effective operation of shipping companies (Celik, 2009a, 2009b; Pantouvakis & Karakasnaki, 2016). For this reason, the development of management systems on the basis of standards relating to quality, the environment, health and safety and energy performance is a widespread practice among companies (Box 9.6). Unlike the ISM Code, which is a mandatory code exclusively for shipping, the ISO 9001, ISO 14001, ISO 50001 and OHSAS 18001 standards are options for companies and have wider application (Box 9.7). Recognising that their joint implementation creates promotional synergies for shipping companies, classification societies advise, and contribute to, the development by shipping companies of integrated management systems.1 Combined procedures ease implementation and reduce conflicting information and documents which are given to crew members, give good guidance to them and prove compliance to the highest degree (Fraunhofer CML & GL, 2013).

BOX 9.6 Standards with common approach The standards ISO 9001, ISO 14001, ISO 50001 and OHSAS 18001 are based on the management principles of continuous improvement, i.e., the Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA) cycle. These four items correspond to the steps in the Shewhart/Deming cycle, which is orientated towards continuous improvement. The Deming cycle includes a series of actions at each step. More specifically, ‘Plan’ involves the defining of the problem, its analysis and the taking of corrective measures; ‘Do’ is concerned with the implementation of the proposal for improvement by the solving of the

272

S A F E T Y M A N A G E M E N T I N S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

problem; ‘Check’ involves confirmation that the results are satisfactory, and ‘Act’ is about securing the benefit derived from the improvement and standardisation of the procedures (Dervitsiotis, 2005). The basic idea and approach of each standard are analysed below. ISO 9001:2015 – Quality Management This is the standard that sets out the requirement for a quality management system (ISO, 2015b). It is based on quality management principles of customer focus, leadership, engagement of people, process approach, improvement, evidence-based decisionmaking and relationship management, and helps businesses and organisations to be more efficient (ISO, 2015a). ISO 14001 – Environmental Management This standard sets out the requirements for an environmental management system. It helps organisations improve their environmental performance through more efficient use of resources and reduction of waste, gaining a competitive advantage and the trust of stakeholders (ISO, 2015c). It specifies requirements that enable an organisation to achieve the intended outcomes it sets for its environmental management system. ISO 50001 – Energy Management This is a standard which gives organisations a recognised framework for developing an effective energy management system. Its purpose is to enable organisations to establish the systems and processes necessary to improve energy performance, including energy efficiency, use and consumption. Implementation of ISO 50001 is intended to lead to reductions in greenhouse gas emissions and other related environmental impacts and energy cost through systematic management of energy (ISO, 2016). OHSAS 18001 – Occupational Health and Safety OHSAS 18001 is a framework for an occupational health and safety management system which can help organisations to put in place the policies, procedures and controls needed to achieve the best possible working conditions, aligned to internationally recognised best practice (BS, 2017).

BOX 9.7 Strategic agility, operational commitment, engaged employees2 Almi Tankers S.A. (www.almitankers.gr) is an operator of a modern fleet of 15 oil tankers. It was established in 2009 when the Fostiropoulos family made the decision to invest in building a tanker fleet. The founding family had been present in shipping

273

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

for many decades, operating a mixed fleet of tankers, which were sold in the early 1990s, and of bulk carries. The operating arm of its bulk carriers fleet was the company named Fairsky Shipping and Trading S.A. The decision to invest in tankers came after the analysis of the trends in the dry cargoes markets and the oversupply of capacity of bulk carriers. After analysing the tankers’ operation standards and the requirements of the oil majors on the one hand, and the characteristics of the existing management company on the other hand, the family considered as a proper alternative the establishment of a new company committed to running tankers, not restricted and depended by the path followed by the existing company. The new company would be able to learn, to be flexible and to change in order to remain competitive in the long run. Thus in 2009, Almi Tankers was established. Within a short period of time Almi’s fleet expanded quickly. Soon after the delivery of the first two modern aframaxes from Teekay, orders for ten suezmax tankers and two VLCCs were placed at Daewoo Shipbuilding & Marine Engineering Co. In July 2017, the fleet consisted of two aframax, nine suezmax tankers and two VLCCs, which, following a balanced approach to chartering, were employed on spot and time charter basis by the oil majors. Two new VLCCs were under construction at Hyundai Heavy Industries, to be delivered in 2018. The decision to establish a new company allowed the top management of the company to build on the vast experience of operating ships and at the same time to seek for the adoption of management methodologies and tools which will allow the creation of an organisation committed to meet the high standards of the tanker industry. The fact that the management of the first two tankers of the company remained with the seller for a period of several months after the acquisition contributed to the proper planning and preparation for the setting of the new structure. Within a period of few months from its inception Almi managed to develop its integrated management system and get certification for its compliance for quality, health, safety and environmental excellence according to ISO 9001, ISO 14001 and OHSAS 18001 standards respectively, from Lloyd’s Register Quality Assurance. Implementing an approach that places human resources’ engagement and motivation as first priority, Almi gained recognition according to the Investors in People (2017) standard and launched the Total Crew Welfare Programme, which focuses on the well-being of seafarers at sea and their families at home. In the context of the latter initiative, back in 2011 the company started to provide all seafarers with free monthly Internet allowance, in addition to their free personal email account. The introduction to the operational philosophy of Almi Tankers has already provided evidence that the company pursued to develop state of the art approaches in tanker operation. The examination of the company’s internal articulation and management systems advocates for this pursuit. Almi adopts the approach proposed by the Primary Colours Model of leadership (Pendleton & Furnham, 2016), acting at three leadership

274

S A F E T Y M A N A G E M E N T I N S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

domains which are parallelised with the human being, i.e., the strategic (the head), the operational (the hand and legs) and the interpersonal (the heart). In the strategic domain, the aim is to set the strategic direction of the company, in the operational domain the aim is to deliver the expected results, while in the interpersonal domain the focus of action is to build and sustain relationships and create alignment. In the strategic domain, the management of the company seeks to develop and implement the framework for the execution of strategy guided by the vision and mission of company. This task is co-ordinated by the Strategy Management Office, which is responsible for the STRATEX budget, a tool aiming to ensure that strategic expenditure will not be re-allocated for requirements of the operational domain. The analysis of its vision and mission reveals that long-term sustainability and social responsibility shape the context for Almi. The Strategy Management Office’s responsibility is the implementation of the Balanced Scorecard framework3 (BSC) and the enhancement of the collective participation in strategic planning and decisionmaking. In this context it co-ordinates four Focus Groups, one for each of the four perspectives of the BSC (or strategic pillars as they are called by the company), communicates the company strategy across the organisation, organises annual Strategy Review Meetings and updates the company’s strategy map4 as required. Strategic Focus Groups have been formed on a voluntary basis. They are key players in the approach of the company, each one of them having the responsibility of developing the strategic agility of the company through the collective participation in the strategic planning process and to promote, monitor and approve the company’s objectives of each pillar. In a sense, they act as integrators by facilitating employees’ participation in strategic planning as well as knowledge creation and dissemination, which in turn make the organisation able to remain agile and to adapt in the turbulent business environment. In the operational domain, the leadership focuses on the internal articulation and organisation of the company’s operation and on the management of its systems. The structure of the company uses as a basis the functional approach, which is explained by the fact that it operates ships of the same specialisation. However, there exist mechanisms, already discussed in the strategic domain section, which differentiate basic principles of functional structure as they facilitate horizontal communication and co-operation. The main platform for horizontal communication is the Strategy Management Office, which is depicted with dotted lines in the organisation chart. Despite the fact that Almi is at the moment a medium-sized company with a fleet of 15 ships and total number of employees ashore not exceeding 63 persons, its organisation is considered rather wide and includes all the functions and the corresponding departments analysed in Chapter 4. From the right to the left the following departments/units are located in the organisation chart:

❚

Technical Department supervised by the Chief Technical Officer, which includes Technical, Supplies (Spare Parts and Purchasing) and Navigation, Communication and Automation

❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚

Marine Department Health, Safety, Quality and Environment Department Communications Development Office Strategy Management Office Information Technology Department Accounting Department Chartering Department Chief Security Department Operations Department Human Resources Department Business Performance Monitoring Unit Crew Department Claims and Insurance Department Designated Person Ashore Department Legal Department

In the organisation chart emphasis is given to the departments which have the most active roles and the main responsibilities for the implementation of the integrated management system of the company. As already discussed in Chapters 4 and 5 the horizontal and vertical complexity as well as the number of employees in each department may differ substantially, depending on the tasks assigned to each one of them. This is valid for Almi’s organisation too, as it contains departments with a wide base of employees, e.g., the Technical Department, and others of rather small complexity, e.g., the Business Performance Monitoring Unit. The size of the department is an indication of the importance and priority devoted by the company to the operations it implements or supports. This is the case with the Technical Department and the DPA. The same applies for departments that are not found in the majority of the medium-size shipping companies or do not exist at all. To the former category belongs the Human Resources Department, while to the latter category belong the departments that support the strategy domain of the company, i.e., the Strategy Management Office and the BPMU unit. The creation of a business environment in which employees feel they can accomplish their expectations with regard to their career contributes to the enhancement of their motivation and engagement. By focusing on creating and preserving such an environment, Almi succeeds in attaining high retention rates of employees ashore and at sea. In the interpersonal domain, the company is striving to ensure that its organisation is the platform to cultivate and exploit its people competencies. In this context, mechanisms and tools which facilitate communication and dialogue between organisational units and individuals and build on the advantages of diversity are in use. These include:

The Communications Development Office, responsible for enhancing communication amongst the team ashore and on board and for promoting a corporate mentality and culture as well as company policies and objectives. Amongst the duties of the department is the publishing of the quarterly company newsletter whose contents support the objectives mentioned above.

Group facilitation methods. The company’s aim is to have its people contributing with their knowledge and creativity to finding solutions. Meetings whose main objective is the discussion of ideas, team building, knowledge creation and dissemination support this aim.

Compliance with Investors in People, which is the standard for people management. Understanding cultural differences and making use of the advantages they can offer by making use of the Lewis Culture Active Model, which helps members of organisations develop an understanding of their own culture through the Culture Active website (CultureActive, 2017; CrossCulture, 2017).

To conclude the analysis of Almi Tankers, the triptych that aptly and comprehensively describes its operation is strategic agility, operational commitment and engaged employees.

❚ Introduction In the analysis thus far, the term ‘shipping company’ has referred to the economic unit which operates the ships and offers sea transport services. The ships may belong either to the company or to subsidiaries or associated companies (for example, through joint ownership). A shipping company may be the owner of a ship or be responsible for operating the ship, without however itself performing all or part of the operations, but instead contracting them out to external partners. In this chapter, the outsourcing of ship management to third-party ship managers as a strategic choice of the company is analysed. The analysis also focuses on the particular types of outsourcing activities in the shipping industry. Independent ship management companies are analysed, namely those companies which are commissioned to perform the operations and activities that the shipping companies outsource.
