❚ Outsourcing as a strategic choice Outsourcing is a process by which activities and functions that have traditionally been performed by the company itself are contracted out to external collabora-

OUTSOURCING OF SHIP MANAGEMENT

tors (Domberger, 1998). The decision to outsource reflects the general outlook of the company and not simply the competitive strategy that it follows, as it changes its shape as an organisation; this decision is thus taken at the top management level (Quelin & Duhamel, 2003). The change in the shape of a company is linked to the fact that, through outsourcing, it gains access to the resources of other companies. The company’s value chain includes those in-house functions performed by the company itself which contribute to the creation of value, as well as others which are carried out externally, yet which add value to its services. For the management of the company to decide whether or not to proceed with the strategic choice of outsourcing a function, it must first answer the following three questions (Quinn & Hilmer, 1994):

❚ ❚ ❚

Could the company gain a competitive advantage by performing the function in-house? If the function is outsourced to an external collaborator, what is the likelihood that there will be a negative effect if problems arise in the market? What can the company do to minimise the possible negative effects by structuring arrangements with the collaborators to control how the function is performed, yet without losing flexibility?

The decision to outsource functions and its implementation require a detailed analysis to have first been carried out by the company, with the use of benchmarking, on two levels:

❚ ❚

internal benchmarking, to control its functions, analyse their effectiveness and select those which will be outsourced; external benchmarking, to select a suitable collaborator to whom the function will be outsourced and the relationship that will be developed with the contractor, the negotiation of the contract, and management of the outsourcing (Franceschini et al, 2003).

Outsourcing as a strategic choice can be analysed using transaction costs economics and the resource-based view of the firm (resources and competencies approach) (Arnold, 2000). According to the theory of transaction cost, each transaction has a cost for those performing it which arises from their behaviour and the conditions in which the transaction takes place. The factors which may influence those involved in the transaction are information asymmetry, the bounded rationality under which decisions may be taken and opportunism. At the same time, the conditions in which the transaction takes place are influenced by the degree of specialisation of the production factors, the uncertainty which may be due to the objective parameters or to the behaviour of individuals and the frequency with which it is performed. The parties to the transaction will attempt to reduce the cost by creating structures with which to regulate their transactions. The forms that these structures might take are continuously being shaped; at one end of the

value chain: the whole of the related business functions which contribute to the creation of value in the form of a product or service.

benchmarking: a process of constant measurement and comparison of the functions of the company with the comparable functions of the best companies in order to acquire information which will help it to improve. transaction cost: the cost created by the carrying out of a transaction in the market. bounded rationality: the person taking the decision does not have full knowledge of the situation and, consequently, must rely upon a partial picture of the given problem. information asymmetry: one of the parties to a transaction has more information than any other. opportunism: the unforeseeable selfish behaviour of a decisionmaker.

283

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

core competencies: combination of resources and skills which competitors do not possess and cannot easily imitate.

spectrum is the free market, in which the transactions are momentary and coordinated by the price mechanism, while at the other end are those companies which provide the functions relating to these transactions in-house. Between the market and the company there may be alternative hybrid forms, such as alliances between companies or the creation of permanent partnerships. When production requires only low specialisation and whatever is needed can easily be found in the free market, then the most appropriate choice is a market transaction. When there is a high level of specialisation and, hence, limited supply in the free market, then the most appropriate choice will be determined by the frequency of the transaction and the uncertainty and difficulty in measuring performance. For repeated transactions of high uncertainty which require specialised inputs that cannot easily be found in the free market, risk is reduced when they are supplied by the company itself in-house (Aubert & Rivard, 1996). According to the resource-based view, the company is a totality of tangible and intangible resources and competencies (Barney, 1991). Whether the company can gain a sustainable competitive advantage depends on its resources and competencies. The resource-based view holds that the company must perform in-house those activities which utilise its core competencies and are linked to its competitive advantage.

❚ Outsourcing ship management to third party ship managers

independent ship management company: company which professionally provides shipping companies with all the services which a ship needs for its operation. ship management: professional provision of a service or of a range of services by a management company unrelated to the ownership of the vessel.

284

The nature of the activities of shipping companies, especially those active in bulk shipping markets, creates the need to seek out services from third parties. Without these services, ships would not be able to offer their transportation services. The shipping agents, for example, who service and support the ships in the ports are external collaborators of the shipping companies and work with them on a longterm basis or to carry out individual transactions. Outsourcing in shipping, however, concerns functions which are part of the core operations of the shipping companies. Below this dimension of outsourcing will be analysed. The outsourcing of the activities of a shipping company involves contracting them out to an independent ship management company, which offers all the professional services which a ship requires for its operations and undertakes its management. Ship management concerns the provision of a service or a range of services by a ship management company which has no connection to the shipowner (Spruyt, 1990). The ship manager provides services in exchange for remuneration to the shipowner according to terms which are stipulated in a contract. The factor which distinguishes the shipowner from the professional manager is economic risk. The shipowner invests, takes on risk and, naturally, enjoys the earnings from the investment, while the professional ship manager is remunerated for the work it supplies to the shipping company. Thus, professional ship management may be defined as the “rendering of services under contract related to the systematic organisation of economic resources and transactions required for the maintenance of a ship as a revenue-earning entity” (Panayides, 2001:7).

OUTSOURCING OF SHIP MANAGEMENT

Factors which favour outsourcing The reasons which induce a shipping company to use the services of an independent ship management company for either the whole or a part of its fleet may vary. Among the most important are:

❚

❚

❚

❚

The lack of resources and competencies relating to the technological expertise of ship management. If a shipping company does not have the necessary resources which will enable it to develop competencies, both threshold and core, then it will resort to the option of contracting out either certain aspects or the whole of its technical management of the ships to an independent company. This category includes those companies which are created by investors who are not active exclusively in shipping and do not have the relevant expertise. For example, some companies founded solely to exploit the favourable tax regimes in Norway and Germany, which acted as an incentive to invest the profits made in other sectors of the economy in shipping, used ship management companies for the technical management of the ships. They can avoid taking on the costs for the creation of on-shore facilities for the management of the ships. In these cases, the company retains only those decisions of a strategic character which concern the commercial management of the ships, while the technical management is contracted to an independent company. This is particularly the case with those companies which see shipping only as an investment and not as a long-term professional activity. Operating with a cost which is not competitive. If the shipping company maintains a facility on shore and has developed competencies in ship management, which are, however, threshold competencies and do not create a cost situation which will increase its competitiveness, then it chooses to outsource either a part or the whole of its technical management of the ships to an independent company. This scenario includes those companies which, because of the size of their fleet, are not in a position to use economies of scale to reduce their operating cost. Access to resources such as crews. Many companies, in order to be able to use the potential advantages available in the global shipping labour market, choose to outsource to independent ship management companies operations relating to crew management (see Chapter 8). This is, of course, closely linked to the company’s priorities. If its priority is not the short-term reduction of costs, then the shipping company has several alternative choices so as to enjoy the advantages of the global shipping labour market. In this case the company may take a selective approach. For example, the TORM company implements a strategy of developing a fully owned process, from top to bottom and considers it as the key to success. The only exception is the use of a manning agency in Croatia for less than 10% of its seafarer pool, approximately 150 Croatian officers. It was a manning agent with which they worked for many years. (Keefe, 2016)

285

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

❚

❚

❚ ❚

❚

The desire to avoid dangers that may result from the increasing complexity and risk involved in managing the shipping company. Complexity is the result of introducing new regulations and reforms with the aim of fulfilling the shipping industry’s attempts to respond to society’s expectations for services which respect the environment, and are safe and of high standards (see Chapters 4 and 9). The desire to differentiate the fleet and acquire different types of vessels. If the company expands to include new types of vessels for which not only does it not have the technological expertise necessary to operate them but the number of ships still does not create economies of scale in order to develop an effective management structure, then the company will select an external collaborator. For example, in 2007, Tsakos Energy Navigation (TEN, www.tenn.gr) decided to expand into the LNG market (see Box 3.1). For the management of its first LNG ship, it involved, through the outsourcing of the technical management of the ship, a third party manager (TEN, 2011). The desire to focus on those operations which are considered the most crucial and productive for the company, such as chartering and financial management (on Frontline’s strategy, see Box 10.1). The desire to keep the operational costs of the ships at the same level, irrespective of market conditions, given that there is a possibility that a company which is not in a position to exploit economies of scale in its management will be obliged during periods of low freight rates to take measures to reduce its costs, which may have an impact on the quality of its services (Drewry, 2011). The use of outsourcing as a tool in benchmarking. Some shipping companies outsource all or part of the management tasks for certain ships in their fleet, in order to collect data which will allow them to compare their operational costs with those of the independent management company. StealthGas and Frontline, for example, use more than one independent management company (for different ships in their fleets), so as to be able to compare their performances or gain knowledge in order to strengthen their negotiating position with them (Boxes 10.1 and 10.2).

BOX 10.1 Outsourcing and benchmarking – Frontline The adoption of benchmarking is fairly common among companies which apply the strategy of outsourcing to their entire fleet. To this category belongs Frontline (www. frontline.bm), one of the largest shipping companies in the world, with a fleet of 18 Suezmax tankers, 20 VLCCs, 15 Long Range Product Tankers (LR2), one Medium Range Product Tanker and three Aframax tankers (as of June 2017), which it has created through the continual buy-out of other companies.

286

OUTSOURCING OF SHIP MANAGEMENT

One of the principles it bases its strategy on is outsourcing technical operations and crewing to independent ship management companies. As mentioned in the Annual Report for 2016, the company has a “strategy of extensive outsourcing, which includes the outsourcing of management, crewing and accounting services to a number of independent and competing suppliers”. Benchmarking is acknowledged as a central part of Frontline’s strategy. Thus, they compare the operational performance and the cost level of the ship managers to whom they outsource the management of the vessels (Frontline, 2017). This strategy is based on the company’s aim of focusing its interests on areas where it feels it has the core competencies. At a round table discussion organised by Ship Management International (Jensen, 2007), the Commercial Director of Frontline at the time stated that the company focuses on making money rather than spending money. In other words, they are more interested in two of the three groups of the task of management described in Chapter 7, namely the group which develops policy and the group which deals with income. For this reason, it has created a lean organisation (which at the time in question had 40 employees), which deals with essential operations such as finance optimisation and commercial matters. The other group which deals with the task of management – the one for expenses which requires more personnel – is contracted to independent managers, even if it is seen as an important part of the company’s operations. In order to be in a position to control the way in which the independent managers work and the extent to which they apply standards, they have technical departments in their offices. In this way they ensure that the quality of the service which each charterer of Frontline ships receives is the same, irrespective of whether or not an independent ship management company manages each vessel (SMI, 2007a). Even so, the strategic choice to outsource the management of the ships has created problems for Frontline, as some of the companies with which it collaborated (which also competed with each other) were merged, with the result that the opportunity for benchmarking was limited, and, moreover, the charterers of the company’s ships have raised points of concern. The reason for this was because one of the companies which was created after the mergers managed ships which belonged to other shipping companies, something which was not to the liking of an oil company which chartered Frontline ships. This resulted in the oil company rejecting the ships of this management company for a period, which had consequences for Frontline too. This led the company to adapt its strategy somewhat, so as to give it alternative options (SMI, 2010b). Before coming to a decision on how to resolve the problem, it examined the available options. One solution was to collaborate with another independent ship manager, although this would not have had any significant results as the operating model would have

287

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

remained the same. Another option was to buy out one of the independent ship management companies or to collaborate with it in order to create a new joint company. Another choice was insourcing and carrying out the ship management in-house. This, however, would effectively have constituted a complete transformation in the company’s strategy, as described above: a lean organisation which focuses on those operations where it is competitive. Frontline thus decided to create a new company, starting from the development of the relevant competencies. In 2009, then, it established its own management company, SeaTeam (www.sea-team.com), which it commissioned to manage a number of ships in its fleet, while the rest would continue to be managed by independent ship management companies. SeaTeam is controlled by Frontline but is autonomous and can operate competitively in the market on its own. What Frontline is trying to achieve, therefore, “is a hybrid between third party management and in-house management” (SMI, 2010a). Through this company, it can perform benchmarking among the independent companies with which it continues to collaborate for the management of the remainder of the ships in its fleet. Apart from a number of Frontline ships, SeaTeam also manages ships which belong to Golden Ocean, the company which manages the dry bulk cargo fleet of the Fredriksen group (Sea-Team, 2017).

BOX 10.2 Outsourcing and benchmarking – StealthGas StealthGas (www.stealthgas. com), which is listed on Nasdaq, established a fleet of 26 liquid petroleum gas (LPG) carriers between October 2004 and June 2005. During this period, it had outsourced the management of its ships to five different companies, in order to be in a position to benchmark their performances. It subsequently ended its partnerships with two of these companies, to which it had outsourced the management of ten ships, because of their poor performance, and decided to organise the functions which the companies had initially been assigned in-house. The reason they gave was that they “realised that with good people and with special attention we can manage our ships with lower running costs” (SMI, 2007b:8). Yet, even though performing these functions in-house reduced costs, StealthGas and the other companies in its group continued to outsource ship management to independent companies as, for the purposes of the effective management of the fleet, apart from hiring a large number of seafarers, the company also had to double the size of its onshore operations, thus also doubling the number of its employees, a feat that was not simple to achieve. According to the CEO of StealthGas, in order to

288

OUTSOURCING OF SHIP MANAGEMENT

manage in-house the 84 ships which the group then owned (October 2008 data), it would have had to secure the required number of crew members and double the personnel in its offices from 100 to 200. This, however, would be difficult to achieve immediately and so the strategy of partial outsourcing continued. “The goal is to continue splitting the whole fleet in three which is one third managed in-house, one third operated on bareboat charter, and one third split between two third party ship managers” (SMI, 2008:9). According to the information given in the company’s Annual Report, this strategy continued (StealthGas, 2016).

❚

❚

❚

Utilising favourable market conditions and the rapid growth of a company’s fleet in a short space of time. Rapid growth can make it difficult to hire the right personnel for the on-shore development and staffing of the ships, as well as for the development of the necessary competencies for the whole of the company’s fleet (Box 10.2). To this category belong, for example, the shipping companies which are listed on the New York Stock Exchange and which, during the period of great growth in the shipping markets (2004–2008), used the opportunity to attract capital and expand their fleets at an impressive rate, outsourcing the management of some or all of their ships to independent management companies. When external factors and the company’s stakeholders encourage outsourcing. This is the option which the Overseas Shipholding Group (OSG, www. osg.com), for example, was obliged to adopt. The situation in the shipping markets at the time and the difficulties this created for the company’s survival should be taken into consideration when analysing and attempting to understand the options which this company took (Box 10.3). The investment strategy of the shipping company. The approaches that a shipping company can take towards its investment strategy can be grouped into two categories. The first category mainly involves planning investments and purchasing ships in order to offer transport services over a long period. The second group sees ships as an asset with the aim of buying and selling ships on the markets (Hope & Boe, 1981). Those companies which apply the second approach may not only have the ships under their management for a short period of time, but they also very frequently expand and reduce the size of their fleets. In these conditions, it is very difficult to plan and organise the on-shore support facilities for the operation of the ships, and even more difficult to hire and retain the necessary number of personnel. The most expedient solution, then, is to collaborate with independent ship management companies, at least for the crewing part of the task of management. In this case, using an independent company so as to maintain the flexibility to sell the ship means that the advantages of working with an independent management company are not made the most of, as the partnership may be very brief (Drewry, 2011).

289

BOX 10.3 From insourcing to ousourcing operations – OSG In the mid-2000s, OSG benefited from the use of a number of in-house technical operations. These included a faster response to customers’ needs and a quicker reaction to regulatory changes, better control over quality and ship crews and a longterm perspective for the management of the fleet and its maintenance. For the in-house management of technical operation of the fleet OSG technical centres in Athens, Newcastle and New York were responsible (OSG, 2006). Nonetheless, in 2014, the company’s strategy changed. OSG formed a partnership with the management company V.Ships (www.vships.com) to outsource the technical management of the OSG International Flag conventional tanker fleet (OSG, 2016). The outsourcing strategy was combined with that of placing the entirety of the fleet into a pool. According to OSG, the incentive behind the decision to outsource was to provide the company with a more competitive cost structure. The company’s decision was the result of the problems it faced within a difficult shipping market environment and its efforts to continue the restructuring process and emerge from Chapter 11 (the chapter of US bankruptcy code which permits the reorganisation of a debtor) (Marketwatch, 2012) as a stronger and more focused business. The company aimed to outsource technical management for international vessels in order to focus on the strategic direction of the business by retaining key operational and commercial oversight (gCaptain, 2014).

Factors against outsourcing The above explains the reasons as to why a company may choose to outsource operations to an independent management company. For which reasons, however, does a company choose not to outsource, or, to the degree that there is outsourcing, why does it change its strategy and insource instead? Several years ago, in a related study, different reasons were proposed as to why a ship-owning company may not choose to outsource the technical management of its ships, which continue to be relevant. These include (Spruyt, 1990):

❚ ❚ ❚ ❚ ❚

290

the fact that it may achieve operational performance, which is key to profit ratio and quality and which may distinguish the shipowner from competitors, something they do not want to lose, the company may wish to keep very close control of its fleet operation, in order to maintain the brand image that it has also built through the human resources which it employs in the long term, so as not to disrupt the conditions which permit the fleet to operate with high safety levels, to remain close to its well-trained ship crews, who have been with the company for many years, and to be able to respond better to their needs,

OUTSOURCING OF SHIP MANAGEMENT

❚ ❚ ❚

so as not to lose the glamour attached to shipping, so as not to lose the national character that they gain from having ships which fly the national flag and employ loyal employees, for reasons stemming from its business culture and philosophy.

In addition to the above, one must consider certain other factors which require comment. As has already been mentioned, the phenomenon can be observed of companies which outsource changing their strategy and choosing instead to insource. This happens when the necessary conditions in terms of resources and competencies have been satisfied in such a way as to favour the in-house management of a company’s ships. This can be verified in the case of shipping companies which are listed on a stock exchange and which initially selected to outsource but which then, over time, gradually chose to insource their technical management. For example, Star Bulk Carriers (www.starbulk.com) managed two of the ships in its fleet in-house and had outsourced the remainder of its fleet to a large independent management company. Its goal was to benchmark the performance of its own management with that of the independent manager (Starbulk, 2008). Over time, however, its strategy changed (Starbulk, 2009) and by the end of 2009 it had decided to create a wholly-owned subsidiary for in-house ship management, a choice which it considered would allow it to take advantage of synergies and economies of scale and ensure quality operations and continuous compliance with environmental regulations and maritime safety standards. As is mentioned in the Annual Report for 2016, the company believes that having control over the commercial and technical management provides us with a competitive advantage over many of our competitors by allowing us to more closely monitor our operations and to offer higher quality performance, reliability and efficiency in arranging charters and the maintenance of our vessels. They also believe that it contributes to maintaining a lower level of vessel operating and maintenance costs. A further reason as to why the shipowner may not choose outsourcing for the whole or largest part of its fleet, which is connected with what has been discussed above, is so as not to lose the competencies and expertise which are part of the specific operations which they carry out in-house. The Norden company carries out the technical management of the greatest part of its fleet inhouse, although it also outsources the management of a part of the fleet to an independent manager. The management of the largest part of the privately owned fleet enables the company to use economies of scale. This choice reflects the desire to maintain the technical competencies in-house as they are important in supporting the strategy of buying and selling ships, an important strategic priority for the company. However, in order to be able to benchmark the vessels’ technical operations and operating costs, Norden also outsources the technical management of part of its fleet (Norden News, 2014). “In short, my ambitions are for NORDEN’s commercial departments to think that the best ship management is the one

The traditional shipowner brings something unique to the business. I know personally from some clients that charterers like traditional family shipowners – good, professional, transparent companies that follow corporate practices, but still family companies where they know the person whose ‘yes’ is actually a ‘yes’. A stock-listed company has no head, but a family company can move quickly, with decision and authority. Andreas A. Martinos (2006:3)1 1 Used with permission from ABS.

291

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

delivered by NORDEN itself.” That answer, given by the head of the Technical Department of Norden shipping to the question regarding his ambitions for his department, underlines the basic motive behind the strategy of Norden in developing technical management in-house for part of its fleet and outsourcing the relevant activity for the rest (Norden News, 2016:14). The available data shows that shipowners of certain nationalities are more open to outsourcing while others are more negative (Panayides & Cullinane, 2002; Drewry, 2006; Mitroussi, 2004a, 2004b; Cariou & Wolff, 2011). To the above factors, then, we should also add the business and national culture of the shipowner, which could lead them to seek to retain complete control of the operations of their companies and thus not have a positive outlook towards the potential use of ship management companies. Greek shipowners, for example, belong to this category, as do the shipowners of national fleets which are dominated by family businesses. To a large degree, the shipowners of this category are personally involved in the crucial areas of technical management, especially if their previous professional experience entailed vessel management (Theotokas, 1997; Theotokas & Harlaftis, 2009). A similarly hesitant stance towards the outsourcing of technical management can be observed among Chinese shipowners. Even so, conditions are changing and the activities of Chinese companies are becoming internationalised, making it necessary for them to seek services for the technical management of their vessels in the open market. As a result, Chinese shipowners have developed a greater understanding of the value of outsourcing ship management to third parties (Fairplay, 2012). Yet another factor in the choice not to assign the entirety of the technical management of the ships to an independent company stems from the desire to maintain flexibility when deciding upon the sale and acquisition of vessels for the purposes of asset play. To this category belong many small fleet managers who wish to be able to take quick decisions and thus make the most of opportunities which arise in the market for vessels (Drewry, 2011).

❚ Independent ship management companies As has already been mentioned, independent ship management companies offer a range of professional services, either one or more, which a ship needs for its operations as well as for its maintenance on shore. They are not, however, the owners of the ship (Spruyt, 1990). The relationship between shipowner and ship manager is one of principal and agent. The fact that independent ship management companies do not have a relationship of ownership with the ships which they manage does not mean that they do not have a more general relationship with owning ships. Several large independent management companies are subsidiaries of large shipping companies, from which they also draw their management experience. At the same time, there are independent management companies which have no relationship with owning ships. V.Ships (www.vships.com), for example, which manages a fleet with over 1,100 vessels of almost all types, as well as 40,000 seafarers and 3,000 on

292

OUTSOURCING OF SHIP MANAGEMENT

shore personnel in its 70 offices all over the world (June 2017), does not own a single vessel. There are, however, companies that began without owning any ships, but gradually started to acquire some, such as, for example, Interorient Navigation (www.interorientshipmanagement.com), which discovered that it could make greater profits from owning ships than managing those of third parties (SMI, 2007d). Similarly, Bernhard Schulte Shipmanagement (www. bs-shipmanagement.com), controlled by the Schulte Group, which provides management services for over 600 vessels, manages 95 fully and jointly-owned ships (BSM, 2017). There are also shipping companies which alongside their main activity of managing their own ships undertake the management of thirdparty shipowners. Rickmers (www.rickmers.com), for example, offers ship management services for its own and third-party vessels through its Maritime Services section. In 2016, it managed 114 ships, 32 of which were group-owned, 15 vessels of Rickmers Maritime, 14 joint ventures with Rickmers co-investment, seven vessels belonging to ship-owning funds (KGs) and 46 owned by third parties (Rickmers, 2017). The independent management companies constitute a sector that is open to competition, without barriers to entry. The sector of third-party ship managers has prospects, a fact that attracts players. Since demand for its services is differentiated according to the individual characteristics and needs of each shipowner, change can also be seen on the demand side. The intensity of the competition between the companies gives rise to two different trends (Panayides, 2003). The first has to do with consolidation. Through increasing their size with acquisitions and mergers, the companies increase not just their size but also their market share and their competitiveness. Economies of scale make it possible to reduce costs and offer more competitive prices to the shipowners or for the services, which provide a higher value for their clients, such as crew training. This can be done in a more organised and effective way by companies which employ a large number of seafarers and are therefore able to achieve lower average costs. The second trend, which moves in the opposite direction, concerns the effort made by some companies to differentiate themselves either through the strategic marketing which they use or through their specialisation in the supply of particular services in specific geographical regions or specific market sectors. The tendency can hence be observed of the creation of companies which choose to manage a fleet size which permits them to build their services around specific client needs. This tendency has a greater acceptance among those shipping companies which appear more hesitant to trust the independent companies. These smaller management companies are created by staff with experience in shipping or by shipping companies which wish to make the most of technical management competencies in order to build new businesses. For example, the Shipmanagement Company-TSC (www.ship-management.de) was established in 2013 by three German shipping companies, H. Vogemann (ww.vogemann.de), Reederei Roth (www.reederei-roth.de) and HJH Shipmanagement (www.hjh-shipping.com) (Horizons, 2014). The first two companies were already collaborating, as ten years previously, Reederei Roth had undertaken the technical management of Vogemann’s fleet. Their decision to set up a new company was also influenced

293

M A N A G E M E N T O F S H I P P I N G C O M PA N I E S

Figure 10.1 Important criteria for choice and evaluation of the independent management companies Source: Based on Panayides & Cullinane (2002: Tables 4, 5, 7 and 8).

by the fact that there was increasing demand for ship management services from shipowners in Germany and internationally, especially those shipowners who had problems and in some cases serious ones with the banks which financed their vessels. Within this context, the partners decided to create a new venture which would be of a size that would permit them to be closer to their clients and develop personal relationships with them. Figure 10.1 gives the most important criteria in the choice and evaluation of the independent management companies, as the research has shown. It appears, then, that those criteria which relate to the management competencies of the company’s human resources (technical specialisation, problem solving) are considered more important for both the selection and assessment of the ship managers. In contrast, the cost criteria, as they are manifested in the price, are not considered as equally important. Criteria associated with the quality of the service, such as how reliable and responsible the managers are, as well as the relations between supplier and client, such as trust and loyalty, are considered more important than price (Panayides & Cullinane, 2002).

Services offered by independent ship management companies Independent ship management companies can offer the shipowner a wide range of services which cover the operations detailed in Chapter 3. The standard services which third-party managers offer are included in the Standard Ship Management Agreement (SHIPMAN), the standard contract for ship management services provision agreement of BIMCO. Even so, the services which are offered continue to expand as third-party ship managers, sensing the trends and needs of the market, are expanding into other services (Panayides, 2001). Services can be grouped together as technical management, crew management, commercial management and ancillary services. The technical management and crew management services can be offered together as full management. Each group contains the following services (Willingale, 1998):

294

OUTSOURCING OF SHIP MANAGEMENT

❚ ❚ ❚ ❚

Technical management: purchasing, maintenance, inspection, performance monitoring, budgeting, reporting, safety and quality management, drydocking, certification, emergency contingency and insurance of ships. Crew management: selection and engagement, manning levels, certification control, performance appraisal, payroll, travel, welfare, drugs and alcohol, training, insurance and reporting. Commercial management: marketing, voyage estimating, chartering, post fixture, voyage accounting, payments and agency. Ancillary services: consultancy, insurance, legal and financial.

The main categories shown in Figure 10.2 cover all those services which are essential for the management of a ship. The analysis contained in this chapter has shown that it is the choice of the shipowner as to how it will put together the basket of services that it will outsource to a third-party manager. As a rule, the most integrated independent managers will offer the full range of services, although it is possible that they may be labelled and grouped differently. For example V.Ships (www.vships.com) groups together the services it offers as Technical Services, Workforce Services and Commercial Services (V.Group, 2017). Wilhelmsen Ship Management offers the following third-party ship management services: technical management; crew management; health, safety, environment and quality; vessel accounting; and procurement services. They also offer a group of value-added services which include lay-up management, drydocking, inventory of hazardous material, green recycling and maritime training (Wilhelmsen, 2017).
